Above all, journal club should be a forum for learning and for the expansion of scientific knowledge in ecology, evolution and behavior.

title: MCB Journal Club
description: This journal club focuses on Molecular Cell Biology literature.

title: Shaping National Science Policy
description: This seminar introduces students to science advocacy. It begins with introductory discussions of the political system. Then, it transitions into hardball politics with guest-hosted lectures from congressional science staff, science journalists, and science lobbyists. The seminar concludes with an examination of five case studies in science advocacy. Students work on one project throughout the semester. They break into teams, identify a politically relevant science issue of their own choosing, develop an advocacy strategy, and then take their issue to Congress. The seminar is three credits. Biology students count one credit towards a degree. Physics students count the class as an elective towards a degree. Course previously numbered BIOL 362

title: Department  Activities
description: Informal discussions of topics of current interest to biologists.

title: Department  Seminar Series
description: Weekly seminar series offered by the Department of Biology.

title: Grad Contin Regis Biology
description: Graduate Student Continuing Registration in Biology

title: Grad Contin Regis Biology
description: Graduate Student Continuing Registration in Biology

title: Continuing Registration
description: Continuing Registration for Graduate Students.

title: Graduate Biology Thesis Research
description: Conduct research towards completion of the PhD or MS degrees under the guidance of a faculty member. Section 01: for students who are taking or have takend 16-cr or less of courses; Section 03: for students taking courses beyond the 16-cr required; Section 05: for students who are paid as teaching assistants.

title: Introductory Biostatistics: Exp Des/Anlys
description: This course is designed for introductory biostatistical theory and application for students pursuing a master's degree in fields outside of the Department of Biostatistics, Bioinformatics, and Biomathematics. Students first learn the four pillars of exploring and displaying data appropriately, exploring relationships between two variables, issues of gathering sample data, and understanding randomness and probability. On these pillars, students then can develop the platform for statistical inference including proportions and means, multiple regression, and ANOVA. Textbook: Principles of Biostatistics, 2nd edition; Marcello Pagano, Cengage Learning ISBN: 9780534229023

title: Epidemiology and Public Health
description: Epidemiology is the scientific discipline of public health. It therefore plays a central role in the identification, characterization, and control of risk factors for human diseases. The course will begin with an overview of the history of epidemiology, followed by consideration of chronic and acute disease rates by time, place, and person, and how the major types of epidemiological study designs (cross-sectional, case-control, cohort, and randomized trials) address these public health concerns. The course will provide information on the basic methods of analysis associated with the study design, with an emphasis on critically reading and evaluating the epidemiological literature. Special topics, such as screening studies, cancer epidemiology and prevention, and infectious diseases will also be introduced. This course includes lectures and discussion sessions. Textbook (Spring 2011): Epidemiology, 4th Edition by Leon Gordis, WB Saunders Co., Philadelphia ISBN: 141-604-0021

title: Probability and Sampling
description: The goal of the course is to convey an understanding of probability and distribution theory. The probability theory is necessary to provide a foundation for statistics. Probability theory: set theory and probability theory, conditional probability and independence, random variables, distribution functions, density and mass functions for continuous and discrete random variables. Transformation and expectations: distributions of functions of a random variable, expected values, moments and moment generating functions. Common families of distributions: discrete and continuous distributions, exponential family, and location-scale family. Multiple random variables: joint and marginal distributions, conditional distributions and independence, covariance and correlation, multivariate distributions, hierarchical models and mixture distributions. Sampling theory: normal theory, limit theorems.

title: Probability and Sampling
description: The goal of the course is to convey an understanding of probability and distribution theory. The probability theory is necessary to provide a foundation for statistics. Probability theory: set theory and probability theory, conditional probability and independence, random variables, distribution functions, density and mass functions for continuous and discrete random variables. Transformation and expectations: distributions of functions of a random variable, expected values, moments and moment generating functions. Common families of distributions: discrete and continuous distributions, exponential family, and location-scale family. Multiple random variables: joint and marginal distributions, conditional distributions and independence, covariance and correlation, multivariate distributions, hierarchical models and mixture distributions. Sampling theory: normal theory, limit theorems.

title: Statistical Inference
description: This course will introduce the basics of statistical inference, parameter estimation, and hypothesis testing in preparation for more in depth coverage of specific models in later courses. Inference procedures: point and interval estimation, sufficient statistics, hypothesis testing, methods of constructing test and estimation procedures. Point estimation: criteria for estimators, maximum likelihood estimators, Bayes estimators, mean square error, unbiased estimators, asymptotic variance of estimators. Hypothesis testing: error probabilities, power function, one-sample inference about the mean with known and unknown variance, comparison of two samples, 2Ã2 contingency tables, shortcuts and non-parametric methods. Modeling and study design: missing data, extreme observations, transformations, factorial experiments, probability sampling, sample size, two-stage sampling, stratified sampling, nonsampling errors.

title: Statistical Inference
description: This course will introduce the basics of statistical inference, parameter estimation, and hypothesis testing in preparation for more in depth coverage of specific models in later courses. Inference procedures: point and interval estimation, sufficient statistics, hypothesis testing, methods of constructing test and estimation procedures. Point estimation: criteria for estimators, maximum likelihood estimators, Bayes estimators, mean square error, unbiased estimators, asymptotic variance of estimators. Hypothesis testing: error probabilities, power function, one-sample inference about the mean with known and unknown variance, comparison of two samples, 2Ã2 contingency tables, shortcuts and non-parametric methods. Modeling and study design: missing data, extreme observations, transformations, factorial experiments, probability sampling, sample size, two-stage sampling, stratified sampling, nonsampling errors.

title: Categorical Data Analysis
description: Textbook: An Introduction to Categorical Data Analysis by Alan Agresti; ISBN: 0471113387

title: Categorical Data Analysis
description: 3 modules: regression and ANOVA, generalized linear models, longitudinal models. Lab teaches SAS procedures to fit these models. Regression and ANOVA : fitting a linear regression, least squares method, regression in observational studies, estimation and prediction of Y for a given X, predicted sample mean of Y, testing large deviation, no intercept model, correlation coefficient, bivariate normal distribution, testing single correlation, comparing correlations, rank correlation, 2ÃC contingency tables, linear trend, RÃC contingency tables, fixed-effect ANOVA, model notation, F-test, planned comparisons, orthogonal comparisons, random effects model, homogeneity of variance, randomized blocks, latin squares. Generalized Linear Models : exponential family, proportion, count and rate data, link functions, estimation, logistic and Poisson regressions, fixed and random effects, repeated measures, model selection. Longitudinal Models: generalized estimating equations, estimation, clustered data, linear mixed models, model fit. Textbook: Applied Linear Statistical Models with Student CD, 5th Edition, by Kutner, Nachtsheim, Neter and Li, McGraw-Hill Higher Education, 2005 ISBN: 9780073108742

title: Survival Analysis
description: TBA

title: Survival Analysis
description: Concepts and methods for analyzing survival time data and life tables. Introduction to basic concepts of survival analysis, including hazard functions, survival functions, types of censoring and truncation, Kaplan-Meier estimates and its extension to the comparison of survival prospects of several groups of courses using the logrank tests (and their generalization) and confidence intervals for relative risks, emphasizing graphical displays and assessing underlying assumptions. The connection between Mantel-Haenszel methods and survival analysis is thus emphasized. Parametric inference includes likelihood estimation and the exponential, Weibull, log-logistic and other relevant distributions. Statistical methods and theory for the proportional hazard models (Cox model) discussed in detail, with extensions to time-dependent covariates. Clinical and epidemiological examples included in class presentation and homework to illustrate various statistical procedures. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: Probability and sampling, statistical inference, and basic SAS/R programming.

title: Linear Models and Multivariate Analysis
description: This course is an applied course on statistical modeling. The linear models module covers simple linear regression, multiple linear regression, analysis of variance, analysis of covariance, regression diagnostics, and model selection. The multivariate analysis module includes multivariate analysis of variance, principal components analysis, canonical correlation analysis, factor analysis, discriminant analysis, and cluster analysis. Students will learn how to use SAS to perform statistical analyses. Textbook: Applied Linear Statistical Models with Student CD, 5th Edition, by Kutner, Nachtsheim, Neter and Li, McGraw-Hill Higher Education, 2005 ISBN: 9780073108742

title: Introduction to Statistical Software
description: BIST 515 is an introductory course to the open-source programming language R and the popular statistical software SAS. Basic syntax and simple applications to Biostatistics and Bioinformatics will be presented.

title: Bioinformatics for Omics Data
description: This course is a combination of theories and empirical skills on managing, processing and analyzing high-throughput biomedical data generated from a variety of "Omics" technologies, which spans genomics, trascriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics. It introduces the students to the conceptual and experimental background, together with specific guidelines for handling raw data. Hand-on skills with R/Bioconductor and other software tools will be covered on popular "Omics" applications, such as microarray gene expression profiling, mass spectrometry-based metabolomics, RNA-seq, pathway analysis, and etc.

title: Principles of Epidemiology
description: Epidemiology overview and history; distributions of disease by time, place and person; association and causality; ecological studies; cross-sectional studies and surveys; case-control studies; analysis of case-control studies; types of bias in case-control studies; cohort studies; analysis of cohort studies; bias in cohort studies; population attributable risk; confounding factors; effect modification (interaction); analysis for confounding and interaction; multivariate analysis; sensitivity, specificity and screening; public health practice and prevention; special issues in cancer epidemiology, infectious disease epidemiology and genetic epidemiology. This course includes a discussion session.

title: Principles of Epidemiology
description: Epidemiology overview and history; distributions of disease by time, place and person; association and causality; ecological studies; cross-sectional studies and surveys; case-control studies; analysis of case-control studies; types of bias in case-control studies; cohort studies; analysis of cohort studies; bias in cohort studies; population attributable risk; confounding factors; effect modification (interaction); analysis for confounding and interaction; multivariate analysis; sensitivity, specificity and screening; public health practice and prevention; special issues in cancer epidemiology, infectious disease epidemiology and genetic epidemiology. This course includes a discussion session.

title: Quantitative Data Analysis and Reporting
description: The goal of this course is to enhance studentsâ skills for identifying appropriate choices of statistical methodologies; and developing statistical approaches to applied research problems and effective communication of the approaches and findings. The course will give students hands on experience in statistical applications. The course will be organized around a series of case studies based on applied problems from different sources with a focus on application of statistical techniques as opposed to the subject matter of the data at hand. Students will formulate statistical approaches to the applied research problems; perform exploratory data analysis, model building and statistical inference; write reports, make oral presentations of results of analyses and interactively discuss analysis aspects of the case studies.

title: Consulting
description: This course offers instruction, discussion and hands-on experience providing statistical consultation in applied scientific situations. These will typically include survival analysis, clinical trial/study design, tumor growth curves, microarray analysis, and proteomics projects. Instruction and experience will focus on consulting strategy. This includes preparing analysis plans and reports, communication and time management skills, and ethics/professional standards. Additionally, students will gain consulting practice to include interactions with investigators with actual projects and problems. Students will attend class and weekly consulting/debriefing sessions, prepare analysis plans and reports, and give oral presentations describing consulting projects over the semester.

title: Practicum
description: Students will be involved in a research project under the supervision of a faculty member. While the consulting class will expose them to short-term projects, the practicum will provide them with an opportunity to implement a combination of the skills they have acquired and to extend them in a limited context. This practical experience should span 3-4 months. The project will be written up as a Masterâs paper including the following sections: background to the problem, experimental design, description of the data, analytical methods, results, and interpretation of the latter. This paper will be defended orally, after no fewer than two faculty members (the advisor and one other) have read it and deemed it ready for presentation.

title: BIST Independent Study
description: This course requires department permission.

title: Independent Study in Epidemiology
description: This course requires department permission.

title: Ideas, Institutions, and the Evolution of Business Enterprise: The Case of Professional Sports Leagues
description: This seminar considers the ideas and institutions that were central to the development of business enterprise as the key productive element in capitalist economies and, second, the forces that led sports leagues to become important in these economies. Students first examine the central ideas for why market capitalism came to assume its current dominant role in the world economy. Next the course turns to the development of a market-based entertainment industry in the middle of the 19th century and the technological and economic factors that determined the timing and nature of this development. The final two phases of the course analyze the growth and evolution of professional sports leagues and examine the opportunities and challenges confronting these organizations in contemporary times.

title: Markets, Businesses, and Governments in a Global Economy
description: In this seminar, students explore the conceptual foundations of the relationships between and among markets, businesses, and government in the U.S. and abroad. Students examine the balance between democracy and capitalism, how economic interests are developed and expressed in democratic societies, and how regulatory institutions shape business strategies. Among the questions addressed are: Why do governments regulate markets? How do voter preferences, firm interests, and legislatorsâ agendas shape regulations? Why do firms sometimes seek and oppose various forms of regulation? Why do domestic and international regulatory institutions emerge, and what are their consequences? What difference do reforms make to national economic performance? The readings and cases come from historical and contemporary sources and will illuminate problems facing the U.S., other advanced industrial nations, and emerging market nations.

title: The Miracle of Markets?
description: This seminar focuses on the potential and perils of markets and questions whether markets are the âmiracleâ to push economic well-being forward or are sowing the seeds of societyâs demise. Discussions will explore: What are the big economic problems of society, and how have different societies sought to overcome these? Can technology and technological change overcome our economic challenges? Do different institutional structures matter? Why have markets and capitalism become central to modern economic life? Under what circumstances might markets fail, and how should (and do) different societies handle these market failures? Are markets ethical? The seminar addresses issues of profound importance to economic life, but does so by engaging students in these issues through the use of modern economic tensions and examples.

title: Grand Strategy: Conceptual Foundations in Strategic Thought
description: This seminar examines the theory and practice of a âgrand strategyâ in a historical as well as contemporary context. A grand strategy is defined as the calculated relation of means to larger ends. The seminar focuses on how parts of a strategy relate to the success of an endeavor and may be applied to the objectives of an individual, a corporation, or a nation. Readings are drawn from a diverse selection of works that explore the foundations of strategy, spanning a course of history that reaches back over two-and-a-half millennia. From a careful examination of the material, students will extract a set of practical principles useful in any future leadership role.

title: Patterns of Global Commerce
description: This seminar examines the impact globalization has generated on global trade and what companies need to do to react. Wage rate differentials, expanding foreign markets, faster information technologies, and improved transportation are breaking down barriers of time and space between countries, forcing companies to take on a global dimension. In other words, the world is becoming flat. What makes some companies more successful than others? The definition of Global Supply Chains is at the core of the answer. Competing in this ânewâ globalized world requires a different approach, and this seminar will replace conventional thinking about management in a global world with fresh approaches. Students will analyze different approaches for coordinating and working with suppliers This seminar focuses on the potential and perils of markets and questions whether markets are the âmiracleâ to push economic well-being forward or are sowing the seeds of societyâs demise. Discussions will explore: What are the big economic problems of society, and how have different societies sought to overcome these? Can technology and technological change overcome our economic challenges? Do different institutional structures matter? Why have markets and capitalism become central to modern economic life? Under what circumstances might markets fail, and how should (and do) different societies handle these market failures? Are markets ethical? The seminar addresses issues of profound importance to economic life, but does so by engaging students in these issues through the use of modern economic tensions and examples. This seminar examines the evolution of commerce between nations from geographical, historical, political, business, and ethical perspectives. It will focus on the dynamics of trade in scarce resources, food, minerals and fuels, armaments, legal and illegal drugs, human beings, intellectual property, and services. Students will explore historical dimensions and different current viewpoints regarding the patterns, benefits, and detriments of global commerce. Why and what do nations trade? How does East meet West and North meet South in international trade and investment? What trade-offs exist between economic and social interests? Can nations be open to trade and still âprotectâ their domestic economies? Is âfree tradeâ a zero-sum game, or does it allow win-win situations? Can trade be âfairâ? The readings and discussions provide and strategic partners in a companyâs global supply chain and strategies for aligning the incentives of the different players throughout the supply chain to develop successful collaborative relationships.

title: The Ethics of Entrepreneurship
description: This seminar examines the theory and practice of a âgrand strategyâ in a historical as well as contemporary context. A grand strategy is defined as the calculated relation of means to larger ends. The seminar focuses on how parts of a strategy relate to the success of an endeavor and may be applied to the objectives of an individual, a corporation, or a nation. Readings are drawn from a diverse selection of works that explore the foundations of strategy, spanning a course of history that reaches back over two-and-a-half millennia. From a careful examination of the material, students will extract a set of practical principles useful in any future leadership role. This seminar considers the ideas and institutions that were central to the development of business enterprise as the key productive element in capitalist economies and, second, the forces that led sports leagues to become important in these economies. Students first examine the central ideas for why market capitalism came to assume its current dominant role in the world economy. Next the course turns to the development of a market-based entertainment industry in the middle of the 19th century and the technological and economic factors that determined the timing and nature of this development. The final two phases of the course analyze the growth and evolution of professional sports leagues and examine the opportunities and challenges confronting these organizations in contemporary times. This seminar invites students to think critically about important questions regarding entrepreneurship, including: What is an entrepreneur? What role do entrepreneurs play in the economy? What makes an entrepreneur tick? Can everyone be an entrepreneur? What are the virtues of an entrepreneur? What does it take to encourage entrepreneurship in a modern economy? What are some of the moral challenges entrepreneurs face? How do they, and how should they, meet these challenges? Students learn how entrepreneurs are the main agents of change in a modern, global economy and confront the responsibilities that come with being such agents.

title: Public Finance and Municipal Capital Markets: An Overview of Infrastructure Finance in the United States
description: This course will provide an overview of how infrastructure is financed in the United States. The course shall identify the various types of infrastructure that require funding including roads, bridges, airports, low income housing, hospitals, learning institutions, water and sewer systems, sports stadiums and what is required to fund each type. The structure of how such projects get funded will be examined through the municipal bond underwriting process, the participants involved in the process, the capital markets in which the issue is brought forward and how municipal credit is determined. The course is designed to satisfy the interest of students who wish to pursue careers in finance and banking, consulting, project finance, sports management, public management, healthcare, educational administration and not for profit management.

title: Project Finance: Emphasis on the Arab-Persian Gulf
description: Project Finance is quite similar from one region of the globe to another. The purpose of this class is to teach not only the nuts and bolts of project finance, but also to make it more lively and realistic by showing how it is applied in a part of the world where about one trillion dollars of industrial and infrastructure projects are being undertaken today. These projects include infrastructure [roads, airports, harbors and logistical hubs], energy [petroleum, LNG, NGLs and non-conventional sources.], chemicals, metals [mainly aluminum], etc. These projects are a sought after market by all the large Western firms in construction, engineering and consulting, but they also are undertaken by local private firms, public private partnerships and joint ventures. They all require extensive and sophisticated financial engineering. The course seeks to give students a good understanding of how the financing of large projects is done in the Arab-Persian Gulf. The class is very âhands onâ and rely a great deal on the experience of the instructor who was a banker and consultant in and to the Gulf states and companies. The instructor will also seek out and try to use the experience, which many of the students may have in project finance and Gulf projects. The class culminates in an extensive simulation which last a full day. The class will review the risk analysis to be undertaken by all the participants to a large project from the local and foreign investors, to the financing institutions and the governments. It will give a detailed view on how to structure the necessary financing ie debt vs. equity, what type of debts. It will go at length into evaluating how the governments of the Gulf look at major projects and what issues are important to them to provide support. Learning Goals: Upon completion of the ILE, students should be able to (1) understand the basics of project finance and (2) determine the appropriate variables used in evaluating whether a project is suitable for a particular region as well a profitable for investors.

title: China Jobs Lab
description: This election season, America's attention is focused on politics. America's politics, meanwhile, is focused on China. From the presidential contest to congressional races around the country, in countless ads, speeches, and debates, China has dominated the rhetoric, portrayed by both Democrats and Republicans as a job-killing economic menace. Yet, trade with China is creating and sustaining millions of jobs across America. In 94% of all congressional districts, exports to China doubled over the last decade -- often tripled, quadrupled, or more. And job-creating Chinese direct investment in U.S. firms has reached a near record high this year. But the din of the "Rising China/Declining America" meta-narrative drowns out this evidence of American resilience and enduring competitiveness. The China Jobs Lab will explore America's competitiveness in the coming century and how jobs can be sustained and created through China trade. We will begin on the ground with China's value chain, analyzing its structure and characteristics through case studies and primary sources. Special attention will be paid to the systemic risks that pervade China's industries, which give rise to China's demand for American goods and services. We will then connect the dots between China's economy and America's, exploring the U.S. services, manufacturing, and agricultural sectors, and their educational, infrastructural, and legal underpinnings. As part of this exploration, we will tour the facilities at the Port of Baltimore, a major artery of trade with China. The coursework will include a final project, in which students design businesses that create U.S. jobs through China trade. Guest lecturers will also be featured. America's enduring competitive advantages will be considered, as we seek to critically analyze the myths of Rising China and Declining America in the coming century. Learning Goals: Upon completion of the ILE, students should be able to understand (1) the basis of American competitiveness vis-Ã -vis China and its relationship to U.S. job creation, (2) Chinaâs value chain and how it generates demand for American products and services, (3) how jobs are being created and sustained today across the U.S. economy through China trade, and (4) how American companies can leverage U.S. competitive strengths to create jobs and build value.

title: Managing in the 21st Century: Leading Teams and Cross-cultural Diversity
description: Managing in the 21st century presents greater challenges due to the increased use of teams and the need to work effectively across cultures and diverse groups. Teams have emerged as a favored work arrangement for such activities as developing new products, providing professional services, and starting new businesses. Indeed, teams can be found at every level of the organization, from production teams on the shop floor to top management teams in the executive suite. Moreover, advances in communication technology have created the potential for people who are dispersed across the globe to collaborate seamlessly in virtual teams, creating many new opportunities and challenges for leaders of such teams. Second, within and across cultures, managers must learn to deal with people that come from various backgrounds and have different values and ways of communicating. Those that develop cross-cultural intelligence will be more successful. This course will look at how to lead teams more effectively â from designing teams, to managing team processes to dealing with different types of teams. We will focus on systems and processes to increase team effectiveness, as well as managing interpersonal dynamics that can help or hurt teams. This course will also focus on building your cross-cultural intelligence by learning about diversity involving gender, ethnicity, and national culture. Learning Goals: Upon completion of the ILE, students should be able to (1) understand the conditions that foster team effectiveness and the disruptive forces that can derail teams, (2) diagnose complex team dynamics and take action to improve team functioning, (3) build the combination of analytic and interpersonal skills you need to effectively lead teams, (4) discern relevant cultural values of those you work with, and (5) communicate more effectively with those from other cultural groups.

title: Understanding Mutual Funds
description: As of December 31, 2011, $23.8 trillion is invested worldwide in mutual funds. Yet, most mutual fund investors do not have an understanding of the basic statistics used to evaluate fund managers. Nor are most mutual fund investors aware of the latest academic research related to the models used to evaluate fund performance and various metrics used to predict future fund performance. This ILE will expose students to both the latest academic and practitioner research related to the mutual fund industry. Learning Goals: Upon completion of the ILE, students should be able to (1)understand the similarities and differences of various mutual fund investing styles, (2) calculate common mutual fund statistics when given a historical time series of fund returns, (3) describe situations where specific mutual fund statistics are more/less informative, (4) understand the strengths and weaknesses of various models used to evaluate mutual fund performance, (5) discuss the most recently developed metrics used by academics to predict fund performance, and (6) make and justify mutual fund recommendations given the profile of a hypothetical investor. Note: the ILE is not intended for those students who are interested in starting their own mutual fund.

title: Startup Factory
description: Have you ever wondered if you have what it takes to be an entrepreneur? Could you have the startup bug? Have you had inspiration strike you, but not known what to do with that idea? Are you interested in experiencing startup life in a fast-paced, experiential-focused course? Then this course is definitely for you. This course has been built to let you learn by doing -- based on the curricula developed by Startup Weekend, active in more than 100 countries, and the Lean LaunchPad, taught at Stanford, Berkeley, Columbia, Caltech and adopted by the National Science Foundation. We emphasize experiential learning, a flipped classroom and immediate feedback as a way to let you, the "want-rapreneur" to engage with real world entrepreneurship. We start with your ideas, problems and solutions. You'll learn by proposing and immediately testing hypotheses -- hypotheses around your own startup ideas and ideas of your classmates. Then you'll get out of the classroom and talk to customers, partners and competitors and encounter the chaos and uncertainty of commercializing innovations and creating new ventures. We focus on 'doing' rather than 'planning to do.' This course doesn't rely on static case studies or fixed models, you'll be challenged to create your own business models based on information derived from personal engagement rather than secondhand market research. Many successful and serial entrepreneurs will tell you that entrepreneurship cannot be taught; it must be experienced. Now is your chance to experience entrepreneurship yourself with successful entrepreneurs and walk away with skills, lessons and experiences to help in your next endeavors. Are you ready to experience entrepreneurship? Soon you will be.

title: Sales -- Art, Strategy, Process and Management
description: Whether selling a firmâs product and services, selling one's ideas and projects internally, or selling oneself to prospective employers or investors, understanding the underlying mechanics, process, strategy and skills of selling are key to every executive's success and advancement. In this class, students will not learn âhow to sellâ or be taught some multi-step sales metrology. Rather, students will be challenged to learn all of the different types and places, in their business life, where sales matters, understand the underlying reasons and motivation that make people âbuyâ, and, finally, to use this knowledge to craft an effective pitch or strategy. Learning Goals: Upon completion of this course, students should be able to (1) understand the theoretical and practical frameworks that support the creation and implementation of an effective sales strategy and (2) be proficient at pitching ideas, products, and services toward the successful sale.

title: Catholic Studies: Introduction and Explorations
description: CATH 111. Fall 2010. Explorations in Catholic Culture The central theme considered in the course is this: how does Catholic faith (as a religion of âboth/and,â not âeither/orâ) interact with various forms of human culture. The class will investigate Catholic tradition to see how it emerged from earlier, existing cultures and how it developed its own forms of cultural expression. We will in addition probe its relevance for contemporary society. We begin with narrative: the earliest stories about Jesus of Nazareth, the problems in reading them, and the central beliefs of Catholicism as they are derived from these accounts. After this overview, we look at four ways in which Catholicism has found expression within human cultural traditions. 1. Catholicism and society. What role do Catholics play in government and social organization and how does Catholicism relate its faith to public policy? We will read the recent papal encyclical on social and economic justice âCaritas in Veritate.â 2. Personal narratives. What meaning have people found in Catholicism in their own personal lives? Here we will read the autobiographies of Ignatius of Loyola [16th C.] and Dorothy Day [20th C.]. 3. The fine arts. How has Catholicism shaped aesthetic imagination? We will study the poetry, architecture, painting, and sculpture of Michelangelo. 4. The physical world. What can Catholicism say about nature, science, and present environmental concerns? We will consider authors such as Teilhard de Chardin and John Haught. There will be four student projects during the semester, two long and two relatively short essays. Tuesday / Thursday 1:15 â 2:30 P.M. ICC 118.

title: Smart, Catholic & Female
description: This seminar will address significant issues facing Catholic women (and so all Catholics) today. We will place particular emphasis on the lens through which women at Jesuit colleges and universities see these issues - and the ways in which their gender and experiences move them to frame these important questions and respond to them. This seminar is of course open to both men and women who want to be able to give an intelligent answer to the question, "How can someone be smart, Catholic and female?"

title: Contemporary Marriage and Family
description: This introductory course explores a contemporary approach to a theology of marriage and family from a Roman Catholic perspective. It provides an opportunity for students to study Catholic teaching and to discuss disputed questions. Students will write and present a 10 page paper that entails a dialogue between Church teaching and contemporary research on marriage and family. A brief sketch of the course follows: 1. The Sacramental Crisis (cultural and ecclesial) and Methodological Considerations (psychological, essentialist, and existentialist) 2. Sacramental Considerations (elements, ritual, definition, eucharist as basic sacrament, marriage as basic sacrament) 3. Philosophical, Psychological, and Sociological Considerations (Existentialism, Family as Developing System, family and social institutions) 4. Contemporary Hierarchical Magisterium (1950âs to present: sacrament of marriage, family as domestic church, sexuality, birth control) 5. Biblical and Theological Tradition (historical overview) 6. Toward a Lay-Centered Spirituality (models of spirituality, marriage and family as creative act, intimacy, facing the crisis) 7. Select Disputed Questions (examples: cohabitation, divorce, remarriage, faith and love) NOTE: This course satisfies one semester of the College Theology requirement. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: None

title: Dialogue & Catholic Life
description: This course explores a vision of Christian life in a pluralistic world based on an appreciation of dialogue as a major dimension of human life today. This Catholic vision that draws from engaging with all sources of knowledge is rooted at the beginnings of Christianity, found expression in major documents and theologies of the Second Vatican Council of the Catholic Church, and is embodied in the lives of Christians fulfilling the mission of Christ to make known the good news of the kingdom of God in the contemporary world. Four perspectives on dialogue in Christian life are explored: 1) dialogue among the voices of New Testament churches as expressed in scripture; 2) ecumenical dialogue among Christians to end the scandal of division, restore unity and unite Christians in their mission and witness to the world; 3) interreligious dialogue among Christians and peoples of other faiths; and 4) the Christian communityâs dialogue and engagement with the world promoting social justice and the freedom. Students will study how Christians live in relationship with all peoples, especially through the ecumenical movement among Christians and interreligious relations among all peoples of faith.

title: Sacred Music: A Survey of Music as Prayer
description: This course will trace the development of music as prayer through the ages, from the earliest recorded history to modern times. We will explore some of the ways in which church history and societal changes have affected the development of sacred music as an art form, as well as its role in worship. Topics to be discussed will include Plainchant, Jewish Liturgical Music, evolution of the Mass as a musical form, Bach and the Protestant Reformation, divergence of Catholic, Orthodox and Anglican traditions, Gospel Music, and music for the contemporary worship service. We will also study non-western traditions from a musical/historical standpoint, in order to gain a better global perspective. We will listen to and explore musical examples representative of each of the topics covered throughout the semester and opportunities for field study will be provided.

title: Yoga and Meditation
description: An introduction to meditation as religious practice through textual exploration of a variety of forms of yoga within the Hindu tradition and comparative theological study of Christian texts on meditation and prayer mostly from the Catholic tradition. Note: This course counts for one semester of the College Theology requirement.

title: Catholic Peacemaking
description: The premise of this course is that the tradition of Catholic social teaching stands on the verge of an important new development in its approach to war and peace -- but one that has yet to be worked out. "Catholic peacemaking," as it may be called, does not fit easily into traditional debates about war. Its focus is not on how to respond to discrete acts of injustice, but on how to help societies to seek the emerging opportunity of peace when it presents itself by seizing the moment and building on lessons learned from the destructive legacies of a violent past. What does the Catholic tradition have to say about actual acts of human destructiveness and about building a just order? What role do forgiveness and apology play? What role can the Church play? What relevance does this role have for politics? The course is unusual in giving students the opportunity to develop, not merely to study, a topic. Catholic approaches will be explored through theology, Christian history, case studies, and biography. Note: This course counts for a semester of the College Theology requirement.
