It will cover the current structure and history of the Social Security program along with the underlying macro and micro economic analysis needed to evaluate recent major reform options. In addition, the course will cover intensively the most recent developments in the reform debate, beginning with the last major legislative changes to the program in 1983, and ending with the 2001 Bush Commission on Social Security and current proposals for reform. Particular emphasis will be focused on the major economic issues underlying the political debate about "privatization". Fall.

title: Intermediate Econometrics
description: This course is a natural next step with respect to an introductory course in Econometrics. The focus will be on panel data, instrumental variables estimation and discrete choice models. Special emphasis will be placed on how to use these estimators in solving specific policy problems.

title: Mathematics for Economics
description: This course will introduce you to the mathematical tools employed in economics. After a brief review of basic concepts, we turn to optimization problems subject to equality and inequality constraints. The method of Lagrange (and generalizations by Kuhn and Tucker) will be introduced and employed to solve static problems. Optimal control and dynamic programming will be used for dynamic problems.

title: Public Sector Economics
description: Part I: Introduction to public economics Part II: Theory 1. Public goods and externalities 2. Redistribution and social insurance 3. Tax policy 4. Voting and public choice 5. Fiscal federalism Part III: Applications 1. Health policy 2. Education policy 3. Social security 4. Effects of tax policy 5. Other topics

title: Economic Crises
description: The subject of this course is economic crises--- their causes, consequences, and prevention. In the fall of 2010, the emphasis is on the global financial crisis that began in 2007. Alternative theories are presented including some drawn from behavioral economics. Considerable attention is paid to the institutional framework . The crises studied are of several kinds, including bank runs, speculative attacks on currencies, and sudden stops in emerging markets. Analytical techniques include formal models and event studies. Access to the âSolverâ utility in Excel is required.

title: Game Theory
description: Game Theory is the study of strategic interaction among rational agents. Its core methodology is mathematical in nature. This course develops the basic tools of game theory, and builds on them to proceed to a relatively advanced level. To complement and illustrate the pure theory, the course also covers a number of economic applications. Students will be asked to complete a number of assignments available via a dedicated web-site. These assignments are compulsory, but do not form part of the final grade. Instead the final grade will be based on a mid-term and a final exam. Fall.

title: Money, Banking & Financial Markets
description: This course provides an overview of the U.S. financial system. We consider the financial decision problems faced by individuals, firms, financial institutions, and the government. These decisions when aggregated will determine prices including interest rates and stock prices, and the allocation of funds. The course material is relevant for (i) personal financial decisions, such as saving for retirement and investment portfolio choice, (ii) financial decisions by businesses such as their financial structure, i.e., debt versus equity, (iii) financial instruments issued by financial institutions, and (iv) the role of public policies such as monetary, fiscal policies and financial regulations in dealing with and preventing financial crises.

title: Unemployment, Search and Matching
description: Matching frictions are important in many markets. In the labor market, workers search for suitable jobs and firms try to hire suitable workers. Some workers can't find a job while at the same time some vacancies remain unfilled. In this course, we will analyze markets with matching frictions with a particular emphasis on understanding unemployment.

title: Environmental Economics
description: How much should we pay to improve air quality or to protect endangered species? Which is better: pollution taxes or cap-and-trade? How concerned should we be about climate change damages that may occur 100 years from now? Does economic growth help or hurt the environment? What about international trade? What about international trade in endangered species? This course is designed to frame economic answers to those questions and more. Readings will include a textbook, several Kennedy School case studies, and articles in leading academic economics journals. Students will be assigned problem-set based homework, short policy memos based on the case studies, and a final exam.

title: Labor Economics
description: This seminar develops the basic economic theory used to model labor market behavior. Particular emphasis is placed on applying the theory to current policy issues such as welfare reform, educational policy, and social security reform. Recent developments in the labor market such as the increased labor force participation of married women with children and the decline of unionism in the United States are also discussed. A term paper is required.

title: Development Economics: Micro and Policy Issues
description: The purpose of this course is to provide you with a basic understanding of a number of important concepts and theories in the literature on economic development. Topics to be discussed include growth, human capital, inequality, poverty and nutrition, rural credit market, tenancy and land markets. The strongest emphasis will be placed on a firm grasp of the conceptual issues involved. In addition, I will often cite data and discuss case studies. At the end of the course, I'd like you to be in a position were you can read about (or directly experience) development issues.

title: Political Economy of Trade Policy
description: This course focuses on the political economy of international economic integration, in particular trade and migration policies. The course begins with an overview of the effect of free trade on welfare and income distribution in standard trade models. Next, we study trade policy in theory and in practice, which leads to an inquiry into the processes by which trade policies are formed. We examine both domestic political determinants of trade policy and models of international trade relations. The second part of the course focuses on the political economy of migration policy. We first investigate the relationship that links the two dimensions of globalization, trade and immigration. Next, we explain the role of migration policy in shaping international migration flows. Finally, we develop a political-economy model of migration policy and investigate its components. Spring.

title: Program Evaluation
description: The purpose of this course is to provide an overview of program evaluation in the context of development policy. How we learn what works, what does not work, and why? The course will cover impact evaluation theory (causal inference, experimental design and basic statistics) as well as methods (randomization, difference-in-difference, regression discontinuity, and propensity score matching). The curriculum will be applied, with weekly case studies of field research drawn from the international development literature. Students who complete this course will be prepared to: 1) distinguish research-based âbest practicesâ from those that have not been rigorously evaluated; 2) design an impact evaluation of a policy or intervention, and 3) evaluate data using a statistical software package.

title: Introduction to Microtheory
description: see description for Econ 101.

title: Introduction to Macrotheory
description: See description for Econ 102.

title: Micro Theory Honors
description: see Econ 103

title: Honors Macroeconomic Theory
description: see Econ 104

title: Economic Statistics
description: see Econ-121

title: Intro to Econometrics
description: see Econ 122

title: International Finance
description: This course examines macroeconomics in open economies that are linked by trade, capital flows, and exchange rate policies. The goal is to provide an analytical framework for understanding current events and major challenges facing the world economy. The course will develop theoretical tools that can be used to analyze the economic performance of open economies and to evaluate alternative policy options for macroeconomic management. Topics include balance of payments accounts, foreign exchange and money markets, monetary and stabilization policies, exchange rate regimes, economic growth, currency and financial crises, and the international financial architecture. Examples from both developed and developing country experiences will be discussed.

title: International Trade
description: Major topics covered include: comparative and absolute advantage; production, trade, and consumption; equilibrium in the international market; trade and factors of production; modern theories of trade; commercial policy; factor movements; U.S. trade policy; and trade problems of developing countries, current trade issues and WTO. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: International Finance, advanced
description: Major topics covered include: theories of balance of payments determination; foreign exchange markets; price and income approaches to balance of payments adjustments; monetary approach to balance of payments adjustments; Euro-currency markets; monetary integration; and the international monetary system. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: International Trade, advanced
description: Major topics covered include: comparative and absolute advantage; production, trade, and consumption; equilibrium in the international market; trade and factors of production; modern theories of trade; commercial policy; factor movements; U.S. trade policy; and trade problems of developing countries, current trade issues and WTO. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: Microeconomics
description: This course covers elements of modern microeconomics. It emphasizes the development of skills needed to evaluate the new generation of economic models, tools, and ideas as they apply to recent changes in our economy. Basic tools of game theory, asymmetric information, and modern general equilibrium price formation are introduced and applied to various topics. These topics include consumer and producer choice, decision under uncertainty, and resource allocation under different market structures such as competition, monopoly, and oligopoly. The course also focuses on some newer innovations to the theory, including the role of politics in the determination of economic policy, the impact of spillovers and network externalities in the information economy, and psychological and behavioral factors in individual decision making For M.A. in Applied Economics Students

title: Macroeconomics
description: This course provides an introduction to macroeconomics and standard theoretical models of how output, employment, and the price level are determined. The course also discusses the role of fiscal and monetary policies, as well as selected topics from international macroeconomics. The course begins with a brief discussion of macroeconomic aggregates and their measurement in practice, but the main focus of the course is analytical. Standard analytical models address two broadly distinct topics: (1) macroeconomic performance and growth in the long run; (2)short-run (business-cycle)fluctuations and stabilization (fiscal and monetary) policies. For M.A. in Applied Economics students.

title: Econometrics
description: This course covers the basic linear regression model but also looks beyond it. Many economic data-sets do not satisfy the strong requirements for the linear regression model.Examples include labor force participation decisions, consumer choice between different brands of a product, and household income and consumption surveys stretching over several years (panel data). In this course more advanced statistical models are covered that are needed to analyze these types of data. Topics include discrete choice models (logit and probit), count data models, censored and truncated regression models, panel data models, and instrumental variables methods. The emphasis of the course is on understanding the need for the various methods, being able to apply them to data, and interpreting the results. For M.A. in Applied Economics students.

title: Data Analysis
description: This course introduces students to how to use a major econometric software package to manipulate data and estimate econometric models. Hands-on exercises using actual economic data are an integral part of the course. The goal of the course is to enable students conduct their own data analyses and research in an econometric software package using the tools developed in Economics-553, Econometrics. For M.A. in Applied Economics students.

title: Industrial Organization
description: Econ 562 is a survey of modern economic analysis of industrial organization. Emphasis will be on theory but with some coverage of empirical techniques. Topics to be covered include static analysis and estimation of imperfect competition, dynamic competition and collusion, antitrust, entry and entry deterrence, product differentiation, price discrimination, incomplete information, and auctions. This is a hands-on course, and students will conduct data analysis using statistical packages.

title: Public Economics
description: This course will cover the major topics in public economicsâthat is, public sector taxation and expenditures. It will encompass normative and positive theories of public economics and a broad range of empirical work. The course will use a well-known textbook in public economics. In addition, optional readings from the economics literature will be assigned every week. A series of problem sets using publicly available data and a major econometric software package will be assigned. A major empirical project will constitute a large portion of the grade. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students only.

title: Program Evaluation
description: This course will introduce economics students to the principles and practice of evaluating economic and social programs. The course will cover the fundamentals of causal models, empirical techniques for estimating counterfactual outcomes, methods for identifying parameters of policy interest, and integration of evaluation findings into policy decisionmaking. These techniques range from randomized field experiments and natural/quasi-experiments to econometric methods such as matching, instrumental variables, and fixed effect/panel data models. Besides covering tools for designing and implementing methods for estimating program impacts, the course will include applications of decision theory to program evaluation, including benefit-cost analysis, cost effectiveness analysis, and Bayesian approaches to decisionmaking. Finally, in addition to relevant economic theory provided at the beginning of each lecture, students will learn through real-world examples of program evaluation in fields such as health, education, training, welfare, anti-poverty, housing, and small-business/agriculture in developing countries. They will be introduced to the practical realities of mounting program evaluations through exercises that simulate the work of real program evaluation researchers. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students only.

title: Monetary Theory and Policy
description: Econ 572 is a masters-level course in monetary economics. The course is based primarily on a well-known textbook on monetary theory and policy, but articles from professional journals and material from central banks are also employed. The course begins with a review of the concepts and definitions of money. It then turns to a consideration of the historical and more recent empirical evidence on the relationships among money, prices, and output. Attention then shifts to the theoretical and empirical literature on money demand. The course subsequently covers money and public finance; money and the open economy; the credit channel of monetary policy; and discretionary policy and time inconsistency. Much of the last third of the course is devoted to a range of practical issues in the conduct of monetary policy, including monetary policy operating procedures and the characteristics of alternative monetary policy rules. Considerable focus is given to the conduct of unconventional as well as conventional monetary policy. Issues pertaining to the relationship between monetary policy and systemic risk are also discussed. Final course grades are based on a midterm and a final exam; on homework exercises, which have a significant empirical dimension; and on class presentations.

title: International Finance
description: Economics 577 investigates open economy macroeconomics and international finance. Topics include the balance of payments, saving and investment, and international borrowing and lending; basics of finance and international finance; exchange rate dynamics; financial crises; and monetary and fiscal policy in the open economy in the context of a basic sticky-price optimizing model with financial market frictions. Supplemental topics may include economic growth and the application of overlapping generations models to issues in international finance. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students.

title: Open Economy Macroeconomics
description: This course focuses on applying empirical and theoretical techniques used in international economics and macroeconomics to current policy issues. The course is geared toward sharpening the skills of students who wish to proceed from the master's program to economic analyst employment in either the private or public sectors.

title: Time Series Econometrics
description: This course will cover the major techniques of time-series econometrics as they are applied in macroeconomics and finance. In particular, the course will cover mixed autoregressive moving-average models, conditional volatility models, nonstationarity tests, vector autoregressive models, cointegration, and nonlinear models. Although the relevant econometric theory is reviewed, most of the emphasis is on applied time-series econometrics. Examples in class will be presented using a well-known statistical and econometric software package. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students only.

title: Microeconomics I
description: This is the first semester of the microeconomic theory sequence for Ph.D. students. The topics covered are Consumer Theory, Producer Theory, Choice Under Uncertainty, and Game Theory.

title: Microeconomics II
description: This course examines topics in Infinitely Repeated Games, Games of Incomplete Information and associated notions of Perfect-Bayesian equilibrium and Sequential equilibrium, General Equilibrium Theory, and, time permitting, Markets under Uncertainty, Adverse Selection, and Moral Hazard.

title: Macroeconomics I
description: This two-semester sequence provides an introduction to the techniques of modern macroeconomic analysis that are needed by all graduate students in economics. Intertemporal frameworks are developed to discuss modern theories of consumption, saving, investment and growth. Other applications may include theories of asset pricing, models of the labor market and search frictions, the analysis of business cycles, and fiscal and monetary policies. The sequence also provides an introduction to mathematical techniques used in dynamic macroeconomic analysis. ECON-605 is the prerequisite for ECON-606.

title: Macroeconomics II
description: This two-semester sequence provides an introduction to the techniques of modern macroeconomic analysis that are needed by all graduate students in economics. Intertemporal frameworks are developed to discuss modern theories of consumption, saving, investment and growth. Other applications may include theories of asset pricing, models of the labor market and search frictions, the analysis of business cycles, and fiscal and monetary policies. The sequence also provides an introduction to mathematical techniques used in dynamic macroeconomic analysis. ECON-605 is the prerequisite for ECON-606.

title: Macroeconomics III
description: This course is designed for graduate students who plan to concentrate in one of the areas of macroeconomics. It develops further some of the techniques that were introduced in Macroeconomics I and II, and it discusses some of the areas of current macroeconomic research. Topics covered will vary from year to year.

title: Macroeconomics IV
description: This course is designed for graduate students who plan to concentrate in one of the areas of macroeconomics. It develops further some of the techniques that were introduced in Macroeconomics I and II, and it discusses some of the areas of current macroeconomic research. Topics covered will vary from year to year, but likely topics include: new Keynesian models, theories of price determination, real business cycle model techniques, endogenous growth, monetary transmission mechanisms, asset pricing models, and models of banking and finance. Prerequisite: ECON-606.

title: Econometrics I
description: Methods of estimation and inference are developed for the classical regression model, the generalized regression model, basic time-series models, and simultaneous equations systems.

title: Econometrics II
description: Methods of estimation and inference are developed for the classical regression model, the generalized regression model, basic time-series models, and simultaneous equations systems.

title: Micro-Econometrics
description: Econometric methods for analysis of microeconomic behavior are developed. Topics include panel data analysis and models with discrete or limited dependent variables.

title: Macro-Econometrics
description: The course is an introduction to univariate and multivariate time series models. Time domain methods, including VAR's, structural VAR's, Bayesian VAR's for linear models and GMM for non-linear stationary models are covered. An introduction to non-stationary time series models is given. Frequency domain methods and their applications to business cycle inference is also covered. The course starts by introducing basic concepts and progresses to more complicated models. The course intends to meet two goals. It provides tools for empirical work with time series data, mostly for macroeconomic applications and provides a heuristic introduction into the theoretical foundation of time series models. Prerequisites: Econ 613 and 614.

title: Economic Development I
description: The purpose of this course is to provide you with a basic understanding of a number of important concepts and theories in the literature on economic development. Topics to be covered include: Coordination failure and history dependence; Inequality; Rural credit markets; Insurance; Savings and other protections from risk; Land; and Institution, geography and political economy. The strongest emphasis will be placed on a firm grasp of the conceptual issues involved, but empirical papers and case studies will be discussed.

title: Economic Development II
description: Concerns about âpovertyâ and âinequalityâ have long been central to economics and policy. The course will begin with a brief historical overview of past thinking in philosophy and economic back to the 17th century. We will then study the concepts found in current literature and policy debates in more formal terms, critically reviewing prevailing measurement theories. Using these concepts, the course will then review the debates, theories and the evidence on the causes of poverty and on the role and effectiveness of specific policy interventions. The lectures will embrace some key questions about economic development: Why does poverty fall faster in some economies than others? Is a rise in inequality inevitable as poor countries grow? Does poverty necessarily fall with economic growth? How does the initial inequality influence the growth process and subsequent distributional changes? Can poor countries or poor areas within countries get stuck with persistently high poverty despite sound macroeconomic policies? What types of policy interventions can help in effectively fighting poverty? The lectures will draw on both economic theory and a wide range of evidence, including both econometric studies and lessons from more qualitative work. Emphasis will be given throughout on understanding and evaluating policy interventions from a distributional perspective, ranging from macro policies to micro-sectoral policies. The course will primarily, but not exclusively, focus on developing countries.

title: Industrial Organization
description: The course studies theoretical models of industrial organization that are used to address competition policy and monopoly regulation. Topics covered will include some of the following: monopoly behavior and welfare losses; strategic interaction in oligopoly--prices, outputs, investments; welfare tradeoffs due to fixed costs, asymmetric costs, and product differentiation; collusion and horizontal mergers; price discrimination; vertical control--incentives and methods, including integration versus contracting, and vertical control for exclusionary purposes; promoting competition in traditionally regulated network industries; innovation and intellectual property rights.

title: Labor Economics II
description: This course presents a survey of the theory and the empirical analysis of unemployment. In terms of theory, efficiency wages, matching models, and equilibrium search models will be emphasized. Hazard models of unemployment duration as well as the econometrics of equilibrium search models will be emphasized in the empirical part of the course.

title: Empirical Microeconomics I
description: PhD students will be able to fulfill a field requirement by taking ECON 671 together with one class from any of the following fields: Public Economics, Labor Economics, Development Economics

title: Political Economy
description: This course studies the fundamentals of social choice and political aggregation. Topics include the Impossibility Theorems of Arrow and Gibbard-Satterthwaite, the Median Voter Theorem, models of strategic voting and endogenous participation, the Hotelling-Downs model of political competition and variants thereof, and informational aggregation results.

title: Workshops in Macroeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in economic development, international finance, macroeconomics, monetary economics, and related fields.

title: Workshops in Macroeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in economic development, international finance, macroeconomics, monetary economics, and related fields.

title: Workshops in Microeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in industrial organization, international trade, labor economics, microeconomics, public finance, and related fields.

title: Workshop: Microeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in industrial organization, international trade, labor economics, microeconomics, public finance, and related fields.

title: Workshop in International Economics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in international economics and related fields.

title: Workshop: International
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in international economics and related fields.

title: Workshop:  Econometrics
description: Presentations of papers of outside speakers, faculty and students on topics in econometrics and applied economentrics.

title: Workshop: Empirical Econometrics
description: Presentations of papers of outside speakers, faculty and students on topics in econometrics and applied economentrics.

title: Thesis Research-Economics
description: Thesis research and completion.

title: Children with Disabilities
description: Research repeatedly indicates that children living in homeless environments experience increased health problems, developmental delays, anxiety, depression, behavioral problems and lower educational achievement. During the past seven years, child poverty rates in the US has increased significantly, leaving 13 million children living in poverty (in 2011, the federal poverty line was an annual income of $22,350 for a family of four.). Extreme poverty rates (those earning wages totaling less than half of the federal poverty line) have increased by nearly 24 percent during this time, with 5.8 million children living in extreme poverty. Young children (ages five and under) are even more likely to live in poverty, with 21 percent (more than two in every nine young children) living in poverty. Washington, DC has the highest rate of child poverty in the United States at more than 33 percent. Young children who are homeless face a multitude of risk factors that affect their happiness, mental and physical health, and subsequent school achievement. For example, homeless children become sick four times more often than other children, experience significantly higher rates of respiratory infections, ear infections, gastrointestinal problems, and asthma. Homeless children are also twice as likely to suffer from hunger as other children and much more likely to experience behavioral problems, depression and anxiety, and emotional problems. Homeless children are more likely to fall behind in school, repeat a grade, require special education services, and less likely to demonstrate academic proficiency or graduate from high school. The availability of high-quality early childhood developmental and family services has the potential to significantly enhance the lives and ell-being of young homeless children, and make a difference in the lives of their families. High-quality childcare strengthens cognitive and language development; increases school readiness and subsequent academic success; builds self-esteem and achievement motivation; and improves health, social-emotional development and behavior. It also significantly narrows the gap in school readiness and educational performance and strengthens family life. High-quality programs influence the way parents support their childrenâs education, how often they read to their children, and the way they participate in their childrenâs schooling. Homeless parents who have access to high-quality child care are more likely to find jobs, stay employed, and offer stability to their children. Georgetown University students who participate in the practicum experience at Bright Beginnings, Inc. will have the opportunity to learn firsthand about the effects of homelessness and poverty on young children and to become aware of and skilled in providing interdisciplinary services which aim to mitigate these effects.

title: SCHOOLS OF THOUGHT: Investigations in American K-12 Education
description: The grand promise America makes its children is that each will be educated regardless of wealth, intellectual gifts or background, and that this education is the essential cornerstone in a life of competence, opportunity, and happiness. What is the origin of the grand promise? In 2012, are we making good on that promiseâacross the country, in states, in school districts, in school buildings, and in classrooms? If we are succeeding, why? If not, what makes success so difficult? We will investigate and make discoveries in three main areas: â¢ The purpose of schools, and what forces determine that purpose/those purposes â¢ The most critical issues in K-12 education, why they are critical, and how they interconnect â¢ What is really happening in K-12 schools today Throughout the semester, weâll conduct our investigations through the lenses of history, theory, policy, and life on the front lines in schools. You will use these tools: â¢ The DC Public School system, as well as a sampling of local charter and independent schools, will be your laboratoryâbecause school reform in the District is thought to be the most ambitious in the nation. You will shadow a principal, school district leader, teacher, operations manager, instructional coach, instructional superintendent, or other DC school professional for a day. In addition, you will interact with 8-10 other school professionals in class; our speaker series will more opportunities for you to learn face-to-face. â¢ A deep dive into the required film and readings â¢ Your own excellent skillsâcareful reading, smart questioning, solid research, precise writing, good listening, and thoughtful analysis. â¢ Data-gathering, reflection on and analysis of your own K-12 experience You will be asked to share your developing knowledge and insights in various ways throughout the courseâin writing, discussion, presentation. At the end of course, you will be expected to demonstrate: â¢ Your comprehensive knowledge of a broad range of elements important in American K-12 education â¢ Your well-substantiated views on key issues, barriers to success, and potential solutions in K-12 schools today through an analysis of your own high school education â¢ Your firsthand knowledge of what is happening in perhaps the most dynamic public school district in the United States â¢ Your beliefs about your own role and responsibility in supporting K-12 education EDIJ 330 will be a lively, collaborative educational experience!

title: Democracy and Education II
description: In Democracy and Education II, we will take the relationship between democracy and education from the realm of theory and discussion to the place where the rubber meets the road >> in real schools and school communities, among students, teachers, administrators, parents, community members, and government officials. We will travel on two investigative paths simultaneously, driven by two sets of questions: â¢ What is the democratic process around American K-12 education? Who is part of that process, and how is the process in evidence on the ground in the business of schooling? â¢ What do we teach about democracy in K-12 schools? Why and how do we teach it, and what results do we expect? Since K-12 education is happening here and now, these investigations will take place in real time. This course has a CBL component. You will use schools across the District of Columbia as your laboratory. You are expected to attend a DC City Council meeting and at least one other DC community meeting that we will discuss further on. In particular, this course reflects a partnership with Two Rivers Public Charter School in northeast DC. You will learn from TR faculty and students both in class and on the Two Rivers campus.

title: Gateway: American Literature
description: This course offers an introduction to the major strands of American literature and culture. In the first two-thirds of the semester, we will proceed chronologically, beginning with the writings of early colonization, moving through the Revolutionary period and mid-nineteenth century period known as the American Renaissance, and ending with the writings of the later part of that century. In this section of the course, our readings of some of the nationâs most important authors will allow us to examine the multiplicity of Americaâs literary traditions and the conflicting ideas that they explore: spirituality, freedom and slavery; nature and the city; nationhood; communal and individual identity; gender, race, class, and power. During the last third of the semester, as we move through early-twentieth-century modernism and into post-modernism, our approach will shift, turning toward a number of major works that reflect and recreate some of our central texts. For example, our initial understanding of Whitmanâs great poem, Leaves of Grass, will be deepened and altered by the poetic interpretations of American T.S. Eliotâs The Waste Land (1922) and Spaniard Federico Garcia Lorcaâs Poet in New York (1930); our investigations of the wilderness, slavery, and masculinity furthered by William Faulknerâs story âThe Bearâ (1942) and Sam Shepardâs drama True West (1980); our interpretations of Nathaniel Hawthorneâs The Scarlet Letter complicated by African American playwright Suzan-Lori Parksâ Red Letter Plays (2001). Such cross-readings will allow us to explore the vexed concept of Americaâits diverse and shifting meanings, its promise, and its critiqueâfrom an equally diverse number of perspectives. Students will thus emerge from this class with a strong grounding in American literature and culture itself, as well as an increased skill in the interpretationâthrough reading, writing, and discussionâof all literary and cultural texts. Requirements: Faithful attendance, consistent class participation, regular response papers, two short essays (4-6 pages), one longer research essay (10-12 pages).

title: Methods of Literary and Cultural Studies
description: This course aims to give students a coherent understanding of various theoretical and critical tools used to interpret texts by introducing them to strategies of close reading and to larger discussions regarding textual analysis. Although the course will not necessarily encompass the entire history of literary and cultural criticism, it will examine a range of schools and methods. These schools and methods will be grounded historically and will be situated and contextualized within larger critical conversations that have developed over time. This course does NOT fulfill the HUMW II requirement. This introductory will familiarize us with the human sciences of Literary Theory and Criticism as practiced in the US today by reading both canonical and newer works in the field, arranged along a thematic axis. We will make a gesture towards broad coverage of 20thÂ C. theory, but will focus rather on a symptomatic approach to issues of contemporary relevance.Â Â Special focus will be given to the artist in movements for social change and the theorization of âsocial practice.â Student involvement in planning the syllabus is encouraged. Oral presentations and vigorous conversation will figure prominently. Two short essays, one long research paper.//This yearâs class also engages the major cultural events on the Georgetown campus under the banner of the Lannan Center for Poetics and Social Practice. While not everyone will be able to attend all events, active participation is encouraged, and class time will be devoted to discussion of events. These mostly include seminars and readings on Tuesday evenings, and culminate in a two day Symposium featuring authors we will read in class. Everything is videotaped and archived, so ample opportunity is afforded to participate. Please see the line up of readings and talks here: //lannan.georgetown.edu/category/events/readings-and-talks/

Although the course will not necessarily encompass the entire history of literary and cultural criticism, it will examine a range of schools and methods. This course does NOT fulfill the HUMW II requirement. This course introduces majors to the diverse critical practices of âEnglishâ as an academic discipline.Â Â In the 21stÂ century, literary study boasts a multiplicity of approaches, offering what can at times appear a rather jumbled set of possibilities: from traditional literary history to critical theories informed by other disciplines such as sociology, anthropology, linguistics, and philosophy.Â Â As such, it has also over time expanded the range of texts to be examined, adding film, television, popular music, and so onâmaterials from our daily livesâto an ever-burgeoning âcanonâ of poetry, novels, and plays.Â //We will conduct a historicized exploration of this disciplineâs evolving, shifting contours in order to understand how and why it arrived at its present form.Â Â Along the way, students will try their hand at diverse critical approaches as they read a variety of cultural texts.Â Â The Great Gatsby, that perennial favorite of high school English courses, will serve as our first âtest caseâ of interpretation; others may include Louise Erdrichâs novelÂ Love Medicine; Anna Deveare Smithâs playÂ Fires in the Mirror; Marjane Setrapiâs graphic novelÂ Persepolis I; and the HBO seriesÂ The Sopranos, along with selected poems and short stories.Â Â Critical texts will include Lois TysonâsÂ Critical Theory Today: A User-Friendly Guide, 2ndÂ Ed. and Jonathan CullerâsÂ Literary Theory: A Very Short Introduction.

title: Literary History I
description: Literary History I is part of a two-semester sequence that surveys Anglophone literary and cultural history. We will be reading texts from the medieval period through the eighteenth century. The course will highlight a number of critical and/or representative texts, genres, debates, developments, and crises illustrative of the time periods studied. This course does NOT fulfill the HUMW II requirement.

title: British Poetry: 1600-2000
description: This course will survey the work of great poets in the English literary canon from Shakespeare to Seamus Heaney. It will aim to prepare students in the art of practical criticism, and place in their hands the rudiments of analytical thought. The emphasis throughout this course will be on disciplined, close analysis of text, and group discussion in which everyone is expected to participate. The aim will be to enable students to reach the point at which they are able to read and analyse poetry for themselves with confidence. The elements of prosody are thus essential concomitants in this study, and for that reason a principal course text will be Philip Hobsbaumâs Metre, Rhythm and Verse Form (Routledge), which students will be expected to read and memorize. In each class, we will read poems together. Texts of these works will be provided by Professor Wu. Students should be prepared to write regular short essays on aspects of individual poems. Each essay will contribute to their overall assessment, as will their attendance record and performance in class, which will be closely monitored. Students are expected also to attend one-to-one tutorials with Professor Wu in order to discuss their work. These various activities will together contribute to each studentâs final grade. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Global Medieval Literatures
description: How does literature shape and express emotion in diverse cultural contexts? In this course, we will put this question into global as well as historical perspective by investigating how one of the most complex and fundamental of human emotions -- romantic love -- is constructed in the courtly literatures of medieval Europe (12th-13th centuries), Persia during the later Abbasid Caliphate (ca. 970-1258), and Heian Japan (794-1185). Each set of texts will give rise to its own set of issues, but abiding preoccupations will include the relationship between âartificialâ conventions and ârealâ emotion (is this a valid distinction?); the powerful role of religion (here, Christianity, Islam, and Buddhism) in shaping forms of desire; the relation between court politics and intimate experience; the cultural construction of the desiring and desirable body; the role of women as writers, court patrons, or courtly audience; how differing ideals of masculinity find expression; love in relation to arranged marriages and polygamy; and the ethical potential of romantic love as a ânobleâ emotion. All readings will be in modern English translation. No previous exposure to medieval literature is assumed or required.

title: Literatures of Medieval Women
description: Do these situations sound like the Middle Ages you know? A medieval girl is raised as a boy. A lady saves knight instead of the other way round. A mother curses unsympathetic men to suffer labor pains when they are needed in battle. A medieval woman constructs a universal history entirely about women to show that misogynists lie. The roots of our modern gender expectations and debates in the West come mainly from medieval society, but you may not have realized how old some of these contentions are, and that women were actively involved in them. This course looks at the writings of medieval women and writings about them, considering the variety of cultures and cultural influences over the thousand years of the European Middle Ages. Literatures from Ireland, Wales, England, France, Germany, and Scandinavia are contextualized against and within cultural expectations of women under such influences as gender roles, religion, national identities, self?identity, and mythology. Outside material on historical backgrounds and literary criticism from multiple perspectives supplement the literature read.

title: Chaucer
description: A reading and discussion of Chaucer's great (if unfinished) master-work, the Canterbury Tales, a work of first importance to anyone who reads (or writes) narrative fiction. Students will read the work in original Middle English in which Chaucer wrote it, and discuss it in small and in large groups, focusing upon the work's narrative strategies, intellectual constructions, and artistic accomplishment. They will also write, in a variety of ways, about those aspects which particularly take their interest. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Gender & Early Modern Lit/Culture
description: How did an emerging capitalist economy affect women in 16th and 17th century England? In a time of imperial expansion, global trade, and rapid urbanization, gender roles necessarily shifted as women entered the merchant class and became more than just commodities to be traded between men. Drawing upon cultural artifacts such as maps, visual representations, and household objects, this course examines the conflicts raised by women's increasing agency within the marital, literary, theatrical, commercial, and political economies. Through a close analysis of plays by Shakespeare and other male playwrights as well as poetry and prose by women, we will explore diverse representations of gender to ask how women's voices can acquire value in a patriarchal society. Critical essays, short Discussion Board posts, a debate, and active participation are required. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Early Modern Marvels & Monsters
description: If the âmonstrous body is pure cultureâ as Jeffery Cohen has argued, then the literature of the monstrous inscribes the boundaries of culture, defining the normal through the deviant, the knowable through the marvelous, the socially and religiously acceptable through the prodigious and deformed.Â Â Starting with the cultural doppelganger Grendel, that founding monster of English literature, this course would trace the geography of early modern culture through a reading of the marginal and the monstrous.Â Â BesidesÂ Beowulf, readings would be framed by selections fromÂ The Travels of Sir John Mandevilleand MunsterâsÂ Cosmographia,Â offering tales and images of an unknown saturated with meaning and at the far end, a cluster of texts such as selections from BaconâsÂ Novum Organum, his essay âOn Deformity,â and selections from Montaigneâs essays that began to naturalize the monstrous.Â Â Within this frame the course would present four subsets of texts:Â Â 1) one that takes on the Jew as monstrous, including the Chaucerâs âPrioressâs Taleâ and MarloweâsJew of Malta; 2) a second that looks at the peril of the transgressive feminine, as bounded by âThe Wife of Bathâs Tale,â the Shakespearian sonnets of the dark lady, and Dekker and Middletonâs playÂ The Roaring Girl; 3) a third that pulls together a set of short historical readings aroundÂ The Tempest, investigating the New World Amerindian as an outgrowth of the mythological Wild Man; and 4) a fourth group clustered aroundÂ Macbeth, but including short readings on the âHidious, Frightfull, and Prodigious Signsâ thought to point to religious and political violations. The goal of this course overall is to explore some of the major issues of medieval and Renaissance English culture through texts that point to the often ambiguous margins of that culture and the strange worlds that lay beyond.Â Â

title: Shakespeare
description: The course will look closely at six of Shakespeareâs plays: As You Like It, The Merchant of Venice, Henry 4, Part 1, Henry 5, Coriolanus, and King Lear. It will focus largely (but not exclusively) on two significant questions: how might these plays have been brought to life by actors on a stage in Shakespeareâs time and how might they continue to be brought to life by actors in our own time. Through the process of seeking answers to those questions the course will hope also to discover what the plays of Shakespeare, on the page and on the stage, might say to us now, some five hundred years after they were written. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their understanding of how a script is brought to life on the stage (the course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so). They will also be asked to attend two production of ShakespeareâHenry 5 at The Folger Theare and Coriolanus at The Shakespeare Theatre. A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the tickets. This course satisfies the College's HUMW II requirement.

title: Shakespeare
description: We will read very carefully four of Shakespeareâs plays, representing aÂ range of the genres in which he wrote â history, tragedy, comedy, romance.Â In conjunction with our reading of the plays, we will also read a selectionÂ of other texts also written in the early modern period. Our attention willÂ focus on the plays, but we will spend a good deal of time on texts writtenÂ by other authors. These other texts address central issues raised in theÂ plays â rebellion, kingship, dueling, passion, marriage, and jealousy, among others â and help provide an understanding of the varietyÂ of opinions available in the period. By familiarizing ourselves with theÂ cultural context, we will develop a historically grounded perspective fromÂ which we can view more clearly Shakespeareâs formulation of these issues,Â and deepen our understanding of both the plays and the culture of earlyÂ modern England

title: Seventeenth Century English Literature
description: "Whispers of Immortality" As an introduction to seventeenth-century literature, this course will explore ideas of immortality and mortality in early modern England through the writings of some of the most powerful and provocative poets, dramatists, and prose writers of the period. It will examine how these writers discuss mortality, imagine immortality, and speculate about what these concepts mean in terms of religion, politics, and social norms. As such, it will look at the metaphysical ways in which these writers conceived of bodies, souls, form, matter, time, and space; and what they thought happened to each at death and, even, after death. That said, the course will also examine how seventeenth-century writers depict being alive: of what they think it means to be embodied, and what value they attach (if any) to distinctly human experiences. Our readings will invite us to reconsider what various literary, rhetorical, and theological maneuvers these writers make in order to re-imagine reality, and to what effect. It will address the question of what happens when the idea of immortality is shown to be bankrupt--when it is either emptied of meaning or reworked into another philosophic doctrine. Throughout the semester, we will discuss how ideas about religion, morality, class, gender, as well as, the social and political culture of early modern England help to shape, and are shaped by, seventeenth-century discussions about our relationship to this world and the next; and how aspects of this tradition remain with us today. One of the fundamental aims of this course is to make you better âclose readersâ of texts: an understanding of what is being said will be integrated with how it is being said. Secondly, in entering into a conversation with literary critics about the relationship between literature and its historical and cultural contexts, the course will help you to refine your historical sensibility. Lastly, the course will encourage you to synthesize both text and context, and to develop this further in your writing. More broadly, the course will invite you to think more carefully about what imaginative literature is, and why it is important.

title: Milton
description: How does a poet make a poem? How does God make a world? How do we make a choice? John Miltonâs great epic, Paradise Lost, is centrally concerned with these three kinds of making â poiesis, creation, and decision â and the relationships between them. To understand what kind of a maker Milton is, we will read his epic alongside the shorter poetry and prose and compare his accounts of making to those of Christian and classical predecessors as well as early modern contemporaries. Students should come ready to try their own hand at making arguments, poems, fateful choices, and imagined worlds. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: 17C-18C Women's Writing
description: A curious thing happened when earlier generations of literary critics began to ask how the English-language novel was created: all of the âcreatorsâ were thought to be male authors.Â Â In similar fashion we acknowledge widely the contributions of Renaissance and early-modern poets and playwrights like Ben Jonson and John Dryden; but only recently, in the long history of studying literature, have we begun to consider the contributions of a wider range of talented and influential female authors from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.Â Â This course focuses specifically on female authors to address two primary objectives: 1) to provide exposure to a mix of well-known and no-so-well-known womenâs writing, illustrative of some of the most important womenâs issues and concerns of the time, including education, property rights, political participation, marriage and divorce customs, and matrimonial law; 2) to examine the question of why many of these influential examples of womenâs writing have been less central to the study of 17C-18C writing as they might have been.Â Â This interactive, discussion-based course will cover a provocative selection of both British and early American womenâs writing from roughly 1600-1800, including novels, plays, poetry, essays, and private correspondences.Â Â These authors include Margaret Cavendish, Mary Rowlandson, Aphra Behn, Charlotte Lennox, Maria Edgeworth, Mary Leapor, Phillis Wheatley, Anne Bradstreet, and Mary Wollstonecraft, among others.Â Â Primary assignments will include analytical essays, multimedia presentations, and public-interest blogging.Â Â Please feel free to get in touch if you would like to know more.Â Â

title: Eighteenth-Century English Novel
description: The novel is called the ânovelâ because, at one time, it was a new art form, and many have made the case that the English novel was so new in the eighteenth century that it was essentially invented then. Not only was the novel new, but the world was, too. Science was challenging traditional beliefs, a myriad of new and often disturbing cultures was revealed by British exploration and colonialism, older attitudes toward sex and gender were being questioned, political and social stability appeared to many to be threatened by unprecedented social mobility. Eighteenth-century novels took it upon themselves to describe, celebrate, satirize or simply negotiate this new world, and in the process they had to create new forms of expression and representation to do so. These became the basis of the novel as we know it today. This course will examine some of the most significant experiments in novel writing in eighteenth-century England: Daniel Defoeâs Robinson Crusoe and Moll Flanders; Jonathan Swiftâs Gulliverâs Travels; Samuel Richardsonâs Pamela; Henry Fieldingâs Tom Jones; and Jane Austenâs Emma. This class emphasizes close reading and textual analysis. It will introduce you to some of the foundational concepts and terminology of the field. In addition to the six novels, we will also read a number of critical essays. There will be several short papers (5-7 pages). This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Colonial American Literature
description: In this course, we will examine some British, Native American, Anglo-American, African-British, African-American, and American literature that grew out of the collision of cultures in the Americas in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. In these fictional and non-fictional works, we will be interested in the ideas of civilization and savagery and the dynamics of slavery and freedom (as historical and economic facts but also as figures for psychological and spiritual processes). We will pay particular attention to how various writers dealt with encounters with the âOther,â with the fact of diversity and difference, and with the shock of the new that came with the settlement of the New World. We will try to chart some of the significant and growing differences between Old and New World identities, and we will investigate some of the anxieties of dealing with a new American reality in the literature of the early Republic. Among the works we will study will be Behnâs Oroonoko; Defoeâs Robinson Crusoe; the captivity narrative of Mary Rowlandson; Sarah Knightâs Journal; Cookeâs Sot-Weed Factor; Byrdâs Secret History of the Dividing Line; Olaudah Equianoâs Interesting Narrative; Franklinâs Autobiography; Fosterâs The Coquette; Brownâs Wieland; and Cooperâs The Prairie. This class emphasizes close reading and textual analysis. It will introduce you to some of the foundational concepts and terminology of the field. There will be several short papers (5-7 pages). This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Early American Fiction
description: One of the least studied and least appreciated periods of American literature is that between the founding of the Republic in the late 1780s and the flowering of the American Renaissance in the 1850s with the writings of Emerson and Thoreau, the poetry of Whitman, the novels of Hawthorne, and the publication of Melvilleâs Moby Dick. And yet, during this period some exciting, innovative, and influential fiction was being written. With the founding of the Republic, American writers turned to the task of creating a distinctively American form of literature, and they found themselves having to deal with some distinctively American problems: though the new nation was established on the principle of human equality, in fact, women possessed few of the rights and privileges of men, black Americans were enslaved, and Amerindians were systematically being driven off their lands; though the Revolution was the product of the rational thinking of the Enlightenment, there was a growing recognition of the power the irrational had in shaping everyday life and an anxiety about how the forces of unreason might affect personal and public life; though it was assumed that the American experiment would succeed only if citizens conducted themselves according to the dictates of âRepublican virtueâ (moderation, self-restraint, and self-discipline), the economy and culture of the new nation was changing so quickly and so radically that many felt compelled to reassess Americaâs past and rethink its future. In this class we will see how some early American fiction writers confronted these and other issues. We will read Rowson and Fosterâs so-called âsentimentalâ novels that dramatize the constraints women lived under immediately following the Revolution; two gothic novels by Brown that deeply influenced Hawthorne and Poe; short stories by Irving and novels by Cooper and Sedgwick which meditate on the past and future of America and (in the case of the latter two novels) the role Amerindians once played in the building of the nation and what role, if any, they would continue to play; short stories by Hawthorne and Poe, which explore the irrational forces of sin, guilt, and self hatred; and two novellas by Melville, which brood over the growing materialism of America and its tragic heritage of slavery. This class emphasizes close reading and textual analysis. It will introduce you to some of the foundational concepts and terminology of the field. There will be several short papers and a take-home final exam. PROBABLE TEXTS: Susanna Rowson, Charlotte Temple; Hannah Foster, The Coquette; Charles Brockden Brown, Wieland and Edgar Huntly; James Fenimore Cooper, The Pioneers; Catherine Maria Sedgwick, Hope Leslie; selected short stories of Washington Irving, Nathaniel Hawthorne, and Edgar Allan Poe; Herman Melville, Benito Cereno and, Bartleby: the Scrivener.

title: Early American Women Writers
description: This course investigates how female writers and performers of various kindsâballad singers, parlor poets, lecturers, pseudonymous autobiographers, playwrights and othersâshaped notions of womanhood, race, and American-ness in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. In disparate ways, these writers passed judgment on the newly emerging American culture. Students will have an opportunity to study the varied and complicated strategies that these women utilized to introduce their ideas onto the public stage. To protect their status as respectable wives and mothers, many women voiced their concerns indirectly, composing ballads and poems rife with hidden meanings. Others published their work anonymously, or under a male or female pseudonym.Â Â Still others cloaked their judgments by populating the stage with dramatic characters who spoke their views indirectly. We will analyze the implications of the womenâs various avenues toward publication as well as their actual songs, poems, lectures, plays, and autobiographical novels. In the process, we will begin to consider gender, race, and nationality as performances rather than biological or geographic identities. In addition to a variety of theoretical articles, our readings may include works by Lucy Terry, Phillis Wheatley, Susanna Rowson, Judith Sargent Murray, Frances Wright, Maria W. Stewart, Anna Cora Mowatt, Ellen Craft, and Harriet E. Wilson.Â

title: 19th Century British Novel
description: The purpose of the novel, dramatically recognized by 19th C readers, is to tell a story of what it means to be human. The significant 19th C novelist Henry James argued that any portrait of a human being â visual or verbal or theatrical or musical â is an artistâs observations of the human experience transformed into that artistâs impressions. Human experience, for James, is in essence the bewilderment at oneâs own condition within a particular space and time and set of circumstances. The novel is the genre that allows the most complex, and therefore the most truly complete, re-enactments of human nature. This course explores the range in the 19th C centuryâs narrative voice as well as examines the tell-tale themes of the era in British literature, including the dynamic conflicts between socio-political change and tradition (rebellions!); urban decay and thrill (just where does decadence appear?); simultaneous loathing and longing for ghosts â those uncanny representations of the past, the monstrous Other, or the fantastical real; scientific and technological invention â progress or intrusion?; industry as both moral and utilitarian; class, gender, and race disenfranchisements and rights; the individual vs. the crowd perception and sympathy; aesthetic (e.g. photography vs. painting) and moral (e.g. Mill vs. Nietzsche) wars; the proud assurance of Home/Empire in light of the savage tantalization of Foreign; the power/ostracization of the traveler. Our novels for Fall 2012 are C. BrontÃ«âs gothic realism ghost story, Villette, G. Eliotâs philosophical explore of gambling, Art, youth, and the Jewish identity in Victorian England, Daniel Deronda, T. Hardyâs brutally raw review of university education and marriage via the ancient man and modern woman in Jude the Obscure, and B. Stokerâs gothic realism vampire story, Dracula. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Victorian Literature & Globalization
description: By the year 1900, a quarter of the earthâs surface was ruled by England and more than four hundred million people calledÂ VictoriaÂ their queen, whether they wanted to or not.Â /This course on British literature of theÂ VictorianÂ period (1837-1901) will examine how works of culture confronted what was arguably the fundamental social fact of the nineteenth century: the empire.Â As we examine the major media forms ofÂ VictorianÂ modernity -- novels, drama, poetry, print journalism, visual art, and early photographyâwe will pay special attention to how those forms began to imagine âthe globeâ as a knowable entity.Â This metaphor emerged in the nineteenth century to describe a networked world not unlike our own, as diverse and cosmopolitan as it was oppressive and violent.Â How did categories of gender, sexuality, and even personhood change in this vast new framework? Â How were the conventions ofÂ VictorianÂ literature affected by the fact of imperial domination?Â Â And was the practice of imperialism itself shaped by the works that described, and sometimes critiqued, it? Â How did theÂ VictoriansÂ imagine their era of globalization?Â How doÂ weÂ imagine ours?Â /Readings may include Wilkie Collins,Â The Moonstone; George Eliot,Â Middlemarch; H. Rider Haggard,Â She; drama from Dion Boucicault; short stories from Stevenson, Conrad, and Kipling; poetry from E. Bronte, C. Rossetti, and R. Browning; nonfiction from Dickens, Darwin, Mill, and Marx.Â Theory and criticism in generous doses.

title: Detective Fiction
description: San Francisco detective novelist Dashiell Hammett wryly observes in The Thin Man, âThe problem with putting two and two together is that sometimes you get four, and sometimes you get twenty-two.â Only the detective, the investigator of knowledge en route to solving a crime, would judiciously perceive that the same evidence could add up in (at least) two entirely different ways. Not all detectives perform quite as Bogartian a wit as Hammettâs Sam Spade, but detectives of any time and place are a rather singular breed, empowered by a second sight often blind to their own natures. Some wield a gun, others yield to the girl (or guy), while still others fascinate with their âlittle gray cellsâ or exude a mystique almost as alluring as following the track of their gum shoes in pursuit of evil. Our own gameâs afoot as from beneath the fedora, we enjoy clandestine encounters with several classic detective stories, ferreting out the origins of the detective persona and his or her story. Many scholars identify the first western detective as none other than Dickensâ Inspector Bucket (Bleak House) whose disarmingly awkward verbosity masks a mind steeled to track down the killer and uncover justice amidst the London fog. Weâll begin the semester reading a medley of short detective fiction in order to meet the detective in all his or her many guises. How does the madman in Poe inspire the raving Sherlock Holmes? Is there a semblance of Baker Streetâs ego maniac in the fastidious Hercule Poirot or the charismatically cool Adam Dagliesh? How does the hardboiled cynicism of Marlowe and Hammet resurrect in the Irish Mick âScorcherâ Kennedy? In spring 2013, we will focus on detective fiction of the 20th and 21st centuries set in London, Dublin, Edinburgh, Stockholm, Prague, and Italy. Thereâs nothing like a chill winterâs eve (or balmy spring afternoon!) to curl up with P.D. Jamesâs A Taste for Death, Agatha Christieâs The Murder of Roger Ackroyd, Tana Frenchâs Broken Harbor, Patricia Highsmithâs The Talented Mr. Ripley, Ian Rankinâs Dead Souls, Philip Kerrâs Prague Fatale, Michael Dibdinâs End Games, and Stieg Larssonâs The Girl Who Kicked the Hornetâs Nest. Student panels will explore the significance of the genreâs identifiable textualities, the transition from detective novel to detective film, and detective fiction as popular culture as well as brain exercise and ethical catharsis.

title: Dickens
description: A close reading of three major novels by Charles Dickens: Bleak House (1852 â 1853), A Tale of Two Cities (1859), and Great Expectations (1860 â 1861) in which we will consider the writer in the context of his era and the practice of fiction in the mid-nineteenth century. Discussion will dominate class time with students reporting on their analytical, inventive, and library research projects. There will not be any exams as such, but there will be short, factual quizzes on the dayâs reading which will begin each class session. The semester ends with a slightly longer essay which discusses a specific aspect of all three novels. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: U.S. Literary History I
description: U.S. Literary History I: American Emergence, 1682-1900 This course surveys U.S. literary history before 1900, examining American literature in the context of broader social and intellectual developments. Throughout the semester we will focus on four themes that figure centrally in the emergence of American writing: 1) citizenship and the contested ideal of the American self; 2) nationhood and the cultivation of national literary and cultural traditions; 3) literatureâs relationship to revolution, political protest, and social change; and 4) the realities and legacies of slavery, emancipation, and American empire. Authors include Rowlandson, Rowson, Child, Apess, Douglass, Hawthorne, Emerson, Whitman, Dickinson, Melville, Stowe, Twain, and Chesnutt. The semester will culminate with a reading of Moby-Dick. This course requires two essays and a final exam, as well as regular response writings throughout the term. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: 19C American Literature
description: This course will focus primarily on the tension between civilization and nature in 19th C American literature and culture. In a century when the frontier was progressively colonized and made into spectacle, and when scientific revolutions in natural history created doubt about human exceptionality, nature became a space of both spiritual refuge and hidden menace. As wildness shifted from an exterior landscape to an interior presence, human nature became a source of intense cultural and literary speculation. By tracing a path from transcendentalism to naturalism we will also explore the competing ideas of regeneration and degeneration, of self-definition and loss of identity, of ecstatic connection and destructive mania that inform many literary texts during this century. Among many questions, we will ask: how are American values, particularly individualism, rooted in âself-relianceâ and a free-spirited relationship with nature? Why is nature often a source of liberation for individuals and of paranoia for the dominant culture? Why did the performance of privilege during this period entail the construction of gender and racial others as dangerously wild and in need of domestication? How did evolutionary theory haunt the 19th C and influence the rise of naturalism and literary expressions of the grotesque? Students will contribute weekly short responses to a class discussion forum, will lead a class discussion on a relevant topic, and will write a midterm paper and a final paper. Major works to be discussed include, but are not limited to: Thoreauâs Walden, Douglasâs Narrative of the Life, Melvilleâs Moby Dick, Chopinâs The Awakening, and Norrisâs McTeague.

title: 19C American Women's Literature
description: In this course, we will study women's literature from the 19th century Americas. These writers weigh in on the major American issues of the era: Â slavery, suffrage, industrialization, feminism, imperialism, manifest destiny, compulsory education, and war. Â We will also focus on women's travel writing from the US and the Caribbean during this period, thinking of the Americas more broadly as a regional identity in the 19th century. Â Authors will include Harriet Jacobs, Pauline Hopkins, Louisa May Alcott, and Mary Seacole, among many others. Â Assignments will include papers, blog posts, and an intensive research project. Â Â

title: Gothic American Fiction
description: In this course we will explore the haunted houses and woods of the American imagination. We will engage the persistent question of why a country that values clarity, freedom, religious purity, inclusion, and progress, produces literature so often characterized by darkness, claustrophobia, madness, monstrosity, and haunting. In other words, how do these novels and short stories challenge the idealized view of America as a beacon to the world and what do they reveal about alternative narratives (especially narratives of otherness) that confront the dominant story of a âself-evidentâ culture? More specifically, we will look at dialogues between the American dream and madness, between ânormalâ communities and maniacal individuals, between âThe City on the Hillâ and the âwildernessâ beneath. Among many other questions we will address: What is the relationship between the distinctly interior notion of America as an idea (a dream) and the psychological nightmares expressed in many of these texts? How is Americaâs roots in Puritanism a source of haunting? Why is nature often the source of paranoia for the dominant culture? Why might the American South be a repository for the Gothic and the grotesque? Students will contribute weekly short responses to a class discussion forum, will lead a class discussion on a relevant topic, and will write a midterm paper and a final paper. Major works to be discussed include, but are not limited to: Brownâs Weiland, Hawthorneâs Young Goodman Brown and Other Tales, Poeâs Great Tales and Poems, Melvilleâs Benito Cereno, Gilmanâs âThe Yellow Wallpaper,â Lovecraftâs Call of the Cthulhu and Other Weird Tales, OâConnorâs Selected Short Stories, and Jacksonâs The Haunting of Hill House.
