title: Literary Modernisms
description: What does it mean for a work of art to be modern? What does it entail to implicate a literary text as modernist? What is the significance of modernism as a descriptive category for the dominant literary movement in early 20th century Europe? Does modernism have a project, and if so, what are its limitations and how does modernism address these limits? Through readings of texts by Joseph Conrad, James Joyce, E.M. Forster, T. S. Eliot, Franz Kafka, Virginia Woolf, Gertrude Stein, Nancy Cunard, and Samuel Beckett among others, this course introduces students to the various critical attempts to define modernism and to comprehend its aims, its intentions, and its modes of resistance to the work of art. Analyzing the historical and cultural pressures informing its emergence, we shall examine both how and why modernist texts enact this rupture. Finally, we shall consider modernismâs surprising comeback that in many ways informs our understanding of globalization, as well as the shared, discrepant experience implicated in modernismsâ transnational return.

title: Literary Modernisms
description: What does it mean for a work of art to be modern? What does it entail to implicate a literary text as modernist? What is the significance of modernism as a descriptive category for the dominant literary movement in early 20th century Europe? Does modernism have a project, and if so, what are its limitations and how does modernism address these limits? Through readings of texts by Joseph Conrad, James Joyce, E.M. Forster, T. S. Eliot, Franz Kafka, Virginia Woolf, Gertrude Stein, Nancy Cunard, and Samuel Beckett among others, this course introduces students to the various critical attempts to define modernism and to comprehend its aims, its intentions, and its modes of resistance to the work of art. Analyzing the historical and cultural pressures informing its emergence, we shall examine both how and why modernist texts enact this rupture. Finally, we shall consider modernismâs surprising comeback that in many ways informs our understanding of globalization, as well as the shared, discrepant experience implicated in modernismsâ transnational return.

title: Modern & Avant Garde Poetry
description: This course will examine diverse currents in the explosive, hybridizing, deregulated, re-forming poetry of the early to mid-twentieth century: the revolution of the word. Film and visual art will necessarily supplement our study, since the â-ismsâ or movements we will be most concerned with found expression in a variety of media. Call it the rise of inter-artistic vanguards. The chief tendencies within French Symbolism, Italian Futurism, Russian Futurism, Dada, and Surrealism, together with the multifarious work of Charles Baudelaire and Guillaume Apollinaire, will surveyed. Careful to understand that these movements do not account for the entire poetic field of Modernism, we will position the impulses ranged under these names--as is customary--in relation to broad historical and social changes then underway in the West, taking special notice of responses to World War I, developments in machine technology, and an ongoing urbanization.

title: Literature of WWI
description: The somberly sonorous voice of Yeats in his 1920 âThe Second Comingâ perhaps most wondrously confronts both the horror of WWI, the brute itself, and the ensuing madness unleashed upon a new century perceiving its ideals lost but not gone: âTurning and turning in the widening gyre. . . Things fall apart. . . The blood-dimmed tide is loosed, and everywhere [t]he ceremony of innocence is drowned. . . And what rough beast, its hour come round at last, [s]louches toward Bethelehem to be born?â Modernismâs vision ghosts the warâs bitter but resilient afterbirth, offering us a battlefield strewn with individual suffering and resurrection beside the mass shame and redemption. And we, the readers in Engl 185.1, enter the surreal fray of chivalric courage that becomes the sacrificial charge to rescue friend and the barbaric attack to defeat foe. This course invites its participants to read a medley of perspectives and genres of WWI literature in order to ask how and why this war both reflected and catapulted change in the ways we understand and value life, provoking such questions as where is our present or immediate identity, both individual and collective? How might we justify an idealistic regard for the past, given our shattered memory? How do we sustain a hope that both trembles before and yearns toward a future? Participants will complete two short argumentative essays and one longer argumentative research paper, come to each class prepared to interact in critical thinking responses to texts and their ideas, and collaborate with colleagues to prepare a panel on such topics as trench warfare; gas: the weapon of moral choice; women on the battlefield; war photography and painting; the Dada art movement; WWI films/series such as Freaks, Dr. Zhivago, A Very Long Engagement, Lawrence of Arabia, and Downtown Abbey; the international politics of peace; and idealismâs resistance to realism. Class texts for spring 2013 include: All Quiet on the Western Front (Erich Maria Remarque), Mrs. Dalloway (Virginia Woolf), the WWI poets, Regeneration (Pat Barker), 1919 (John Dos Passos), and Birdsong (Sebastian Faulks). Excerpts from Vera Brittain, Peter Englund, Hermione Lee, Robert Graves, David Jones, Lytton Strachey, Paul Fussell, and John Keegan will be available via course reserve.

title: Modern British Literature
description: The course will look closely at work by some of the major writers of the 20th Century in an attempt to discover how that work might reflect and respond to some of the important historic, political, and intellectual forces of its time. It will consider, among other writers, the poetry of Hardy, Hopkins, Yeats, Thomas, Auden, and Larkin, the fiction of Conrad, Joyce, and Woolf, the plays of Synge, Beckett, Pinter, and Friel. The course, which carries four credits, will include a session on Friday to allow time for some close and careful attention to the very challenging poetry that students will be asked to read. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Modernist Women Writers
description: In the early twentieth century, modernist writers in America and Europe developed literary techniques, such as stream of consciousness, to represent the experience of inhabiting the modern world and to separate themselves from past literary, cultural, and moral traditions. In this course, we will explore the major contributions women writers in particular made to modernism. Specifically, we will be concerned with how these writers reflect on the process of writing itself. That is, what do these writers perceive as the challenges to women writing? Do they believe that there is a distinct way that women write or should write for the modern world? How and why do they innovate narrative, poetic, and dramatic form? How does the emergence of new fields of study, such as psychology, anthropology, and sociology, in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries inform their understanding of identity and sexuality? How do friendships between female writers lead to creative partnerships? To answer these questions, we will read essays, manifestos, poems, short stories, and novels by authors, such as Virginia Woolf, Katherine Mansfield, Charlotte Perkins Gilman, Gertrude Stein, Mina Loy, Jean Rhys, and Zora Neale Hurston. In this course, students will practice close reading and critical thinking skills, write analytical papers, and give one presentation.

title: British Theatre since 1950
description: The productions in London of Samuel Beckettâs Waiting for Godot (1954) and of John Osborne's Look Back in Anger (1956) at the Royal Court Theatre are said to mark a turning point in the history of British theatre in the twentieth century. The plays that followed on the British stage were different in theme and usually in form from those that had been written and produced in the first half of the twentieth century, and they reflected not simply a new kind of theatre in Britain, but a new way of seeing the world outside it. /The course will consider some memorable plays by important British playwrights of the last fifty to sixty years, giving attention to theme, form, and possibilities for performance. It will define British loosely, including not only such English playwrights as Terence Rattigan, TS Eliot, John Osborne, Harold Pinter, Tom Stoppard, and Caryl Churchill, but also such Irish playwrights Samuel Beckett, Brian Friel, Martin McDonough, and Conor McPherson, and the South African playwright Athol Fugard. /The course hopes to come to some understanding of the distinct focus and idiom of the British theatre over the last 50 to 60 years and to discover how it might reflect the values and beliefs of the world in which the plays were written and performed. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their appreciation of the theatrical power of these plays. (The course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so). They will also be asked to attend at a performance of a relevant play, probably Martin McDonoughâs A Behanding in Spokane at The Keegan Theatre in Washington.

title: Orwell Between Culture and System
description: Orwell Between Culture and System The course examines the texts of George Orwell, among the most widely read and visible figures of twentieth-century literature. In our reading of works such as The Road to Wigan Pier, Homage to Catalonia, Keep the Aspidistra Flying, Burmese Days, Coming Up for Air, Animal Farm, Nineteen Eighty-Four, as well as his essays, we shall consider the interrelated subjects of Orwellâs representation of Englishness, his accounts of the role of the writer, and the confluence of forces under which he was writingâthe Spanish Civil War, the rise of Fascism, Stalinism, the growing resistance to British colonial rule, and the lateness of empire. A strong emphasis will be placed on the problems raised by Orwellâs literary techniques, particularly as they relate to questions of ideology, knowledge, and subjectivity. MUTUAL EXCLUSION: This course carries a mutual exclusion with the following courses; you may not entroll in it if you have completed any of the following with a passing grade: ENGL 242

title: African Novel
description: This course examines novels from the twentieth and twenty-first centuries by writers from Zimbabwe, Nigeria, Kenya, Botswana, South Africa, and Ghana. We read these works in conjunction with critical and theoretical writings to discover how histories of colonialism and anti-colonial struggle, apartheid and globalization have interacted with indigenous practices to help shape the varied and shifting forms of novel-writing across the continent. The course provides an introduction to issues animating Africana and postcolonial studies, while aiming to develop and hone skills of close reading literary and critical texts. We will read works by Tutuola, Ng?gi, Head, Adichie, Aidoo, Emecheta, Coetzee, Dangarembga, Mpe, Gordimer, and others. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: 20th-C. Irish Literature
description: Twentieth-Century Irish Literature Revolution and Independence; Colonization and Decolonization; Nationalism and Disillusion; Development and Underdevelopmentâthe history of Ireland in the twentieth century is the history of the world writ small. Irish literature of the twentieth century has, not coincidentally, played a central part in the global library, and in this course we will engage with the complex interactions between politics and aesthetics that produced a rich tradition of writing in Ireland. This will be a survey course, covering the best of Irish writing from 1900 to the present. The course will cover prose, poetry and drama, giving a sense of the full range of literary production across genres. Authors will include W.B. Yeats, Augusta Gregory, George Bernard Shaw, James Joyce, Elizabeth Bowen, Samuel Beckett, J.M. Synge, Flann OâBrien, Seamus Heaney and others. Our approach to the literature will be broadly postcolonial, and guided by the pairs of abstract nouns above, which is to say that we will be thinking about how Irish literature in the twentieth century responds to the global legacy of colonialism. While the course title and reading list gesture towards the nation, we will venture into this material remembering always that the frame of the nation, which has so often defined how we organize courses in literature, might not always be sufficient or appropriate. We will look with a skeptical eye on nation and nationalism. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Contemporary India: Film & Lit
description: Understanding contemporary India through Film and Literature This course attempts to introduce students to major issues and debates in contemporary India. Literary texts and films are examined in some detail to reflect on critical/theoretical writing on India, not merely as illustrations of a fully formed theory but as instances of elaborations and enunciations of social and political change. The period under consideration begins roughly with the lifting of the State of Emergency in 1977 and witnessed the eruption of multiple crises that scholars have attributed to the breakdown of the nationalist consensus of the Independence period. This was accompanied by the rise to prominence of new political constituencies and subject positions alike. Textual forms and their thematic concerns too underwent significant changes. Students will read a range of literary and film texts alongside critical writings to understand the socio-political context as well as the transformations occurring in the domain of cultural representation. Students are expected to be familiar with the readings/films listed for discussion and make short classroom presentations, which will be assessed. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: American Expatriate Writers
description: This course will explore the fiction and poetry of some of the principal American Expatriate writers, a tradition which has emerged as a rich vein in American Literature. In particular, we will investigate how their perception of America, coupled with their interaction with modern art and with each other, stimulated an experiment in realignment of traditional American values with corresponding innovative literary techniques. Short stories by Hemingway, Fitzgerald and Gertrude Stein; novels by Hemingway, Fitzgerald and Virginia Woolf; poetry by Ezra Pound, e.e. cummings and T. S. Eliot. (Virginia Woolf was not American, but a significant feminist contemporary.) This literature is best appreciated in the context of the history of the times and the writers; consequently, we will view film documentaries which will entail a few additional evening classes. Students will be invited to offer power point presentations on significant issues for their peers in class. Requirements: Three analytical papers or a journal with entries written and submitted throughout the course. Three quizzes and no exams. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Science Fiction and Fantasy
description: âBut the future must be met, however stern and iron it beââElizabeth Gaskell This course will investigate the concept of the future within a global cultural imagination, examining a wide range of literary and cinematic genres in science fiction & fantasy. The class will read a short selection from earlier science fiction writersâfrom H.G. Wells and Arthur C. Clarke to Philip K. Dickâas a means to discuss how speculations of the future have evolved over time. We will address how such classic works speak to contemporary issues about the science of geophysical disasters, the destruction of the environment, financial Armageddon, pandemics and contagions, governmental control and the chilling prospects of nuclear war and global terrorism. Other topics will encompass Afrofuturism, utopia and dystopia, myths and legends, vampires, epic quests in historical and fantasy novels and Japanese anime. Other writers may include Haruki Murakami, Octavia Butler, Ted Chiang, Amitav Ghosh, Elizabeth Kostova and J. R. R. Tolkien.

title: Science Fiction and Fantasy
description: âBut the future must be met, however stern and iron it beââElizabeth Gaskell This course will investigate the concept of the future within a global cultural imagination, examining a wide range of literary and cinematic genres in science fiction & fantasy. Dickâas a means to discuss how speculations of the future have evolved over time. We will address how such classic works speak to contemporary issues about the science of geophysical disasters, the destruction of the environment, financial Armageddon, pandemics and contagions, governmental control and the chilling prospects of nuclear war and global terrorism. Other topics will encompass Afrofuturism, utopia and dystopia, myths and legends, vampires, epic quests in historical and fantasy novels and Japanese anime. title: Literatures of the American West
description: What is the west? Where does the west begin, and where does it end? In this course, we will understand the American west in at least three distinct ways. First, we will approach the west as a concept and a myth that exists within cultural and popular imagination and that changes over time. Second, we will consider the west as a place of lived-experiences within specific historical and political contexts. Third, we will contemplate the west as a landscape that has and continues to inspire conservation movements. With these categories in mind, we will read a broad range of literary texts from 1860-present that represent the frontier, meditate on migration (forced and voluntary), and explore identity, race, and gender. We will discuss the formal choices these writers make in their texts to represent the west. The authors that we will read for this course may include Mark Twain, Willa Cather, Zitkala Sa, Edward Abbey, Wallace Stegner, Annie Proulx, Sherman Alexie, Maxine Hong Kingston, Cormac McCarthy, Terry Tempest Williams, Gary Snyder, and N. Scott Momaday. In this course, students will practice close reading and critical thinking skills, write analytical papers, and give one presentation.

title: American Detective Fiction
description: As the twentieth century began, a new literary form emerged, a kind of urban poetry constructed out of images of gals, guts, guns, and gumshoes with a drinking problem and a fondness for chivalric myth. This course explores the evolution of American hard-boiled detective fiction and the larger mysteries lurking in, (to use Raymond Chandlerâs immortal phrase) âa world gone wrong.â Beginning with the âurban shockâ tales of Edgar Allan Poe and proceeding into the Golden Age British mystery tradition, this course goes on to discuss pulp fiction detective stories of the teens and twenties. Hard boiled detective fiction begins in earnest with the short stories of Dashiell Hammett and his classic novel, The Maltese Falcon. Raymond Chandler and Ross Macdonald--the other two members of The Holy Trinity of Crime Fiction--are represented by The Big Sleep and Black Money, respectively. Other hard-boiled writers whose work will be covered include: James M. Cain, Mickey Spillane, Robert B. Parker, Sara Paretsky, and Richard Stevenson. In addition, we will discuss highlights of the complementary tradition of film noir, including the classic films: âDouble Indemnity,â âThe Maltese Falcon,â âMildred Pierce,â âThe Big Heatâ and âA Touch of Evil.â This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Literature of the City
description: "New York City is, arguably, the cultural capital of twentieth- (and, so far), twenty-first century Americaâthe center of literature, music, art, architecture, and fashion as well as the focal point for political debates on the viability of cities, immigration, and the meaning of America itself. This course proposes to study autobiographical, fictional, non-fictional, poetic, and cinematic accounts of twentieth-century life in New York City in order to understand the dreams and nightmares embodied by the city in the popular imagination. Beginning with the first decades of the twentieth century, we will investigate the decline of âold guardâ aristocratic society in the early 1900s and the nature of the immigrant experience in New York. The course will then focus on the Jazz Age when New York artists, writers, and architects shaped a new ethos of modernity that would define âThe American Century.â Attention will also be devoted to the various âcounter-culturalâ movements (e.g. the rise of the American Communist Party, the Beat Movement) that erupted in New York City during the early and middle decades of the last century. The course will conclude with contemporary dystopian and stubbornly utopian predictions about the future of New York City and urban life in America. A class walking-tour of New York City is scheduled for Saturday, March 16th. Attendance on this field trip is mandatory and any student who has conflicts should not enroll in the class.

title: Pulp Fiction
description: This course will investigate the highly stylized world of popular fiction, including the literary genres of the western, the adventure story, hard-boiled detective fiction, true crime, the historical romance, the suspense thriller, sci-fi and fantasy. We will observe the beginnings of pulp fiction from the dime novels and the penny dreadfuls in the 19th century to the literary boom of pulp fiction in the 1920s and 1930s. We will be connecting major themes in popular fiction to the growth of old time radio programs and to the study of manga, media, television and film and will be watching a series of documentaries and analyzing pulp magazine jackets. Pulp fiction writers may include Edgar Rice Burroughs, Zane Grey, Dashiell Hammett, Mickey Spillane, Ted Chiang, Koushun Takami, Truman Capote, Margaret Atwood, Susan Elizabeth Phillips and J. K. Rowling. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: African American Literature 1903-Present
description: The purpose of this level one course is to introduce students to African American literature and literary/cultural criticism. As a âsurveyâ of 20th century African American literature, we examine how African American writers transformed the different literary modes in which they wrote, and consider how the literary texts articulate the cultural, political, and national concerns that shaped 20th century America. Throughout the semester, we will return repeatedly to issues of race, class, gender, sexuality, and religion to consider why these authors are concerned with these particular matters. We will think energetically about the historicity of the texts, and the textuality of history; in other words, we will consider how history enriches our understanding of the texts, and how the texts enrich our understanding of history. While previous familiarity with African American literature is not a requirement for this course, regular attendance, active reading, and a firm commitment to succeed are. Students should expect 4 short (2-3 pages) close reading assignments, and midterm examination and final examinations. Class participation and attendance also will factor into course grades.//Authors will include: Booker T. Washington, W.E.B. Du Bois, Langston Hughes, Nella Larsen, Richard Wright, Ann Petry, Zora Neale Hurson, Amiri Baraka, Toni Morrison, August Wilson, and asha bandele.

We will think energetically about the historicity of the texts, and the textuality of history; in other words, we will consider how history enriches our understanding of the texts, and how the texts enrich our understanding of history. While previous familiarity with African American literature is not a requirement for this course, regular attendance, active reading, and a firm commitment to succeed are. Students should expect 4 short (2-3 pages) close reading assignments, and midterm examination and final examinations. Class participation and attendance also will factor into course grades.//Authors will include: Booker T. Washington, W.E.B. Du Bois, Langston Hughes, Nella Larsen, Richard Wright, Ann Petry, Zora Neale Hurson, Amiri Baraka, Toni Morrison, August Wilson, and asha bandele.

title: 20th Century Black Women Writers
description: In her 1993 Nobel Lecture, Toni Morrison stated: "Narrative has never been merely entertainment for me. It is, I believe, one of the principal ways in which we absorb knowledge." In the twentieth-century, many African American women writers, including Morrison, have used narratives to transmit knowledge. In so doing, they participate in the enterprise of self-definition and self-representation while challenging the "always already" body of knowledge concerning Black life in the US. By documenting and revealing African American culture and history in their writings, Black women writers also serve as cultural and historical conservators. Beginning with Nella Larsen's modernist novel, we will read representative texts by African American women writers from a variety of critical approaches, employing especially cultural criticism, critical race theory, and Black feminist thought. We will also read essays by literary critics and theorists. We will consider: what have historically been the concerns of African American women writers, what is the significance of race, gender, and class in African American literature, and how do Black women writers expand our understanding of African America, of America, of African American literature, of American literature? Novelists may include Zora Neale Hurston, Alice Walker, Gloria Naylor, Octavia Butler, and Toni Morrison. This course is reading intensive. Students may expect quizzes, a midterm, short papers, and a final examination.

title: Survey of US Latino Literature & Culture
description: English 233 offers students chronologically organized surveys of literary and other cultural products by US-based writers and artists of âLatino/Hispanicâ descent and of non-literary, mostly critical and scholarly writings which position US Latino/a literary and cultural production in larger, more complex, inter-disciplinary contexts. These latter include fields as diverse and as intersecting as history, politics, geography, linguistics and popular- and mass-cultural studies, as well as inter-disciplines as established, inter-active, and evolving as American, Latin American, Cultural, Gender and Queer Studies. As an introduction both to a set of texts and artifacts which in part comprise the object(s) of âUS Latino/a Literary and Cultural Studies,â and to the very field and practice of study which names itself thus, English 233 challenges students to think, and work, outside the familiar terrain explored by more traditional courses in literary history and interpretation. Literary writers may include MarÃ­a Amparo Ruiz de Burton, JosÃ© MartÃ­, Esmeralda Santiago, Cristina GarcÃ­a, Julia Alvarez, HÃ©ctor Tobar, Nilo Cruz and Junot DÃ­az. Visual, pop- and mass-cultural work will include audio and video work by musical and other performing artists, select works covering cinema, television and new media histories from the 20th to the 21st centuries, as well as alternative expressive forms, like mural/graffiti art, guerrilla street activism, and live musical performance.

title: Asian American Literature
description: This course introduces students to Asian American fiction, autobiography, poetry, film, drama and the graphic novel. Beginning with an investigation into the seminal literary works by Frank Chin and Maxine Hong Kingston, we will discuss the broad social contexts in which Asian American literature had emerged in the 1970s. We will approach the issue of Asian American cultural authenticity in light of the âculture warsâ and the âgender wars,â and read works of social document fiction by Carlos Bulosan and Milton Murayama. Other readings may include literary works by Don Lee, Thien-bao Thuc Phi, Laura Joh Rowland, Jhumpa Lahiri and Kaiji Kawaguchi. Requirements: two short papers (4-6 pages), a class presentation, a take-home midterm exam and a finalresearch paper or final exam.

title: 20th Century American Nature Writing and Ecocriticism
description: This course examines key twentieth-century texts in the American tradition of writing about human relationships with the natural world. We will read canonical non-fiction nature writing by authors such as Mary Austin, Aldo Leopold, Edward Abbey, N. Scott Momaday, Barry Lopez, and Leslie Marmon Silko, and we will tackle fiction that addresses environmental issues by authors not usually considered to be nature writers. The non-fiction for the course may include: Leopoldâs Sand County Almanac (1949), Abbeyâs Desert Solitaire (1968), and Momadayâs The Way to Rainy Mountain (1969), and the novels may include: Wallace Stegnerâs Angle of Repose (1971), Louise Erdrichâs Tracks (1988), and Barbara Kingsolverâs Animal Dreams (1990). The course assumes that nature is a social and linguistic construct and that depictions of the land and its inhabitants both reflect and influence the way Americans relate to the natural world. Aiming to deconstruct dualisms such as human/nature and civilization/wilderness, the course will explore a variety of questions: How is nature represented in the books we read? What characterizes nature writing as a genre? How have concepts of nature and wilderness changed over the century? How do contemporary environmental events and perceived crises impact the literary imagination? What relationship do the texts suggest between environmental degradation and human exploitation? To help us make sense of this literature, we will read ecologically informed criticism by scholars from interdisciplinary fields. As this course fulfills a humanities writing II requirement, assignments will center on the writing process (prewriting, drafting, responding, revising, editing, and publication) to develop critical thinking skills that can be used across disciplines. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Faulkner
description: This course will investigate one of the greatest American literary achievements of the modern era. We will begin with Go Down, Moses, a collection of interrelated short stories as an entree into the world of Faulkner. Subsequently, we will read and discuss, in chronological order, the major works of Faulknerâs most prolific years: The Sound and the Fury; Sanctuary; As I Lay Dying; Light in August; and Absalom, Absalom. We will discuss these works in terms of predominant themes, psychological and sociological, and Faulknerâs corresponding experimentation in a rich variety of literary techniques. We will also view and discuss The Reivers in movie form. Requirements: a mid-term examination, two 5 page papers and one 10-12 page final paper (or a journal). This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Literature and Photography
description: This course explores words and photographic images as ways of seeing the world, and as evidentiary ways for human beings to represent the world around them. We will explore the interactions between text and image in both literary works that include photos as well as by photo sequences accompanied by written texts. We will pay particular attention to the philosophy, history, and current role of the camera as an instrument for political activism, social reform, and cultural identity. Readings include: How the Other Half Lives, Let us Now Praise Famous Men, American Exodus, Rings of Saturn, Coming Through Slaughter and the humbly entitled What Matters: The World's Preeminent Photojournalists and Thinkers Depict Essential Issues of Our Time. No prior knowledge of photography is necessary.

title: TS Eliot
description: We will begin the semester by reading several of Eliot's earlier poems to investigate both his experimentation with poetic technique as well as his thematic concerns. The course will concentrate, however, on The Waste Land. Properly to understand Eliot's opus, however, requires reading its principal allusions: Scripture (Old and New), Dante, Virgil, St. Augustine, Tarot cards, fertility gods and rituals, Grail Knight romances, Shakespeare, Thomas Kyd, Baudelaire, and the Baghavad Gita--virtually a liberal arts curriculum in itself. The final re-reading of The Waste Land, however, with allusions understood and experienced, should represent an extraordinary experience of poetic technique and literature, both East and West.

title: Philip Roth: Secular Jewish Fiction
description: This course is devoted to a study of the fiction of Philip Roth focusing mostly, but not exclusively, on the âKepeshâ novels, the lighter âZuckerman cycle,â and some of his more recent work. Perhaps no author, living or dead, has interrogated the problems of identity, self and the dilemma of artâs relation to reality more than Roth has. We will explore those issues as well as the recurring exploration of the modern Jewish predicament and paradox that has surfaced consistently in his work across five decades. This will give us ample opportunity to linger on the âsecularâ dimensions of his oeuvre; Roth is at once a champion, a chronicler, and maybe even a eulogist for a secular Jewish-American way of life whose future is no longer assured.

title: Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: This course will focus on a history and examination of the set of theories, practices and methodologies which define the field of Cultural Studies. By reading and situating the theory, we can critique the production and consumption of cultural objects, including popular culture and avant garde art. In order to pursue critical analyses of our own in the class, we will interrogate concepts such as culture, ideology, representation, taste, style and subculture, with attention to the specific tensions between language and visual images. Texts will draw from critical and cultural theory, literature, film, video, music and the graphic novel, with a particular emphasis on contemporary artistic modes of expression.Â This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Introduction to Cultural Studies

Texts will draw from critical and cultural theory, literature, film, video, music and the graphic novel, with a particular emphasis on contemporary artistic modes of expression.Â This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Documentary Storytelling
description: Documentary work in radio/audio, video/film, photography and non-fiction writing capture and fix the ways historical events are judged and remembered. This course investigates the rhetorical, cultural, and political work of documentary expression within specific social and historical contexts of the 20th/21st centuries. The class will be organized around three controversial subjects that have been focal points for documentary inquiry: poverty, racism, and the war on terror. We shall explore the elusive nature of âtruthâ in photographic and cinematic works and the often tenuous relationship between a documenting image and reality. Questions we will consider include: how do documentary works seek to inform, reform, challenge, or stabilize politics, cultures and our minds? How do our own beliefs determine what we see? What are the ethical and ideological issues raised by the genre? What does the seduction of spectacle sometimes conceal or allow us to forget? We will study a range of written and visual texts including: How the Other Half Lives, Without Sanctuary: Lynching Photography in America, Regarding the Pain of Others, NPR This American Life, Believing is Seeing, Fahrenheit 9/11, Standard Operating Procedure, and a selection of theoretical essays. As a Level I elective, the class emphasizes close reading, textual analysis and writing in the form of several short papers. No prior knowledge of photography or film is necessary. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Problems in Postmodernism
description: This course examines a variety of literatures that are crossed or mixed or remade in some way, and as such, complicate our understanding of authorship, creativity and literary value.Â Â Mashed-up film clips and remixes that we find on the internet are the most obvious examples of such forms today, but there are many more less obvious examples dating back to the last century and earlier.Â //These works give us an opportunity to ask important questions about our current cultural moment and literature generally: What makes something literary?Â Â How is literature adapting to digital culture and the globalizing consumer culture connected to it?Â Â How do changes in the way we view creativity, originality and authorship affect literature, and what do these changes require of us as readers, viewers consumers and citizens? Â To answer these questions we will take a critical, often comparative, approach to the material.Â Â In addition to examining primary works in fiction, poetry, the graphic novel, film and audio, we will follow developments in cultural theory, remix theory, visual rhetoric and semiotics (the theory of signs).//The course material, as well as the concepts we will discuss and develop in class, will be the basis for two writing assignments and an in-class presentation.Â Â In connection with these assignments, we will discuss topic and thesis development, paper organization and research and examine a variety of secondary sources that will help us situate our comments and critiques. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Introduction to U.S. Film History & Technology
description: This course is an introduction to the history of US film: its technological evolution, economic development, and cultural impact over the past 115 years. We will focus on how different film technologies affect the moviesâ social and political significance in the US and examine the role technology plays in the âculture industries.â Specifically, I want us to ask how innovations in film production and exhibition have been used to refresh or redirect the moviesâ effects on their audiences. Film and the cinema may seem like well-established phenomena today, but they have been the newest of new mediaâmultiple times! âNew mediaâ often seems synonymous with âdigital mediaâ in contemporary discourse, but in fact there is nothing inherently digital about newness. This course will study the evolution of film as a new medium, from its birth in the 1890s through its popular and technical reinventions and the (allegedly) spectacular future of digital cinema. âIntro to US Film History and Technologyâ will also acquaint you with the basic vocabulary and methodologies of film studies, including formal analysis, historicism, and ideological critique. Weâll read famous and foundational articles in the field of film and media studies and analyze how critics have chosen to study motion pictures, considering what their approaches exclude or overlook. So in addition to studying various incarnations of ânewnessâ in films like Modern Times, Singin' in the Rain, Jaws, and Terminator 2: Judgment Day, we will read articles by Tom Gunning, Janet Wasko, Douglas Gomery, and Paul D. Miller to understand how ânew mediaâ have always been with us and how the term is deployed strategically to serve different economic, cultural, and political ends. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Post-WWII American Cinema
description: This course will examine media production in the United States afterÂ WWII--a period in which the terms "The Cold War," "ContainmentÂ Culture," "The Red Scare," "The Atomic Age," and "The Children'sÂ Decade" have been retroactively applied. Students can expect toÂ examine a variety of cinematic texts from a mix of critical lenses,Â including class, race, gender, sexuality, and disability.

title: Bollywood and Indian Cinema
description: The term Bollywood is used as a shorthand to refer to either Hindi language films produced in Bombay/Mumbai or Indian cinema in general. Film scholars note that Bollywood is not all of Indian cinema but a global culture industry that is the point of intersection of music, television, food, advertising and fashion industries. Film viewing is a small part of Bollywoodâs offerings and pleasures and only a small number of films produced in Bombay become Bollywoodised. This course begins by asking what Bollywood and Bollywoodisation is. It then elaborates on how Bollywood distinguishes itself from the rest of Indian cinema: aesthetically, economically and politically. Drawing on a range of critical writings on Indian cinema, the course analyses films in Hindi, Telugu and Tamilâthe largest industries in the countryâand across a variety of genres.

title: SEM: Question of Equality
description: Literature and Political Theory in Africa and the West "Equality," is, in many ways, the cornerstone of modern conceptions of society, government, and global human rights. Since the Enlightenment, it has been the express concern of the "Western" world. But it is also, historically and culturally, controversial: What does "equality" mean? Or, as a famous law article on the topic asks, "Equality of what?" This course will trace the European and US philosophical traditions of equality alongside cross-cultural conceptions and critiques of the term, particularly those produced by African authors and artists. In doing so, it hopes to unsettle easy assumptions about the term's meaning, and to bridge American ideals of equality to a global understanding of the term. This interdisciplinary seminar, funded by an NEH Enduring Questions grant developed by English (Prof. Samantha Pinto) and African Studies/SFS (Professor Lahra Smith), will include a field trip to Thomas Jeffersonâs estate, Monticello, as well as to other DC area cultural sites. Assignments will include group presentations, critical analysis papers, web postings, and research intensive, independent final projects; Authors will include Plato, Locke, Nkrumah, Coetzee, James, and Ba, among others. Students from English, Political Science, Philosophy, Comparative Literature, Justice and Peace Studies, Womenâs and Gender Studies, African Studies, African American Studies, Government, History, Culture and Politics, and all other disciplines are welcome. In order to take this course, students are required to attend a Saturday field trip to Monticello. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Law and Literature
description: The course takes up the issue of justice in the law and literature context. How can we read or reread classics likeÂ To Kill a Mockingbird,Â Billy Budd,Â andÂ The TrialÂ through the lenses provided by the great philosophers of justice like Hume, Wollstonecraft, Bentham, Mill, Kant, Rawls, Gilligan and Sen and by neuroscientists and post-modern philosophers? Students will be asked to bring contemporary issues on justice to the class and to write short essays weekly as well as two longer essays. Some of the questions we will be asking include: Can justice be reduced to a rationally-imposed formula? What is the relationship between emotions and justice? Do babies have a sense of justice? How about animals? How do individual justice, social justice and global justice approaches interact? And what do we make of trolleyology?Â

title: Introduction to Transatlantic Studies
description: What makes American literature âAmerican,â British literature âBritish,â post-colonial literature âpost-colonialâ? How should we read or re-read an authorâs work when we discover that the author lived in multiple countries, or drew influences and formative experiences from a life of travel, of military service, of exploration, or of forced migration or captivity in a foreign land? From the earliest histories of European encounters with the Americas, European and American encounters with Africa, and Anglo-American interrelationâwhat contemporary politicians often call the âspecial relationshipââthe Atlantic ocean has been a conduit for trade, nation-building, colonial expansion, slavery, warfare, the mixing of cultures, and the exchange of ideas. In short, the Atlantic has been an active globalization network, for good and for bad, for centuries before the phrase âglobalization networkâ ever existed. This course will provide an introduction to the concept of âtransatlanticâ relations as both a literary-historical phenomenon and a field of study, with the aim of addressing the central questions of how the Atlantic mediated the literatures, politics, and trade relations of the âAtlantic worldâ from roughly 1500-1900, as well as how the idea of transatlanticism changes the way we read and understand ânationalâ literatures affected by transatlantic relations. This interactive, discussion-based course will cover a range of literatures, correspondences, and historical accounts related to the Americas, Africa, the Caribbean, and Europe, including Caminhaâs letter to King Manuel I of Portugal in 1500, Equianoâs Interesting Narrative of the Atlantic slave trade, Defoeâs Moll Flanders, Gilbert Imlayâs account of transatlantic marriage and divorce law in The Emigrants, and C.L.R. Jamesâ riveting history of Toussaint LâOuverture and the Haitian Revolution, The Black Jacobins. Primary assignments will include analytical essays, multimedia presentations, and public-interest blogging. Please feel free to get in touch if you would like to know more.

title: Narratives of Travel
description: In this class, we will examine some accounts of travels and encounters with the exotic, the foreign, and the alien. We will begin the class by considering two narratives that have incalculably influenced the Western concept of travel and encounter, the Exodus story from the Bible and Homerâs Odyssey. Next, we will exam two nineteenth-century textsâTwainâs Huckleberry Finn and Conradâs Heart of Darknessâand then move to some representative twentieth-century works: Forsterâs Passage to India, Faulknerâs As I Lay Dying, and Pirsigâs Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance. Throughout the course, we will exam a number of related concerns (for instance, the ideas of civilization and savagery and of slavery and freedom) and we will pay particular attention to the way the âOtherâ is used to construct various kinds of identity. /There will be several paper assignments. Each paper will ask you to answer a specific question or to pursue a specific task. These papers will be short (about five pages long). There will also be a take-home final exam. It will ask you to synthesize important elements of the course through the lens of Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance./ This class emphasizes close reading and textual analysis. The class will introduce you to some of the foundational concepts and terminology of the field./This class is governed by Georgetownâs Honor Code.

title: Literature & Human Rights
description: Global warming, environmental calamities, radioactive fallout, toxic drift, and the carcinogenic residues of modern warfare are forms of what this course defines as âslow violenceââdeadly catastrophes that occur gradually over a large geographical distances and spans of time that escape our public attention. In an age when the media fetishizes the spectacular, eye-catching, immediate, and dramatic events of violenceâcrashing airplanes, exploding towers, showers of incendiary bombs â this course asks how is it possible to provide a narrative account of environmental disaster and the contaminating aftermath of warfare that occur slowly, over long durations of time. If the fatal calamities of slow violence challenge our conventional conception of event of violence, these slow and creeping forces of ecological destruction often go undetected and unremembered. They disrupt the stability of place and render them irrecoverable to âdevelopmental refugeesâ and unimagined communities places of slow violence produce. If new technologies have accelerated the experience of time to renders fast faster, the destructive effects of slow violence appear as non-eventsâpart of another realm out of sync with electoral cycles, news hours, and the capacity of international courts and international laws to redress these experiences on the basis of testimony against threats that go unwitnessed and undetected over vast transnational spaces and slow and long durations of time. In our reading of works such as Indra Sinha's Animalâs People, Abdelrahman Munif's Cities of Salt, Rebecca Solnitâs Savage Dreams, Arundhati Royâs The Cost of Living, Ken Sero-Wiwaâs Genocide in Nigeria, Charles Bowdenâs Some of the Dead are Still Breathing, and W. G. Sebaldâs On the Natural History of Destruction, this course will consider the often unquestioned perspectives that obscure the relationship between imperialism, slow violence and human history. Throughout this course, we shall examine what imaginative and narrative forms can possible testify to the invisible and injurious toxins and radioactive ions of slow violence. If slow violence demands new imaginative forms to constitute its effects as a catastrophic event, then what kind of representational practices render them an event at all, if they have yet to run their course, and can only be mitigated or attenuated? Interdisciplinary readings in selections from Rachel Carsonâs Silent Spring, Raymond Williamsâs The Country and the City, Susan Sontagâs Regarding the Pain of Others, Rob Nixonâs Slow Violence and the Environmentalism of the Poor, Ursula Heiseâs Sense of Place and Sense of Planet, and Slavoj Å½iÅ¾ekâs Living in the End of Times will permit us to reconsider the representational strategies and narrative forms necessary to make the effects of slow violence comprehensible to a culture collectively distracted by its changing technological experience of time, unaware of how slow violence marks the gap between individual present and our collective planetary future.

title: Introduction to Disability Studies
description: This course explores disability from an interdisciplinary perspective: literature, first-person accounts, public policy advocacy, and the basic legal framework. Texts include short stories, personal narratives by individuals who have disabilities or by their family members, and articles by disability rights activists. We integrate film study with our readings to critically examine how pop culture represents, and misrepresents, disability and bodily difference. We will watch and discuss classic film productions of "The Christmas Carol" and "The Glass Menagerie," as well as clips from recent episodes of "Glee" and "Bones." We begin the course with a cluster of related topics: What is Disability? Why do definitions matter? How is disability socially constructed? We explore challenges for individuals with autism spectrum disorder, psychiatric disorders and PTSD, deafness and/or blindness. We also consider the intersection between disability and aging, focusing on Alzheimerâs as an example. We examine the advocacy efforts by parents of children with disabilities as they seek to access resources to meet their childrenâs needs. We consider the difficult policy choices that arise in health care allocation, educational opportunities, and social services in view of budget constraints. All readings are on Blackboard or on the Internet so students do not need to purchase a text for this course.

title: Motherhood: Representations
description: Motherhood is deemed one of the most ânaturalâ experiences binding women together across time and space.Â Â But as feminist poet and essayist Adrienne Rich famously argued in her landmark workÂ Of Woman Born, it is also a social institution with its own history and ideology. Our course examines this institution as a shifting, historically and culturally specific phenomenon given particularly potent life in cultural representations: that is, the literature, film, television, advertising, video, comics, etc. that surround us in everyday life.Â Â Analyzing foundational criticism and theory about motherhood alongside a variety of predominantly U.S. cultural textsâfromÂ I Love Lucy,Â Imitation of Life, and Sylvia Plathâs poetry toÂ The Cosby Show,Â Roseanne, and Toni MorrisonâsÂ BelovedÂ to Wendy LeeâsÂ Happy Family, and the recent documentaryÂ Google Babyâwe explore how differing notions of motherhood are constructed, contested, negotiated. One premise of the course is that motherhood cannot be universalized as an experience or as a right (not all women are urged or even permitted to mother); it is not innate nor necessarily a biological relation.Â Â And while the syllabus focuses largely on the U.S., its structure will address how western and non-western political relations are increasingly embedded in global circuits of motherhood via transnational adoption, surrogacy, and reproductive technologies.

title: Performance Theory
description: In this course students have the opportunity to analyze theories about everyday life as well as theories about dramatic and literary texts. Performance theorists investigate how embodied human behavior is represented on many different types of stages. Using Performance Studies methodologies, the students in this class will have the chance to study the representational strategies that shape and reshape disparate traditions, both cultural and literary. Weâll explore discrete events such as dramatic productions, poetry readings, Valentineâs Day celebrations and religious rituals, as well as the everyday performances of identity categories such as gender, race, and sexuality.Â Â This is an interdisciplinary course, drawing from linguistics, anthropology, philosophy, psychoanalysis, critical race theory, and gender & sexuality studies--as well as theatre studies.Â Â

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: This course is an introduction to the art and discipline of creative writing. Our exploration will take a number of forms: close study of the elements of craft (imagery, voice, character, etc.); vigorous discussion; analysis of the fiction, poetry, drama and creative non-fiction of published authors and of your peers, with special emphasis on the workshop process; and, above all, intensive writing of your own, both inside and outside of class. The course will culminate in a portfolio of your selected and revised work. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: This course is an introduction to the art and discipline of creative writing. Our exploration will take a number of forms: close study of the elements of craft (imagery, voice, character, etc.); vigorous discussion; analysis of the fiction, poetry, drama and creative non-fiction of published authors and of your peers, with special emphasis on the workshop process; and, above all, intensive writing of your own, both inside and outside of class. The course will culminate in a portfolio of your selected and revised work. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: "LINE, SENTENCE, SPACE" This is an introduction to the practice of imaginative writing. The course is divided into three parts, according to the form of the writing. The first part deals with verse and covers the elements of verse composition and a few of its modes: location, syllable, line, sound structure, figure, and the rest. The second part deals with prose: the basic unit of composition is the sentence rather than the verse line. We will try or hand at fabulous documents, multiple points of view, and iterated memories. The third ventures into the domain of visual literature, which seeks to reconcile the disparate systems of the word and the image. Here we will consider the calligramme or pattern poem, the use of the alphabet as a basic element in concrete and visual poetry, and the merging of form, color, and text in narrative space. The main work of the course will be the making of your own imaginative works, which you will circulate for discussion from time to time. In the belief that writing and reading are inseparable, you will also read a range of creative and critical texts, in order to expand your awareness of the possibilities of the written word. The close study of these works is meant to suggest approaches you might take in your own creative projects.

title: Introduction to Fiction Writing
description: In this class, youâll immerse yourselves in the art and discipline of fiction writing, with particular focus on the short story. Partly this means acquiring a writerâs critical eye for fiction, so youâll study the basic elements of successful fiction (character, plot, description, etc.) and use these tools to read and analyze contemporary short stories. Of course, the writer above all learns through doing; youâll therefore write a great deal of your own fictionâregular exercises as well as more fully developed and revised work. Much of this development will happen as a result of the workshop process, where youâll give one another extensive feedback on work submitted to the class as a whole.

title: Introduction to Fiction Writing
description: In this class, youâll immerse yourselves in the art and discipline of fiction writing, with particular focus on the short story. Partly this means acquiring a writerâs critical eye for fiction, so youâll study the basic elements of successful fiction (character, plot, description, etc.) and use these tools to read and analyze contemporary short stories. Of course, the writer above all learns through doing; youâll therefore write a great deal of your own fictionâregular exercises as well as more fully developed and revised work. Much of this development will happen as a result of the workshop process, where youâll give one another extensive feedback on work submitted to the class as a whole.

title: Introduction to Poetry Writing
description: This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Creative Nonfiction Workshop
description: An intensive writing workshop focused on discovering, researching and crafting creative nonfiction narrative. Readings and writing across the spectrum of this innovative âfourth genre.â Students should expect to write constantly and to contribute, as critical readers and writers, to the common cause of the workshop.Â

title: Film-making for Writers
description: Introduction to Writing for the Screen This course is designed to introduce students to the fundamental elements of writing for the large (film) and small (television) screen. It roots in the core precept that plot is character is action. Which means that the first object of study for all dramatic writing must be the examination of character. In workshop exercises, complemented by theoretical models from Keirsey and Greimas, students develop techniques for character analysis and development. At the same time, week by week, they examine character development in iconic films from a wide range of periods and genres. By the end of the term each student will have generated a short (20 minute) film for television and a short (20 minute) film for the wider screen. Students should note that in addition to class meetings during the week, they are also required to attend throughout the term regular film screenings on Sunday afternoons. Instructor Permission Only All interested students should contact Prof. Glavin (glavinj@georgetown.edu) for further information about the course. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Introduction to Rhetoric
description: How would Plato react to the characterization of Mitt Romney as a flip-flopper? What might Quintilian say about LeBron Jamesâs âThe Decisionâ? Questions like these direct our attention to rhetoric as an ancient art and as a controversial subject of contemporary relevance. This class will explore both the concerns of ancient rhetoriciansâthe arts of persuasionâas well as trends in contemporary rhetorical thought and performance. We will move back and forth between the ancient and the contemporary as a way of enriching our understanding of rhetoric itself and its legacy. The coursework for this class will include the reading of ancient texts, contemporary scholarship, and popular texts, as well as a significant amount of writing for a variety of audiences. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: CBL: Reading, Teaching, Social Reflection
description: Reading, Teaching and Social reflection is intended for students interested in teaching, and besides the usual classes, involves working for two hours one morning a week (the morning is chosen by the student) as a teacher's aide in one of three inner-city Catholic schools. These schools include Assumption School in Anacostia, Immaculate Conception School in Shaw, and Sacred Heart Bi-lingual School in Mount Pleasant, all of which have been welcoming Georgetown students for the past 13 years. The focus of the course is on teaching reading in the primary grades, and students from this class have gone on to Teach for America, the Inner-City-Teaching-Corps and to degree programs in Education at Berkeley, Columbia Teachers College and Harvard, among other places. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: CBL: Issues in Education
description: This course is intended primarily for students in the Georgetown University Catholic Schools Project who wish to continue in their placement during the second semester, though other students who have worked actively in education can add in with the Instructor's permission. It assumes, however, a good knowledge of teaching strategies and a reasonable amount of practice in executing them, and considers such topics as the role of standardized tests, the place of whole language and of phonics, and the impact and meanings of inner-city Catholic primary education, among other topics. It requires, among other things, an extended research paper on an educational topic agreed to in consultation with the instructor, and researched in Washington libraries other than our own.

title: Sursum Corda
description: This course is designed for students wishing to tutor, on Tuesdays and Thursdays evenings, K-6 children at the Sursum Corda community. Founded in 1970 by Rev. Horace McKenna, S.J. and attached to the Sursum Corda Tenants Association, the Sursum Corda-Georgetown University Tutoring Program is the oldest such program in the country established between an American university and a specific urban community. Students learn in a weekly seminar what is involved in teaching a young child to read, and work, twice a week, in the Sursum Corda Community Center, teaching young learners the language arts, and learning themselves about the community of which they are a part. This course satisfies the College's Humanities & Writing II requirement.

title: Medieval Sexualities
description: This course aims to understand the variety of medieval sexualities from a comparative sampling of varied textual types and social institutions. We will study depictions of sexualities and gender issues in European literatures, classical and medieval biological and medical texts, religious thought and law, depictions in art, and the cultural variation present across regions and periods from c. 500-1500. Students might expect, for example, to learn more of Anglo-Saxon attitudes concerning masculinity from penitentials or Beowulf, to think about the implications of Irish laws for reading women in myth, to read courtly French literature through the lens of contemporary medical knowledge, to weigh the impact of clashing cultural attitudes on sexual mores, or to consider the connection between prosecution of heresies and accusations of deviance. Those interested in material culture may extend their work to include art, manuscripts, and archaeological evidence. You will be expected to build up your understanding of medieval contexts and to learn to research sources in medieval studies and in the fields of sexuality and gender studies, working on smaller papers and research during the term and culminating in a longer, final written project on a topic developed in consultation with the professor.

title: Worlds of Beowolf
description: The recent translation of Beowulf by Nobel laureate Seamus Heaney has reawakened interest inÂ the Anglo-Saxons, yet stereotypes and misinformation persist in popular culture. This seminarÂ will cover the intellectual and physical cultures of the Anglo-Saxons by sampling the texts,Â archaeology, artifacts, language, and art of the sixth to eleventh centuries in Britain to testÂ our assumptions and develop new ways of seeing this ancient society in an interdisciplinaryÂ way. You will learn to read the physical culture alongside textual culture, creating a dialogueÂ between them and using the different perspectives of each to test your perceptions of theÂ period and its peoples. Is the term "Anglo-Saxon" useful at all, given the hybridity of theÂ populations of the British Isles? How do we read early burials: do they reveal somethingÂ about Anglo-Saxon beliefs or more about those of later scholars? How do the secular andÂ spiritual worlds interact and influence each other? Do site excavations and architectural spaceÂ reveal attitudes towards power, privacy, gender, access? Where were the women in thisÂ heroic world, and can we find their voices? We look at such issues as cultural interaction andÂ opposition; the marginal vs. the normative; mythmaking and identity; gender constructionsÂ and representations; and the social, religious and political impacts of Christianity. We willÂ read selections from Old English poems, riddles, sermons, histories, saints' lives, and legalÂ documents against the physical cultures of sites, sculpture, artifacts, and manuscripts toÂ study the many approaches and contexts for the worlds of the most well-known period work,Â Beowulf. All texts will be read in translation, but there will also be some original language studyÂ so that students can avoid being misled by translations. (Language study will not be graded butÂ is required to pass. This is not a language course.) Varied writing assignments throughout theÂ term will develop a longer final paper (15-20 pp.) at the end of the course.

title: 17C Poetry and Prose
description: Prompted by theological debates over original sin, seventeenth-century English writers sought to discern the limits of the human and, in some cases, to transcend them. How much, they asked, can we know, desire, live, enjoy, and suffer? What are the outer boundaries of our understanding? To what extent can we master our fortune or manipulate nature? How far can human language be perfected? What role can we play in our own salvation? The obsession with limits produced transformative re-imaginings of science, politics, and religion, and it gave rise to both a poetry of humility and a poetry of extremity. We will seek to understand the periodâs contested understanding of human finitude by reading the works of Johnson, Donne, Herbert, Bacon, Hobbes, Marvell, and Cavendish, among others.

title: Milton II
description: "Milton II: Passionate Engagements" This is an upper-level course that will cover most of Miltonâs poetry and some of the prose. The title, âPassionate Engagements,â is meant to refer as much to Miltonâs engagement with his own poetry and poetic creations as it does to our own engagement with Milton. The course asks us, first and foremost, to consider what (if anything) is âpassionateâ about Miltonâs poetry: is it his poetic engagement with great literary interlocutors and texts? Or his depiction of the poemâs protagonists and/or their relationships with one another? Or is it a combination of these two and much more? In addressing these questions, we will consider what role the emotions play in Miltonâs poetry, and how they are integrated with, or divorced from, its theology. Through close readings of his poetry, especially Paradise Lost, we will investigate how the poetry treats the relationship between reason and passion, and how the different worlds within the poem reflect, and, also, respond to, this charged dynamic. If Milton is a great creator, or maker of poems, he is also deeply engaged with his creation; and it is here that the âengagementsââof previous scholars and literary critics with Milton--invite us to consider more carefully the ways in which Miltonâs poetry challenges us to read and engage with it. Last but not least, we will discuss how passionate engagementsâwith Miltonâs poetry, as well as those taking place within itâput pressure on notions of genre and the role of the imagination. It is strongly suggested that you have either taken a course on Milton or had some previous exposure to Paradise Lost. If you have questions, please email the instructor.

title: Dickens
description: A close reading of three major novels by Charles Dickens: Bleak House (1852 â 1853), A Tale of Two Cities (1859), and Great Expectations (1860 â 1861) in which we will consider the writer in the context of his era and the practice of fiction in the mid-nineteenth century. Discussion will dominate class time with students reporting on their analytical, inventive, and library research projects. There will not be any exams as such, but there will be short, factual quizzes on the dayâs reading which will begin each class session. The semester ends with a slightly longer essay which discusses a specific aspect of all three novels. Mutual Exclusion: This course carries a mutual exclusion with the following courses; you may not enroll in it if you have completed any of the following with a passing grade: ENGL 182

title: Wordsworth and Coleridge Pre-1801
description: This course will examine the work of the principal Romantic poets of the first generation. It will pay particular attention to writings of the 1790s, including Coleridgeâs millenarian epic, âReligious Musingsâ and Wordsworthâs âRecluseâ. The emphasis throughout this course will be on disciplined, close analysis of text, and group discussion in which everyone is expected to participate. The aim will be to enable students to reach the point at which they are able to read and analyse poetry for themselves with confidence. The elements of prosody are thus essential concomitants in this study, and for that reason a principal course text will be Philip Hobsbaumâs Metre, Rhythm and Verse Form (Routledge), which students will be expected to read and memorize. Students should be prepared to write regular short essays on aspects of individual poems. Each essay will contribute to their overall assessment, as will their attendance record and performance in class, which will be closely monitored. Students are expected also to attend one-to-one tutorials with Professor Wu in order to discuss their work. These various activities will together contribute to each studentâs final grade.

title: Staging Anti-Slavery
description: In this course, weâll examine late eighteenth and early nineteenth-century womenâs anti-slavery texts, in hopes of understanding how concepts of religion, race, and gender became entangled during the formative years of the American republic. What propels black and white women to enter the risky world of abolitionism, and how do they protect themselves as they enter fiercely contested public debates about race? What are the limits of their literary imaginings? What is the relationship between self-love and sympathy within women's abolitionist performances? Weâll track the changes in how abolitionists gained access to the public sphere or redefined the âprivateâ sphere, and search out archival materials as we analyze anti-slavery poems, essays, plays, speeches, short stories, letters, and autobiographical novels. In addition to a variety of theoretical articles, our readings may include Phillis Wheatleyâs poems, Susanna Rowsonâs plays, Maria W. Stewartâs speeches, and Ellen Craftâs narrative, along with a host of lesser-known anti-slavery texts. Students in American Studies, African American Studies, Performance Studies, Womenâs Studies, Literary History, and Justice & Peace Studies may be especially interested in the material of this course.

title: Modern Irish Novel
description: The realist novel flourished in nineteenth-century Britain and France, but the modern novel has a more geographically dispersed genealogy that is closely tied to the high point of European empires and the beginnings of their demise in the early decades of the twentieth century. In this course we will look at just one of the sites of the emergence what we now call (though not without controversy) modernism, concentrating not on its British and American canonical authors, but on the anomalous example of Irish modernist novels, produced in a colony, yet squarely centred in the European metropole. We will read some of the best of Irish modernist writing alongside critical writings on the politics of modernism, the history of the novel, as well as literature and colonialism. Authors to be covered will George Moore, Elizabeth Bowen, James Joyce (we will readÂ Ulysses), Flann O'Brien, and Samuel Beckett.

title: Migration, Detention, Asylum
description: This course explores migration, detention, and asylum in broad terms as formal as well as thematic problems that shape modernist and contemporary literature and critical theory. Focusing on British and Irish modernist texts, and Caribbean, African, and South Asian postcolonial works, we will consider how literary aesthetics intersect with the politics of movement and stalled movement. Some issues we will discuss: What is the relationship between movement or stalled movement and narrative time? What are the âmediaâ of movement and stalled movementâland, air, or sea? Stream of consciousness, secret epistles, or vernaculars associated with a particular class, ethnic, or racial group? How do works engage the aesthetic techniques of other media, such as photography, painting, sculpture, television, or radio, and might this engagement tell us something about both desires to move and resistances to and incapacities of moving? In addition to critical theories that explore these topics, we will read novels by Virginia Woolf, Jean Rhys, George Lamming, Samuel Beckett, Caryll Phillips, Anita Desai, J.M. Coetzee, Nadine Gordimer, and Abdulrazak Gurnah. Papers, presentations, Blackboard postings, and quizzes.

title: Reading Toni Morrison
description: In 1993, Toni Morrison became the eighth woman, the second American woman, first African American, first African American woman, and first Black woman to win the Nobel Prize in literature. In its citation, the Swedish Academy praised her as one âwho, in novels characterized by visionary force and poetic import, gives life to an essential aspect of American reality.â This courseâReading Toni Morrisonâseeks to identify and to examine that âessential aspect of American reality.â We will read the writings of Morrison, exploring the ways in which she represents history, identity, nation, race, gender, sexuality, and community in novels and essays that both challenge and complicate our understanding of these themes. We will read fiction and nonfiction by Morrison as well as essays of literary criticism that engage her works specifically and African American literature in general. We will examine her works as cultural and social historiography while also analyzing the formal/literary aspects of her novels. This course is reading intensive. Students can expect several short papers, quizzes, a midterm, and a research project.

title: Horror: Tech & Techniques
description: Many scholars and filmmakers have asked why people want their movies to scare them, which is an interesting but perhaps unanswerable philosophical question. This course accepts the enduring popularity of frightening films and asks instead how and why movies horrify us. In order to answer that question, we will study some of the most effective scary movies of the past eighty years, focusing on the various cultural and mechanical components that contribute to this strange conglomeration of emotions we call "horror." By the end of the course, we should have a better understanding of what different film cultures mean by âhorrorâ and how they have attempted to produce this state in viewers.Â To that end, this course interprets âtechnologyâ broadly as tools and concepts developed for practical purposesâin this case for the practice of scaring spectators. Thus the technology of horror includes everything from lighting and squibs to actors and sound design. It also includes social modes of perception that contribute to our spectatorship, such as Renaissance perspective and gender roles. This course wants to know how music and editing, shadows and CGI all contribute to fear, disgust, and mortification at the movies.

title: Class Fictions in Contemporary US
description: This interdisciplinary course examines U.S. literary and popular cultural representations of âlowâ classes/cultures (working class/poor/âunderclassâ) from the 1990s to the present. With the aid of recent theory and cultural criticism, we will focus on ways in which categories such as race, gender, and sexuality help to construct our contemporary notions of what âclassâ means in America. We will be studying a wide variety of texts, forms, and genresâfiction, autobiography, film, musicâand taking up a broad spectrum of issues, including theories of working class identity, social/cultural resistance, and the notion of ârepresentationâ itself. Course work will include 1 short paper (5-7 pp.); 1 class presentation; 1 midterm exam, and one final paper (12 pp.).

title: Poetry & Poetics
description: We will be reading a wide range of poetry, from oral poetry and ballads to many of the canonical figures to contemporaries of varying stripes. In addition, we'll read a number of the major statements in poetics, from Plato to Shelley to the contemporary avant-garde. This field, obviously huge, will be focused by continual reference to Modernist through contemporary writing, where histories of primary and long lasting questions continue to surface. Does poetry derive from divine inspiration or personal imagination or collective deliberation? What is the relationship between poetic language and the ârealâ? Is poetry civilized or elemental?
