Was Plato right in kicking the poets out of his Republic? What sort of work (imaginative, emotional, social, political) can poetry do?

title: Poetics of Diaspora
description: This course will primarily examine poetry out of the anglophone Caribbean (including poetry being written in England and Canada), together with pertinent essays and literary criticism. Situating this work the context of colonization, plantation slavery, decolonization, and its aftermath, we will pursue these general questions: How do Caribbean poets view their own relation to this history, and how do they go about creating a poetry distinctively inflected as Caribbean--as other than English? What is, or ought to be, the relationship between poetic practice and the cultural environment? We will give special attention to matters such as the use of standard English in collaboration with the Creole vernacular; the handling (or mis-handling) of British literary tradition; Africa in the New World imaginary;Â articulations of the black female subject within, and against, the history of male mastery;problems of culture and hybridity; and the place of poetry in the project of national formation. We will read texts by Kamau Brathwaite, M. Nourbese Philip, Derek Walcott, Mervyn Morris, Edouard Glissant, Louise Bennett, and many others. (Since the bulk of our time will be devoted to poetry, it would be helpful if students already had some familiarity with the techniques of close reading. Such familiarity is not, however, a pre-requisite for the course.)

title: Gender & Authority in 20th Century Poetry
description: In this class we will examine the nature of authority in the field of literary production and the ways in which gender shapes the possibilities for poetic voice, form, and affect. Grounded in theories of gender and poetics, we'll examine the writing lives of selected 20th century women poets who played significant roles in shaping the literary fields in which they worked. Specifically, we'll be studying poets who doubled as editors, critics, publishers, as well as canon- and scene-makers. We will read closely the careers of two major figures--Gwendolyn Brooks, and Adrienne Rich âas well as networks of cultural workers. We will have particularly rich connections to explore during the two periods in which women wielded the most power to create the conditions for their own writing and its reception: Modernism (1910-30), and the re-emergence of women's poetic communities as second wave feminism made its mark in the late 60s and 70s. This class will explore poetry written by men as well. Our broadest questions will have to do with the role gender plays in the creation, dissemination, and reception of poetry, and the role poetry plays in the creation, disruption, and reconstruction of gender ideologies.

title: Gender & Authority in 20th Century Poetry
description: In this class we will examine the nature of authority in the field of literary production and the ways in which gender shapes the possibilities for poetic voice, form, and affect. Grounded in theories of gender and poetics, we'll examine the writing lives of selected 20th century women poets who played significant roles in shaping the literary fields in which they worked. Specifically, we'll be studying poets who doubled as editors, critics, publishers, as well as canon- and scene-makers. We will read closely the careers of two major figures--Gwendolyn Brooks, and Adrienne Rich âas well as networks of cultural workers. We will have particularly rich connections to explore during the two periods in which women wielded the most power to create the conditions for their own writing and its reception: Modernism (1910-30), and the re-emergence of women's poetic communities as second wave feminism made its mark in the late 60s and 70s. This class will explore poetry written by men as well. Our broadest questions will have to do with the role gender plays in the creation, dissemination, and reception of poetry, and the role poetry plays in the creation, disruption, and reconstruction of gender ideologies.

title: Lannan Poetry Seminar
description: The Lannan Poetry Seminar encourages dynamic critical thinking and creativity with a view to examining literary forms and practices; the work of individual authors; the relationship between writing and other arts; and the place of writing in contemporary culture. Fellows study and discuss the work of the yearâs scheduled guests in the Lannan âReadings and Talksâ series. They will keep diaries of the readings they attend, which will include five outside those provided by the Lannan center, and produce an independent appreciation of a poem that has made an impression on them.

title: Advanced Poetry Workshop
description: A workshop for students who have written some poems already, have taken a workshop course (in any genre), and who want to produce a short manuscript of poems.Â Â In a workshop format, we will study a wide variety of published poems, pictures, and some songs--pushing toward topics, forms, and voices you may not have faced before.Â We'll write and discuss our own poems, paying close attention to matters of form, image, figure, rhythm, voice, the âplot.âÂ Â Other fun bits: a short critical paper on a recent book of poems; memorization of single poems; revision;Â making an anthology of poems by others; a class reading; visit by poets; more revision of poems.Â Our goal: you'll finish a short book of new poems.Â Â We assume you are interested in presenting your new poems for (helpful) discussion and revision, that you will write analytically on published and student poems, and that you will edit your drafts.

title: Advanced Fiction Writing
description: This course will build on a basic familiarity with fiction writing in order to deepen, develop, and complicate that understanding. Class sessions will consist of intensive workshopping, analysis of contemporary fiction, and discussion of complex issues of craft and fictional purpose. Admission by permission of the instructor ONLY. Interested students should e-mail sample writing (5-10 pages) and personal statement about what they hope to get out of the class, by April 2nd, to dhe8@georgetown.edu.

title: Advanced Fiction Writing
description: This course will build on a basic familiarity with fiction writing in order to deepen, develop, and complicate that understanding. Class sessions will consist of intensive workshopping, analysis of contemporary fiction, and discussion of complex issues of craft and fictional purpose. Admission by permission of the instructor ONLY. Interested students should e-mail sample writing (5-10 pages) and personal statement about what they hope to get out of the class, by April 2nd, to dhe8@georgetown.edu.

title: Intermediate Scriptwriting
description: Students who demonstrate aptitude in Script Writing may apply for permission to take Intermediate Scriptwriting. Working in small groups, accepted students will write an original short script which they will then shoot during the second half of the term. Because time within the semester is limited, students are encouraged to discuss script ideas with the instructor before the beginning of the term. All interested students should contact Prof. Glavin (glavinj@georgetown.edu) for further information about the course.

title: Intermediate Scriptwriting
description: Students who demonstrate aptitude in Script Writing may apply for permission to take Intermediate Scriptwriting. Working in small groups, accepted students will write an original short script which they will then shoot during the second half of the term. Because time within the semester is limited, students are encouraged to discuss script ideas with the instructor before the beginning of the term. All interested students should contact Prof. Glavin (glavinj@georgetown.edu) for further information about the course.

title: Tutorial:  Advanced Scriptwriting
description: An individual tutorial offered to students who have successfully completed Script Writing. The student will complete an original, full-length feature script in a genre and with a story line of the student's choice. All interested students should contact Prof. Glavin (glavinj@georgetown.edu) for further information about the course.

title: The Writer's Perspective
description: The Writer's Perspective: The Significance of Detail in Literature and Art. The students will write and workshop short creative pieces (both fiction and nonfiction) while also reading and responding to essays, poems, stories, and photographs.

title: Hybrid Forms
description: This writing focused seminar will examine the literary techniques and approaches used by novelists, journalists, and essayists in creating narratives that challenge the distinction between fiction and nonfiction, novel and autobiography, journalism, essays, and short stories. We will study the âliteraryâ techniques used by contemporary essayists and journalists, such as John DâAgata, Katherine Boo, and Mark Danner along with hybrid texts from writers such as Philip Roth, Junot Diaz, Sherman Alexie and Hans Magnus Enzensberger.

title: Capstone: Medieval Literature & Culture
description: "The Poetics of Emotion in Medieval Literature and Culture: A Global Perspective" In this capstone seminar for senior English majors, we will move beyond the borders of England and English literature in order to investigate a broad question: How does literature shape and express emotion in diverse cultural contexts? We will put this question into global as well as historical perspective by investigating how one of the most complex and fundamental of human emotions -- romantic love -- is constructed in the courtly literatures of medieval Europe (12th-13th centuries), Persia during the later Abbasid Caliphate (ca. 970-1258), and Heian Japan (794-1185). Starting with a set of readings designed to open up theoretical issues concerning diversity in emotion across cultures and how literature, as a primary form of cultural poetics, participates in forging such diversity, we will then turn to an in-depth consideration of selected literary texts and their contexts, devoting four weeks to each cultural setting. Each section will give rise to its own set of issues, but abiding preoccupations will include the relationship between âartificialâ conventions and ârealâ emotion (is this a valid distinction?); the powerful role of religion (here, Christianity, Islam, and Buddhism) in shaping forms of desire; the relation between court politics and intimate experience; the cultural construction of the desiring and desirable body; the voice given to women as writers, court patrons, or courtly audience; how differing ideals of masculinity find expression; love in relation to arranged marriages and polygamy; and the ethical potential of romantic love as a ânobleâ emotion. All readings will be in modern English translation. For Europe, they will include lyrics of the troubadours and the women trobaritz, ChrÃ©tien de Troyesâ Lancelot, the Lais of Marie de France, and Gottfried von Strassburg's Tristan; for Persia, ghazals by Hafiz and others, the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam, and Niz?m? Ganjav?'s Layla and Majnun and Khusrau and Shirin; for Japan, Murasaki Shikibuâs Tale of Genji, the love poems of Ono no Komachi and Izumi Shikibu, selected waka by other courtiers, and excerpts from The Pillowbook of Sei Sh?nagon and similar poetic diaries by women of the Heian court. Throughout, we will embed these literary works in a wider cultural matrix by reading extracts from additional primary sources such as chronicles and conduct books as well as brief selections from secondary sources. Student work will culminate in an extensive independent research project; guest speakers and at least one off-campus excursion (a viewing of the Persian miniatures at the Sackler museum) will also enrich this course. This course is a Doyle Seminar, part of the Doyle Engaging Difference Program, a new campus-wide curricular initiative, and gives faculty the opportunity to enhance the student research component of upper-level seminars that address questions of national, social, cultural, religious, moral, and other forms of difference. The Doyle seminars are intended to deepen student learning about diversity and difference through enhanced research opportunities, interaction with thought leaders, and dialogue with the Georgetown community and beyond.

title: Capstone: Early Modern Literature & Culture
description: How did Shakespeare become a cultural icon known across the globe? How do writersÂ and artists today grapple with the long shadow cast by this literary precursor? ThisÂ course pairs Shakespeareâs plays with modern adaptations to explore changing notionsÂ of gender, class, race, empire, power, and history. Critical and theoretical readingsÂ willÂ help frame our analysis. To further understand the craft and genre of theatre, studentsÂ will have the opportunity to âwrite backâ to Shakespeare by devising and staging theirÂ own creative work. Using canonical texts such as Hamlet, Othello, and The TempestÂ as a springboard, we will then examine contemporary adaptations such as SuleymanÂ al-Hassanâs The Al-Hamlet Summit, Tom Stoppardâs Rosencrantz and GuildensternÂ are Dead, Ann-Marie MacDonaldâs Goodnight Desdemona (Good MorningÂ Juliet), AimÃ© CÃ©saireâs A Tempest, and Timothy Findleyâs Elizabeth Rex. We may alsoÂ consider adaptations in other media such as film and opera. Students will work toward aÂ substantial final project on a topic of their choice.

title: Capstone: American Culture
description: "Mass Media/Mass Culture" This advanced interdisciplinary course explores novels, essays, and films that take both âthe mediaâ and âthe massesâ as points of imaginative departure. Focusing on the United States from the 1920s to the present (but with some important trips to Germany, Canada, France, Brazil, and the post-apocalyptic future along the way), we will consider a wide variety of texts that address the mediaâs power to inform and delude, persuade and seduce, galvanize and atomize, terrify and entertain. Films by Orson Welles, Charlie Chaplin, Billy Wilder, Alfred Hitchcock, Francis Ford Coppola, Sidney Lumet, and Pixar; novels by Nathanael West, Sloan Wilson, and Don DeLillo; plus selected critical and historical readings. Two basic questions will guide our approach throughout the semester. What constitutes âinformationâ in an age of proliferating cultural data? How do understandings of âcommunityâ change in response to increased technological connectivity? In addition to an extended research paper, this course requires eight mandatory film screenings outside of class. Students who are concentrators in Georgetownâs program in Film and Media Studies may be admitted by permission of the instructor.

title: Capstone:  Film Studies
description: New Hollywood From the late 1960s through the early 1980s, American cinema underwent a significant sea change. From the fall of the Production Code to the rise of the youth market, the end of the family prestige picture to the beginning of high concept filmmaking, dramatic cultural shifts placed traditional subjects, audiences, genres, and conventions up for grabs. This eraâknown as âNew Hollywood,â âthe American New Wave,â and âthe Hollywood Renaissanceââwas one of the most artistically innovative and productive periods in US film history, yet in ended in a resurgence of corporate cross-marketing, franchise filmmaking, and studio control. Why? In this course, we will examine the social, industrial, and economic trends that led to the creative flowering of 1970s US film culture, analyze what made the films of this period unique, then investigate how Hollywood changed as a result of its renaissance. Together weâll ask what was extraordinary about New Hollywood and how it continues to influence contemporary genres, including the âindieâ film and the blockbuster. In addition to reading articles by Justin Wyatt, Jon Lewis, Kristin Thompson, and Steven Jay Schneider, we will study movies from the Film School Brats (including Francis Ford Coppola and Martin Scorcese), the Black Independent Movement (including Charles Burnett, Julie Dash, and Melvin Van Peebles), the New American Cinema (including John Cassavetes, John Waters, and Kenneth Anger), and the exploitation underground (including Richard Sarafian and Wes Craven). From Bonnie and Clyde to Manhattan, Chinatown to Halloween, the movies of the New Hollywood era demonstrate a dramatic rethinking of what American cinema can say and show. This class will discover how their conclusions changed the future of film.

title: Capstone:  Critical Theory
description: We will familiarize ourselves with the specialized languages of criticism and theory as applied to literary and cultural texts today. We will read both canonical and newer works in the field to understand both their historical trajectory and contemporary application. While gesturing towards broad coverage of 20th century theory and its various schools and movements (structuralism, poststructuralism, psychoanalysis, Marxism, feminism) we will focus on a symptomatic approach to issues of contemporary relevance by examining some of the great themes of 20th century criticism: difficulty, fragmentation, trauma, science, technology, dialectics, justice, revolution. Special attention will be given to the artist in movements for social change and the theorization of âsocial practice.â Our approach overall will be to highlight strong readings by powerful critics engaged in interpretive struggle with texts, and to understand how that process is transformative. We will attend to cultural events on campus such as those hosted by the Lannan Center. Readings will include Marx, Freud, Zizek, Butler, Jameson, Mulvey, Derrida and others.

title: Capstone:  Poems & Poetics
description: In this course we will closely study the most significant Modern and contemporary poems, as well as the prose arguments by poets that (seemingly) advocate for such poems. Changes in aesthetic theory have been triggered by groundbreaking poems, and also by the essays and letters of poets. But groundbreaking poems may also undermine the aesthetic theory proposed by their makers, and by others. So we'll be testing theory against practice, that is, against our own, developed readings of individual poems. We will begin by taking a brief tour of 'pre-Modern' arts movements such Romanticism, then look closely at the major Modern poets, and at some of the outwardly spiraling clusters of significant poets who have been producing notable work since that period. The course assumes that you have a workable literary background; it does not assume that you are very experienced in reading poems; and it may even recognize that you find poems difficult, murky, or bewildering. We will read from Ramazani & Ellmann's Norton Anthology of Modern & Contemporary Poetry, and several single volumes. Your work will include: many explication of texts; short essays; in-class report; some poems composed by you (ungraded); a research essay, and more.

title: Capstone:  Honors Thesis
description: This course is for students accepted into the Honors Program. Students will be expected to read and comment on critical and theoretical texts related to the honors projects, to read and comment on each other's work, and to present their own work for critique. Attendance and engagement as well as periodic written responses are required. Student should expect to complete about 25 pages of the honors thesis by the end of the course. Permission from instructor required.

title: Medieval Sexualities
description: Is the concept of sexual identity transhistorical? To what extent do social, religious, and political institutions form and deform erotic choices and definitions? This course aims to understand the variety of medieval sexualities from a comparative sampling of varied textual types and social institutions. We will study depictions of sexualities and gender issues in European literatures, classical and medieval biological and medical texts, religious thought and law, depictions in art, and the cultural variation present across regions and periods from c. 500-1500. Students might expect, for example, to learn more of Anglo-Saxon attitudes concerning masculinity from penitentials or Beowulf, to think about the implications of Irish laws for reading women in myth, to read courtly French literature through the lens of contemporary medical knowledge, to weigh the impact of clashing cultural attitudes on sexual mores, or to consider the connection between prosecution of heresies and accusations of deviance. Those interested in material culture may extend their work to include art, manuscripts, and archaeological evidence. You will be expected to build up your understanding of medieval contexts and to learn to research sources in medieval studies and in the fields of sexuality and gender studies. You will compile a weekly critical file of articles, write a short (7-8 pp.) midterm essay that proposes and initiates your research on a topic developed in consultation with the professor, and submit a longer written project (20 pp.) developed from that midterm effort as your culminating project for the course.

title: Transformational Tudor Theater
description: Habermas describes the public sphere as a space of public discourse outside official culture. He locates it in the newsletters and coffee houses of eighteenth-century Europe, but historians have since attempted to make claims for the earlier development of a public sphere in seventeenth-century England, especially in connection with the Civil War. This seminar will test the idea of taking the concept yet another step back, to the commercial theater of Tudor and Stuart London as a site of public culture. Building also on such classic work as Jonathan Dollimoreâs argument about âradical tragedy,â we will search out plays that engaged actively with contemporary political, social, and economic issues and events. These may include the collaborative Gorboduc (1561), the anonymous Arden of Feversham (1592), Shakespeareâs Richard II (1595), Thomas Heywoodâs If You Know Not Me You Know Nobody (1605), the collaborative Witch of Edmonton (1621), Thomas Middletonâs Game at Chess (1624), and others. We will discuss ways in which these works address political thought, social theory, and international relationsâas well as the citations and censorship that give evidence of their impact on public discourse.

title: Milton & Reader Response Criticism
description: If the New Criticism found the ideal objects for its analysis in the well-wrought urns of John Donne, and the early debates of New Historicism played out on the Shakespearean stage, then Reader Response Criticism developed largely in response to John Milton. The aims of this course are twofold. First, we will examine the comparatively brief life of Reader Response Criticism. What prompted this critical and theoretical undertaking? To what kinds of reading practices did it give rise? How do we account for its swift demise? How did it contribute to later critical methods like Reception Studies or Book History? Second, we will study the poetry and prose of John Milton. Why has Miltonâs poetry proven particularly susceptible to this critical method? What about his works makes the focus on the experience of a reader and/or readers so necessary? How does Milton understand, structure, and manipulate the responses of his readers? We will use Miltonâs writings as opportunities to practice and critique the kinds of criticism pioneered by critics like Rosenblatt, Fish, Iser, and Jauss, as well their psychological, idealist, and classical predecessors.

title: Early American Women Writers
description: This course investigates how women writers and performers of various kindsâballad singers, parlor poets, lecturers, pseudonymous autobiographers, novelists, playwrights and othersâshaped notions of womanhood, race, and American-ness in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. In disparate ways, these writers helped shape and pass judgments on the newly emerging American culture. Students will have an opportunity to study the varied and complicated strategies that these women utilized to introduce their ideas onto the public stage. To protect their status as respectable daughters, wives, or mothers, many women voiced their concerns through texts rife with hidden meanings. Others published their work anonymously, or under a male or female pseudonym, so that they could articulate their critiques more safely.Â Â Still others cloaked their judgments by populating the stage with dramatic characters who could debate the issues of the day with impunity. We will analyze the implications of the womenâs various avenues toward publication as well as their actual songs, poems, lectures, plays, and autobiographical novels. In the process, we will begin to consider gender, race, and nationality as complex, performative negotiations. Our readings will range widely, perhaps including 17thÂ century poet Anne Bradstreet; 18thÂ century authors Lucy Terry Prince, Phillis Wheatley, Judith Sargent Murray, and Susanna Haswell Rowson; 19thÂ century writers Lydia Maria Child, Emily Dickinson, Harriet Wilson, Sara Payson Willis Parton, Maria Amparo Ruiz de Burton, Sarah Winnemucca Hopkins, and sisters Sui Sin Far and Onoto Watanna.

title: 19C Comedy
description: A seminar discussing comedy as a genre, the comic or what causes people to laugh, and hence joking and laughter. We will read some theoretical work both by classic writers like Freud, Bakhtin, and Frye, and also many recent discussions of comedy theory by a wide range of contemporary theorists and critics. Nevertheless the central focus of the seminar will be on primary texts including: Austenâs Pride and Prejudice, a hefty portion of Byronâs Don Juan, Thackerayâs Vanity Fair, dramatic monologues by Robert Browning, the Alice books of Lewis Carroll, examples of mid-nineteenth century cartooning from Punch and other illustrated magazines, Gilbert and Sullivanâs operetta Patience, and Oscar Wildeâs The Importance of Being Earnest. Students will take responsibility for leading seminar discussion and will report on recent scholarship and criticism dealing with the course topic and its texts. Comedy theory emerges from curious absences. A. Aristotleâs book on the topic never existed or has been lost. Nevertheless his theory has been reconstructed from his other works and even in classical times dominated the formulation of ideas about comedy. Using binary opposition we get these pairs: as Tragedy deals with gods, heroes, and tragic errors in choice ending in death, so Comedy deals with ordinary people, every day occurrences, mistakes which can be corrected, and ends in marriage and fertility. Tragedy purges us through terror and pity; comedy purges us through laughter at the foolishness of others. They humanize the audience but in opposite ways. Aristotle valued aesthetic unity and propriety. Epic and drama imitate actions; each successful work has a beginning, a middle, and an ending. All the elements they contain are suitable for the kind of genre used and for the action being imitated. They satisfy the human pleasure in observing, analyzing, and understanding and make us better as we learn from them. There is a clearly defined tradition of Aristotelian comedies from Menanderâs ânew comedies,â through Plautus, to medieval rhetoricians who repeated Aristotleâs ideas, to Renaissance comedies which began to appear all over Europe in the sixteenth century. Shakespeare is the most notable example of this Renaissance flourishing. The tradition continues to the present day. The seminar begins with a novel, Jane Austenâs Pride and Prejudice, shaped by the Aristotelian tradition. B. The second absence is even odder than the first. All of the writings of Menippius, a Greek writer of the third century BCE, have been lost save for fragments, but upon this basis a tradition emerged for a very different kind of comic, if not comedic, genre, satire. His mockery of hypocrisy and folly in texts which mixed poetry and prose led to a Menippean tradition of satirical writing in the works of the Roman poets Juvenal and Lucan, prose narrative writers Apulius and Petronius, and then on down into a strangely rich alternative tradition which includes Rabelais, Swift [especially The Tale of a Tub and Gulliverâs Travels], Sterneâs Tristram Shandy, Carlyleâs Sartor Resartus, and Carrollâs Alice books. Many twentieth century writers such as Joyce carried on this tradition. Menippean texts refuse the elegant propriety and order of Aristotelian. They often have rambling narratives which appear to lack any internal logic; things just happen. They are full of the odd, the physical â especially the taboo, and the fearful. They muddle social class and propriety. They mock the rich, the powerful, the sacred, triumphing in foolishness and excess. If comedy prizes the witty riposte in the brilliant interchanges of Congreve and Shaw, carnivalesque fooling likes a good fart. In the twentieth century the archetypal theorist Frye and the Russian formalist Bakhtin were equally fascinated by the otherness of this alternative approach to what is comic. The seminar continues with a substantial portion of Byronâs Don Juan in which many of the traits of Menippean tradition emerge in a rambling narrative of adventures that are sometimes erotic, sometimes heroic, sometimes shameful, but usually funny, and this narrative is so dominated by its narrator that as the poem develops the reader begins to realize the poem is about its narrator much more than itâs about its handsome Spanish pseudo-hero. As the seminar continues it explores how nineteenth century writers then experiment with these two comedic traditions. Vanity Fair is an elegantly shaped historical novel which carries its large and heterogeneous cast of characters from the start of the nineteenth century through the wars with Napoleon and on into the early Victorian era. Here as with Byron the narrator is a principle character and source of complex ironies â which is to say that in many ways it experiments with a mingling of the Aristotelian and the Menippean; further the novel is illustrated by a large number of drawings by Thackeray which play an important part in its meaning. Here the seminar begins a recurrent interest in visual comedy. The discussion of Thackerayâs novel will be interrupted every other week by explorations of related texts. The dramatic monologues of Robert Browning: their voices, the ironies emerging from the efforts of their speakers to justify themselves and fault others. Mid-Victorian periodicals: their cartoons, comic verse, and humorous prose sketches. The Alice books of Lewis Carroll â Menippean comedy at its best. At the semesterâs end, then, two works for stage performance. First, an operetta by the most successful British musical comedy team of the century Gilbert and Sullivan, Patience. And, for the final session, arguably the most perfect comedy in in the language, Wildeâs The Importance of Being Earnest.

title: British Gothic Novel
description: The Gothic Novel and Its Aftermath The late eighteenth-century Gothic novel in England was both wildly popular and potentially shocking to contemporary sensibilities, dealing as it did with topics such as abduction, rape, supernatural forces, and secret sacrilegious rituals. But alongside the sensationalistic aspects of the Gothic novel is also frequently a sustained, though distorted, examination of the social and cultural anxieties of the age that produced it, anxieties frequently of sexual, religious, or national transgression. This course will move from the traditional Gothic novel to some of its later manifestation in the nineteenth century, analyzing the ways in which shifting moral, scientific, and social contexts shaped the dystopic world of the Gothic. At the same time, it will explore the relationship of certain formal conventions of the Gothic novel (including structure and narrative technique) to this broader cultural work. Texts will include The Castle of Otranto, The Mysteries of Udolpho, The Monk, Zofloya, Melmoth the Wanderer, Carmilla, and Dracula as well as a series of horror and suspense films and theoretical accounts of the Gothic.

title: Conversations in African American Literature and Criticism
description: In 2011, Kenneth Warren published a provocative and controversial work, What Was African American Literature? (Harvard UP), in which Warren argues that the end of Jim Crow effectually ended âAfrican Americanâ literature. This supposition leads him to conclude (perhaps too prematurely) that the end of segregation marked African American literatureâs end; the literature does not have anything else to which it might respond and therefore the classification of âAfrican Americanâ literature has to cease. This brief synopsis of Warrenâs argument clearly is fraught with complications, and our examination of African American literature and literary criticism this semester will provide contexts through which we might apprehend Warrenâs thesis and the arguments of contemporary African Americanists who call it into question. Our examination of African American literature will focus on the paradigmatic theories that have shaped the contours of African American literary criticism, as well as the recent trends in criticism that problematize those claims and carve innovative ways to investigate the rich body of primary texts. We will move chronologically throughout the 20th century, placing in conversation primary texts, âthenâ criticism, and ânowâ criticism in order to develop a breadth and depth of knowledge in this particular field. Finally, we will also devote time to developing and discussing pedagogical strategies for teaching African American literary and cultural studies, as the graduate program develops teachers in addition to training scholars. Students enrolling in this course should expect to give a conference style/length presentation, write a 15-20 page research paper, produce a syllabus for a âtopics in African American literatureâ course, and be active and engaged citizens during weekly seminars. The conference/length paper may be the basis from which the final research paper emerges.

title: Poetics of Diaspora
description: This course will concentrate on Anglophone poetry of the Caribbean, with special attention to the work of Derek Walcott & Kamau Brathwaite, and including poetry produced in North America & England by poets of Caribbean origin. We will be concerned broadly with the meaning for this poetry of British colonization, the Atlantic slave trade and the plantation system; and more specifically with various problems pertaining to language, culture, tradition, race & gender as articulated by the poets themselves and in recent literary criticism and cultural theory. Readings will include texts in translation, notably from the work of AimÃ© CÃ©saire, Frtantz Fanon, and Edouard Glissant, to provide a context and a point of reference for our study the diasporic imaginary after 1960.

title: Class Fictions in Contemporary US
description: This interdisciplinary course examines U.S. literary and popular cultural representations of âlowâ classes/cultures (working class/poor/âunderclassâ) from the 1990s to the present. With the aid of recent theory and cultural criticism, we will focus on ways in which categories such as race, gender, and sexuality help to construct our contemporary notions of what âclassâ means in America. We will be studying a wide variety of texts, forms, and genresâfiction, poetry, autobiography, film, musicâand taking up a broad spectrum of issues, including theories of working class identity, social/cultural resistance, and the notion of ârepresentationâ itself. Course work will include 1 short paper (5-7 pp.); 1 class presentation; 1 seminar paper (15-20 pp.). Some likely texts include: Fiction: Allisonâs Bastard Out of Carolina, Viramontesâ Under the Feet of Jesus; Meyerâs American Rust; Autobiography/Memoir: hooksâ Bone Black and Marâs Paper Daughter; Film: Hustle and Flow, SherryBaby, Frozen River, Real Women Have Curves, Precious (a selection from this possible mix!); Music: recent country and hip hop.

title: Literature and Commodity Culture
description: This course will examine the meaning and representation of âthingsâ in a diverse group of U. S. cultural texts from the late-nineteenth and into the early-twentieth century, a period widely identified as critical in the consolidation of mass consumerism and an era of U. S. external imperialism and economic expansion. We will also read selected works from the mid-nineteenth century, when an urban consumer culture was first being established and represented. Surveying a variety of cultural forms--including literary and film texts, advertising manuals, newspaper and magazine advertisements, and promotional and documentary materials about the display of âthingsâ in the 1893 Chicago Worldâs Fair--as well as a range of critical and theoretical essays on the subject, we will focus on the interrelations of gender, sexuality, race, ethnicity, class and nation in U. S. commodity culture. In particular, we will examine the diverse desires and identifications articulated through commodities in U. S. cultural texts, and the complex ways that individuals use commodities to construct (or deconstruct) individual and communal identities. Issues addressed will include: the "feminization" of consumption, and the complex relationship between feminism and consumerism, during this period; the ideological import of representations of acculturation or "Americanization" through consumption that were especially widespread in immigrant fiction during this era; the international, imperialist significance of tropes of American "overcivilization," "abundance" and "conspicuous consumption" in advertisements and fictional narratives; interrelations between depictions of commodity culture and the politics of "passing" in African American fiction; the saturation of commodity culture (especially advertising) with racial signifiers and imagery, and the racial fantasies activated in and by mass consumption; and the historically and theoretically complex relationship between cinema and commodity spectacle. Selected literary authors will likely include: Willa Cather, Kate Chopin, Stephen Crane, Maria Cummins, Theodore Dreiser, Pauline Hopkins, Henry James, Nella Larsen, Frank Norris, Edith Wharton, Anzia Yezierska, We will also watch at least one film: John Stahlâs Imitation of Life (1934).

title: Mass Media & the American Mind
description: âMedia,â in the words of Friedrich Kittler, âdetermine our situation.â During the twentieth century, cinema, radio, and televisionâas well as that abstract network of information-shaping institutions known simply as âthe mediaââcolonized how we came to understand the world around us. Today, in the twenty-first century, the digital revolution is fundamentally altering how we (or, at least, some of us) produce and consume cultural information. This interdisciplinary course uses novels, essays, films, and even a few court cases to examine the centrality of mass media to modern life. In the process, it also serves as an introduction to major ideas, debates, and themes in contemporary Literature and Media Studies. Focusing on the United States from the 1920s to the present (but with some important trips to Germany, Canada, France, Brazil, and the post-apocalyptic future along the way), we will consider a wide variety of texts that address the mediaâs power to inform and delude, persuade and seduce, galvanize and atomize, terrify and entertain. Novels by Nathanael West, Sloan Wilson, Don DeLillo, and Erik Loyer; films by Orson Welles, Charlie Chaplin, Billy Wilder, Alfred Hitchcock, Francis Ford Coppola, Sidney Lumet, and Pixar; theory and criticism by Walter Benjamin, Theodor Adorno, Harold Innis, C. Wright Mills, Marshall McLuhan, Benedict Anderson, Jean Baudrillard, Noam Chomsky, and N. Katherine Haylesâamong many others. Please note that this course requires eight mandatory film screenings that meet outside of class.

title: Orientalism and the Human
description: Examining the ways that Edward Saidâs Orientalism endures as a seminal text that has fundamentally transformed the study of culture and the politics of knowledge throughout the humanities, this course analyzes the challenges the text poses in the conjunctures of the present. By asking what kinds of critical demands Saidâs Orientalism continues to place on us, we shall explore the methodological implications that it and later reconsiderations of it raise for the present and future possibilities of new humanisms. Apprehending Orientalism as much more than a critique of the politics of cultural representation, as so many critics of the book have assumed, we shall examine the changing structures and persistence of Orientalism to produce bare life and terrains for its containment in the context of the vast changes that have taken place since the bookâs publication in 1978. In conjunction with Saidâs other major works, readings include texts by Adorno, Agamben, Appadurai, Apter, Auerbach, Casanova, Conrad, Gramsci, Fanon, Foucault, Mufti, Naipaul, Robbins, Spivak, and Williams.

title: Introduction to Critical Theory
description: We will familiarize ourselves with the specialized languages of criticism and theory as applied to literary and cultural texts today. We will read both canonical and newer works in the field to understand both their historical trajectory and contemporary application. While gesturing towards broad coverage of 20th century theory and its various schools and movements (structuralism, poststructuralism, psychoanalysis, Marxism, feminism) we will focus on a symptomatic approach to issues of contemporary relevance by examining some of the great themes of 20th century criticism: difficulty, fragmentation, trauma, science, technology, dialectics, justice, revolution. Special attention will be given to the artist in movements for social change and the theorization of âsocial practice.â Our approach overall will be to highlight strong readings by powerful critics engaged in interpretive struggle with texts, and to understand how that process is transformative. We will attend to cultural events on campus such as those hosted by the Lannan Center. Readings will include Marx, Freud, Zizek, Butler, Jameson, Mulvey, Derrida and others.

title: World Literature & Human Rights
description: The Global Good (Life)?: World Literature and Human Rights This graduate course will serve as both an introduction to world literature and to postcolonial theories of globalization, ethics, and human rights. In 1948, the UN Declaration of Human Rights introduced the term into the public sphere, and with it came questions of aid, intervention, democracy, and sovereignty that continue to define debates about global politics. In this course, we will ask what constitutes a global idea of âdoing goodâ? What defines the âgood lifeâ? Is that different across cultures and/or time periods? What place does literature have in thinking about these questions? How can literature engage questions of rights in relation to and different from legal, economic, scientific, religious, and/or political frameworks of individual and social rights? How might literary aesthetics imagine new and potentially âbetterâ worlds, or reimagine the histories of rights as we now come to know and expect them? Primary texts may include: Black Girl, Graceland, Of One Blood, The Whale Caller, Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl, Zong!, She Tries Her Tongue Her Silence Softly Breaks, Anowa, Fire, American Sublime, My Beautiful Laundrette, The Youngest Doll, The Calcutta Chromosome, Persepolis, The Book of Salt, Chicas 2000, Wonderful Adventures of Mrs. Seacole in Many Lands, So Long A Letter, and Disgrace.

title: Approaches to Teaching Writing
description: This course offers a practical introduction to the history, theories, and issues that shape the field of Writing Studies. The systematic study of the production of texts â rhetoric and composition --began as a âschoolâ discipline, and these origins are preserved in the title of our course. Written assignments and class discussion will focus on teaching and course design, with particular attention to the integration of writing and reading. Weekly readings will assist you to theorize your own practice in the assignments you compose and the courses you design. But the teachable parts of writing need not be reduced to techniques, whether methods of close reading or mechanisms of process, skill, and craft. In the words of media theorist Walter Ong, âWriting is a technology that restructures thought.â We will explore issues of cultural and critical literacy as these concepts inform the teaching of writing. By definition, the graduate seminar offers the opportunity to enter an ongoing conversation among scholars in a particular field as well as a forum for intellectual exchange among peers. Students will be expected to take an active role in the course as evidenced by close, critical attention to the readings; contributions to discussion; organized seminar presentations of their research; and engaged, informed writing.

title: Approaches to Teaching Literature
description: This course is intended to be a graduate introduction to the practice of teaching literature: its texts, its history, and its methods and theories.Â Â We will examine practical and theoretical issues related to teaching, including exploring the pedagogical experiences that have informed our own personal understanding of literature. Over the course of the semester, we will address practical issues such as developing course goals, creating a syllabus, and choosing primary and secondary texts, but we will also pay particular attention to a variety of techniques for reading and interpretation that will enable us to engage deeply with our students about the field and the works we have chosen to teach.Â As a graduate seminar, students will be asked to engage in a dialogue with each other and with the scholarship in the field about teaching and about literature.Â Â Course assignments will include weekly readings and critical writing, seminar presentations, and a final research project that develops an approach to teaching a particular of work of literature.

title: Rhetorical Ecologies
description: Aristotleâs On Rhetoric responds to the charge that successful rhetors merely demonstrate a âknackâ for flattery by mapping the art (techne) of rhetoric, systematically describing its aims and tools. Â And, to a degree that would be hard to overstate, rhetoricâs legacy has been shaped by variations on the Aristotelian model, especially insofar as it presumes an orderly language system driven by rational choices. Â Most rhetoricians, certainly in the American history of the field, have imagined rhetorical practice as a set of formal tools and analytical models. College composition, from its beginnings at Harvard in the 19th Century, has enthusiastically embraced this systematic account of rhetoric, and not unreasonably: it can be, and has been, packaged in fairly simple terms for diverse students. Contemporary rhetorical theorists and teachers of writing have increasingly questioned this framework, in order to re-conceptualize rhetoric as a complex system or an ecology. Novelist Jonathan Lethem, in Chronic City, captures the sentiment of this change when he writes, âTo live in Manhattan is to be persistently amazed at the worlds squirelled inside one another, the chaotic intricacy with which realms interweave. . . we only pretend to live on something as orderly as a gridâ (8). Theories of rhetoric as an ecology complicate, all aspects of rhetoricâs âgridâ: a rhetorâs cognition and intention (Marilyn Cooper), rhetorical situation (Jenny Rice), audience (Michael Warner), and certainly the teaching of writing (Margaret Syverson and others). This class will work through a representative sampling of the literature and will consider its theoretical and practical implications, especially with regard to the university writing classroom. Your work, of course, will consist of reading, discussion, and writing. Â Major assignments will include 4 genres central to academic work: a conference proposal, a conference paper, a seminar paper, and pedagogical design.

title: MA Capstone Seminar
description: The MA capstone seminarÂ provides students with the opportunity to develop an independent piece of work, one based on but not identical to a collection of projects from previous coursework, which can act as a ratification of the defining element of their academic or professional work in the program. The hope for students producing capstone projects is that, with the help of a faculty mentor and the capstone seminar instructor, they will be able to demonstrate in the tangible form of that capstone project a meaningful representation of the specific professional, intellectual or creative expertise acquired in the process of earning the Masterâs degree in English at Georgetown.Â The course will consider matters of audience, genre and form as students develop, revise and publish their projects.

title: Grad Thesis Research Seminar
description: The seminar is required in the fall term of the second year for all English Master's students opting to complete their degree by producing the conventional research-based thesis project in the appropriate sub-field of their choosing. The seminar guides students through the early and foundational phases of producing the Master's thesis. By the start of the fall term all thesis writers must have chosen a topic and a mentor; they must also have spent the summer doing initial exploratory reading and research in preparation for the work of composing the thesis in the second year. The seminar prepares students for the production of the thesis in the following ways: early in the fall term students will submit a brief, formal thesis proposal to their thesis mentors and the seminar instructor for approval; class time early in the fall will model possible thesis topics, methodologies and strategies for production in a variety of ways, including shared readings on research and writing in interdisciplinary English studies fields, reading actual published projects in a sampling of those fields, and visits by Georgetown English faculty to discuss their own strategies of conceptualization and execution of research projects. Students will also devote time in the course of the term to consulting with Georgetown's library staff and others qualified to support them in their thesis work. The seminar devotes later weeks in the term to student presentations on their individual research topics; these presentations will prepare students for the oral defense of the thesis scheduled for the spring term of year two, and they will help shape students' thinking as they prepare to submit the first 20 pages of their thesis to their mentor and the seminar instructor at the end of the fall term. The fall thesis research seminar encourages students taking the thesis option to form an intellectual and scholarly community devoted to an ethic of collaboration and mutual support, and to sustain that community for their entire second year in the program.

title: Thesis Research I
description: Graduate students enrolled in a master's thesis program or a doctoral program who have completed all coursework must register for Thesis Research - 999, section 1, in each Fall and Spring semester; the registration fee is currently $2,750 per semester. This includes students who have completed all course work and plan to take language proficiency examinations or comprehensive examinations before beginning research and writing of the thesis or dissertation.

title: Thesis Research III
description: Students enrolled in a master's thesis program or a doctoral program who are near the end of their coursework, and for whom registration for the remaining required course credits would result in less than full-time enrollment, may register for Thesis Research - 999, section 3. Those who do not need to be certified as enrolled full-time are not required to do so. No additional registration fee will be assessed for this enrollment (i.e., the student will be charged only the tuition rate for the enrolled course credits).

title: Intensive EFL Basic
description: Students establish a firm foundation of English sounds, forms, and functions in order to communicate with English speakers in everyday settings and to function in an English-speaking environment. Reading and writing activities reinforce the oral communications work and help students develop a balanced language skill profile.

title: Intensive EFL Basic
description: Students establish a firm foundation of English sounds, forms, and functions in order to communicate with English speakers in everyday settings and to function in an English-speaking environment. Reading and writing activities reinforce the oral communications work and help students develop a balanced language skill profile.

title: Intensive EFL Low Intermediate
description: Integrated learning activities involving all skill areas help students practice and apply the fundamentals of English in more varied settings and develop more accuracy and control of their spoken and written English. Reading activities promote vocabulary expansion while writing activities teach the basics of paragraph development and essay organization.

title: Intensive EFL Low Intermediate
description: Integrated learning activities involving all skill areas help students practice and apply the fundamentals of English in more varied settings and develop more accuracy and control of their spoken and written English. Reading activities promote vocabulary expansion while writing activities teach the basics of paragraph development and essay organization.

title: Intensive EFL Intermediate
description: Students learn the more complex forms and functions of English to express their ideas more clearly and accurately. Classroom and independent reading texts include both adapted and authentic material. Student writing activities include journals, short compositions, and longer essays. Students select two elective classes that focus on either pronunciation or vocabulary in different content areas.

title: Intensive EFL Intermediate
description: Students learn the more complex forms and functions of English to express their ideas more clearly and accurately. Classroom and independent reading texts include both adapted and authentic material. Student writing activities include journals, short compositions, and longer essays. Students select two elective classes that focus on either pronunciation or vocabulary in different content areas.

title: Intensive EFL High Intermediate
description: Students target their English skill development toward more specialized language learning goals. Core classes help students develop accuracy and fluency in speaking and writing, understand the main and supporting ideas of authentic listening and reading materials, and apply their language skills to more complex communicative and guided research tasks. Students choose four elective classes that focus on refining specific language areas including grammar, vocabulary, and pronunciation.

title: Intensive EFL High Intermediate
description: Students target their English skill development toward more specialized language learning goals. Core classes help students develop accuracy and fluency in speaking and writing, understand the main and supporting ideas of authentic listening and reading materials, and apply their language skills to more complex communicative and guided research tasks. Students choose four elective classes that focus on refining specific language areas including grammar, vocabulary, and pronunciation.

title: Advanced English
description: Advanced English courses are offered with content and skill focus in three areas: academic, professional, and cultural. Advanced Academic is designed to prepare students for study in a university setting. Advanced Professional is designed for students who expect to work in areas that demand a high level of English proficiency. Advanced Cultural English is designed for students who are interested in increasing their use of English for cross cultural communication. Courses include instruction in advanced reading, writing and research skills as well as aural/oral communication skills. Students also enroll in two advanced elective classes that address communication skills in specific content areas.

title: Academic Bridge
description: The Academic Bridge course is designed for students who have been or expect to be accepted into a U.S. university and want to polish their research, academic writing, and classroom presentation and discussion skills. Academic Bridge students also audit one unversity course during the first half of the semester in order to prepare for the academic demands of university courses.

title: Academic Bridge
description: The Academic Bridge course is designed for students who have been or expect to be accepted into a U.S. university and want to polish their research, academic writing, and classroom presentation and discussion skills. Academic Bridge students also audit one unversity course during the first half of the semester in order to prepare for the academic demands of university courses.

title: Expository Writing for Foreign Students
description: The primary aim of this course is to assist foreign students in preparing themselves for the type of writing required for themes, essay examinations, term papers, and lengthy reports. The principles of rhetorical organization and development are thoroughly presented within the context of each student's language and cultural background. Frequent individual conferences with the instructor are scheduled. Four class hours weekly. Pass/fail grade only.

title: Expository Writing for Foreign Students
description: The primary aim of this course is to assist foreign students in preparing themselves for the type of writing required for themes, essay examinations, term papers, and lengthy reports. The principles of rhetorical organization and development are thoroughly presented within the context of each student's language and cultural background. Frequent individual conferences with the instructor are scheduled. Four class hours weekly. Pass/fail grade only.

title: Critical Writing for International Affairs
description: This course is designed to help you to improve your reading and writing skills so that you can communicate clearly and effectively in your academic writing assignments, including essay assignments, essay exams, and research papers. You will read articles on political issues, government policies, and international affairs in order to identify how good writers communicate effectively in English. We will focus on reading materials both from your textbook and from newspaper, professional journals, and other sources in the field of international affairs. These texts will serve as models of good writing, and you will analyze them and apply what you learn to your own writing. This course is for SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Literature and Writing for Foreign Students
description: Through the use of selected American writings, supplemented by other media, this course provides an introduction to American literature, life, and culture which will help foreign students understand and give written expression to their personal experiences here. The course requires extensive writing and discussion. Fall semester focuses on twentieth century American fiction. Spring semester covers American literature to 1900 in survey format. (Foreign students who are not native speakers of English may elect this course to satisfy the requirement in literature.)

title: Literature and Writing for Foreign Students
description: Through the use of selected American writings, supplemented by other media, this course provides an introduction to American literature, life, and culture which will help foreign students understand and give written expression to their personal experiences here. The course requires extensive writing and discussion. Fall semester focuses on twentieth century American fiction. Spring semester covers American literature to 1900 in survey format. (Foreign students who are not native speakers of English may elect this course to satisfy the requirement in literature.)

title: Gateway to Film and Media Studies
description: This course will introduce students to the major elements of the Film & Media Studies Minor: the fundamentals of film composition and theory; media histories; practical experience of producing visual images and sound; and awareness of issues of social justice. We will also discuss spectatorship, audiences, and industrial structures of film, in addition to examining newer media and distribution platforms, ranging from radio and television to videotape and the internet. A portfolio of assignments will give students an opportunity to demonstrate their proficiency in analysis and technology as well as to reflect on aspects of production, post-production, and reception. Pre-requisites: Admission to the Film & Media Studies minor

title: Documentary Film: History and Theory
description: Documentary Film: History & Theory will examine the history and development of documentary film from the proto-ethnographic cinema of Robert Flaherty (Nanook of the North) to cutting edge documentaries screened at the 2011 Silverdocs International Film Festival. Through screenings, readings, and discussion, the course will consider questions of truth, creative representations of reality, methods of filmmaking, and the unique development of genres within the large umbrella of documentary film. The first half of the course will concentrate on the historical develop- ment of socially conscious documentaries, political and war propaganda, the evocation of cities, and experimental documentary filmmaking. The latter part of the course will focus on a series of subgenres: direct cinema and cinÃ©ma vÃ©ritÃ©, the first-person and activist film, the rise of the documentary celebrity and blockbuster (Michael Moore, Morgan Spurlock), and culminating in recent hybrid forms and transmedia storytelling (Ari Folmanâs Waltzing with Bashir and Clio Barnardâs The Arbor). Documentary Film: History & Theory will be taught by Sky Sitney. Professor Sitney is the Director of Silverdocs, the largest documentary festival in the U.S. She is the former Director of Programming of the Newport International Film Festival and Programmer of the New York Underground Film Festival. She is an authority on the history of documentary film with unique expertise in contemporary documentary production internationally. FMST-355-01 will meet on Thursdays from 3:30-6 pm in the New South Film Screening Classroom. Mandatory screenings for the course will be held on Wednesdays from 6-9 pm in the New South Film Screening Classroom.

title: Fundamentals of Finance
description: This course is a survey course, designed to explore the major concepts in corporate finance. Topics include Time Value of Money; Net Present Value/Internal Rate of Return; Valuation of Stocks and Bonds; Financial Statements/Ratios; Managing Growth; Discounted Cash Flow; Risk, Return and the Cost of Capital; Capital Structure; Dividends; Raising Capital/Going Public; Mergers and Acquisitions; and Corporate Restructuring. The course focuses on applications of finance to assist students outside the Business School to understand financial statements, apply financial analytical techniques, and learn the basics of the capital markets. Note: students will not be expected to have prior knowledge of course concepts.

title: Business Financial Management
description: This basic course in finance covers the functions of financial managers, financial markets, and financial analysis tools, and corporate financial decision making. It provides an introduction to cost of capital, investment analysis, capital budgeting and the valuation of securities.

title: Advanced Financial Management
description: This course provides an extensive coverage of corporate finance theory and the applicability of this theory for the financial manager. Topics include: capital budgeting under uncertainty; the relevance of capital structure decisions on security valuation and riskiness; the theory and practice of dividend policy; implications of financial market efficiency for management; risks and rewards of international financial markets; security valuation including the use of option pricing models; the market for corporate control; risk management through the use of options and future contracts; financial engineering; corporate restructuring and bankruptcy. Cases are used. Prerequisite: Business Financial Management.

title: Real Estate Finance
description: The course is structured to give the student an introduction to the principles of real estate with an emphasis in finance. Topics include real estate and the economy, financing residential properties, sales contracts, leases, appraisal, land use controls, and the real estate development process.

title: Investment Banking
description: The primary goal of the course is to provide a broad overview of the investment banking business and mergers and acquisitions. The first half of the class will deal with general investment banking topics, including the structure and direction of the securities industry and its participants, the securities offering process (focusing on initial public offerings), convertible and high yield debt securities, spin offs and carveouts. The second half of the course will examine mergers and acquisitions. Topics to be covered include deal design and structure, valuation of synergies, deal tactics, hostile takeovers, and private equity. The course will have a case orientation.

title: Principles of Investment
description: Course Objective: The objective of this course is to teach finance students about investment theory and practice. The course will utilize a combination of theoretical studies, problem sets and a portfolio project to teach students about: 1) the structure of the capital markets; 2) theories and practice in portfolio management; 3) asset pricing theories used to analyze securities; 4) equity and debt securities; and 5) derivative instruments. The course will focus primarily on the U.S. markets, although we will touch briefly on international investments. This course will provide students with information and skills needed to succeed in careers in asset management, corporate finance, research, sales or trading.

title: Fixed Income Securities
description: The objective of this course is to provide an on overview of fixed income markets and securities. The course will be useful to students who are interested in pursuing careers in corporate finance, banking or investment management. The course will provide highly practical information as well as theoretical information regarding US Government and foreign government securities, corporate bonds, municipal bonds, asset backed securities, hybrid securities, bond funds, and strategies related to investing in and issuing fixed income securities. During each class, in addition to discussing the theory critical to understanding the fixed income markets, we will discuss the current market environment and real-life decisions that finance professionals must make in this environment.

title: Global Financial Markets and Institutions
description: A study of the flow of funds through the domestic and international markets and institutions. Emphasis will be on the roles of money and capital markets and financial institutions. Topics will include interest rate determination, financial intermediation, regulation of financial services, and the role of the Federal Reserve and other central banks.

title: International Finance
description: The course addresses key issues of international financial management. It studies the international financial environment as well as corporations' strategic alternatives and operational practices. The following topics are among those covered: foreign exchange markets and the management of foreign exchange exposure; capital budgeting in multinational corporations; cross-border lending by banks; trade finance; and foreign direct investment. The reading materials and class discussions include both theoretical analyses and cases.

title: Corporate Governance & Valuation
description: Recent corporate scandals have put the spotlight on the importance of effective corporate governance. This course will examine the financial aspects of corporate governance and its effect on value creation for shareholders. Various readings and case studies will be used to provide students with real-life examples of the key elements of corporate governance. The course is geared toward undergraduate business students who are majoring in Finance. This course is ideal for students who are considering careers in corporate finance, investment banking, auditing, or law. To be successful in this course a student should possess strong analytical and communication skills. The emphasis of the course will be on the impact of corporate governance decisions on the value of the firm and value creation for shareholders of publicly traded firms. The course will apply valuation techniques to case studies involving corporate governance decisions within firms.

title: Derivatives and Financial Markets
description: This course program is designed to expand participants' understanding of derivative-related financial instruments (forwards, futures and options) and their use in investment and corporate financial management.

title: Financial Modeling
description: This course is about financial models and their use and simulation using Microsoft Excel. The models covered consider Portfolio Optimization, Option Pricing, and Fixed Income Securities. While you have been presented with these topics in other classes, this course emphasizes on the hands-on experience with spreadsheet modeling.

title: Entreprenuerial Finance
description: The course would seek to examine entrepreneurial firms and challenges in financing such firms. The course would be heavily based on cases, many of which would be based on young firms although some would be about more established firms. An entrepreneur needs to understand how to identify a business opportunity as well as how to marshal resources (especially capital) to and exploit the business opportunity. The course would reinforce skills needed to analyze a business (with a strong emphasis on valuation techniques). The course would then focus on issues relevant for financing of entrepreneurial ventures. This would include understanding financial institutions (Angel investors, Venture Capitalists, and Banks) that provide risk capital for such firms. The course would conclude by examining the harvesting of entrepreneurial ventures where by the entrepreneur and the financiers realize the returns for their effort.

title: Fundamentals of Finance
description: This course is a survey course, designed to explore the major concepts in corporate finance. Topics include Time Value of Money; Net Present Value/Internal Rate of Return; Valuation of Stocks and Bonds; Financial Statements/Ratios; Managing Growth; Discounted Cash Flow; Risk, Return and the Cost of Capital; Capital Structure; Dividends; Raising Capital/Going Public; Mergers and Acquisitions; and Corporate Restructuring. The course focuses on applications of finance to assist students outside the Business School to understand financial statements, apply financial analytical techniques, and learn the basics of the capital markets. Note: students will not be expected to have prior knowledge of course concepts.

title: International Finance
description: This course extends the principles of financial management and investments to the international context. The course will be concerned with understanding the international finance environment and the implication of exchange rates on firm value and investments. This includes the understanding of the determinants of exchange rates and the international currency markets, including institutional structures and underlying economics. This section includes hedging practices of international firms. This course is designed from the viewpoint of someone that participates in the international financial environment, whether this person is a CFO, General Manager or Treasurer of an international firm, an investment banker, an investor, or a consultant.

title: Financial Markets and Corporate Decision Making
description: TBA

title: Investment Analysis
description: This course focuses on the application of investment theory and contemporary research to the practice of equity securities analysis and portfolio management. Topics include valuation of equity securities, investment management for private clients, institutional equity portfolio management, hedge funds and alternative investments, and portfolio performance evaluation. The primary focus of this course is to demonstrate the relationship between investment theory and the practice of equity portfolios management.

title: Investment Banking
description: The primary goal of the course is to provide a broad overview of the investment banking business. Topics will include current industry issues and trends, the basics of valuation, the securities offering process, a review of various types of debt securities, and an overview of leveraged buyouts and private equity.

title: Derivatives and Financial Markets
description: This course develops understanding of the basic derivative-related financial instruments (forwards, swaps, futures and at-the-money options), and their use in transforming and managing risky investments and projects. Objectives are (1) to provide a basic understanding of derivatives practice and use in financial markets, (2) to provide practical and simple investment and corporate financial management strategies using derivatives and (3) to allow students to apply these concepts and skills to meet investment and corporate finance objectives, using a series of examples.

title: Corporate Finance
description: This course focuses on aspects of corporate financing decisions including cost of capital, capital structure, dividend policy, and an introduction to the use of derivatives for risk management. Emphasis is placed on the application of finance principles to business cases. Prerequisite: FINC 551. MBA 1st years students only.

title: Real Estate Finance
description: This course covers the financing of residential and commercial real estate from the standpoint of both equity and debt investors. Major topics include valuation of income-producing real estate, analysis of markets & risk factors unique to real estate & development, mortgage financing, secondary mortgage markets, land development and construction loans, legal and regulatory issues.

title: Fixed Income Securities
description: This course provides highly practical information as well as theoretical information regarding US Treasury securities, corporate bonds, municipal bonds, asset backed securities, hybrid securities, bond funds, and strategies related to investing in and issuing fixed income securities. We will also discuss the current market environment and real-life decisions that finance professionals must make in this environment.

title: Project Finance
description: Project finance is an approach that uses the cash flows generated by a new project to repay the debt used to construct the project, and to provide a return to the equity investors in the project. Project finance debt is typically borrowed on a non-recourse or limited-recourse basis in which the cash flows generated by the project, and the assets of the project, serve as the collateral for the lenders. This approach enables large projects to be financed that otherwise would be too large, or represent too much of a resource allocation, for individual corporations and host governments. This course provides a presentation of the current state of project finance, and the use of the project finance approach to finance energy and infrastructure projects. A special focus of the course will be on the use of project finance to support projects in emerging markets given the applicability of this approach to provide the financing necessary to meet the needs for investment in infrastructure. The course will provide a comprehensive examination of the current global "best practices" in project finance. To accomplish its objectives, the course will use Harvard Business School, and other business school cases, that will serve to illustrate and bring to life the subject areas covered in the lectures. The cases selected will highlight the major issues in project finance.

title: Corporate Risk Management
description: Corporate Risk Management is the sequel to Advanced Corporate Finance. Topics include: extensions of capital budgeting, mergers and acquisition, and valuation into an international context; foundations of option pricing and application of option pricing to capital budgeting and security valuation including real options; measuring and managing a firmâs risk exposures. Topics in international financial markets are studied in depth from a managerial perspective with an emphasis on hedging exchange rate risk.

title: Real Estate Financial Markets
description: This course will cover highly specialized topics in real estate finance, underwriting and market analysis, including: Advanced financial modeling/underwriting techniques for real estate investments & Excel spreadsheet analysis; advanced issues in real estate capital markets, risk management & insurance; international real estate investments; corporate real estate applications; advanced issues in real estate taxation and entity structuring; regulatory & legal issues in real estate investments; regulatory response to 2008 credit markets crisis & role of real estate finance. â¢ Capital markets for real estate are similar to other asset classes, in the sense that equity and debt interests are valued using similar methods of cash flow discounting and multiples. The underlying risk factors of owning real estate (or taking real estate as loan collateral) involve some similar factors such as market, interest rate, term and credit risk, but also require nuanced understanding of risks somewhat unique real estate - particularly cyclical supply/demand dynamics which may vary widely in terms of capital markets (cyclicality of financing and liquidity) as well as geography (cyclicality of supply/demand for real estate assets). â¢ The course will examine the process of financing commercial real estate from the standpoint of both debt and equity investors. Consideration will be given to asset valuation methodologies, debt and equity structures, examination of special risks and risk management techniques.
