With this background we will move on to an explorations organized by historical and functional components such as the colonial period (historical) and the Information Technology sector (functional). In the historical and functional weeks of the course, material will introduce the colonial and post-Independence systems of political economy characterizing and explaining patterns in those systems. We will then move on to a study of the political economy of economic reform starting in the 1980s and reaching their crescendo in 1991. We will then move on to functional components that will (where possible) look across time at a range of actors (formal and informal institutions, firms) to explore factors of production (land, labor and capital), economic sectors (agriculture, industry and services), and industrial-commercial sectors (finance, pharma, infotech). Along the way we will look at ways to understand how and why India is globalizing and integrating with the world economy. We will look at patterns of continuity and change in the organization and behavior of key actors such as the firms, civil society, as well as the state, its institutions and the political system (which is much more than the state). The main aim of the course is to familiarize students with patterns of continuity and change in the interaction between political power and economic activity in India. The approach is interdisciplinary drawing on work in political science, history, anthropology, sociology, demographics, and economics. Although the main focus is on economic and political issues in India and the South Asian region, the course will also provide a basic understanding of the problems faced by developing countries more generally. Students will be expected to think critically about debates of fact and theory related to economic and political change (often referred to as âdevelopmentâ) and the associated policy implications. Policy issues pertaining to economic growth and development will be studied from a broad and rigorous analytical base. Examples of topics covered include: contemporary and historic features of âdevelopmentâ in India; poverty and inequality; financial inclusion; trade and financial liberalization; democracy and caste; institutions and governance.

title: Informal Economic Institutions
description: Blatâ, Guanxi, Wasta and Jeito are forms of informal exchange found in Russia, China, the Arab world and Brazil. They are sometimes criticized as corrupt, but are recognized by many economists as providing crucial âgreaseâ that makes the economic system work. These informal systems are perceived as unique in each of the cultural settings where they prevail, yet all societies have patterns of informal relationships that help the system to function. The course will explore similarities and differences in these relationships across a range of countries. Students will read and discuss material on five cases: Russia, China, the Arab world, and Brazil, along with one example from the U.S. In addition, each student will write a case study focusing on informal relationships in a country/culture/subculture of his/her choice.

title: Political Economy of Human Security
description: Why do many developing countries lack human security? In this seminar, we will analyze the leading theories of civil conflict and the breakdown of states. We will first unpack national-level conditions present in countries that experience human insecurity. Second, we will investigate the micro-logic of human insecurity. Our goal is to evaluate the logic behind these theories, critically assess the quantitative and qualitative evidence, and develop questions for new research.

title: Sport and Peacebuilding
description: During the first years of the new millennium the United Nations, the Olympic Movement and a number of civil society organizations across the globe, joined their forces to promote the concept of Sport for Development and Peace. In this class, students will learn the key theoretical and practical foundations of the conditions under which the institution of sport can help build peace in deep-rooted communual conflicts. This inter-disciplinary course will introduce students to a number of Sport for Peace programs and teach them how to design, monitor and evaluate programs and curricula.

title: Approaches/Topics in American Politics
description: This course introduces students to the major political institutions and processes of American government and to some of the major theories, concepts, and methodologies in the literature in the field. The major goal of this course is to demonstrate to students that our understanding of American politics is best enhanced by a variety of approaches and methods.

title: MA Capstone: Leadership & Process
description: This is the first semester of a sequence required for and limited to American Government Accelerated M.A. Students. The seminar compares theoretical models of American government and politics with lessons learned from the experience of practitioners charged with making the system work. A practicum experience of twenty hours per week based on the student's personal interest and professional objectives accompanies the seminar. The format is intensive discussion of the readings and the case studies and of the student's practicum experience. [American Government]

title: Values in Political Advocacy
description: Politics is often the selling of values. Political advocates claim that their preferred policies are more fair, or increase security, or enhance liberty, or improve efficiency, or reduce injustice, and so forth. In this course we focus on the values used in political advocacy. The course has two main elements. First, what are the most important values defined? How are they contested? Second, how do advocates seek to use values to obtain political change. The class will be both normative and positive. By the end of the course the successful student will be more effective in defining and promoting values in the political process.

title: Democracy & Stabilization
description: TBA

title: American Political Development
description: TBA

title: Program Design and Evaluation
description: Over the past several years, the democracy assistance community has been challenged to more effectively monitor and evaluate democracy assistance programs. Implementers are being encouraged to develop more robust and refined measures to monitor and evaluate their work, with overall efforts under increasing scrutiny by policy makers. However, there is no consensus on how best to evaluate democracy assistance. The nature of complex systems makes democracy assistance particularly difficult to evaluate using traditional M&E frameworks. The field of monitoring and evaluation for democracy and governance (DG) programs is being shaped by debates over research design and methodology, and broader debates over issues such as ethics, utilization, and what â in effect â we should be evaluating. Policy makers, funders, implementers, and aid recipients themselves are in the midst of this debate. This course will review concepts and theories behind monitoring and evaluation with respect to democracy assistance, instill transferable skills through hands-on evaluation work, and explore the current debates that are shaping this dynamic field.

title: Research Methods in Democracy & Development
description: This is a course on research design for social sciences. The course will cover how to create a useful research question, how to develop, refine, and test hypotheses, and how to select the appropriate research methodology. Each student will identify a research question, develop a research design for testing it, and present the research design to the rest of the class.

title: Civil Society in Transitioning Countries and Emerging Democracies
description: TBA

title: History and Politics of the Caucasus
description: This graduate-level seminar examines the history of the north and south Caucasus from the late eighteenth century to the present. Major themes include the origins of group identity, imperial rule and resistance, modernization and development, social conflict and cooperation, and liberalization and democratization. Readings include major works on the modern history and contemporary politics of the region, as well as primary texts. Grades will be based on completion of a major research paper using original sources or other empirical data. MA/PhD only. Reading knowledge of Russian, Turkish or a regional language of the Caucasus is required.

title: Democratic Change: Policy/Practice/Politics
description: To be added.

title: Governance and the Rule of Law
description: This course is an advanced seminar that brings together the conceptual foundations of development paradigms in the context of rule of law. It seeks to accomplish the following goals: 1. To provide students with a background in the theory and practice of governance and institution building with a specific focus on challenges in the 21st century. 2. To review the conceptual foundations of development paradigms in order to examine the extent to which law is key to the management of public institutions and their response to shifting political, economic and judicial trends. 3. To sharpen students' ability to think and write analytically about current trends and potential policies to improve institutional incentives and reforms to promote good governance and the rule of law. The class is structured as a seminar in which students are expected to complete each week's assigned readings and to be prepared to rigorously discuss the readings with fellow classmates. Each student will serve as a discussion leader for one week's reading assignment in the course of the semester. Students should be prepared to make original critical analyses of reading assignments in their capacity as a discussion leader. The purpose of this requirement is to prepare students for speaking in public forums, delivering presentations in a professional environment and serving on conference panels.

title: International Electoral Policy and Practice
description: The objective of this course is to provide the student with knowledge of the history of electoral policy and practice and the analytical frameworks for electoral policy-making, design and evaluation. With the completion of this course, the student should be capable of engaging in elections and political process policy design with international electoral assistance programs, electoral reform initiatives, or state building activities for international organizations, diplomatic missions, bilateral development agencies, or national election authorities.. Approach to Subject Matter Each class session will focus on a different topical element of electoral process as shown below: â¢ Introduction to elections as instruments of governance; â¢ History of electoral process development; â¢ Elections and the international community; â¢ Election Management Bodies and other electoral regulation institutions; â¢ Non-governmental elections; â¢ Elections and technology; â¢ Electoral systems and delimitation; â¢ Political party systems and resources; â¢ Enfranchising marginalized electorates; â¢ Electoral conflict and security; â¢ Electoral fraud and manipulation; and â¢ Electoral observation and validation. Each class session will involve a lecture and discussion on the topics listed above. A preparatory readings list will be developed on each topic. Where possible, guest lecturers such as visiting election officials, will be invited to address the class.

title: Post-Communist Politics
description: This course traces the processes of economic, social and political change in Russia and Eastern Europe beginning with the dramatic period of reform and revolution in the 1980s. Students will investigate the break with communism and simultaneous efforts at market building and democratization. For the contemporary period, we will inquire into the causes of the divergent political development evident in the current regimes that have emerged from the ruins of the old Soviet bloc.

title: Comparative Democratization
description: This course will serve as an introduction to the vast literature on democratization in comparative politics. Democratization is studied here from the perspective of the transition and consolidation processes of the late 20th century. The principal goal of the course is to understand the social, economic, cultural, institutional and political conditions, both domestic and international, that help explain democratic development and, in particular, the multifaceted problems of transition and consolidation of democracy. We shall also consider the reasons for the more recent resurgence of various forms of authoritarianism.

title: U.S. Intelligence and Law
description: To be added.

title: Transnational Justice: Truth Commissions, Prosecutions &  Peacebuilding
description: Transitional justice refers to the processes and mechanisms which enable a state to move from a situation of massive human rights violations and undemocratic rule to the beginning of the rule of law and participatory democracy. Central to this question is the need to ensure both peace and justice in fragile states living at the interregnum between the past and the future. Which comes first â justice or peace? Is it possible to have both? A related question concerns the relationship between justice and reconciliation? What is meant by political reconciliation? How is this distinguishable from personal reconciliation? Those who prioritise justice argue that the promotion of international law, through mechanisms such as the International Criminal Court (ICC), is imperative to prevent impunity, as an act of deterrence and to provide a basis for the establishment of the rule of law in an emerging new society. Those who prioritize peace and reconciliation emphasize the need for political and other moral and political trade-offs that may be required to ensure peace. Under what circumstances, if at all, does this require the suspension of the demands of international law regarding genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes? Are there situations where restorative and distributive justice should take precedence over the demands of retributive justice? Is it possible to have both? The course considers mechanisms of transitional justice in different historical and political contexts. These range from the Nuremberg and Tokyo trials in the latter part of the 1940s, through the international criminal tribunals of Yugoslavia and Rwanda, the emergence and proliferation of truth commissions, and the genocide trails in Cambodia. Attention is given to the South African Truth and Reconciliation Commission as an example of what truth commissions can and cannot achieve. The International Criminal Court is considered in relation to the challenges to its legitimacy from the African Union and other quarters.

title: Conflict Resolution Theory
description: This course will explore âPeaceâ and âConflict Resolutionâ; what they mean for different people and different approaches to âresolving conflictsâ and establishing a âpeacefulâ world. The objective of the course is to provide a broad understanding of central peace and conflict resolution theories including positivist theories such as realist conflict resolution theory, liberal peace theory, and human needs theory and post-positivist theories including critical theories, post-structural theories of peace and conflict resolution. Course will explore ontological, epistemological and methodological differences between these theories and discuss how each theory defines the core concepts of the field such as âconflictâ, âviolenceâ, âpeaceâ, âpowerâ, among others; explains sources of conflict and violence; and how each theory envisions resolution of conflicts and requirements of establishing peace. The course will also look at the practical application of these theories in real-life contexts and discuss strengths and limitations of each theory.

title: Conflict Resolution Skills
description: In order to pursue a career in conflict resolution, whether as a researcher, scholar or practitioner, a strong foundation in conflict resolution skills and processes such as conflict analysis and assessment, communication, cross-cultural facilitation, and dialogue are necessary. The course focuses on practical and experiential learning through exercises, simulations, films, case studies, discussions and interaction with experts in the field. Since many individuals pursuing careers in conflict resolution will find employment in diverse roles in related sectors, this course will also address how mainstream and market conflict resolution expertise.

title: Arabs in Africa: Conflict and State Formations in Africa
description: The encounter between Arabs, Africans and colonialists on the African continent has a long history. This has over the years resulted in both sporadic and lasting conflicts. It has also seen the birth of nations with both a strong Arab identity, as well as Afro-Arab forms of coexistence. The recent so-called âArab Springâ in North African countries, the difficulties in establishing post-revolutionary states (in, for example, Egypt and Libya) the separation of Sudan and South Sudan and continuing tensions in the Horn of Africa suggests that Arab influences, African nationalisms and the involvement of the United States, Europe and China is likely to intensify in the years ahead. The course is developed at the nexus between history and contemporary politics. As such, consideration in this course is given to the history of this encounter in specific countries, the influence on the Arab League and the African Union in the contemporary period, the involvement of the West and China in Africa, and the likely scenarios in the Afro-Arab countries regarding trade, resource allocations and political priorities in transitional situations. In each case students will be required to consider transitional justice and conflict resolution resources to enable the countries under consideration to move towards transparent, democratic and peaceful resolutions of conflict. The focus of the course will be on shared independent research. Invited to undertake a journey towards understanding the roots and contemporary manifestations of conflicts in certain African countries, students will be required to access their own reading and research material for each seminar. Students will especially be required to guide the class regarding appropriate readings for the seminars they are required to lead, as well as for their final group presentations. As such, the prescribed reading for each week, as reflected below, is brief and of an introductory nature only. The attached bibliography provides a broader range of background reading material.

title: Food Security and Conflict Resolution
description: Course Objective: This course will provide students with an operational perspective about food security, defined broadly, and various programming tools used to enhance food security that are used to support conflict mitigation/resolution. This course will combine elements of theory and a more hands-on "how to"perspective of food security analysis and programming in support of conflict mitigation/resolution. The course analyzes the international communities' use of food assistance, their effective use of food aid and other more innovative types of assistance tools in addressing food insecurity which, left unaddressed, can be an underlying factor leading to instability and conflict. The first few classes will introduce students to relevant terms and concepts on food security, programming tools as well as assessment methodologies used by the international community. Later classes will look at land and conflict and delve into nutrition as part of a greater understanding of food security. Specific case studies on Somalia and the Sahel will also be included.

title: Business and Conflict Resolution
description: Over the past decade, local and international businesses have become an important actor in supporting conflict resolution work. International NGOs, the UN, the World Bank, and the private sector are increasingly involved in the intersection of business and conflict. While international donor aid can make a positive contribution to peacebuilding, private business activity can also have a significant impact in conflict areas. Current efforts in the area include the UN Global Compact, the emergence of corporate social responsibility, and fostering peacebuilding through economic development in conflicted regions. This course will provide an overview of the intersection of business and conflict resolution by focusing on emerging theory and practice. We will explore the myriad of business activities across diverse sectors (including extractive industries, finance, etc.) and levels of activity (local, national and international actors). The potential roles of business in conflict will be explored such as a generator of conflict, advocate for peace, financer of peace, promoter of joint economic activity, etc. A core component of the course will be discussing the concept and practice of conflict sensitive business. Through taking this course students will be better prepared to undertake research, practice and policy careers within conflict resolution and related fields. The course will cover a combination of theory and real-world cases, helping to contextualize many of key concepts covered in the course.

title: Managing Organizational Conflict
description: Effectively managed organizational conflict is something to which managers often aspire, yet often fail to accomplish. Why is this so? What makes managing organizational conflict such a difficult task? Today, in a globalized economy, the consequences of poorly managed organizational conflict are many. Poorly managed conflict gives competition an advantage where one may not have existed previously. Misunderstanding, enmity and conflict have derailed many projects, people, and ideas. In the short run a poorly managed employee may result only in slightly lowered morale, in the long run, however, the poorly managed employee transmogrifies into a lawsuit, bad publicity and sometimes even violence. The unit Managing Organizational Conflict is designed to canvass a variety of issues that impact upon the effective management of organizational conflict. In this unit we will examine the dynamics of poorly managed organizational conflict, to better determine what goes wrong. We will also examine effectively managed conflict, so that we may ascertain the many ways in which conflict can be used constructively. Also canvassed with a range of subjects addressing directly methods for encouraging effective conflict management.

title: Criminal Law and Conflict Resolution: Victims/Witness/Defendants
description: In a courtroom setting, there are three major players in the search for conflict resolution: victims, witness, and defendants. This course will consist of a combination of theoretical and hands-on clinical explorations of domestic and international justice systems from the perspective of these three groups, with the goal of better understanding how courts impact the resolution of conflict. Included in the course will be meetings with and interviews of victims of crime and survivors of genocide and other mass atrocities. In addition, the course will provide the opportunity for courtroom-style examinations of individual perpetrators of individual and societal violent crime, in such places as Rwanda, Cambodia, and Washington, DC.

title: Gender and Peacebuilding
description: This course is based on the premise that gender equity is a prerequisite to any sustainable development or peace building initiative (Reinman, 2001). The course integrates theory and practice, and will cover the main theoretical underpinnings of gender including key definitions and explore how gender issues began to be integrated into peacebuilding The course also places a strong emphasis on applied skills including how gender can be used as a tool of analysis; how to perform gender assessments; how to mainstream gender into peace building projects; how to design gender and advocacy campaigns and factor gender into conflict-related research. This course will provide students with: ? Solid theoretical foundations on gender and its connection to peace building ? Understanding how to perform gender assessments ? Ability to mainstream gender into programming ? Skills in designing advocacy campaigns at the intersection of gender and conflict ? Advanced research tools that integrates gender into the study of conflict settings

title: Conflict Resolution and Development
description: It is becoming increasingly evident that violent conflict and underdevelopment mutually reinforces each other. For instance, although there is no direct causal relation between conflict and poverty, poor countries are more at risk of conflict. At the same time violent conflict, its threat, and the build-up of arms in many countries have enormous long-term social, political, and economic costs. Conflicts cause significant damage to physical infrastructure, unemployment, lack of rule of law, and collapse of key social institutions. In order to establish sustainable peace, impact of conflicts in terms of human suffering, economic and social dislocation and wasted developmental opportunities must be addressed and this viscous cycle must be broken. This course focuses on the uncanny relationship between conflict, development and peace building. It takes a multidisciplinary approach, which is essential for grasping the complexity of the development process and introduces a range of concepts, theories and approaches raised in this field. The course also explores various concerns and challenges faced by practitioners working in the fields of peace building and development, as well as policy responses that seek solutions to these concerns and challenges. This course also highlights the importance of protecting and managing natural resources, mobilizing domestic resources, coordinating external assistance, fostering good corporate citizenship, and providing adequate and appropriate infrastructure and services within the context of development and peace building.

title: Culture and Conflict Resolution
description: This course focuses on culture and conflict resolution by exploring the role culture plays in conflict and how it impacts its resolution. This course treats culture and religion as aspects of individual and group identity which influence the causes, dynamics, outcomes of conflicts, and intervention methods. It identifies core âeticâ (culture general) and âemicâ (culture specific) patterns of cultural differences in such areas as values and beliefs, patterns of thought, interpretive frames, and behavior in peace-building and conflict resolution efforts; explores concepts such as high context/low context cultures, intercultural communication approaches, stereotyping, prejudices, and analyzes role of culture in peace-building processes such as mediation, negotiation, and facilitation. Cutting edge issues in theory development, research and practice in integrating cultural insights into the international peace and conflict resolution domain will also be discussed during the course. The course also focuses on specific skills, concepts, and stories relating to effective ways to handle cultural differences in conflict and peacebuilding training. It introduces skills and approaches to deal with issues of prejudice, stereotyping, and ethnocentrism in conflict settings as well as interactive exercises, role plays, and real life case studies to learn constructive ways of addressing intercultural encounters. Additionally, participants will identify their own intercultural conflict style through the Intercultural Conflict Style Inventory (ICS) developed by AUâs Dr. Mitchell Hammer. This course includes lecture presentations, personal development, in-depth group discussions, role-plays, films and guest speakers. Experiential learning methods are extensively used in the course.

title: Evaluation of Conflict Resolution
description: Effective monitoring and evaluation are critical for designing successful development projects, conflict resolution interventions as well as building sustainable peace and avoiding harm in the process. Yet assessing the effects of peace building interventions in volatile contexts can be a challenging endeavor. This course aims to provide an overview of the leading organizational, practical and theoretical approaches to the evaluation of conflict resolution initiatives. The course will approach monitoring and evaluation from a reflective perspective and will introduce theories of change, indicators, monitoring, evaluation design, and various tools for reflective practice. The course will focus on applied research; students will meet with leading practitioners, review evaluation frameworks from development, humanitarian and peace building agencies, and submit case studies of evaluation methodology and practice in the organization or project of their choice. At the end of the course students will have a better understanding of key elements of project design, monitoring, evaluation and challenges thereof.

title: Evaluation and Conflict Resolution
description: This class will teach students the principles of evaluation. Students will learn how to create a logic model, establish variables and indicators, construct standards, and synthesize and integrate outcomes to obtain a judgment of worth. Students will also learn the basics of writing a questionnaire and conducting an interview, as evaluators often rely on interviews for data gathering. We will then survey how evaluation strategies have been applied to the study of conflict, including to balance of power, conflict termination, and post-conflict issues. Guest speakers for this class include a former insurgent from Rwanda, a former counterinsurgent from Colombia, and a former negotiator in many African conflicts.

title: Capstone Course in Conflict Resolution
description: One of the challenges in the field of conflict resolution, given the diversity of theories and practices, is integrating a broad range of ideas, concepts and practices. In addition, as students near the end of the degree they often see things in a new light, with new insights, questions or interests. Finally, the challenge of integrating âtheory and practiceâ often remains elusive. Professionals often grapple with the nexus between theory and practice. The Capstone Course provides students with an opportunity to reflect upon the theory-practice nexus, fill in gaps in previous learning, and develop new ideas in conflict resolution great! Selecting an area for further work students will collaborate with other students and the course instructor to 1) reflect upon their own areas of interest in the conflict resolution field, 2) to examine the link between theory and practice, 3) further investigate areas relevant to conflict resolution, and to 4) develop a project that assists in their own understanding of the theory-practice link, expands their knowledge base, and makes a contribution to the wider field. Much of the course will be built around student projects. These projects can help students integrate ideas, flesh out new areas of interest, develop new ideas and develop career paths.

title: Environment and Conflict Resolution
description: Environmental conflict occurs at all levels of society, from local or regional disagreements about watershed management to national debates about managing nuclear waste to international disputes about how to address climate change. Understanding the sources of environmental conflict and adopting effective resolution techniques are critical to achieving an appropriate balance between the natural world and humanity in the 21st century. This course offers students the opportunity to explore the multifaceted relationship between the environment and social conflict from both theoretical and practical perspectives, with an emphasis on the scholarly literature and real-world cases. Topics we will cover include transboundary water conflict, climate change, environment and war, environment and peace, and international environmental negotiations. Approximately half of the course will be devoted to contemporary environmental conflict resolution (ECR) practice, including situation assessments, public involvement, negotiation, and environmental mediation, and how ECR practitioners assist stakeholders in navigating challenges posed by organizational dynamics, the policy setting, and scientific and technical complexity.

title: Environment and Conflict Resolution
description: Environmental conflict occurs at all levels of society, from local or regional disagreements about watershed management to national debates about managing nuclear waste to international disputes about how to address climate change. Understanding the sources of environmental conflict and adopting effective resolution techniques are critical to achieving an appropriate balance between the natural world and humanity in the 21st century. This course offers students the opportunity to explore the multifaceted relationship between the environment and social conflict from both theoretical and practical perspectives, with an emphasis on the scholarly literature and real-world cases. Topics we will cover include environment and war, environment and peace, ecoterrorism, and international environmental negotiations. Approximately half of the course will be devoted to contemporary environmental conflict resolution (ECR) practice, including situation assessments, public involvement, negotiation, and environmental mediation, and how ECR practitioners address process issues such as organizational dynamics and scientific and technical complexity.

title: Conflict Resolution/Terrorism
description: Over the past decade, there has been an increasing focus on the threat of domestic and international terrorism around the world. Groups and individuals who carry out acts of terrorism often fall outside the scope of traditional state-sponsored armed actors. Therefore, responding, preventing and stopping terrorism requires a host of traditional (policing, military, economic sanctions, intelligence) and non-traditional (cultural, social and development initiatives) initiatives. This course will cover alternative means of understanding, preventing and responding to terrorism. Topic will include: * Explore the current and potential contributions of the conflict resolution field to developing effective policies related to ending terrorism. * An overview of sources of terrorism and the motivations of individuals who engage in such acts. * Discussion of existing tools to motivate armed actors to transition from terrorist actions to joining the political process. * A review of how governments transition from a refusal to engage with armed actors to political engagement will be covered. * Review of how to integrate a conflict sensitive approach with traditional security responses. Through taking this course students will be better prepared to undertake research, practice, and policy careers within the security and conflict resolution fields. The course will cover a combination of theory and real-world cases, helping to contextualize many of key concepts covered in the course. In addition, the course will include several simulations, the screening of several short films on the topic and presentations by experts in the field.

title: Federalism and Intergovernmental Relations
description: This course investigates how various aspects of federalism, intergovernmental relations, and multi-tiered government affect public action. The first part of the course will be dedicated to the normative, theoretical, and historical content of federalism and intergovernmental relations in the United States and other countries. For example, how should federal and state responsibilities be allocated? Should federal mandates and federal transfers go hand in hand? Should the federal government rely more on persuasion and less on coercion? The second part of the course will be dedicated to the workings of federalism and intergovernmental relations in different policy areas. For example, how does federalism affect crucial regulatory areas such as environmental policy? How can equal educational and social opportunities be granted in a federal system? [American Government]

title: Welfare States in Transition
description: This course examines competing political and economic theories for social welfare policy design and reform in recent decades. Concentrating on the main areas of social policy - health, labor regulation and unemployment insurance, and old-age pensions - it examines divergent trends in policy scope, coverage, and spending both between countries and programs within the same country. The contrasting outcomes between the advanced industrial economies of Western Europe and the United States, as well as between the developing economies of Latin America and East Asia, illustrate the importance of domestic political and economic institutions in shaping diverse responses to the forces of globalization.

title: Congressional Politics
description: This seminar is designed to introduce students to cutting edge debates in the study of the U.S. Congress and to prepare students to carry out research that engages these debates and furthers our understanding of congressional politics. This course assumes a basic understanding of congressional behavior and processes. Each week we will examine an important area of research such as representation, elections, political parties, and presidential-congressional relations. The weekâs readings will address important questions within each field such as âwhat explains the decline in the competitiveness of congressional elections?â Students will read both classic works in the field and important new research. During class discussion and in research critiques, students will evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of the literature and will develop a research agenda that will advance our knowledge of the field. Students will create a final research project that takes on an important theoretical debate and develops a research design to address that question.

title: The American Presidency
description: [American Government]

title: Presidential Electoral Politics
description: This course examines some of the major research on the presidential nominating and election process in the United States. Each student will be required to write a research paper âpredicting presidential elections.â The paper will be an original piece of research using either political or economic variables to explain election outcomes. Class sessions will be devoted to discussing the required readings and individual research projects. Students will present their papers and findings in class at the end of the semester. [American Government]

title: Psychology & Politics
description: This graduate course in the Department of Government examines political leadership from a psychological perspective. Focusing on the psychological dimensions of character, style, and world view, it provides a foundation for the assessment of political leaders: their rhetoric, decision making, beliefs, and the style of their activities in office. We begin with an overview of the psychological dimensions of political leadership. In that overview, we examine mental health in general, suitability for office in particular, data sources on personalities, and methods of evaluation. After identifying and exploring the psychological components that relate to performance in office--character, style, and world view--we discuss character attributes, both positive and negative, which political leaders have evidenced. The second part of the course applies theory to practice; it looks at the leadership styles and personality types of political leaders, mostly but not exclusively, U.S. presidents. We begin this section by examining the case of Woodrow Wilson, comparing him to other American presidents. We then turn to Mahatma Gandhi, and finally to contemporary U.S. presidents beginning with Bill Clinton. Our objective here is to examine the influence of the presidentâs personality on his principal policy decisions and the manner in which those decisions were made. A full period examination of course readings and seminar discussions follows this part of the course. The remainder of the course will be devoted to individual research and the presentation of case studies by all members of the class. Each student will be expected to choose a political leader (excluding the individuals we have examined in the course) and provide a psychological profile that discusses that personâs character, style, and world view. A variety of references, including the personâs own writings and speeches, should be used to complete the profile. Students will be expected to illustrate how that leaderâs character, style, cognition, and beliefs affected his/her behavior and/or major policy decisions. The psychological profile should be used to supplement or substitute for the environmental, situational, and political factors that normally are used to explain and anticipate behavior and decision making. Students will present their leadership profiles in the final three meetings of the courseâ11/22, 11/29, and 12/6. The research paper is to be submitted no later than 5pm on the Monday following the end of classes, December 10th. Students should aim for a paper of approximately 15-20 pages in length, double spaced with at least 1 inch margins, pages numbered, and with standard citation procedures.

title: Comparative Political Economy
description: This course studies the political dynamics of economic relations, examining cross-national variation in the role of the state and private actors, relationships between labor and capital, and exposure to the international system. We will focus on the interaction of political institutions and systems of economic organization, including production regimes, financial markets, and social welfare policy. We will give special attention to recent reforms and restructuring in the face of globalization and economic crises. Examples will be drawn from Western and Eastern Europe, Latin America, and East Asia. As a seminar, this course will require active preparation and participation by students. Requirements include: â¢ Roughly 150-200 pages a week of reading. This will generally include one extended work or related set of papers by a single author. â¢ Regular participation in the discussion in class each week. â¢ Three short response papers (3 pages maximum) in response to a particular weekâs reading. These papers should be posted on the Blackboard discussion board no later than midnight the night before our class session, and all students should read them prior to coming to class. â¢ One five-page paper prospectus due before the middle of the term. â¢ One research paper, examining a specific labor-related policy or political outcome, in the region or country of the studentâs choosing. In consultation with the instructor, this paper should be tailored to fit with the studentâs academic program and educational goals

title: Religion and Globalization
description: The seminar will serve as the forum for a systematic analysis of global religious trends, for a critical discussion of contemporary debates on religion and politics around the world, and for the presentation of student research papers on related themes. Among the topics covered will be: Secularization and religious revivals, private and public religions, religious fundamentalisms and fundamentalist secularisms, Catholic and Muslim politics and aggiornamenti compared, transnational migrations, transnational religions and diversity, and the emerging global denominationalism.

title: Theories of Political Development
description: Theories of Political Development provides a comprehensive and critical overview of the major conceptual paradigms, theories and approaches that have guided Western studies of Third World societies over the last half a century. We are concerned first and foremost in understanding how analytical paradigms have guided, shaped and sometimes limited our analysis of development politics. We will also explore how these paradigms have helped shape the perceptions of US policy makers, particularly as regard questions of political and economic reform. Readings are drawn from a wide range of scholars working in fields of political economy, comparative politics, anthropology and sociology. Topics include regime transitions, the persistence of autocracy, ethno-religious conflict (and conflict resolution), and the political economy of reform. These topics will be explored comparatively, both within and across many regions, including the Middle East, Sub-Saharan Africa, South and Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe and Latin America. All students must have taken a basic introductory course in comparative politics. Further coursework in related fields is highly recommended.

title: Limits to Authoritarian Upgrading
description: TBA

title: Graduate Research Seminar on Russian Politics
description: Welcome to Government 648. This is a graduate research seminar on Russian politics. The focus of this seminar will be on the continuing politics of the presidential succession. For the past four years, this issue has been at the center of Russian politics, providing a rich source of insights into the workings of power in the Russian political system. Now the succession is a factâor is it?. Dmitri Medvedevâs election as president and his appointment of Vladimir Putin as prime minister have been widely dismissed, both in Russia and in the West, as a change without a difference. Vladimir Putin is expected to remain the most powerful figure in the Kremlin, the president in all but name, while Medvedev is viewed essentially as a loyal figurehead. This is indeed how things may be for the first year of the new administration. Putin and Medvedev enjoy an unusually close relationship, based on long-established trust and common views. Both men are determined to preserve the political stability of the last eight years. Indeed, it cannot be ruled out that Dmitri Medvedev will remain smoothly obedient to Putin throughout his four-year term, following which Putin may run again, as the Russian constitution allows him to do. Yet there are three reasons to suspect that this simple picture will not prevail much beyond the first year: â¢The institutional power of the Russian presidency: The position of the Russian president is far more powerful than that of prime minister. The president commands a vast apparatus of power and patronage. The so-called âpower ministriesâ all report to him, as does the diplomatic machinery. The prime minister, in contrast, is mainly responsible for social and economic policy, yet even in these domains the key ministries look to the Presidential Administration. To be sure, the position of prime minister can be strengthened and that of the president weakened. Reporting lines can be changed, budgets can be shifted, and staffs can be moved. But short of removing the entire presidential apparatus from the Kremlin to the White House and rewriting the Constitution, the presidency is likely to remain the seat of power. Over time, the person who holds it will become stronger. â¢A destabilizing economic and social environment: The domestic economic and social environment is likely to be less benign than it has been in the last eight years. The government is likely to have fewer resources at its disposal to support its rapidly growing welfare and investment programs. This will force painful reappraisals and catalyze political conflict. Even now, the âreformâ wing of the government is issuing public warnings of what could lie ahead, and is calling on the president and his prime minister to re-think some of the more conspicuous prestige programs, such as the vast spending planned for the Winter Olympics in Sochi and the Vladivostok ASEAN conference. The reform wing is tending to line up behind Medvedev, in the belief (which may turn out to be mistaken) that he is more attuned to economic realities than Putin may be. â¢The battle of clans will sharpen, but Putinâs power to moderate it may decline: Vladimir Putin is widely viewed as the only man who can hold the middle against the competing clans in the Russian political and business elite. Yet it is noteworthy that even at the height of his power he was barely able to keep the peace among themâwitness the failure of his attempt to merge Rosneft and Gazprom, his mixed success in moderating jousting among competing FSB factions, and his reluctance to intervene to defend finance minister Kudrinâs team against attacks by prosecutors. The successful management of the succession issue, which had divided the Russian elites for much of Putinâs second term, has not resolved the increasingly bitter rivalries among them. Yet if Putin is perceived as having to spend his political capital maintaining order, that will weaken both him and the president. The âbattle of the clans,â if not successfully contained, periodically breaks out in the form of raids, media attacks, exchanges of kompromat (âcompromising materialâ), arrests, indictments, and a general politicization of the prosecutorial and judicial system. The misuse of state coercion as an instrument of clan warfare weakens property rights and saps investor confidence, at a time when Russian needs more investment than ever. In particular, the clans will continue to do battle for ownership and control of oil and gas properties and other commodities. Indeed, such battles never stopped during Putinâs eight-year term, and there is little reason to believe that they will lessen in the period ahead. These will be the central research questions in Government 848. The aim is to examine the interplay of power, personalities, and policies shaping the new government under the âOdd Coupleâ in the Kremlin, and thereby to understand better the dynamic of power in Russian politics. The seminarâs in English, but the research materials must all be in Russian. That is because one of the main aims of the seminar is to encourage students to use their Russian skills for active research, mainly using the Internet to explore the many kinds of resources available there. Together we will explore useful websites, share lore and data, and expand our knowledge of the range of sources available for covering opinion poll data, legislative politics, policy initiation and debate, the law and the courts, economic policy, and the rough-and-tumble of todayâs Russian politics. To be in the course, you must have a minimum of three years of Russian. Advanced Internet skills are not necessary. The instructorâs permission is required. The course requirements are straightforward: 1) three short reports (4 pages each) on weekly class topics; 2) a 30-page seminar paper based exclusively on Russian-language sources; and 3) active participation in class discussion.

title: Politics of Economic Reform
description: Why are some countries rich and other countries poor? This has been a subject of heated debate among policymakers and scholars stretching as far back as Ancient Greece. Until recently, modern thought on why some countries are wealthy and others are not has tended to focus primarily on narrow economic issues, such as openness to trade and macroeconomic policy. However, a consensus is growing that politics, not economic policy, is the most important factor that determines countries growth trajectories. Macroeconomic policies may be proximate causes of economic growth (or lack thereof) yet these policies derive from political incentives. This raises the fundamental paradox of the political economy of development: why do leaders choose policies that discourage economic growth? Understanding why countries choose the economic policies they do is the main objective of this course.

title: Politics of Economic Reform
description: Why are some countries rich and other countries poor? This has been a subject of heated debate among policymakers and scholars stretching as far back as Ancient Greece. Until recently, modern thought on why some countries are wealthy and others are not has tended to focus primarily on narrow economic issues, such as openness to trade and macroeconomic policy. However, a consensus is growing that politics, not economic policy, is the most important factor that determines countries growth trajectories. Macroeconomic policies may be proximate causes of economic growth (or lack thereof) yet these policies derive from political incentives. This raises the fundamental paradox of the political economy of development: why do leaders choose policies that discourage economic growth? Understanding why countries choose the economic policies they do is the main objective of this course.

title: Democracy & Reform in SE Asia
description: This course examines the causes, consequences and contingent success of democratization and reform in Southeast Asia. It considers the classical and post-colonial context of the region, then focuses on contemporary patterns of social and institutional change. Transitions from autocracy to pluralism are examined in detail, and contrasting theories of democratization - cultural, socioeconomic, institutional - are tested against case evidence. In parallel, it considers efforts at economic and political reform, including anti-corruption campaigns, as part of the democratizing process. Themes include the development of civil society, ethno-national mobilization, religious parties, and populist politics. Examples are drawn from ASEAN member states, but three cases receive special attention: Indonesia, Thailand, and the Philippines.

title: Political Institutions
description: The electoral institutions that link voters to the government they elect profoundly affect the incentives that politicians encounter. For example, some systems encourage elected officials to appeal to broad constituencies while others motivate them to satisfy the interests of only narrow segments of their population. Moreover, political systems that work well in relatively homogeneous societies can have catastrophic effects in countries that have deep social cleavages. The purpose of this course is for you to learn how political institutions connect voters to the governments they elect and the incentives these systems create for citizens and politicians. Understanding these links is central for comprehending how democracies function, and why variations exist among democracies. In the course we will examine what these institutions are, the differences between these institutions in democracies and non-democracies, institutional variation within democracies, and why countries choose different types of democratic institutions.

title: Politics of Sub-Saharan Africa
description: While most of the world is growing economically, sub-Saharan Africa continues to stagnate. Moreover, whereas political stability and state consolidation characterize most regions of the world, political volatility and failed states remain ubiquitous in Africa south of the Sahara. Why does Africa seem to encounter greater obstacles to economic development and political stability than any other region of the world? The purpose of this course is to consider these questions by analyzing contemporary politics and political economy in sub-Saharan Africa. In the first part of the course, we examine the regionâs pre-colonial and colonial political structures. Subsequently, we will survey the political continuities and changes that have occurred since independence. The main topics we will cover are state consolidation, democratization, patronage politics, economic stagnation, civil war, ethnicity, failed states, and HIV/AIDS. The course provides an overview for why the region remains economically depressed and politically volatile.

title: Contemporary Debates In International Security
description: This course examines contemporary debates in international security. Each week we will focus on one main debate. The readings, which have been chosen to reflect competing points of view, will serve as a springboard for class discussions. The course will examine the conceptual issues that lie behind contemporary debates as well as the policy implications of these debates.

title: Future of International Legal Order
description: TBA

title: Sources of Nationalism
description: This course examines what nationalism is, where it comes from, and what effects it has on societies and international systems. We will cover material that presents contrasting historical and theoretical interpretations of nationalism. In class, we will carefully examine the main arguments contained in the readings and the historical evidence used to support these arguments. Students will be responsible for developing expertise in at least one historical or contemporary case of nationalism. Students will be expected to draw on their area of expertise in class discussions and in their research paper.

title: Civil-Military Relations
description: The study of civil-military relations addresses a simple puzzle: can we have a military strong enough to protect civilians yet not so strong as to ignore or subvert civilian direction? After all, a military that is strong enough to defend the state from its external enemies is also strong enough to seize power so as to rule for itself. How do you have a strong military capability without being swallowed by it? In this course, we will examine the variety of ways that the civil-military relationship can vary â how political leaders, society and military organizations interact, and how their cultures overlap and diverge. We will explore how this relationship can matter for important political outcomes, such as regime change, democratization, military policy, decision-making in crises and wars, military innovation and defense transformation, and military effectiveness. Students will master the basic theories of civil-military relations, study past and current controversies, and develop awareness of issues that remain unsettled, such as the use of military forces to handle domestic crises like Hurricane Katrina. The course will focus primarily on the U.S. case, but the theoretical frameworks covered have broad application to other advanced democracies and developing countries, and students will write papers that apply these theories to civil-military relations in one country.

title: Law & Religion Seminar: Acc.
description: Should a health care professional be able to refuse to deliver services or medicines they deem to be immoral, such as abortion or birth control pills? Should laws permitting same-sex unions include accommodations for businesses and government officials to be able to refuse to participate in the union? Should parents who object to public school curricula on grounds of faith be allowed to selectively withdraw their children from particular classes or curricular assignments? What is the moral and legal sources of such ârights of conscience"? In this seminar, we will examine the theoretical arguments about conscience-based claims to exemption from legal obligation in moral and political theory, theological ethics, and legal theory, and we will study the works of recent theorists concerning the wisdom and viability of conscience-based exemptions in the law. We will also explore the historical and recent landscape of legal cases and proposed legislative schemes involving conscientious objector status, medical services (e.g., abortion, sterilization, fertility, removal of life support), homeschooling and same-sex marriage. Full details at //apps.law.georgetown.edu/curriculum/tab_courses.cfm?status=course&detail=2259

title: Scope & Methods of Political Science
description: This course is a non-credit required course for first year Ph.D. students in the Department of Government. Students will meet to discuss the norms and goals of the discipline and to hear from a cross-section of political scientists at Georgetown. Students will be required to attend academic talks and write very brief response pieces.

title: Analysis of Political Data
description: This course provides an introduction to statistical research methods. The course has two important goals: 1) to understand work that uses empirical research methods; and 2) to provide the skills necessary to analyze data. No previous experience in statistics is assumed. [American Government]

title: Advanced Political Analysis
description: Advanced Political Analysis continues the study of quantitative methods begun in 701 Analysis of Political Data. Topics covered include violations of Gauss-Markov assumptions, GLS and other extensions of the linear model, causal inference, and maximum likelihood estimation. Students will complete a quantitative paper on a subject and with a dataset of their own choosing.

title: Qualitative Methods
description: This course is a seminar for PhD students in political science and related fields on the design of case study and qualitative research projects. It covers the philosophy of science of qualitative methods as well as specific methods such as process tracing, comparison, typological theorizing, and multimethod research. Students will present their own research designs for constructive critique at the end of the course. Depending on the space available, Georgetown MA students and PhD students from area universities are welcome to join the course. Permission needed from instructor.

title: Game Theory
description: In the last few decades game theory has become one of the most powerful analytical tools in the study of politics. In this approach, human interactions are analyzed in situations in which individual decisions depend on expectations as to what others will do. Game theoretical models have been applied to the analysis of international relations, collective action, leadership, voting and elections, negotiations and conflict settlements, coalition formation and other relevant political dilemmas. This course will: 1) provide a set of tools of the theory of games of strategy with analytical value, 2) develop some relevant applications in the field of political science and international relations, 3) introduce a number of normative solutions to conflict, competition and negotiation problems. Numerous examples, exercises and applications of the models to the analysis of relevant problems will be presented, required and promoted.

title: Approaches/Topics in American Politics
description: This course serves as the field seminar in American politics. It will introduce you to the basic approaches and research on some key questions in American politics. The course entails a significant amount of reading, mostly journal articles. The course will feature at least one journal article or book chapter by every member of the American politics faculty. In addition, there will be some readings in comparative politics and political theory that help students put readings in context. Students are expected to complete the readings before class and come prepared to discuss them.

title: Research in American Politics
description: to be added

title: Methodology of Comparative Politics
description: The purpose of methods is to help us generate persuasive answers to questions that matter about the world. This course will provide an overview of research methods used in one subfield of the discipline: comparative politics. The intent of the course is to explore why methods matter, to familiarize students with a range of comparative methods, and to explore how they are used in the design and practice of comparative research. We also intend to review current debates about the use of methods in comparative politics, and to discuss the limits of comparison.

title: Civil Society and Democracy
description: The purpose of the course is to introduce students to a variety of topics, theories, and approaches related to the study of civil society and democracy. The course will not focus on any one particular world region; rather, we will cover themes that can be applied to a variety of different countries and regionsâincluding the United Statesâas well as to the fields of political theory and international relations. Each class session, we will be addressing a different theme, and we will generally read and discuss multiple works within that theme. Although I have sometimes assigned books in their entirety, it is essential that you learn to âreadâ them in a way that allows you to understand the main argument, structure, and context of the book, without necessarily reading every word. Overall, the course is intended to provide students with a broad and comprehensive foundation in this important area of political science.

title: Ph.D. Sminar in Fundamentals of International Relations
description: This course provides a graduate-level survey of the fundamental literature, concepts and controversies in the contemporary study of international relations, and it seeks to establish a foundation for more advanced study, research and understanding. While no one course can offer a comprehensive treatment of all the relevant literature and paradigms, Government 760 encompasses a body of knowledge broadly familiar to analysts and scholars. It prepares students for comprehensive exams, but more importantly, it prepares them for what these exams represent: the ability to participate in the scholarly discourse on international relations at a high professional level. After this course, students should be able to read any article in a leading international relations journal or attend any panel presentation at an international relations conference and understand and critique the arguments being put forth.

title: IR Theory & Approaches
description: Government 761 is an advanced class in the understanding and production of international-relations theory. This course has two primary objectives. First, to enhance studentsâ understanding of aspects of international-relations theory and contemporary debates in the academic study of international relations. Second, to help prepare students to be producers of international-relations scholarship by focusing their attention on issues in epistemology, methodology, and research design beyond those addressed in their standard allotment of methods and introductory courses.

title: International Political Economy
description: Economics is the study of the production, distribution, and consumption of scarce resources. Political economy considers the role that the state plays in such production, distribution, and consumption.International Political Economy (IPE) considers the flows of such production, distribution, and consumption across national borders, recognizing that not just national governments play a role, but foreign governments and international institutions must also be taken into account. For more details on this course, please visit: //www9.georgetown.edu/faculty/jrv24/govt762.html

title: Ph.D. Seminar in International Security
description: This course surveys the major debates in security studies. Scholars of security studies focus on a range of topics involving conflict and the use of force. Among the issues we examine in this seminar are coercion, the security dilemma, insurgency and counterinsurgency, terrorism, and the causes of war.

title: Seminar: International Legal Philosophy
description: Course will examine philosophical approaches to international law. It will explore major jurisprudential schools of thought, including natural law, positivism, critical legal studies, Lasswell-McDougal, regime analysis, feminist approaches, and non-western approaches. It will also examine several of the most important issues of international legal theory. [International Relations]

title: Ph.D. Seminar in Substate Vioilence
description: International relations and security studies focus heavily on wars between and by states, but since the end of World War II violence within states and by non-state actors has been far more lethal. The ongoing civil war in Congo has killed more than four million people, and the violence is far from over. The United States faces a bitter insurgency in Afghanistan, which is bound up in the broader U.S. struggle against terrorism, commonly epitomized by the 9/11 attacks. If anything, this discrepancy has grown dramatically since the end of the Cold War. Currently there are dozens of ongoing civil wars and insurgencies, as well as dozens more active terrorist groups. Interstate wars, in contrast, are few and far between. Unfortunately, many students of international relations have at best a rudimentary understanding of these types of struggles. This course is intended in part as a field seminar for students wishing to pursue further work on substate violence. As such, it is intended to expose students to a broad array of theoretical concepts and models. Each class will also go into empirics, but the history and current state of various civil wars and terrorist groups is not the focus of this class. The reading list is dominated by articles published in the fieldâs top journals. A focus on articles allows us to engage my theoretical ideas, though of course it often means that our engagement is more superficial than if we read a longer book. I have sought to minimize overlap with other Government Department courses, particularly GOV 760, International Security.

title: State Formation
description: TBA

title: Fundamentals of Political Theory
description: The purpose of this course is to provide first or second year graduate students with a strong grounding in some of the more important texts that helped to construct the tradition of Western political philosophy. We shall be not so much following its history as studying different formal and substantive views of the enterprise -- what it tries to achieve and how it works. The texts considered will be, in order: Aristotleâs Politics, The Prince and Discourses on Livy, Hobbesâs Leviathan and Platoâs Republic.

title: Political Theory Methods Seminar
description: This course is for doctoral students majoring in political theory. It is a required part of the course of study for all theory students who are candidates for the doctorate in the Department of Government. The focus of the course is on ânormativeâ political theory, and it has two purposes. One is to acquaint students with the recent history of the field as a specialization within the academic discipline of political science, including past and current debates about the identity and prospects of political theory. The other is to enable students to become methodologically self-conscious by acquainting them with the theoretical strategies and methods commonly employed today by scholars working in the field as well as the philosophical issues raised by those strategies and methods. [Political Theory]

title: Liberallism, Capabilities, and Citizenship
description: This course in political theory examines some of the central theoretical tenets of liberalism. We will be particularly concerned with liberalismâs expectations about the moral qualifications for citizenship. The course will trace the evolution of these assumptionsâboth in terms of minimum qualifications and ideals of the âgood liberal citizenââover time from classical to modern to contemporary liberalism. Readings will include Thomas Hobbes, John Locke, Adam Smith, J.S. Mill, John Rawls, Judith Shklar, and Ralph Ellison. We will also explore how these assumptions of canonical liberal political philosophy may relate to current debates over citizenship, immigration and naturalization, education, and poverty.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Greek I
description: This course is designed for students with little or no previous background in Greek. Intensive instruction in phonology and oral communication, the fundamentals of grammar and the development of basic vocabulary are emphasized, along with an audiovisual introduction to Greek culture.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Greek I
description: This course is designed for students with little or no previous background in Greek. Intensive instruction in phonology and oral communication, the fundamentals of grammar and the development of basic vocabulary are emphasized, along with an audiovisual introduction to Greek culture.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Greek II
description: Students with previous background are normally placed in -012 for an intensive review of fundamental structures and vocabulary and development of all skills.

title: Intensive First Level Modern Greek II
description: Students with previous background are normally placed in -012 for an intensive review of fundamental structures and vocabulary and development of all skills.

title: Intensive Second Level Modern Greek I
description: This course is designed to develop studentsâ ability to communicate in Modern Greek and to help them acquire the skills necessary to produce oral and written expression. Grammar, structure, vocabulary, listening and reading comprehension are included in the teaching. Aspect of Greek culture will be introduced in reading passages. More specifically, the general objective is the students to be able to use the language in order to express feelings and thoughts, to describe ordinary situations, to exchange information, to communicate for a significant number of every day events, to understand the main cultural features of the Greek people.

title: Intensive Second Level Modern Greek I
description: This course is designed to develop studentsâ ability to communicate in Modern Greek and to help them acquire the skills necessary to produce oral and written expression. Grammar, structure, vocabulary, listening and reading comprehension are included in the teaching. Aspects of Greek culture will be introduced in reading passages. More specifically, the general objective is for students to be able to use the language in order to express feelings and thoughts, to describe ordinary situations, to exchange information, to communicate about everyday events, and to understand the present-day Greek culture.

title: Intensive Second Level Modern Greek II
description: Prerequisite: -011, -012 or the equivalent.

title: Intensive Second Level Modern Greek II
description: Prerequisite: GREE 111 or the equivalent.

title: Intensive Third Level Modern Greek I
description: This course focuses on mastering all requisite language skills while integrating some aspects of Greek culture and civilization in fun and interactive ways! Emphasis is put on expanding the vocabulary and developing conversational skills to prepare the student to enjoy discussions on a variety of topics with accuracy and fluency. Class instruction is supplemented by weekly grammar review and conversation sessions.

title: Intensive Third Level Modern Greek I
description: This course focuses on mastering all requisite language skills while integrating some aspects of Greek culture and civilization in fun and interactive ways. Emphasis is put on expanding the vocabulary and developing conversational skills to prepare the student to enjoy discussions on a variety of topics with accuracy and fluency. Class instruction is supplemented by weekly grammar review and conversation sessions.

title: Intensive Third Level Modern Greek II
description: Prerequisite: -111, -112 or the equivalent.

title: Intensive Third Level Modern Greek II
description: Prerequisite: GREE 211 or the equivalent.

title: Tutorial: Greek
description: Independent Tutorial (contact Prof. Lamb to arrange)

title: Radiation Science
description: This course covers the principles of radiation science which are relevant to health physics and nuclear nonproliferation. Lectures will include atomic structure, nuclear structure, radioactivity, interactions of radiations with matter, production of X rays, sources of radiation, radiation quantities and radiation dosimetry.

title: Radiation Biology
description: The focus is on the interactions of ionizing radiations with living matter, including in-depth discussions of biochemical, cellular, systemic, and population events leading to detectable radiation injury. This course is taught every other year. It will be offered Spring 2012

title: Cancer Risk Assessment
description: This course covers the scientific principles underlying the assessment and quantification of the cancer risks posed by environmental agents. The focus is on ionizing radiation risks and how how they compare with risks from chemical carcinogens. The various methodologies for conducting cellular, animal, and human risk studies are explored, and issues such as the weight of evidence and level of uncertainty are discussed. The role of risk assessment in risk communication, public policy, and the regulatory process, is reviewed. There are also case studies of environmental breast cancer, electromagnetic fields, and radon in homes. Prerequisites: RASC-500 or RASC-501 recommended but not mandatory COURSE REQUIREMENTS: A mid-term and final exam, as well as a short classroom presentation on a science/ policy topic will we required. Successful completion of optional problem sets will earn extra credit.

title: Molecular Basis of Carcinogenesis
description: In-depth discussion of fundamental and advanced understanding of the mechanisms of carcinogenesis. Various biological, chemical and physical carcinogens will be discussed. Lecture topics also include damage-responsive signal transduction, molecular biology of carcinogen-induced programmed cell death, repair mechanisms, tumor suppression, oncogenes, and molecular diagnosis of inherited disorders.

title: Introduction to Nuclear Nonproliferation
description: This is an introductory course and will cover a wide area of nuclear nonproliferation topics, including: Nuclear fission, nuclear weapon types, the nuclear fuel cycle, nuclear reactors, the history of nuclear nonproliferation, legal issues, international safeguards, the Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty (NPT), nations of proliferation concerns and current nonproliferation regime. Some of these topics will be covered in depth in the other more advanced courses.

title: Environmental Health Physics
description: The main focus of this course is on environmental radioactivity and radiation protection. This course will prepare the students to deal with issues associated with the environmental radiation background, radiation accidents, environmental restoration and clean-up.

title: Radiation Detection
description: In-depth discussion of the various aspects of radiation detection, including: GM counters, scintillation counters, solid state detectors, gamma ray spectroscopy, neutron detectors and thermoluminescence dosimetry (TLD).

title: Nuclear Weapons Detection
description: This course will cover the properties of nuclear and radiological weapons, scenarios for their acquisition and transport, and detection methods and technologies.
