This course meets the general education requirement in History for College students when taken in combination with an EARLY introductory-level history course (HIST 007; before Fall 2010, this course was known as HIST 001, 003, or 033).

title: Middle East I
description: The course examines the principal social, cultural, economic and political features of the Muslim world from the late sixth to the early sixteenth centuries. It focuses on the geo-strategic and cultural conditions that attended the rise of the new monotheistic faith of Islam; the formation and evolution of classical and medieval Muslim institutions; the local diversities within the unifying systems of Muslim beliefs, law, and administration; the material and intellectual exchanges and interactions between the Muslim world and non-Muslim communities and polities; and Muslim reactions to the Crusades and the Mongol invasions of the eleventh-thirteenth centuries. This course meets the general education requirement in History for College students when taken in combination with a LATE introductory-level history course (HIST 008; before Fall 2010, this course was known as HIST 002, 006, or 034).

title: Middle East II
description: The course outlines the factors that have shaped the political and social features of the modern Middle East from 1500 to the present. Its geographic scope comprises the central provinces and territories of the former Ottoman and Safavid empires: Turkey, Iraq, Syria, Palestine, Egypt, Arabia, and Iran. The syllabus emphasizes three analytical themes: first, the historical evolution of "Middle Eastern" polities from dynastic and religious empires in the 16th century to modern "nation-states" in the 20th; second, the impact of industrial capitalism and European imperial expansion on local societies and their modes of production; and third, the socio-cultural and ideological dimensions of these large-scale transformations, specifically the rise of mass ideologies of liberation and development (nationalism, socialism, rights movements, political Islam), and the emergence of structural and social imbalances (economic polarization, cultural/ethnic conflicts, demographic growth, urbanization). This course meets the general education requirement in History for College students when taken in combination with an EARLY introductory-level history course (HIST 007; before Fall 2010, this course was known as HIST 001, 003, or 033).

title: History of Russia I
description: The Slavs, Origins of Russia, Kiev, the Mongol period, Muscovy, Imperial Russia to 1825 with special attention to autocracy, serfdom, foreign policy, the Orthodox Church, Westernization, society, culture, and the birth of the revolutionary movement. Fall.

title: History of Russia II
description: The Old Regime: society, industrialization, revolution, war. The Bolshevik Revolution. The Great Transformation. World communism, World War II, and the Stalinist Empire. Post-Stalinist politics and society. The Gorbachev revolution. Spring.

title: History of East Central Europe I
description: From the Medieval Kingdoms to 1918. Development of the East European states. Formation of the multinational empires of the Habsburgs and the Jagiellons. Expansion of the Ottomans and Romanovs. Influence of the Enlightenment, Romanticism, and Liberalism. Social revolutions and national uprisings. The rise of nineteenth century nationalism. World War I and the rebirth of the national states. Fall.

title: East Central European History II
description: 1918-Present. The euphoria of independence. Parliamentarism and democracy. Attempts at industrialization. Decline of democracy and resurgence of traditional conservatism and native fascism. The cauldron of World War II. The fate of the Jews. Sovietization. Titoism. Socialist society in Eastern Europe. The unraveling of Communism.

title: United States History to 1865
description: This course explores the history of North America from the arrival of the first Europeans in the Caribbean to the conclusion of the American Civil War. Focusing on the colonies that became part of the United States, this course explores the dynamics of imperial rivalry, relations between European, African and Amerindian peoples, economic development and regional differentiation, the emergence of revolutionary nationalism, the westward expansion of the United States, the collapse of the Union into civil war, and emancipation. We will read extensively from primary sources.

title: The U.S. since the Civil War
description: This course traces a few themes in American history from the end of the Civil War through somewhere close to the present day. We will discuss the history of American politics, economy, society, and culture. It is designed as an introductory course, so no prerequisite knowledge about American history (except in the broadest sense) is required. However, historians frequently complain that there is too much history for the brief time we are allotted in each semester, and this course is no exception. Thus, in an attempt to give the course focus, we will be paying special attention to the word âAmerican.â Who did it apply to? Who did it not? What characteristics qualified a person or an idea for the label? What did Americans believe made their community and nation unique? These are, of course, still relevant questions today. I hope that you will leave this course with a greater understanding of your own experience in the United States. As in all (good) history courses, this course will not merely present students with timelines to memorize and famous people to identify. Rather, history courses should primarily teach you a way of thinking, and this one will present you with information in lecture and readings, and encourage you to analyze that information in discussions and written assignments. After your time in this course, you will hopefully be equipped to examine evidence critically, to place claims about American history in context, and to understand the ways in which your world is the product of the past.

title: The atomic age
description: The Atomic Age refers to the era of human history that began with the detonation of the first atomic bomb weapon in 1945 and is still with us today. Early on the era shaped a generation trained in the art of civil defense, spawned a culture now regarded as kitsch, and reconfigured the globe according to who possessed nuclear weapons, nuclear know-how, nuclear materials, nuclear energy, and nuclear radiation. The course will begin with the Manhattan Project and the detonation of the first atomic weapons over Japan. It will then turn to the multifarious dimensions of the culture of the Atomic Age. Students should not take both this course and STIA 427: you cannot get credit for both HIST 209 and STIA 427.

title: Health and disease in African history
description: This course has two goalsâto introduce students to the historical study of disease and medicine and to examine African cases within global and regional patterns. The premise of this course is that health and disease in Africa lie at the intersection of social, political, economic, biological, and cultural issues. While disease is often understood as a fact of life, and, with regard to Africa, an urgent challenge, it has a history that is shaped by realities and representations. The course will provide broad historical overview and delve into regional and local case studies covering topics that include: environmental history and disease patterns, the social basis of health, epidemics and upheaval, traditional medicine, gender and sexuality, race, science, colonialism, and postcolonial development, moving historically to contemporary situations on the continent. The course is not confined to biological models of disease and health, and students will learn about multidisciplinary approaches to sources, including demography, documentary analysis, visual images and art, and oral histories, to understand health and disease as important factors in African and global dynamics.

title: Religion and politics in South Asia
description: Virtually every religion in the world is represented in South Asia, a region of immense regional and cultural diversity. The rise of religious identity politics over the course of South Asiaâs modern history has become one of the central issues of historiographical debate amongst scholars of this complex region. Focusing on the key themes of secularism, colonialism, liberalism, nationalism, and communalism, this course will highlight several issues. After a brief introductory survey of pre-colonial and early modern India regarding the encounters between peoples of various religious communities, we will delve into examining the historical legacies of the colonial era, and the period of decolonization. Issues such as the rise of the colonial state and shifts in sovereignty, the remaking of social categories (caste, religious community, gender), the codification of religious laws, social reform, revivalist movements, as well as regional politics, and the Partition of India and Pakistan, will be investigated from historical and anthropological perspectives. We will analyze what was at stake in formulations of âtraditionâ and âmodernityâ. Texts and readings will draw upon recent secondary research as well as historical and literary primary source materials as well as films.

title: Europe from the Fall of Rome to the Millennium
description: After the fall of the Roman Empire, three main cultural elements--classical, Christian and barbarian--blended to form a distinctive European civilization, in the face of recurring invasions and economic stagnation. In other words, early medieval Europe started out multicultural. This course will study how these elements influenced and were influenced by each other in the process of producing an entirely new cultural synthesis by the year 1000. The class emphasizes active learning. Along with traditional lectures, there will be both discussions and structured exercises, with a focus on analyzing primary sources in their historical context. Fall.

title: Europe from the Millennium to the Black Death
description: In the period between 1000 and 1450, Europe was transformed from a provincial backwater into one of the most dynamic regions of the world. This course will explore how this transformation took place. It will provide a survey of the second half of the Middle Ages, concentrating on the political, economic, social, ecclesiastical, artistic and intellectual developments of the period. We will examine how some of the most important institutions of western civilization--representative assemblies, universities, and the nation-state, to cite a few examples--developed in this period. Classes will contain a mixture of lecture, discussion, and structured exercises (such as debates and re-creations of historical events), with a focus on analyzing primary sources in their historical context. Spring.

title: History and legend in medieval Britain
description: Focuses on contrasting modern views of British medieval history with medieval perceptions. The historical evidence and the legends for figures such as Arthur, King Alfred, Thomas Becket, Robin Hood, and Richard II are compared.

title: The Vikings
description: The ravages of the Northmen throughout Europe and beyond have been an area of fascination and of historical interest for centuries. Yet few students understand or are aware of the actual history of the people that had such a tremendous influence. This course will attempt to remedy that gap while at the same time offer a deeper understanding of the Vikings themselves. Throughout the semester we will follow a multi-disciplinary approach to the history of the Vikings in the âHeroic Age.â We will survey the history of Scandinavia and examine the history of the Northmen within both the European and world context. As such we will look at Norse activity in continental Scandinavia, in Western and Eastern Europe, the North Atlantic and beyond examining the many ways in which the Vikings interacted with foreign peoples - as merchants, conquerors, pilgrims, colonists, mercenaries, and as pirates. The course will move thematically and chronologically from c. 600 CE to c. 1200 CE examining a range of topics. We will discuss the legendary history of early Scandinavia and the consolidation of the Scandinavian kingdoms. We will attempt to recreate the early Norse world view including the creation of the world and other aspects of the Viking religious experience. Students will be introduced to a wide variety of Norse Gods and heroes and understand how the mythological and cosmological lore of the region helped shape an ethos of the North. The course will also examine the great period of the Viking expansion which in many ways shaped the history of Europe. We will look at, among other things, the Danish invasion of England, the settlement of the Normans in Northern France, the Rus and the origins of Russia as well as the discovery of America around the year 1000. While the course does move chronologically during this period, we will also look at a wide variety of historical themes including the development of Europeâs first parliamentary government (the Thing); the arrival of Christianity in the North; the legal status of feuding; and the role of women in Viking society. Within this historical framework, a good deal of attention is devoted to Viking art and archaeology, to the pagan religion of early medieval Scandinavia, and to its system of writing (the celebrated runes) and its literature (including the mythological and heroic poetry of the Edda, the court poems of the skalds, and the Icelandic sagas). Finally, the course will address the modern ramifications of the Viking Age as well as the depiction of the Vikings in modern art, music and film.

title: Age of Revolution: Europe 1789-1848
description: Many historians have said that we have been living in a revolutionary age for well over two centuries now, and certainly the world is still experiencing revolutions today. One has only to think of âthe Arab Spring.â What people may not realize is that the concept of political revolution was born in Europe. The Glorious Revolution of the English in 1689 was the start of modern revolutions, but there was no revolution in Europe again until the big one of 1789-1799 in France. Since that time, something was triggered and revolutions have been erupting on a regular basis; ârevolutionâ became the ultimate political tool for oppressed and deprived peoples. Indeed, in Europe from 1816 to 1849, there was constant political unrest, demonstrations, revolts, and revolutions â virtually every year -- until the apex of that wave arrived in 1848 and persisted until more than a year later in many places. This tumultuous period was where so many of the ideologies and political strategies developed, and where the concept of a âprofessional revolutionaryâ was born. This course will explore and analyze the varying, yet often general causes of this discontent, the aspirations of âthe peopleâ and their leaders, and the political, economic and cultural theories and movements that fueled them. Our quest will be to understand why ârevolutionâ came to be seen as âthe solution,â and has continued to inspire suffering people world-wide until this very day. This is a lecture class which will also include in-class discussion of primary source documents and some books. There will be a mid-term and final examination, two shorter papers, and a longer paper of roughly twelve to fifteen pages.

title: Europe since 1945
description: Based on weekly lectures and discussion classes, this course will provide a wide-ranging examination of the political, cultural, and social history of Europe in the decades after the end of the Second World War. Rather than offering a neat chronological survey of major events, the course will investigate European history after 1945 through a number of themes. The topics covered include, but are not limited to, the multifaceted legacies of war; reconstruction on both sides of the Iron Curtain; the establishment of Socialist regimes in Eastern Europe; the quest to establish new identities; memories of war and fascism; popular culture and the impact of consumerism; gender, the family, sexuality and private life; âAmericanizationâ and âSovietizationâ; youth culture, protest and political violence.

title: The age of Dante
description: The "Age of Dante" is primarily a readings and discussion course, with some lectures. The central text for the course is Dante's encyclopedic Divine Comedy, which anchors discussions of Dante's other works and his literary, political, ecclesiastical, and philosophical context. At thematically appropriate moments the course discusses Danteâs earlier works, his use of scripture and of classical texts, the writings of his poetic forebears and contemporaries, and contemporary chroniclers who describe the turbulence of late thirteenth- century Florence. Some of the central themes in the course will be Dante's love of Beatrice, his love/hate relationship with the city of Florence, the impact of his exile, his views on the papacy and the empire, his radical re-envisioning of the Christian afterlife, and the choice of personages he encounters on his journey through hell, purgatory and paradise, particularly his strange choice of a pagan guide (Virgil) through hell and purgatory. The course will also discuss the impact of Dante's work, as well as scholarly responses to it.

title: The Crusades
description: This course will look at the history of the Crusading movement from its ideological beginnings, through the fervor of the First Crusade and through the many waves of crusades that followed (some of them numbered and some not!). While tracing the rise and fall of the movement, the course focuses on the role of the western church, the colonizing activities of Franks in the Holy Land, the crusading orders, and the popular crusading responses and legends and then growing disillusionment with crusades. The purpose of this course will be to tie together the western European experience with that of the eastern Mediterranean (Byzantines, other eastern Christians, Muslims, and âFranksâ who settled in the East). We will be looking particularly at the spiritual, ecclesiastical and popular culture of the crusades, contextualized within the currents of east and west. There will be some reading in primary sources and a concern to examine the motivations for crusading, the impact of the crusades on western culture and society, and the cultural clashes and accommodations between the Latin West and the Islamic East. Since the memory of the medieval crusades remains a touchstone for the modern discourse regarding the Middle East, we will also look at modern understanding of the crusades. This course will help you to understand the importance of this historically defining movement, which, in the minds of some, is still an ongoing concern.

title: History of international Law
description: This course aims to provide an overview of the different legal regimes or paradigms that have existed in western international relations since the "Age of Discovery" and to examine when, how, and why changes occurred in international legal thinking and practice.

title: Labor and civil liberties
description: This course explores the interplay between labor and working-class movements and the development of modern civil liberties from the Civil War through Citizens United. Weâll study the rise of the private detective agency and J. Edgar Hooverâs FBI and consider how anarchist bombers and African American boycotters challenged Americans to grapple with liberty and its limits. Readings and assignments will involve analyzing both primary and secondary sources, and class participation in discussion is required.

title: American Diplomatic History I
description: This survey of the history of American foreign relations follows the development of diplomacy in its international and domestic contexts from the colonial era to the aftermath of World War II. Topics covered include: the problems of organizing a new nation, expansion in North America and beyond, the impact of racism, war, and revolution, the rise to world power, as well as consideration of the economic, political, and social imperatives behind foreign policy making. Fall.

title: American Diplomatic History II
description: This survey of the history of American foreign relations follows the development of diplomacy in its international and domestic contexts from the aftermath of World War I to the present. Topics covered include: the impact of racism, war, and revolution, the United States as a world power, as well as consideration of the economic, political, and social imperatives behind foreign policy making. Spring.

title: History of American Labor Since 1870
description: This course surveys the history of American labor from the nineteenth century to the present. It examines the history of work, unions, and labor relations and connects the story of workers and their struggles to the larger history of the United States from the era of industrialization to globalization.

title: North American Slavery
description: This course traces the origins, development, and demise of chattel slavery in North America. Topics will include the connections between North America and the Atlantic slave trade, the diverse slave societies that emerged in the colonies, the consequences of the American Revolution for slaves and slaveowners, the expansion of slavery in the southern United States, the rise of an organized antislavery movement, the destruction of slavery during the Civil War, and the efforts of freed people to give meaning to their "new birth of freedom" after slavery.

title: Intro to African-American history
description: Intro to African-American History is a survey course in African-American life and culture. Through the close readings of primary documents, we will focus on prominent themes in African-American history. Beginning with the arrival of slaves to the New World, the course will cover slavery, Reconstruction, the Great Migration, the Civil Rights Movement and contemporary African-American politics.

title: Women and the Making of Modern America
description: This course offers a survey and discussion of the history of women in the United States from 1865 to the present. Major themes include the class, race, and regional diversity of women; history of social movements like suffrage and womenâs liberation; history of everyday life; and sexuality and family. The course will be a mix of lecture and discussion, with an equal emphasis on understanding historical arguments and using primary sources. The class will require three papers and two exams.

title: Radical tradition in the US
description: This course offers an interpretive survey of the history of the American left, from the early 19th century to the present. The focus is on the intersection of politics, culture, and society. We begin with the abolitionists, move through labor radicals and Populists, spend time with Socialists, anarchists, and Communists, examine the New Left and the black power movement and radical feminism, and end with a look at Occupy Wall Street. And everyone will sing.

title: Religion, ideology, culture in Black America
description: RELIGION: This class is designed to show how Blacks have used Christianity /Islam and other ideas to forge social progress. We will view the ideas of the founders of the Black Church Richard Allen and Absolom Jones and the Black preacher in the fight against slavery. We look at modern Black Liberation Church leaders like Martin Luther King Jr. We look at Black Catholics. We explore the role of Islam in early America beginning with the bringing of Muslim slaves to America. We look at the origins of the Nation of Islam and work our way to Malcolm X and his movement to orthodox Islam. IDEOLOGY: We look at competing ideologies Christian nationalism and Socialism. CULTURE: We look at the role of music in the struggle for social justice. Starting with slave songs and Negro spirituals we study Marian Anderson, Paul Robeson, the Freedom Singers, during the Civil Rights Days, and finally soul music and Jazz.

title: Independent Study in History
description: Special reading tutorials or independent research projects may be proposed by students; ordinarily, only students with a 3.5 average may request such tutorials. The student who wishes to propose such a tutorial of project must gain the approval and consent of a Faculty mentor within the Department. The tutorial has to be on a topic not usually covered in regularly offered courses, and the student must demonstrate his/her need for the course. The Faculty mentor will supervise each project which must result in a substantial historical essay. Approval by the departmental Director of Undergraduate Studies is required.

title: Independent Study in History
description: Special reading tutorials or independent research projects may be proposed by students; ordinarily, only students with a 3.5 average may request such tutorials. The student who wishes to propose such a tutorial of project must gain the approval and consent of a Faculty mentor within the Department. The tutorial has to be on a topic not usually covered in regularly offered courses, and the student must demonstrate his/her need for the course. The Faculty mentor will supervise each project which must result in a substantial historical essay. Approval by the departmental Director of Undergraduate Studies is required.

title: Global History of Revolutions
description: This upper-level seminar will explore in a comparative fashion modern, global revolutions since the English Civil War in the mid seventeenth century, in part to respond to the spellbound interest of students in the current Arab Spring and to furnish a solid theoretical and historical context in which to understand current events.

title: Topics in world history: Europeans and the Tropics
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of world history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will receive its own sub-title. The following course under this number is planned for Spring 2013. European and the Tropics: This course surveys the European encounter with the global tropics. Since the middle of the fifteenth century, Europeans have ventured to the band of earth between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn in search of wealth, markets, labor, and souls. In the process they built empires in the Caribbean, Latin America, Africa, India, and Southeast Asia that affected the lives and cultures of millions of people. By studying the economic, cultural, environmental, and scientific consequences of the interaction between European empires and the people who lived in these regions, students will consider the significance of the global tropics to modern world history. The class will consult primary and secondary sources ranging from ancient geographical theories to the modern tourism industry, tropical medicine to the consumption of tropical commodities, the rise of empires to the elaboration of racial and gendered discourses. Ultimately this course will challenge students to think about the following questions: What are the tropics? Do they differ from other parts of the world, and how? How have these regions contributed to economies and cultures throughout the world? How and why have they figured as both paradise and hell on earth for some five hundred years? History majors will be able to place this course in several world regions, depending on their written work.

title: International Perspectives and Global History
description: Processes of historical change have become increasingly global during recent centuries. This colloquium explores diverse approaches to historical globalization: political, diplomatic, economic, ecological, cultural. In addition, it examines the relations between globalizing processes and history as it is experienced, discussed, and debated in nations and communities. It asks why historical understandings have focused on national developments, while the forces of change have operated on ever larger scales. This course is targeted primarily at SFS juniors and will be taught on a rotation by different faculty members.

title: Witches and Witchcraft, 1400-1750
description: What is a witch? This seminar first investigates the phenomenon of witchcraft in Europe before exploring the transmission of witch beliefs across the Atlantic to America, focusing especially on the outbreak at Salem. It also explores the variety of witch beliefs held by Africans and Native Americans in the Atlantic world and the impact of colonization and cultural contact on the expression and meaning of those beliefs. History majors can apply this course to whichever region best reflects their work in the seminar.

title: City life in Africa
description: In 1960, some African countries classified a city as any location with just a 2,500 people. Within 50 years, some of these same countries have half of their populations living in cities. What are the reasons for rapid urbanization? What are the consequencesâfor food production, health, work, and politics? These are just of the questions we will explore, going back into time to examine the rise of the first cities in Africa and moving to the present, as African cities are lively hubs of great music, rough and tumble outdoor markets, youth gangs, prostitutes, and prim security-fenced housing estates. Main themes we will consider are: ancient cities and global exchange, consumption of luxury commodities, rising inequality, food availability, water and sanitation, urban-rural relationships, gender relations in changing social norms, ghettos and segregation, and cultures of resistance.

title: China from Liberation to the Olympics
description: The Peopleâs Republic of China (PRC) was established in 1949 after a century of foreign humiliation, subjugation, and internal turmoil. Today, the PRC is poised to emerge as one of the most dominant and vibrant players in the 21st century world. The intervening six decades of Chinese history is a story of epic proportions. In 1949, Mao Zedong, leader of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), put the world on notice that âthe Chinese people, comprising one-quarter of humanity, have now stood up.â That same year, he reminded the Chinese people that the end of the Chinese civil war was âonly the first step in a long march of ten thousand liâ¦The Chinese revolution is great, but the road after the revolution will be longer, the work greater and more arduous.â This purpose of this seminar is to trace that journey. As such, this course will survey the major domestic and international trends, policies, events, and individuals that played a role in shaping the PRCâs path to the present. The format of the course will be discussion. Each week, the class will begin with a general review of the historical context of the period being examined, followed by a student-led discussion of the weekâs assigned readings.

title: Cold war in East Asia
description: Across the world, the end of the Second World War brought transformative challenges and opportunities. Soon, this new reality was distorted significantly by the forces of a new international conflict - the Cold War. Global in its reach, the Cold War spanned nearly fives decades and stands as one of the defining periods of the post-WWII world. Indeed, as David Painter has noted, âUnderstanding the Cold War is central to understanding the history of the second half of the twentieth centuryâ (Painter, The Cold War, 1). Although centered in Europe, the effects of the Cold War nonetheless were felt globally. East Asia was no exception. Indeed, the impact of the struggle between Washington, Moscow, and their respective allies was felt almost immediately in the region after the conclusion of the Pacific War. As the Cold Warâs intensity increased, and particularly as it increasingly became entangled with regional and local aspirations and tensions, the Cold War in East Asia came to be viewed by Washington as crucial, so much so that this part of the world recently was referred to as the âsecond front of the Cold Warâ (Tsuyoshi Hasegawa, ed. The Cold War in East Asia, 1). As a result, the period of 1945-1991 served as a defining era â diplomatically, politically, socially, culturally, and economically â not only in US history but also in the stories of the nations and peoples of East Asia. And though the Cold War ended some two decades ago, the legacies of that era continue in many ways. While this course will consider the East Asian experience from a multi-lateral perspective, its primary focus will be the ways US global and regional perceptions, interests, and actions during the Cold War attempted to shape, and were shaped by, Asian realities. Given the depth and breadth of actors and issues involved, this seminar will not attempt to deal with the entirety of US policies toward and actions in East Asia. Rather it will focus on pivotal/defining moments in that history. Topics to be examined during the semester include the Occupation of Japan, the Chinese Civil War, decolonization and national liberation movements, US-China relations, the Korean and Vietnam Wars, and the legacy of the Cold War in Asia. The format of the course will be discussion. Each week, the class will begin with a general review of the historical context of the issue and period being examined, followed by a student-led discussion of the assigned readings.

title: Martin Luther: Devil or Saint?
description: Martin Luther: Devil or Saint? âLuther is a child of the devil, possessed by the devil, full of falsehood and vainglory.â Johann Cochleus. âFor he [Luther] was without doubt the angel concerning whom it is written in Revelation 14, who flew through the midst of heaven and had an eternal Gospel.â Johann Bugenhagen These two sixteenth-century descriptions of the Protestant Reformer Martin Luther attest to his controversial stature during and after the German Reformation. This seminar will study Luther and his critics, paying particular attention to his more polemical writings on Jews, women, Catholics, Radicals, and the Peasants. Readings will include a mix of primary and secondary sources, covering the history, theology, politics, and culture of the sixteenth century.

title: Welcome to Paris!
description: Paris -- âthe city of light.â Paris â âthe city of love.â Paris â âthe hub of Franceâ. How could anyone who has visited the city (or heard a great deal about it) not be moved by these images of Paris, or, for that matter, by Cole Porterâs âI Love Paris,â especially when sung by Maurice Chevalier? Yet none of these images or sentiments would have been articulated in the early centuries of Western civilization. Paris, as we know it today, was created only in the 19th and 20th centuries This course will examine the evolution of Paris from a crowded and dirty old medieval city into an elegant and modern city, one that became a political, economic and cultural powerhouse by the 19th century. We will start with Paris during the period of the French Revolution and will follow seminal developments up to the post-World War II period, if not beyond. Readings will include both primary and secondary sources, all of which will help illuminate a widely diverse set of topics â the demographics and topography of Paris; political, economic and social realities; disease; prostitution; art, music, architecture; urban reform efforts; the appearance and development of new forms of commercial and social life such as department stores, cabarets, and cafÃ©s; and so forth. We will strive, as much as possible, to come to know Paris as Parisians of the past knew it. And, on the first day of the course, we will all adopt first names that Parisians would recognize as supremely French. There will be no exams in this course. The final grade will be based on participation in the twice-weekly discussions on the assigned readings, four to six 300-word reflection papers on certain of the course readings, one or two short class presentations by each student over the course of the semester, and a research paper of 12-15 pages.

title: Christians and the Holocaust
description: This course addresses the reactions of European Christians to the Nazi persecution and killing of Jews. The course starts with an overview of the history of antisemitism throughout the centuries, and then moves to the core of the course: an exploration of the specific attitudes and actions of both Catholics and Protestants in the inter-war and World War II period. In the section on Catholics and the Catholic Church, two to three weeks is spent examining the issue of the papacy and the Jews, concentrating especially on the controversy over Pius XII. Students will read some of the most important literature by the detractors and by the supporters of Pius XII so as to understand the chief arguments and the evidence offered by the two sides. Following that, the course considers the role of the Catholic clergy, the religious orders, and the laity, with particular attention to the efforts of a distinguished Georgetown professor, the late Dr. Jan Karski, sometimes known as "the man who tried to stop the Holocaust." The section on Protestants will survey the actions of the various denominations, certain key pastors, and rescue operations on the part of brave Protestant families or individuals. The course will conclude with a short survey of post-WW II efforts to clarify the historical record and improve Jewish-Christian relations. The course is being offered in the two and a half hour format this Spring because the once-a-week, longer class will better accommodate the handful of excellent documentary and re-enacted films the professor has to enrich the class.

title: Eternal city: a history of Rome
description: This seminar class focuses on the history of the city of Rome, from its foundation in ancient times through its contemporary role as the capital of Italy. Each week we will focus on a different period, and examine the history of the city, in terms of both the life of its population and the development of city buildings, neighborhoods, and structures. We will discuss political, economic, social, cultural, religious, intellectual, and other changes, with a special focus on the architecture and urban structures of the physical city itself. Though the history of the city and its people will be our main focus, we will also discuss the image of Rome, the perception of the city by outsiders, its broader role in European and western culture, and the legacy of its history as the seat of antiquityâs greatest empire, the main center of western Christianity and of global Catholicism, and the capital of a modern European nation state. Please note however that this class will not offer an overall history of antiquity, of the papacy, or of the Christian church in general (or of modern Italy per se). The course aims thus to allow for a close analysis of specific themes and topics and of how they developed throughout a significant span of time. The course also has a methodological aim: to introduce students to the advanced use of primary sources and to further their understanding of historical thinking and analysis. Both class discussion and writing assignments will push students to hone their critical reading, writing, and analytical skills. In particular, we will try to understand how to read textual, visual, and other sources with an awareness of historical context and with attention to the specifics of genre, authorship, and audience. The readings consist of both primary and secondary sources. There will also be student presentations on particular buildings, sites, spaces, or builders, and a variety of writing assignments.

title: Origins of the state in Europe
description: How did todayâs state system come into being? We often use the term âStateâ to refer to any political unit, but the modern State came into being in Europe around 1600. Understanding todayâs state system is much easier if you understand those origins. We have heard much in the last two years about âsovereignâ debt: what does the word âsovereignâ mean? In the Middle Ages, it meant simply âthe highestâ â âsouverainâ [the French word came first in vernacular languages] was the French translation for the Latin, âsuperior.â In 1576, however, a French lawyer, politician, and political theorist, Jean Bodin, coined a neologism, âsovereignty,â to describe the âindivisible, inalienable, and perpetual powerâ vested in the person or persons who had the right to make law âbinding on all in general and each in particular.â We will look at the tension between the political community, what early modern Europeans called the commonwealth, and the state. The theoretical and practical origins of the State have as much to do with regicides, with Ranters (a dissident radical group during the English Civil War/Revolution), and with popular resistance of all kinds, as they do with the construction of state institutions (like the military) and international diplomacy. Weâll be looking at topics like the origins of modern diplomacy, witchcraft, political theory, state-sponsored culture, gender and state building, and popular rebellions. At the end of the class, weâll look at the commonwealth-state dichotomy in contemporary politics (issues like the European Unionâs economic crisis or creating a âtwo-stateâ solution in the Middle East). Readings are a mixture of shared texts and individually assigned books. There are two papers: a 7-page paper, analyzing an assigned source; and a 20-page research paper, on a topic of the studentâs choosing [subject to the professorâs approval].

title: Heresy and authority in the Middle Ages
description: This course is about âfalse beliefsâ in medieval Europe, the individuals and groups who adhered to them, and the authorities who prosecuted them. The course is structured around case studies and primary materials, and its goal is to shed light on legal, institutional, intellectual, and cultural developments in the West: we will begin chronologically with the Athenian condemnation of Socrates and conclude with the Churchâs seventeenth-century condemnation of Galileo. Intervening topics include medieval heresy, civil suppression of heresy, judicial torture, and the Inquisition. Throughout the course, we will be attempting to define and understand historically such western concepts as social consensus, right authority, tradition, dissent, and heterodoxy. We will also be investigating the role that ideological uniformity plays in social cohesion and the extent to which opposition to orthodoxy is not merely to set oneself in opposition to civil and religious authorities, but also to a whole culture. Spring.

title: Collaboration and Resistance in Europe under Hitler and Stalin
description: Using a mixture of film and written sources (memoirs, autobiographical accounts, collections of documents), we will examine the question of collaboration and resistance in the decades around World War II. The classes will be a mixture of films, lecture, and discussion.

title: European Fascism
description: This course will look at the origins, practice, and nature of Fascism as it developed in Europe between the world wars. To that end, readings by Fascist theorists such as Mussolini and Hitler, as well as by historians who analyze and describe the phenomenon will be used. Films from and about the era will also be part of the material for the course. In addition to discussing historical Fascism, the course will also take a look at what, if anything, connects the meaning of Fascism as it existed in the past and how that term is used today.

title: Self and World: Autobiography and Identity in Europe, 1400-1800
description: In this colloquium we will use autobiographies and other documents of a personal nature to examine the development of the conception and analysis of the individual self among Europeans in the fifteenth through eighteenth centuries. We will also use our sources to study the relationships between varied individuals and the societies and cultures around them. The documents will consist of autobiographies, letters, diaries, travel narratives, essays about individuality, and other types of sources. The authors represent both genders and a variety of ethnic groups, ages, social classes, and educational levels. The emphasis will be on close reading and discussion of primary sources. The course aims thus to allow for a close analysis of specific themes and topics and of how they developed throughout a significant span of time. The course also has a methodological aim: to introduce students to the advanced use of primary sources and to further their understanding of historical thinking and analysis. Both class discussion and writing assignments will push students to hone their critical reading, writing, and analytical skills. In particular, we will try to understand how to read sources of a personal nature with an awareness of historical context and with attention to the specifics of genre, authorship, and audience.

title: Topics in Latin American History: Sports in the Americas
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of Latin American history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Fall 2012 the following course is offered: Sports in the Americas. From its origins in the industrial revolution to the current billion-dollar enterprise, modern sport has evolved along with new and reborn political, economic, social, and cultural norms. Sport played a role in supporting imperialism but also in building independence movements; it helped to solidify communist regimes just as strongly as it supported democratic ones. Sport has supported racial segregation even as it has challenged traditional racial understandings and aided healing after national and international trauma. Through international competition and media, sport has become increasingly global, yet at the same time it can be used to emphasize local identities and differences. With all these roles, we see that sport matters. But what can sports tell us about history? If sports have been used in all these contradicting ways, can we say that sports move history? Have sports had a positive or a negative impact on historical processes? This course takes these questions as departure points as we examine case studies of sports in the history of the Americas with a particular focus on Latin America. Note that in this course âAmericaâ refers to the American continent(s) rather than the United States. Sport will serve as both a subject and a lens to explore key historical themes and processes of the late nineteenth century to the present. We will begin chronologically with the origins of sport and industrialization, conflicts with modernization and traditional societies, and the push-pull factors of imperialism before heading into a more thematic approach that touches on events and issues such as international relations, race, gender, political ideologies, immigration, and globalization and violence.

title: Environmental history of pre-Columbian Latin America
description: This course addresses how Pre-Columbian peoples interacted with and perceived the natural environment, using archaeological, paleoenvironmental, and ethnohistorical evidence. By the end of the course, students should have an in-depth familiarity with the history of relevant Pre-Columbian cultures from prehistory to the time of Spanish contact. Primary readings will cover selected cultures of Central and South America, with special emphasis on Central Mexico, the Maya area, and the Andes. Class sessions throughout will focus on different culturesâ sophisticated water management, agricultural practices, and urban planning, as well as the cultural role of the environment shown through representations in writing, art, and architecture. Students will also address case studies through various methods, such as Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and remote sensing. One objective of the course is to evaluate popular and scholarly claims about the legacy of indigenous environmental management. For example, such works as Charles Mannâs 1491 and Jared Diamondâs Collapse have sparked various debates about how Pre-Columbian cultures shaped the New World and the role that the environment or human action played in the so-called âcollapseâ of some civilizations. Throughout, the course will explore current events that pertain to the multiple stakeholders in modern Latin American environmental archaeology, including indigenous peoples, environmental and social scientists, policy makers, and advocates of sustainable development and protection of cultural patrimony.

title: Resistance and Rebellion in the Andean World
description: The course analyzes peasant rebellions and mobilizations in the Andes from the Tupac Amaru/Tupac Katari rebellions in the 1780s to the present. Students will gain an understanding of the literature on peasant rebellions and will apply this knowledge to the Andean region through a combination of lectures and discussion. In addition to papers and exams, all students will do research on a contemporary indigenous movement in the Andes for presentation at the end of the course.

title: Islam in the West
description: The course will examine the formation and growth of the Muslim communities in North America and Europe. It will explore the history and dynamics of community development in a western context, the creation of an Islamic minority perspective, the challenges the community and the host countries face, and the variety of efforts to meet these challenges.

title: Revolutionary Thought in Islam
description: This course examines the history of revolutionary activity in Islam. It will focus on the interaction between the paradigm of the Prophet's example during the formative period of Islam, the Muslim debates on issues of peace, jihad, revolutionary change, government accountability and human responsibility for social, political and economic decay. It will study how these paradigms functioned in a variety of revolutionary movements in Islamic history including the Abbasids, Ikhwan al-Safa, the Fulani Jihad in Sub Saharan Africa, the Mahdi revolution in the Sudan, the Muslim Brotherhood of Egypt, Hamas, Jihad Islami and Tahrir in Palestine and Hisbullah in Lebanon.

title: Piracy in Middle East history
description: This seminar covers the history of piracy in the Middle East in the early modern and modern periods (from roughly 1500 to the present), with particular reference to the Mediterranean and Arabian Gulf coasts, two areas where we find relatively high levels of piracy. Pirates were, and are, far from a monolithic group and we pay attention to the variety of pirate identities, including those of corsair, maritime warlord, sea bandit, and entrepreneur. Topics include the organization of the pirate ship, the fate of booty and captives, the changing political context of Middle Eastern and European maritime interests and relations, and the evolution of international law. We are also interested in how pirates and piracy were depicted in popular culture, in both the Middle East and Europe. At the end of the semester, we briefly examine 21st century piracy in the region.

title: Soviet dissident movements
description: This seminar addresses the rise, activities, and achievements of anti-Soviet opposition movements during the last decades of the USSR (1960sâ1991) and their subsequent transformation in the post-Soviet space. It examines these movementsâ wide variety of ideological currents and political strategies, surveys their contribution to the emergence of an alternative human rights culture, and reexamines their impact on the fall of the Soviet Union. It addresses the human rights activistsâ debates over the defense of human rights, the right to national self-determination, the right to emigrate, religious and cultural freedom, and environmental issues. Special emphasis is put on how dissidents were perceived by Soviet authorities and in the international arena and how those perceptions have contributed to shape East-West diplomatic relations during the Cold War. This seminar also surveys the instrumental role dissidents played in the aftermath of the collapse of the Communist rule before being isolated from post-Soviet politics.

title: Russia in the Silver Age
description: At the turn of the 20th century, during the last few decades of its existence, the Russian Empire experienced a period of intense artistic and philosophical innovation. Russiaâs short lived, âSilver Age,â as this era has come to be known, is often overshadowed by the two wars and three revolutions that ultimately cut it short in 1917. Some facets of the Silver Age, such as its rebellious avant-garde artists and radical intelligentsia, fed into the destructive forces of revolution. Others, such as its idealist philosophers and symbolist poets, resisted the dogmatic materialism preached by Marxist revolutionaries. Silver Age Russia was also an integral part of the cultural and intellectual world of fin de siÃ¨cle Europe. One of the great novels of the age, Andrei Bely's Petersburg, will be our window into the artistic, philosophical, religious, and revolutionary forces that shaped the culture of turn-of-the-century Russia.

title: History of New Orleans
description: This colloquium will focus on the rich, unique history of New Orleans and its people from the city's founding in the eighteenth century to the present day. We will pay special attention to the intertwined themes of cultural diversity and mixture, class and race inequality, and the changing urban ecology -- concluding with a close look at Hurricane Katrina and its consequences.

title: Military History of NATO
description: This colloquium will trace the development of the Atlantic Alliance of 1949 into a military organization under United States leadership. The impact of particular events from the Korean War in 1950 through the Berlin and Cuban crises of 1948-1962 to the INF agreement of 1987 and the subsequent implosion of the Soviet Union will be examined. Special attention will be paid to the differing views on nuclear weaponry between the United States and its European allies. The course will conclude with an evaluation of NATO's status in 2009 and of its potential roles in the immediate future.

title: Topics in US History: see descriptions
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of American history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Fall 2012 the following courses are offered: US Social movements (Professor Adler): This seminar explores the histories of five U.S. social movements - abolition, women's suffrage, labor, the black freedom struggle and post-1960s human rights and environmental activism - with two primary goals. First, the class aims to provide students a solid grounding in the histories of these movements, from how they first emerged and grew to measuring their ultimate successes and failures. Second, the course will allow students to examine how each of these movements was both affected by and had effects upon politics and social movements in other nations. How was the rhetoric and tactics of these U.S. movements influenced by activism in other nations? In what ways did U.S. groups and movements build ties globally? How did these interactions impact the ability of U.S. movements to create change at home and abroad? US at war since 1898 (Professor Packard): This course examines the development of the military institution within the United States from the Spanish-American War to the present. It explores the changing means and uses of military force as well as the role that the military and war have played in American society more broadly. Although the conduct of war and the experience of combat will be addressed, particular emphasis will be placed on how war the military, war, and preparations for war shaped the United States â its politics, its foreign policy, its culture, its economy, its race, gender, and class relations â during the twentieth century. The U.S. currently maintains what has been referred to as an âarchipelago of empireâ comprised of upwards of 700 overseas military facilities. To sustain its military commitments it spends nearly as much on defense as the rest of the world combined. Beginning with the Spanish-American War in 1898, the U.S. fought six major wars in the twentieth century, two in the twenty-first, and conducted countless lesser armed interventions throughout the period under consideration. The influence of military-related events in U.S. history cannot be overstated. For example, no single event so dramatically transformed the U.S. than did World War II. Apart from the Civil War, one would be hard-pressed to find a period in which Americans were more divided than during the Vietnam War. Along these same lines, veterans often look back on their wartime service as the most important period of their lives. Considering the frequency with which the U.S. has engaged in armed conflict and the energy and resources it has devoted to defense, this course will use the evolution of the U.S. military as a framework through which to understand the history of the United States in the twentieth century. This course asks students to consider a number of questions. Why have wars occurred? How are military policies arrived at? How have preparations for war influenced society? What is the role of the military in a free society? Is there an âAmerican and way of warâ? What is the role of the military in domestic and foreign policymaking? What can military history teach us about issues of race, class, gender, and citizenship? How are we to understand the relationships between military affairs, technology, politics, and social change? The intent of this course it to provide students with a historical perspective on these questions and a broader understanding of military matters so that they may be better able to place on-going debates in historical context. There are no prerequisites for this course, but a basic familiarity with U.S. history will help.

title: What Is an American? Cultural Identity in the United States
description: This course will explore the evolving question of what constitutes American identity from the time of the American Revolution to the present. The focus will be the history of community, group experiences, major American autobiographies, and the continuing shifts and changes in how Americans defined and redefined their understanding of the nation. The American Revolution, slavery, white-Indian relations, Civil War, industrialization, gender and race, Civil Rights and the "cultural wars" of the late twentieth century will be examined in depth.

title: Recent US political economy
description: This course will examine the development of the U.S. economy, economic policy, and economic politics since World War II. Among the topics it will engage are: the roots of postwar prosperity; the economic crisis of the 1970s; the Reagan Revolution; the impact of globalization; the emergence of neoliberalism; and the growing problem of economic inequality. The course will examine the intersection of politics and the economy in order to provide a historical context that can clarify contemporary problems and debates.

title: Conservatism
description: Conservative thinker Richard Weaver once wrote, âIdeas have consequences.â This colloquium examines the evolution of the American Right â as both an intellectual phenomenon and a political movement. It focuses on the period since 1945, when conservatism became a mass phenomenon. We will pursue such questions as: how have conservative ideas changed? What impact did the Right have on cultural change in the U.S. and vice versa? Were conservatives able to convert their electoral successes into major changes in policy? Readings include primary texts and recent scholarly works. This course emphasizes class discussion and is limited to 20 students.

title: Geneaology and US history
description: This course offers students a unique opportunity to research a family history (likely oneâs own but could be another one of their choosing) in the larger context of US history, using the latest genealogical resources on the internet and in the National Archives, while also maintaining the critical lens of historians. We will have a few weeks of shared reading on immigration and family history, as well as on the practice and theory of genealogy; following this shared introduction, students will craft individual bibliographies to augment their own genealogical research. The final product will be a research paper that puts one familyâs history in historical perspective. Were these people (perhaps your ancestors) typical of their era? What do their lives, or what you can glean about them, reveal about their neighborhood, region, and era? How did gender, race, and class shape their experience? This course will require a general knowledge of US history, a schedule that can be adapted to archival research, and a willingness to cast a critical eye on personal and family stories.

title: New perspectives on the Cold War
description: The Cold War dominated world politics for 45 years, and its myriad legacies continue to shape the contemporary world. This course takes a global approach to the Cold War and examines such factors as changes in the global distribution of power, the dynamics of the arms race, the evolution of the world economy, shifts in the balance of social and political forces within and among nations, and the transformation of the Third World.

title: Global environmental history
description: Because of the fundamental interconnectedness of the earthâs ecosystem, integrating the natural environment into the study of history require a global perspective. Taking the world as its unit of analysis, this course investigates intersections between the environmental change and major developments in world history, focusing on the last 500 years. Major themes will include the significance of environmental factors in the making of the modern world, globalization and imperialism, the role of energy and climate in world history, and the impact of pollution on cities. We will also look comparatively at a few case studies â the environmental histories of the United States, China, or the Dust Bowl. We will stay attuned to the natural environmentâs role as an active agent in history, paying particular attention to the environmental causes and consequences of world-historical events.

title: Senior Honors Seminar in History
description: HIST 408-409 form a two-semester study of History as an intellectual discipline. Enrollment is by invitation of the Department. Fall: Readings and discussions with departmental faculty on the various methods, concepts, and philosophies of history, and the development of a research prospectus with a faculty mentor. Spring: Research seminar under the guidance of the mentor and Seminar Director. (Enrollment only by permission of the Director of Undergraduate Studies). Students must commit themselves for the full two semesters.

title: Senior Honors Seminar in History
description: HIST 408-409 form a two-semester study of History as an intellectual discipline. Enrollment is by invitation of the Department. Fall: Readings and discussions with departmental faculty on the various methods, concepts, and philosophies of history, and the development of a research prospectus with a faculty mentor. Spring: Research seminar under the guidance of the mentor and Seminar Director. (Enrollment only by permission of the Director of Undergraduate Studies). Students must commit themselves for the full two semesters.

title: The African Atlantic
description: Africans were crucial to the development of the Atlantic World from their early encounters with Europeans in the 1400s through the era of abolition and the first modern human rights movement in the 1800s. Team-taught by historians of different sides of the Atlantic ocean, this course focuses on the causes and effects of Africaâs integration into Atlantic commerce, especially the slave trade, and the traumatic experience of millions of captive Africans forcibly transported to the Americas. Themes to be covered include African concepts of slavery; the linkage between war and captivity; the technological transformation of trade; the dynamics of the Middle Passage; African labor, knowledge, and culture in the Americas; Africansâ participation in the fight against slavery; and the long-term impact of the slave trade and its abolition on Africa. We will study historiansâ diverse perspectives on these issues and explore the astonishing array of primary sources, from slave tradersâ letters to ex-slavesâ folktales, that reveal the participation of Africans themselves in the making of Atlantic history.

title: European encounters with Asia 1450-1750
description: This upper-division seminar in global history will explore the creative encounters as well as turbulent clashes that took place between European explorers, merchants, and missionaries and the peoples and cultures of India, Japan, China, and the Philippines, from the late 15th to the late 18th centuries. The European maritime empires involved in this colonial, commercial, and religious enterprise included Portugal, Spain, England, and the Netherlands. From the moment Europeans first reached the shores of Southeast and East Asia, they struggled to come to terms with the cultural divide between East and West. Students will analyze a number of key primary texts written as a result of this encounter by famous European explorers, missionaries, and merchants, as well as by their Asian counterparts. Students will also explore how the invention of the printing pressâthe first IT revolutionâhelped to disseminate knowledge about Asia in Europe & the Americas.

title: Chinese environmental history
description: The human toll, political challenges, and global impact created by environmental degradation in contemporary China have attracted widespread attention. But because most commentators link Chinaâs environmental problems to the countryâs post-1978 economic growth, they tend to overlook the historical roots of many of these ecological problems. To gain a fuller understanding of the current environmental dilemmas facing China and the world, this seminar will investigate key topics in the environmental history of China over the last three thousand years.

title: The French Republic and its religious minorities
description: This course explores the encounter, from the French Revolution to the present, between the modern French concept of the nation-state with its ideology of a unifying political and cultural identity and Franceâs cultural, religious, and ethnic diversity. It highlights three distinct minorities, Protestants, Jews, and Muslims, as particularly relevant case-studies for understanding the making and transformation of the modern French state, nation, and culture. By comparing their paths of integration and the public discourses and policies surrounding their assimilation, it explores how they have negotiated and reshaped their identities as they struggled to internalize the common French cultural and historical legacy. By doing so, this course also compares and contrasts the French paradigm of citizenship and integration with its neighboring European models and engages in the contemporary political and historiographical debates over the relationship between political citizenship and religious/cultural identity.

title: Outer space from Plato to Newton
description: Human beings have looked up and asked âwhatâs up thereâ in all societies and from time immemorial. Answers and speculations have found their way into religious texts and science books. Some explanations focus on the differences between the world above and the world we live in, others on similarities. But from horoscopes to tide tables, the most common conviction linking Antiquity to the present is that the world(s) above influence the world below. This course focuses on how western thinkers have understood the significance of the heavens for the earth. It will focus on thinkers drawn from antiquity, the Middle Ages, and the Scientific Revolution. We will read together great works of science, religion, and magic by such thinkers as Aristotle, Ptolemy, Augustine, Roger Bacon, Thomas Aquinas, Nicolas of Cusa, Copernicus, Bruno, Kepler, Galileo, and Newton. We will investigate historical debates over the structure of the cosmos, geocentrism and heliocentrism, calendars and telling time, the âscienceâ of astrology, teaching astronomy in the medieval university, the possibility of parallel universes, extra-terrestrial life, infinity/beginnings/ends, the ârealityâ of voids and occult forces like gravity. The course will be organized as a colloquium and consist mainly of our discussions of the historical literature. Lecturing will be resorted to minimally to provide the background for the primary literature. Research papers will be required in lieu of examinations.

title: Sex and Celibacy: Nuns and Prostitutes in Europe
description: From the beginnings of Christian Europe, the role of sex and celibacy have been highly contested and debated areas. The development of a celibate clergy, the role of sex within marriage, and the relative merits or not of virginity have formed a core part of the development of Western culture. By studying the debates over sex and celibacy from the perspective of two specific groups of women (nuns and prostitutes) who were intimately defined by the categories "sexual" or "celibate", this course analyzes their role in society and history. The course begins with a discussion of sex and celibacy in general and theological debates over them in early Christianity and the Middle Ages. It then looks more specifically at the role of nuns and prostitutes in premodern Europe--their social utility, religious justifications, changing role in culture--paying special attention to reform movements (both lay and religious) which focused on "cleaning up" abuses within both Nuns and prostitutes were often conflated in premodern Europe, in large part because of their sexualized identity--either shunning sex or wallowing in it--and desires to control or reform these groups crossed religious, geographical, political, and even chronological boundaries. The course will include readings from primary and secondary sources, as well as some video events throughout the semester. Requirements include attendance and participation, two short papers on the weekly readings, and a longer bibliographic essay.

title: The Americanization of Europe
description: This colloquium will be a historical inquiry into the following questions: Has Europe been Americanized? Have Europeans in the course of the 20th century been Coca-colonized or McDonaldized? Have Americans and Europeans grown increasingly alike with respect to adopting mass culture, consumer society, and market economies? If so why? What is the explanation for the seemingly irresistible power of Americanization? When did this process begin and what has America's role been in this process? Or, conversely, is Americanization an illusion? Has there been appropriation and resistance so that national identities and diversity have been sustained and even intensified? Has it provoked a search for identity in national or ethnic difference? Above all what does this transformation mean?

title: The Enlightenment
description: This course examines some of the most important European thinkers of the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, a period in which literacy was growing rapidly and printing made new ideas accessible to a widening reading public. Its purpose is to get students acquainted with the historical origins of ideas that can seem to us self-evidentâfor example, that authority rests on rationality rather than appeals to tradition, sacred texts, or some unmediated concept of nature; that toleration is a virtue; that freedom and equality are fundamental to both public and private life; that self-determining individuality is a higher value than obedience. Texts will vary from year to year but will usually include works by Diderot, Goethe, Hume, Kant, Montesquieu, Rousseau, and Voltaire.

title: European intellectual history, 20th century
description: This course considers the failure of the Enlightenment project, the collapse of traditional forms of representation, and the search for meaning in the midst of war, genocide, and economic recovery from WWI to the early 21st century. Topics include: the critique of modernity, the status of the author and metanarratives, 20th c. Marxism, critical theory, the theory of the novel, structuralism, poststructuralism, and deconstruction. Readings from Freud, Woolf, Sartre, Levi-Strauss, Habermas, Foucault, Derrida, Lyotard and others.

title: The First World War
description: This course, conducted in the format of a colloquium, will explore the First World War in its multiple dimensions. Most attention will be devoted to events in Europe, but just as the warâs origins and consequences were global in derivation and impact, theaters of operation were to be found in parts of Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, and the Middle East, and these geographical areas, too, will be examined. Above all, the war will be situated in its broader temporal context, if only because it is often said to have been the defining moment of the twentieth century. Its connections to the Russian and Chinese Revolutions, the Arab Awakening, the collapse of the Ottoman Empire, the rise of colonial nationalism, especially in India, the Great Depression, and, not least, the Second World War will therefore require some consideration.

title: Food in European History
description: During the Renaissance, the foods and cuisines of Europe bore a strong family resemblance to their counterparts in late Roman antiquity and the Muslim cultures of the medieval world. Cereals dominated the diet and the prevailing culinary style favored complex, spicy sauces. Wine was the universal beverage of choice, distilled spirits were rare, and coffee, tea, and other caffeinated drinks were unknown. Medical advice about diet and health drew on the teachings of Hippocratic medicine (which originated in ancient Greece) as expanded and amended by Roman and Arab physicians. Shortly after 1500, however, this picture began to change, laying the foundations of the dietary and culinary regimes with which we are familiar today. This course will examine the transformation of the habits of cooking, eating, and drinking in Europe between 1500-1900 with an emphasis on France and England. Topics will include: the reception of new foods from the Americas; the growing availability and popularity of sugar and colonial beverages (coffee, tea, and chocolate); the uneven impact of economic growth on the diets of rich and poor Europeans; changing ideas about diet, health, and disease; changing manners and patterns of social behavior associated with food and drink; the rise of the restaurant, and the emergence of modern culinary styles, including national ones.

title: Mexican migration to the US
description: This course will examine mexican migration, the U.S.-Mexican borderlands, and Mexicans in the United States during the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The long and dynamic history of mexican migration to the United States is rooted in a binational history of shifting labor conditions, and in social and cultural networks forged by a century of border-crossings. In this course students will examine migration within the broader context of Mexican history and focus on migrant narratives that reflect immigrant and migrant realities on both sides of the border. Seminars will examine how and why migrants made their way north in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, the impact of deportations and repatriations of Mexicans during the 1930s, war-time contract labor agreements during the Bracero era, and the rise of legal and illegal immigration during the second half of the twentieth century. Assigned readings and discussion will be designed to help students place current debates about migrant rights and immigration policy within a larger historical context.

title: Native peoples making modern Latin America
description: At a time when most people did not live beyond 45, she was certainly one of the oldest persons in the world. Maria Ana Osorio was a 91-year-old indigenous woman from central-west Mexico. It was 1872 and she had lived enough to witness many of the events that we now perceive as fundamental in the history of modern Latin America. She lived through several wars, political strife, economic downturn, and experienced the often contentious process of creating new institutions and laws. Many of who she closely knew during her long life-span actually participated in these conflicts and debates. Had she lived longer (!), she would have witnessed the expansion of the railroad and the rise of agribusiness and other industries. This course is an invitation to explore how indigenous people, such as Maria Ana Osorio, navigated through some of these momentous changes during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. It is an invitation to examine the many ways in which indigenous people contributed to shape the political, social, cultural, and economic history of the region (especially in the Andes and Mesoamerica). The course will also pay significant attention to the general conditions and many constraints that made possible and limited this contribution. Hopefully, you will find the accounts included in this course not only useful, but also appealing and passionate.

title: History of Islam in Africa
description: This course examines the development of Muslim communities in Africa from the time of Muhammad to the present. All regions of Africa will be discussed, noting the changes in the regional societies and their relationships over the centuries. Some emphasis will be given to analysis of jihad traditions, and relations between Islam and the state in the colonial and contemporary periods.

title: Islamic Modernism
description: This course examines the development of efforts to create an intellectual and religious synthesis of modern and Islamic thought. The works of major figures in the definition and development of this Islamic Modernism from the early presentations of thinkers like al-Afghani) and Abduh to twentieth century scholars like Iqbal and Fazlur Rahman will be studied, both in terms of their intellectual context and their historical contexts.

title: Nation and empire in Russian history
