Of central concern here will be power-base theory and the measurement of relative national capabilities, as well as the definition and measurement of great powers, structure, global reach and the loss-of-strength gradient, absolute and relative power, polarity, and systematic concentrations of power. Second, this course will examine the dynamic relationships between and among great powers, world order, geopolitical transitions and global change, and "showdown" or global wars. Our focus here will be on the nature and requirements of global equilibrium and international order, the rise and fall of great Powers and world orders, geopolitical transitions, the theoretical linkages between macroeconomic trends and the timing of global wars, global change and the future of transitional wars. All of these concepts and issues will be examined and analyzed within the context of six broad theoretical frameworks: "Power Transition Theory"; "Leadership Long Cycle Theory"; "Long Wave/Leading Sector Theory"; "Hegemonic Stability/Leadership Theory"; "World System Theory"; and the rise and fall of "World Orders." Throughout the course, equal attention will be paid to both theory and empirical research, to the historical past, present, and the future (from about 1000 to 2050 CE), and to the definition, operationalization, and measurement of key variables. Fall.

title: Cultural Geography of Turkey and Eastern Med
description: This course will survey the cultural, political, and economic geography of Turkey and the Eastern Mediterranean with the aim of preparing students to make informed observations about life and politics in contemporary Turkey. We will first consider the many layers of civilization encountered in Eastern Mediterranean landscapes and the ways in which successive groups have attempted to absorb, appropriate, re-use, or destroy the cultural legacies left behind by those who have lived on the land. We will then study the map of the modern Middle East and the geopolitical forces that brought it into being, with particular emphasis on the creation of the modern Republic of Turkey. Our discussions will focus on the contestation of space in terms of national, regional, and ethnic identity as well as attempts to shape or re-shape the landscape with regard to the practice of religion in the modern world, the differing experiences of men and women, the social and environmental consequences of rapid urbanization, and the growth of the tourism industry.

title: Wealth and Poverty: The History of Development
description: At the beginning of the 21st century, the gap between rich and poor nations is larger than it has been in history. This course introduces students to long-term effects that historic events can have on current economic development. We will provide an interdisciplinary overview of different explanations of the fluctuation of wealth and poverty in the historical context. We will study the causes and results of the hegemony of the West since the 15th century and pay special attention to the impact of Western development on the rest of the world. We will then survey the evolution of the development framework in the Post War period.

title: 21st C Geopolitical Realities
description: The 21st Century has ushered in a global transformation that is redefining the world order. This transformation is shifting geo-political centers of gravity and is re-casting geo-political influences as the world experiences an unprecedented diffusion of power. This course will examine the above thesisâ¦ is this in fact happening? Are the six and a half billion people in the world â soon to be eight billion â and the nations they reside in, redefining geo-political relationships governed by the new realities of global challenges? Global challenges such as an interconnected global economy, proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, terrorism, environmental and energy issues, pandemic health threats, poverty, despair, and new technologies. Are we entering a new era of global engagement and accommodation? Is all of this reshaping and redefining relationships? This course will focus on a wide-lens optic of global relationships and its impact on American foreign policy and Americaâs future. In this course students will examine the past, present, and future. They will seek to determine whether geopolitical relationships are shifting, why, and how they may be impacting the world order. Students will go beyond their survey of scholarly information to draw realistic and useful conclusions about how organizations (private, public, non-profit, transnational, etc.) and individuals can best prepare to take on the challenges of the 21st Century. Students will learn from and consult scholarly research, a variety theoretical perspectives, case studies, foreign policy leaders, and current events to contribute to class discussions, assignments, and overall expertise. We will also invite experts and leaders from the international affairs community to select seminars to provide on-the-ground insight. Beyond acquiring new knowledge and perspectives, building strong analytical writing and presentation skills will be important. In and out of class exercises will illustrate key concepts and challenge students to be thinking, articulate, and conscientious international affairs professionals.

title: International Affairs Research & Internship Seminar
description: This multidisciplinary seminar allows students to combine original academic research for a selected topic in International Affairs with the practical experience acquired during a part time internship during the semester. The seminar teaches students to do research to consult relevant primary sources, to review major theoretical approaches to a selected topic, and to test the strength and weakness of these approaches by and drawing upon the practical experience in their internships. The design of this course takes into consideration the proposal by the SFS Academic Council to the SFS curriculum committee. It is open to students from the sophomore to senior level.

title: Krogh Honors Seminar
description: This seminar explores the role international organizations (IOs) play in world politics. We start with a brief exploration of the history of IOs and discuss some of the conceptual issues that lie at the hard of many contemporary debates about IOs. We then investigate the impact of specific IOs. We start with security issues, where we will in particular investigate the UN Security Council. We then move to the economic arena, where we will look at the WTO, the IMF, and the World Bank. Finally, we move to the role IOs play on the environment and human rights. In the latter area, we will investigate the development of international courts, such as the ICC and the ECHR. The central questions we will ask about these IOs are: why were they created, and by whom? What roles were they originally expected to play in world politics, and if those roles changed over time, how and why? What contributions have these IOs made in their particular policy areas; and what factors shape the depth, scope, and effectiveness of these contributions? Throughout the seminar, the relationship between the United States and IOs receives special attention. Permission of SFS Dean's Office required.

title: Addressing State Failure
description: This course will be co-taught by a State Department Foreign Service Officer and an Army Foreign Area Officer. Since the end of World War II, the United States has led numerous efforts to reconstruct states and societies torn apart by violent conflict. The record of these interventions is decidedly mixed. Yet the diminishing strength of central governments vis-Ã -vis the persistence of extreme poverty and the rise of heavily armed, aggressive subnational organizations in many parts of the world has not only increased the number of countries at risk of failure but also the human and geopolitical costs associated with state failure. This course will introduce students to the principal issues, actors and challenges associated with contemporary ânation-buildingâ exercises. We will use case studies to examine the historical record as well as contemporary, on-going efforts. Students will be exposed to the evolving role of the United States and its relationship with partner nations, the United Nations, non-governmental organizations and host governments. With each case study, we will analyze the determinants of success or failure and take note of the âlessons learned.â Taught from a practitionerâs perspective, this course will closely examine the inter-agency process for developing and implementing USG policy, both in theory and in practice. Particular emphasis will be given to the increasingly important role of civil-military relations in stabilization and reconstruction efforts.

title: Grand Stragety in Comparative and Historical Perspective
description: This course focuses on the grand strategy of imperial powers. It will begin with an examination of the components of imperial power and of the political, economic, and strategic issues entailed in its management and allocation. We will next examine in comparative perspective the grand strategies of past great powers, including the Roman, Ottoman, British, German, and Japanese empires. The course will then focus on American grand strategy during different phases in the countryâs rise as a great power. It will end with an examination of American grand strategy for the twenty-first century.

title: Dept Sem: Sr Sem on Asia
description: This seminar is designed to assist senior candidates for the Certificate in Asian Studies with the research and writing of the Asian Studies Certificate Research Paper. Participants in the seminar are required to attend weekly meetings and undertake formal writing exercises aimed at production of the Research Paper. Grades are based on participation in the seminar and the quality of the final paper.

title: African Studies: Capstone Course
description: This course will explore the representations of Africa and Africans from various perspectives, focusing primarily on literature and film. Academic scholarship in the natural and social sciences has some influence over how the continent and its inhabitants are viewed, including their histories, present conditions and future possibilities. But perhaps even more influential in representing the challenges and hopes of the African continent is that which derives from fiction, both literature and film. For those who do not come from and/or study âaboutâ Africa, which is the vast majority of people, any knowledge or opinions they have are driven by imagery created in books and on the screen. In this course we will collectively analyze the way that the diverse cultures, polities and geographies of the 54 countries of Africa are presented. We will do this by reading fiction, as well as viewing films by diverse producers.

title: Philip Roth: Secular Jewish Fiction
description: This course is devoted to a study of the fiction of Philip Roth focusing mostly, but not exclusively, on the âKepeshâ novels, the lighter âZuckerman cycle,â and some of his more recent work. Perhaps no author, living or dead, has interrogated the problems of identity, self and the dilemma of artâs relation to reality more than Roth has. We will explore those issues as well as the recurring exploration of the modern Jewish predicament and paradox that has surfaced consistently in his work across five decades. This will give us ample opportunity to linger on the âsecularâ dimensions of his oeuvre; Roth is at once a champion, a chronicler, and maybe even a eulogist for a secular Jewish-American way of life whose future is no longer assured.

title: Religion and International Affairs
description: Since the time when the United States of America justified its entry into the Second World war in 1917 on the basis of âMaking world safe for democracyâ the mere avocation of the âReason of Stateâ has not been sufficient to explain and / or justify sate behavior Rather, whatever, the real motives states have since justified their policies on the basis of more transcendent ideals an since the 1930s comprehensive ideologies. Consequently, since then one of the key issues in the study of international affairs has been how to ascertain the relative role of ideas, values, and ideals, versus more tangible factors such as overall power, quest for security, economic, and political interests and political institutions , leadership characteristics , and the dynamics of domestic politics, in determining the pattern of statesâ behavior. During the Cold War era, this issue was raised principally in connection with the assessment of the role of ideologies of the East and the West in determining statesâ behavior and, more fundamentally, in shaping their views of what constitutes their vital interests. However, very seldom if at all did religion factor into analysis of state behavior. Of course religion did play an important role in shaping the values and ideals of states and hence impacted their behavior. However, it was not factored in as an independent variable in the study of international affairs and foreign policy of state. Since the Soviet Unionâs collapse in December 1991 and the end of the ideological Cold War this situation has changed and religion has acquired a greater saliency as an important factor in determining state behavior and even as a n overarching paradigm explaining how and why states relate to one another and behave toward each other. The best example of this new attitude is the thesis of the Clash of Civilizations advanced by Samuel P. Huntington. The greater role of religion in determining state behavior is emphasized especially in the case of Muslim states, because of the following reasons: First, Muslim societies are still highly religious, and religion plays an important role in societal relations and deeply influences the cultural environment; Second, Muslims view themselves as a single community of faith (Umma) and have always aspired to unity. Yet, how justified is this renewed interest in the role of religion in international affairs after a long period of neglect. Does religious characteristic now trump all other determinants of state behavior or its impact is less determinant than assumed by some analysts depending on other variables and specific circumstances? More specifically, have Muslim states been unduly influenced by religious factor in their behavior? This course will try to answer these questions by analyzing : 1) the basic determinants of statesâ external behavior; 2) the relative impact of ideals and values in shaping statesâ behavior; 3) the role of Key leaders and their viewsâ of religion in shaping statesâ behavior; 4) the Impact of religion in the post âSoviet foreign policies of: a) Russia; b; The US; c) Britain; d) The EU with emphasis on debate about Turkish membership. 5) the history of the Muslimsâ view of international affairs and the relations between Muslim empiresâ and statesâ relations with the rest of the world ; 6) the foreign policies of key Muslim states in the last fifty years, including their approach to relations with other Muslim states and intra-Muslim cooperation.

title: Refugees in International Relations
description: This course examines the causes and consequences of forced migration, focusing in particular on displacement from conflict and human rights violations. It begins with the history of the concept of 'refuge' and then traces the evolution of the international regime for protecting and assisting refugees and displaced persons. The course then focuses on current examples of displacement in Africa, Latin America, the Middle East, Central and South Asia, and Europe.

title: African Politics/Governments
description: Most sub-Saharan African governments began independence as democracies. With few exceptions, they gave way to authoritarian rule and remained so for 25 years. At the beginning of the 1990s, a wave of political liberalization began to sweep across the region. This course will examine the nature of political institutions and political rule in sub-Saharan Africa since independence, including the factors leading to these political changes. Key topics will include the colonial political inheritance, ethnicity and social characteristics of African societies, the nature of and role of the military, political parties, legislatures, judiciaries, the media, interest groups, patronage networks, external powers, and the role of economic conditions (including structural adjustment programs) in influencing African politics. The course will conclude by asking whether the current trend toward democratization will endure.

title: Catholic-Muslim Relations Post Vatican II
description: The Second Vatican Council inaugurated a new phase in Muslim-Christian relations. For the first time in its history, the Catholic Church made an official pronouncement on how that Church âregardsâ Muslims. The Council encouraged dialogue with Muslims and gave the two communities a common mission: to work together for the common good to build peace, establish justice in societies, defend moral values, and promote true human freedom. At the same time, Muslims were developing their own initiatives and responding to those of the Catholic Church. In addition, political and geopolitical issues, (the Israel-Palestine conflict, Gulf War, Algerian Civil War etc.) affected the course of Muslim-Catholic relations, as did issues resulting from the breakup of the Soviet bloc. The goal of this course is to review closely these developments in the relations between Catholics and Muslims since Vatican II.

title: Peacemaking and Peacekeeping: A Middle Power Perspective
description: Middle powers play an important role in peacemaking and peacekeeping. Great powers (US, Japan, PRC and Russia) often have large political and economic interests in a given conflict. While great powers possess considerable leverage that can be useful in moving conflicting parties toward peaceful settlement, they are also constrained by their great power status. Unlike great powers middle powers are perceived to have less at stake in any given conflict. Middle power may not possess the same capacity to leverage the parties in a given conflict, yet there are often perceived as being neutral and less inclined to want to settle a conflict "their way". Middle powers can bring unique and often inventive approaches to the problem of violent conflict. This course will examine the dynamics of middle power involvement in peacemaking and peacekeeping. Examples of middle powers playing this role include Australia in East Timor and Papua New Guinea, Norway in Sri Lanka and Indonesia.

title: Practicing Diplomacy Abroad
description: This seminar will look at diplomacy as a political process and as an instrument of foreign policy. It will examine the role and responsibilities of the ambassador and other members of an overseas mission, explore the resources and techniques available to them, and review the way diplomats relate to the government they serve and the one to which they are accredited. It will also look critically at the backup at the headquarters end of diplomacy (e.g. Washington), examining the functioning of the foreign policy bureaucracy and its interaction with overseas operations. It will survey the historical evolution of diplomacy and the impact of recent political, economic, social, and technological changes on diplomatic practices. It will also consider the relationship between diplomacy and intelligence operations and the growth in importance of "new" areas of foreign policy concern such as counter-terrorism and the prevention of drug trafficking and cross-border crime. The course will focus primarily on U.S. diplomatic practices, but much of the material is also relevant to the way other governments organize their diplomatic activities. In a final session, three foreign diplomats representing countries of differing size and circumstance will discuss the way their countries practice diplomacy and compare this with the U.S. approach. The instructor is a retired U.S. ambassador. Several other American career diplomats will also meet with the class.

title: Land/Indig Peoples/Conflict
description: One can be forgiven for thinking of land as a commodity, after all, in the West thatâs what land is. For many people around the globe, however, land is not a commodity to be sold or traded. Rather, land and life are synonymous. A reading of history will remind us that wars have been fought over land. Settlers coming to the Americas, Australia, New Zealand and other places have fought with indigenous people over the possession of land. All too often we think of these conflicts as historical relics, and the stuff of legend and film. Nothing could be further from the truth. Today, numerous conflicts simmer over land tenure and sometimes they boil over. In this course we will examine the nexus of land, conflict and indigenous peoples. What are some of the contending views of land and its use? What are the histories of indigenous people losing their land? How have indigenous people come to fight for their land? How have land conflicts been managed? What is the way forward?

title: Land/Indig Peoples/Conflict
description: One can be forgiven for thinking of land as a commodity, after all, in the West thatâs what land is. For many people around the globe, however, land is not a commodity to be sold or traded. Rather, land and life are synonymous. A reading of history will remind us that wars have been fought over land. Settlers coming to the Americas, Australia, New Zealand and other places have fought with indigenous people over the possession of land. All too often we think of these conflicts as historical relics, and the stuff of legend and film. Nothing could be further from the truth. Today, numerous conflicts simmer over land tenure and sometimes they boil over. In this course we will examine the nexus of land, conflict and indigenous peoples. What are some of the contending views of land and its use? What are the histories of indigenous people losing their land? How have indigenous people come to fight for their land? How have land conflicts been managed? What is the way forward?

title: America and the Arab-Israeli Conflict: Issues in Policy and Diplomacy
description: This seminar focuses on American policy towards the Arab-Israeli conflict. It is neither a comprehensive history of the conflict nor of US policy. Instead it focuses on key issues that shape and define Americaâs approach to the conflict, its management, and resolution. The course is very much Washington-centric, taught from the blended perspective of a practitioner but with an analytical and historical bent. It will examine core issues, such as the problem of bias and objectivity, the US-Israel relationship, the American approach to negotiations, the role of domestic politics in shaping US policy, and the internal dynamics involved in the formulation of US policy.

title: International Conflict Resolution
description: This course surveys major theories and processes in international conflict resolution. First, the course covers relevant concepts, models, and frameworks to the analysis of conflict as a social phenomenon. The underlying causes and conditions of international conflict will be analyzed by discussing major theories such as basic human needs, relative deprivation, and social identity theory. On the resolution end, the course will discuss processes in international conflict resolution including, mediation, negotiation, peace implementation and reconstruction of war torn societies, and reconciliation. Furthermore, the course examines the applicability of these theoretical concepts to real world conflicts including conflict cases like Nagorno Karabakh and East Timor. Case studies are selected to reflect the various types of international conflicts such as long-lasting, successfully resolved, and internationally mediated.

title: Asia-Pacific Integration
description: The course will examine the various tracks to achieving economic integration through a region-wide free trade agreement, the geopolitical issues that will have to be addressed in the process, and why this is important. Starting with how to define Asia-Pacific, its importance to the United States, and Australiaâs and New Zealandâs Asian-Western identities, the course will consider the regionâs post WWII history and alliances and the more recent political architecture such as APEC, ASEAN, and the East Asia Summit. The course then will focus on the global post-WWII trading system and the range of free trade agreements among countries in the region, with an intensive examination of the origins, prospects and goals of the Trans Pacific Partnership (TPP), and how that possible track to a region-wide agreement such as FTAAP compares to a broader Asian track that includes China and perhaps India. Finally the course will consider security and political challenges to regional integration, how cooperation on a range of science, technology, and transnational issues may affect the outcome, and what is possible versus what is likely in pursuit of a transformational 21st Century goal.

title: Political Violence in Africa
description: This course will allow the students to deconstruct the specific causes, meanings, and consequences of political violence in 20th century Africa. We will explore recent and current categories of political violence including state violence against ethnic and regional minorities (Darfur, Southern Sudan, Ethiopia); the use of paramilitary militias by states to defeat oppposition groups (Zimbabwe, Rwanda, Kenya); and movements by militias and private armies to carve out control of resource-rich areas (the DRC, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Cote d'Ivoire, Nigeria). This course will also explore the changing nature of political violence over the past 50 years, emphasizing the continuities and changes from the period of anti-colonial wars (Algeria, Kenya's 'Mau Mau' war, and the Liberation wars against the Portuguese); examples of Cold War related political violence and wars (Mozambique, Angola, Ethiopia/Eritrea, and Somalia), and urban anti-apartheid political violence in South Africa. The changing nature of violence itself will be a main theme, examining the role of child soldiers, political motivations behind ethnic 'cleansing' and genocide, and the influence of external military aid, private soldiers, and the international arms trade. We will also investigate how African peace movements and the international community have responded to political violence. The role and limits of non-violence, humanitarian aid, and peace keeping to confront these new realities will also be examined. A fundamental goal of the course is to gain a better understanding of how political violence has transformed notions of sovereignty, citizenship, human rights, and the rule of law in specific African contexts.

title: The Association of Southeat Asian Nations (ASEAN): Past and Present
description: This seminar traces the evolution of ASEAN from its formation in 1967 to the present. The regional grouping began with five founding members, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand, and the Phillipines, but has since doubled in size with the addition of Brunei, Vietnam, Burma(Myanmar), Laos, and Cambodia. Topics to be examined include the reasons for ASEAN's establishment, its early problems, its major reorganization in 1976, its importance for trade, its policies, its growth in membership, its strengths and weaknesses, and its many political, economic, social, and religious challenges at the start of the 21st century.

title: Theory and Practice of International Negotiation
description: This course offers a multidisciplinary perspective on international negotiation. It examines the role of negotiation in international affairs, the nature of the negotiating process, and the differences among states in terms of their negotiating style. The goal is to let each student form and test some judgments about how the relationships among the dynamics of the negotiating process, national negotiating styles, and the international system affect the outcome of negotiations.

title: Micro Foundations/Growth+Devmt
description: What drives productivity and innovation at the microeconomic level? This course addresses current debates on competitiveness and globalization using econometric analysis to identify and understand the microeconomic determinants of growth in developing countries. While macroeconomic stability, favorable geography, and strong institutions generate and shape a countryâs potential for national competitiveness, wealth and long-term prosperity are created at the microeconomic level. The strategies of private enterprises, the skills and education of workers, and the quality of local regulatory environments within which competition takes place are the main variables that ultimately determine a countryâs productivity and innovativeness. As a result, empirical investigation of the drivers of growth must shift down to a more microeconomic level; such an approach has become more feasible as sub-national, industry, firm, and household data have become more available. In this course, we will survey a growing body of empirical studies that focus on the firm, on the worker, and on the local regulatory environment. By studying the microeconomic environment in which firms operate, we will seek explanations to the core development question of why some countries produce more output per worker than others. For example, we will identify the microeconomic determinants of financial access in Latin America; characterize the types of firms in Sub-Saharan Africa that are more likely to pay bribes and investigate why; measure the technological spillovers from foreign direct investment to local suppliers in Southeast Asia; explore the links between excessive regulatory burden and large informal sectors in South Asia; and examine how changes in the business environment affect the productivity growth of firms in Eastern Europe and Central Asia. By examining the recent econometric evidence on the drivers of growth, students will develop a microeconomic reform agenda for international development. The course will be taught with lectures and readings, as well as econometric analysis of firm-level data from over 100 countries where âWorld Bank Enterprise Surveysâ have been conducted.

title: Soviet Dissident Movements
description: This seminar addresses the rise, activities, and achievements of anti-Soviet opposition movements during the last decades of the USSR (1960s-1991). It examines these movements' wide variety of ideological currents and political strategies, surveys their contribution to the emergence of an alternative human rights culture. It addresses the human rights activists' debates over the defense of human rights, the right to emigrate, religious and cultural freedom, and environmental issues. Special emphasis is put on how dissidents were perceived by Soviet authorities and in the international arena and how those perceptions have contributed to shape East-West diplomatic relations during the Cold War. This seminar also surveys the instrumental role dissidents played in the aftermath of the collapse of the Communist rule before being isolated from post-Soviet politics.

title: U.S.-Southeast Asian Relations in the Post 9/11 World
description: This seminar course will examine the relationship between the United States and Southeast Asia in the aftermath of the September 11, 2001 terrorist bombings. The United States is paying more attention to this region due to the links between several militant Islamic groups in Southeast Asia and the Al Qaeda network of Osama bin Laden. The course will cover U.S. relations on the political, economic, and socio-cultural levels with the eleven countries of Southeast Asia: Brunei, Burma (Myanmar), Cambodia, East Timor, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam. The United States is especially concerned about Indonesia, the largest Muslim nation in the world.

title: Econ Analysis of Sub-Sn Africa
description: This course covers issues in development economics that familiarize student with the most pressing development challenges and opportunities of Africa. The first part of the course provides an historical overview of the development experience of Africa from the African empires to colonization and political independence in Africa. It also defines development and explains the major theories of economic development. The second part of the course covers microeconomic issues in development in Africa including: household economics, poverty, human capital and vulnerability, migration, urbanization, informal sector, land reform and rural development. The third part focuses on macroeconomic issues in development in Africa such as the determinants of economic growth, innovation, monetary policy in the CFA zone, foreign aid, trade agreements, the NEPAD, economic unions and international finance in Africa. This part consists in providing a strong analysis of the development experience of some representative economies of Africa, namely Nigeria, Angola and Ivory Coast. An in-depth analysis of the economy of South Africa, as the top innovation driven economy on the continent, will also be included. Zimbabwe and Cameroon host the headquarters of the two regional intellectual property organizations in Africa. An analysis of their economies will help unravel the potential of innovation as an engine of growth. The course puts a strong emphasis on the policies implemented in Africa that resulted in the current state of development and on the economic policy recommendations that follow from the current state of development in Africa.

title: Islam and Politics
description: The course will discuss Islamic notions of politics, government, sovereignty and legitimacy and their varying interpretations throughout history. It will also discuss the more recent emergence of Islam as a political force and the reasons behind it. It will address how Islam has been used both as a factor of legitimating power by governments and as tool to challenge the legitimacy of existing systems. It will use case studies to illustrate these points.

title: Israel,Hamas&Hezbollah:21st C.
description: Since the 1980s, the violence of the Arab-Israeli conflict has not been between Israel and the surrounding Arab states, but rather between Israel and a host of non-state militant groups. Hamas and Hezbollah are the two most prominent of these groups and now figure prominently in the political and security dimensions of the Arab-Israeli conflict. This seminar will examine Hamas and Hezbollah as organizations; Israel's political and security strategies in dealing with "asymmetric" conflict; the interactions between Israel, Hamas and Hezbollah; and place those interactions in the broader context of the Arab-Israeli conflict and the geopolitics of the Middle East. Prior knowledge of the Arab-Israeli conflict will be helpful but is not required as the first weeks will be spent reviewing its current and historical dimensions. Class exercises will include debate, simulated negotiations, and guest speakers.

title: The Pivot:Us & Contemp SE Asia
description: The Pivot: The United States and contemporary Southeast Asia With Americaâs âstrategic pivotâ toward Asia under the Obama Administration, Southeast Asia will be a higher priority for the United States in all areas of strategic power. Democratic developments are largely moving in positive directions, but necessitate even stronger engagement with Washington. Virtually all of the Southeast Asian economies are growing, and, as a whole, the region remains a larger trading partner than China for the United States. The Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN)continues to knit not only its members closer together, but all of the East Asian region. What are the implications for the United States posed by a more vital ASEAN? The course will examine these areas, as well as the role of China in the region, and explore the possible paths forward as Washington seeks to ramp up its engagement.

title: The United States and Latin America in the Modern Era
description: Latin America was a major battleground for competing political, economic and ideological interests of the United States and the Soviet Union in the post WWII era (1945-1991). Both Washington and Latin Ameria capitals had strategies for dealing with this bipolar competition. This course, divided into three parts, will begin with a series of readings on the background and objectives that North America and the Southern Hemisphere pursued dealing with the Cold War in the Americas. Second, the course will require an essay on how a Latin American leader or a country proposed a strategy while confronting the U.S. or the Soviet Union as the two global bipolar states sought allies in Latin America. Last, an essay will provide a case study on how a Washington initiative responded to a real or perceived Russian prescence in the Western Hemisphere in the time period (1945-1991).

title: China and India as Emerging Global Technological Powers
description: The Emergence of China and India as Technological and Economic Powers looks at the history, and recent performance of these two giant countries. They account for 38% of the world's population. If measured in terms of purchasing power parity, they already are the second and fourth largest economies in the world, although they are still poor developing countries. There are many similarities and differences in their development strategies. Both turned inward and went left in the middle of last century; and adopted Soviet style five year industrialization plans. Both have been opening up to more interaction with the rest of the world, albeit at a different pace and to a different extent. China is becoming the worldâs manufacturing hub; India is becoming its information technology enabled service exporter. China is a one party state, India is a tumultuous democracy. Furthermore they are growing at more than three times the world average, and have critical mass in terms of highly trained people, scientists and engineers, and R&D expenditures. Where are they coming from? Where are they going? What can we learn about development strategy from their experience? How sustainable are their strategies? The course looks on the impact they are already having on other developing countries, as well as on developed countries. It also explores what further impact they may have under different scenarios.

title: Japan's Search for Strategy
description: This course will examine the search for security, prosperity and identity in modern Japan. The course opens with an introduction to the enduring elements of Japanâs Strategic Culture. In the second session the course introduces the Yoshida Doctrine of anti-communism, pro-business economic policies, alignment with the United States and passive security policy that guided Japanâs international orientation throughout the Cold War. The next three classes explore the post-Cold War transformation of Japanâs domestic political economy as well as legacy issues from the â1955 Systemâ and the Second World War that have shaped the strategic debate. The second half of the class then turns outward to explore the dynamics of Japanâs contemporary relations with the United States and the other powers in Asia. The final class returns to the question of Japanâs strategy drawing on these case studies.

title: Shariah Law and its Discontents
description: There are few topics as controversial globally and in the United States as poorly understood as âThe Shariahâ. This course will explore the actual nature and history of the Shariah tradition, with special focus on how it is understood and implemented today. It will also investigate the resonance of the Shariah amongst many Muslim populations.

title: Citiz, Belong, Mobility in Eur
description: Globalization, European integration, increased mobility and migration have profoundly changed relationships between state, territory and individuals. Multi-level policies, political challenges with social cohesion and active involvement and participation of immigrants and refugees within and across nation states induce rethinking conventional notions of citizenship and membership, culture and national belongings as well as architects and trespassers of borders and boundaries in Europe. This seminar will focus on different socio historical and political moments of conceptualizing citizenship and belonging in contemporary Europe. It will analyze and discuss from cross-national perspectives case studies on belonging and contemporary practices of politics of belongings as well as postcolonial, post-national and multilayered conceptions of citizenship in Europe and North America. This seminar is designed as an interdisciplinary Seminar. As such, it enables studentsâ interdisciplinary knowledge of and critical approach to concepts of citizenship, membership, belonging and mobility in Europe while drawing connections between comparative cases in Europe and in North America. Students will develop skills of oral communication through group work and discussions.

title: Cultures & Society of Modern India
description: This course will provide an overview of many facets of modern Indian cultures and society through ethnographic readings and films. The course will begin with an examination of the relationship between postcolonial national identity formation and ethnic/linguistic diversity, including studies of ethnic conflict and ethno-nationalist movements. We will then move on to examine religious pluralism as well as conflicts resulting from religious nationalism/communalism. The next section of the course will focus on caste and class relations and transformations of caste under colonialism and postcolonial democracy. The fourth component of the course will explore relationships of gender, kinship, sexuality, and the body, and the impact of globalization on these dynamics. The course will conclude by demonstrating the importance of understanding the inter-connections among all of the topics explored for a deeper understanding of contemporary India.

title: Politics of International Religious Freedom
description: Note: This class will be held at the Berkley Center, 3307 M Street, NW, Suite 200 The slaughter of Christian minorities in Iraq; the emergence of democracy in Muslim Indonesia; the rise of the Muslim Brotherhood in Egypt; the Catholic contribution to "the third wave" of democratization. For better or worse, religion is assuming an increasingly important role in international affairs. Although the "secularization theory" holds that religion will diminish with the advance of modernity, religious ideas and actors have become major players in the 21st century. This is a development with enormous implications for American national interests. Perhaps fortuitously, the United States is the only country in the world with an official policy of advancing international religious freedom (IRF). This course will examine the politics of U.S. IRF policy, its origins, critics, victories, and defeats to date, as well as its prospects for development. Along the way it will explore the attitudes about religion and religious freedom which have helped to mold that policy. It will ask whether U.S. strategies have reduced religious persecution, or advanced religious freedom, and what impact they have had on American interests in the greater Middle East and China. In addressing these questions it will draw on various academic disciplines, including political science and international relations, history, theology, philosophy and sociology.

title: Religious/ Secular Divide In the Muslim World: Its Roots and Implications For Security and Politics Today
description: The developments which began in the Middle East in January 2010 raised the hopes of many people in the Muslim World and elsewhere that politics in this region would evolve in an orderly and democratic fashion, and eventually would lead to the establishment of long lasting and democratic institutions. However, while countries such as Egypt, Tunisia and Libya, have held presidential, legislative elections and in the case of Tunisia elections for Constituent Assembly, their politics have remained volatile and marked by sharp divisions regarding what a democratic government should look like. Meanwhile, in many other Muslim countries politics remain frozen and any signs of popular desire for change are received with apprehension by the existing power elites. This is not surprising because most Muslim countries are divided along many economic, social, religious and cultural lines .Of these divisions, however, what could be described as Religious / Secular divide, or in other word words the divide between those who desire a basically liberal, secular, western style democracy on the one hand and those who want an essentially Islam âbased or, at least Islam inspired, political system and government system is the most significant. But why this divide? What are the roots of this divide in Muslim societies? And what could be their consequences for the future of politics and security in Muslim regions. This course will analyze these issues. It will show that this divide is the consequence of the Muslim societiesâ encounter with modernity, and the socio-political, economic and cultural consequences of modernization so far, and the still unfinished project of modernization. It will also discuss the consequences of this divide in the Muslim societies for their politics and security, notably their potential for causing civil strife and even civil war.

title: Islamic Mystical Texts & Law
description: This course will introduce students to the genre of Islamic mystical texts in Arabic. It will focus on how Sufi writings influenced the classical discourse of Islamic law and how they used legal language to articulate mystical thought. Reading knowledge of Arabic required.

title: Coporate Finance
description: This course is a âdeeper diveâ into the fundamentals of corporate finance as provided by the Business Financial Management course. The course is intended for students that are looking to extend their understanding of the corporate finance world and provides insight to the finance concepts, accounting and analytical tools that are comparable to what the typical MBA student has upon graduation. The course includes discussions of critical finance analysis, valuation techniques, financial analysis, and includes working with/building actual modeling tools, similar to those used by Corporate Finance professionals. Further, we will cover critical corporate risk management policies/processes and discuss why these are important in the Corporate Finance world. We will utilize several case studies â some that I will select and others that you will select to demonstrate âin real life situationsâ the âunderpinningsâ of why the analytical approach is so important to this area. The ultimate goal of this course is for the students to develop skills in recognizing business problems, understanding the analytical tools to define such problems and being able to see how financial tools actually are deployed in corporate decision-making. This course will focus more on analytical thinking and assessment techniques and less on âmemorizing the formulas of various financial analyticsâ. Finally, we will utilize a number of teaming arrangements, especially for case studies, in order to increase your team building skills (a critical element to the business world).

title: Problems in Multilateral Diplomacy
description: A series of case studies illustrative of the most serious problems in modern multilateral diplomacy. Cases will include intervention in intrastate conflict; UN Security Council reorganization; arms control; trade and environmental policy; and human rights.

title: International Financial Markets
description: This course provides an introduction to modern finance theory and its application to international financial markets. The course examines the foreign exchange market and related markets in derivatives such as foreign exchange futures, currency and interest rate swaps, and currency options, the eurocurrency market, international bond markets, and cross-border flow of equity investment. The course, which is open both to undergraduates and masters students, emphasizes computer and quantitative skills that are essential to understanding the operations of modern financial markets.

title: Devt Pract in Fragl/Confl Sts
description: Organized as a seminar this course examines the evolving development agenda in fragile and conflict affected states. The seminar discussions will draw on research, commentary and field work, with an emphasis on the practice: the how to, what works, what does not. The course will focus on five core areas. Each core area will be discussed with references to specific country cases to illustrate operational responses, products and outcomes. The core areas are: * The challenge of conflict and fragility - dimensions of the problem, definitions, conflict and political economy analyses * The international aid architecture - the actors, their relational dynamics, differentiated responses and harmonization efforts (OECD principles), donor conferences and modalities of pooled resources * State building - consideration of governance (including various donor and beneficiary views), security concerns (physical and economic), capacity building, public resource management for the authoritative allocation of resources for "public" goods, hopefully with transparency, equity and accountability * Modalities - policies, programs, projects and their delivery mechanisms with consideration of how to measure and evaluate outputs, results and impact * Peace building - the goals of violence abatement, reconciliation and sustainable development juxtaposed with expectations of quick "peace dividends"; the role of conflict prevention, interventions at the community level, private sector initiatives, decommissioning, demobilization and reintegration (DDR) of combatants and also reintegration of displaced persons The course would be informed by the instructor's experience as World Bank Director for programs in West Bank Gaza (1997-2001), Iraq, Iran, Lebanon, Syria, and Jordan (2001-2008) and recent experience in Sudan and Afghanistan.

title: Religion, Ethics and World Affairs
description: The last several decades have seen a reemergence of religious and ethical issues in world politics. With the end of the cold war in 1989 and the attacks of September 11, 2001, issues with a faith and values dimension have moved up the global political agenda. War and diplomacy in the Middle East; the struggle against terrorism and extremism; efforts to reduce poverty and disease worldwide; the challenge of climate change; controversies about the religious and cultural dimensions of universal human rights â these are among the issues we will be exploring over the course of the semester. Working individually and in groups, students will develop and present original research papers. The course is the capstone seminar for the new SFS Certificate in Religion, Ethics, and World Affairs. For more information, visit //berkleycenter.georgetown.edu/programs/religion-ethics-and-world-affairs-certificate [website]. Please note this course is held at Berkley Center, 3307 M St, Suite 200

title: Muslim-Christian Relations in World History
description: This course examines the nature of Muslim-Christian relations in the different contexts and eras of world history. Particular case studies ranging from medieval debates and the Crusades to modern experiences in Bosnia and in globalization will be discussed. The course concentrates on historical and societal dimensions rather than theology.

title: Islam, Pol & Sec in Mod C Asia
description: The course will examine the role of Islam in politics and society in Central Asia, the Caucasus, and the Muslim-inhabited parts of the Russian Federation. It will provide an historical background of Islam in these regions especially under Russian and Soviet rule, and in the post-independence era. The course will cover topics such as the rise of extremist groups, the response of the state to Islam, the relationships among the Muslims of Central Asia, the Caucasus, the Russian Federation, and the rest of the Muslim world, Islam and security related issues, the political uses of Islam and the impact of these interactions on the evolution of Islam in Central Asia and the Caucasus especially in the post-Soviet Era.

title: Jewish Civilization Senior Colloquium
description: Individual directed research with a faculty member working in an area relevent to student's research interests in Jewish Civilization. The semester's work will culminate in a spring colloquium where students will present condensed oral versions of their 30-40 page senior thesis. PJC certificate/minor students only.

title: Statecraft and Negotiation
description: Statecraft and Negotiation: This seminar course will look at American foreign policy through the lens of statecraft. Statecraft involves the orchestration of all the instruments of power and influence to protect against threats and to promote broad national interests. Key elements of statecraft include developing strategy, defining objectives and purposes, identifying the means available for pursuing strategy and knowing how best to employ them. Because negotiations represent one policy tool that is central to nearly all forms of statecraft, the course will explore negotiations and mediation. It will look at the American approach to negotiations and also examine mediation as an instrument for settling or defusing local and sectarian conflicts.

title: China's Evolving Role in Africa
description: China and Africaâs economic and diplomatic relations have developed rapidly in the last twenty years. Media stories depict Chinaâs engagement as a new âscramble for Africaâ reminiscent of the Westâs colonial engagement with Africa in 20th century. What is the real story? What is China getting from its engagement with Africa? Even more important, what are the Africans getting from the relationship? Will it be transformative or a repeat of the Western colonial extractive experience? This course will provide students with a balanced and nuanced understanding of the China-in-Africa story, as outlined in the syllabus posted on blackboard.

title: African Development
description: The course will survey the development of the Sub-Saharan African economies since independence. The focus will be on economic development, but aims to place the subject in its broader political, social and cultural context. The approach will be historical: starting with a brief review of the colonial inheritance, then covering the largely optimistic period to the early 1970s, moving on to the problems and stagnation of the 1980s, followed by the slow recovery of the 1990s which has accelerated in the first half of this decade. The survey will conclude by looking at where Africa is today and the prospects going forward. The course will explore key issues and themes that marked each period and try to piece together an overall story concerning where African economies have been and where they are going.

title: Turkish Foreign Policy
description: Turkey has always been renowned for occupying a strategic position and having a foothold in one of the most sensitive regions of the world. For many years, Turkish diplomats were keen to point out thatTurkey was located in an âunfriendly neighbourhoodâ. During the Cold War, Turkey was a front-line state, situated on the edge of the USSR and was perceived to be an indispensible actor against the spread of communism and has been the only Muslim member of NATO since it became a member in 1952. Furthermore, Turkey is the only Muslim country which has begun full accession negotiations with the European Union and the possibly the only country which has strived for over half a century to consolidate liberal democratic norms. The country and its people pride themselves in the fact that they have never been ruled by a foreign country and the modern republicâs predecessor, the Ottoman Empire was a land-based imperial power. In todayâs world, interest in Turkey and Turkish foreign policy is growing exponentially, particularly following the so-called Arab Spring, where Turkey is being portrayed as a âmodelâ for regional Muslim countries to emulate. Over the last two decades, Turkey hasgrown economically, as well as become increasingly politically stable (in terms of government longevity). As Turkeyâs national and per capita incomes have risen, so too has the countryâs economic interests, both regionally and globally. This has prompted several questions: to what extent is Turkey becoming the regional power and to what extent is Turkey likely to move beyond its traditional western foreign policy commitments in pursuit of its diversifying economic and strategic goals? The course is designed to focus on providing students a clear understanding of the determinants that go into the making of Turkish foreign policy in republican times (1923-present). A general survey of Turkish foreign policy will be completed in the first half of the semester. This will give students both a chronological and historical understanding of the development of international politics surrounding Turkey. This is followed by a regional analysis of foreign policy goals and developments in the contemporary period, in aid of answering the questions posed above.

title: America's National Security Tool Box
description: The primary objective of this seminar is to investigate the tools available to todayâs foreign-policy practitioner. Students will evaluate the strengths and limitations of individual policy instruments in the areas of traditional diplomacy, economic measures, military force, and public diplomacy by looking at them in the context of recent cases. Role-playing scenarios will challenge students to design U.S. policy responses to foreign affairs crises, thereby testing the application of their policy âtoolboxâ in a complex, dynamic, inter-agency environment.

title: European Studies Colloquium
description: This interdisciplinary reading and research colloquium is intended to serve as the capstone course for senior candidates for the Certificate in European Studies. Particular topics of inquiry will vary according to the interests of both students and instructors; course requirements will typically include a substantial research project(s).

title: Salafi Islam: From the Mosque to Parliament
description: Since the beginning of the Arab Spring, various Salafi movements have risen to prominence in Egypt and other Muslim countries. This course will explore the historical development of Salafism, its modern manifestations, controversies and the relationship between a religious-scholarly movement and its political involvements.

title: Emerging Powers(IBSA)in Africa
description: Since the beginning of the 21st century, the emerging economies of China, India, Brazil, and South Africa have rapidly expanded their engagement with Sub-Saharan African economies through trade, investment, development cooperation and aid. Among these countries, Chinaâs program is the largest and most visible, as a result it was dealt with as a stand-alone course (INAF 449 ) in the Spring Semester. This course (INAF 459-01) will focus on the engagement by the IBSA countries (India, Brazil, and South Africa) in Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) economies. The course will cover a number of overarching themes. First will be the history of South-South co-operation; second, the rise of the IBSA economies during the past two decades which now provide the platform on which these countries are able to engage SSA economies. Thirdly, the course will cover the engagement by each country through trade, investment, development assistance and aid in some detail. Fourthly, the course will examine the results, impacts, and modalities of engagement as seen by Africans; is this involvement beneficial or exploitative? Also a comparison of the approaches used in Africa by IBSA, China as well the traditional donors will be made. Lastly, the course will end by speculating about the future evolution of South-South co-operation and the roles that the emerging powers are likely to play not just in the South but in the global economy at large.

title: Is There an Islamic Political Philosophy?
description: The simple answer is no. The more complex answer is no, but there is something resembling it. That something, at least in the medieval period, is the political philosophy founded by Alfarabi and developed by those who followed his example in the Maghrib, Ibn Tufayl, Ibn Bajjah, and Averroes (Ibn Rushd). At the very end of the medieval period, Ibn Khaldun formulates a rebuke of sorts to Alfarabi and his followers in presenting his own manner of political analysis. Jurists, theologians, and princely advisors also contribute to the development of political thought and deserve attention. To grasp the contours of the complex answer, it is necessary to examine writings by these different thinkers and some secondary literature that addresses the question. Fortunately, translations of the major texts are now available in English.

title: International Relations of ME
description: INAF460: International Relations of the Middle East. Professor Mehran Kamrava This course examines the causes and consequences of international developments in the Middle East since the end of World War II. Contemporary international relations in the Middle East have been shaped by three dominant factors: domestic political dynamics within each Middle Eastern country; the Arab-Israeli and more recently the Palestinian-Israeli conflicts; and superpower politics in the region, involving Great Britain, the former Soviet Union, the United States, and other aspiring global powers. The course examines the causes and effects of the interplay of these three broad factors on the international relations of the Middle East both within the various state and non-state actors located inside the region and between them and the larger international community.

title: US-China Relations
description: This course examines the evolution of US-China relations in the 20th and 21st centuries. Students will develop an understanding of critical historical junctures and important themes in US-China relations. Key themes include diplomacy, security, trade, energy, culture, human rights and Taiwan. The course will address both Chinese and American perspectives on this important bilateral relationship.

title: Govt/Politics of Turkey
description: This course proposes to examine in depth some of the major debates and issues faced by the citizens of the Turkish Republic at the present time. In doing so this course will briefly examine the origins of the modern Turkish State with a focus on the origins of Kemalism and will give particular emphasis to the interplay of domestic and international agendas in the larger framework of the current Turkish debates on such topics as accession negotiations to join the European Union, the Kurdish Opening, and other key domestic political changes during the multiparty era.

title: Medicine, Science & Power in S Asia
description: This advanced-level seminar examines the ways in which cultural anthropologists and historians have analyzed medical knowledge and practice in colonial and postcolonial South Asia. The course begins with an exploration of colonial and nationalist debates about what constitutes medicine and science and the development of institutions of biomedicine and âtraditional Indian systems of medicineâ (Ayurveda, Unani, Siddha). We then consider transformations of Ayurveda, yoga, and indigenous pharmaceuticals in the context of postcolonial globalization and consumerism. Next, the course will examine the biomedicalization of pregnancy and birth as well as aging and dying, emphasizing how global medical systems of knowledge and practice become localized in the South Asian context. The course also considers how global health policies and programs (such as those designed to prevent and treat HIV/AIDS) become localized and have socially and culturally distinctive consequences when implemented. Finally, we will examine the politics and debates surrounding the ways in which bodies and body parts (such as kidneys, embryos, wombs, blood, and genes) in South Asia become âbioavailableâ for global and national medical and pharmaceutical research, markets, and healthcare interventions in the 21st century.

title: The Chinese Economy
description: The Chinese Economy: Transition to the Market, Economic Development, and Integration into the Global Economy. The course analyzes Chinaâs economic transformation and growth with particular emphasis on Chinaâs economic reforms since 1978, including the restructuring of its agricultural, industrial, and services sectors; the evolution of its financial, fiscal, and legal systems; and the roles of trade, foreign investment, and technology in driving Chinaâs economic advance.

title: Islam & Study/Mslm-Chrstn Rel
description: The course will provide an overview of the study of the history of the encounter between Christians and Muslims over fourteen centuries, a history that has been marked by conflict and cooperation, diatribe and dialogue, hatred and tolerance, hostility and personal friendships. It will focus on particular periods that are perceived as having particular impact in shaping the relationship.

title: American Entrepreneurship in China
description: This course looks at China's relative might in the global economy through the perspective of business on the ground: How stuff actually gets made in China -- from oil rigs to nuclear power plants to medicines -- and why that stuff is so systemically risky. What threats does this systemic risk pose to America's national interest and security? What opportunities? China's true competitive capability will be assessed as we seek to answer the question, "Can America rebuild its economy in a world with 'a rising China?'"

title: Europe's Promise and Predicaments
description: Looking back, Europeans have every right to be proud of their accomplishments since the end of World War II. Looking forward, though, they have good reason to be worried, even fearful. The sovereign debt crisis has called into question the viability of the Euro -- the crowning achievement of post-Cold War European integration -- and has exposed deep rifts among the 27 members that extend well beyond fiscal and monetary matters. To the extent that Americans take note of Europe, the current thinking takes two basic tacks. According to the one prominent narrative, Europe is now the land that time has forgotten. The world's problems and crisis points no longer reside there, and as such the United States would do well to focus its energy and attention elsewhere in the world. According to a second narrative, Europe is a ghost continent of failed socio-political engineering, a moribund socialist backwater that the United States should under no circumstances seek to emulate, to say nothing of rely upon as a partner. Neither of these narratives hold water, despite their popular currency. Global challenges and crises can still originate in Europe, even if their form and effects are very different from those half a century ago. Moreover, effective solutions to a host of domestic and global challenges can spring from a close transatlantic partnership. And Europe is no more a socialist graveyard than the United States is a model of capitalist free enterprise. Reality is far more complex and interesting. This course will examine Europe's promise and predicaments, whenever possible with an eye for productive comparison to the United States.

title: Religion and American Foreign Policy
description: U.S. foreign policy has always been influenced by domestic religious traditions and religiously informed purposes. Since the early days of the Republic faith-based groups have lobbied for or against particular policies, and American exceptionalism has been energized by a religious sense of mission. During the late 20th century the âfourth great awakeningâ intensified the involvement of some religious groups in foreign affairs, especially in advocacy for human rights and religious freedom. More broadly, evangelical Protestants, Catholics, Jewish supporters of Israel and, particularly after 9/11, American Muslims, have had a significant impact on our foreign policy. Abroad, religious actors and communities have become major players on the international stage, with enormous implications for American national interests. This course will examine the impact of religion on U.S. foreign policy and its salience in the contemporary international order. It will begin with an historical survey of religion in U.S. foreign affairs. It will then examine contemporary religious movements and their influence on foreign policy, and conclude by exploring the effects of religion on the major international issues of the day, including terrorism, war, economic development and the spread of democracy.

title: Dvmt Challenges of the BRICs
description: Although Brazil, Russia, India and China are often lumped together since a report in 2001 by Goldman Sachs famously grouped them as the BRICs and projected that they would be larger economically than the developed countries by 2050, they are very different economically, politically and structurally. The growth and development of these emerging economies raises many challenges and opportunities, not just to their own development strategy, but also for the rest of the world. In the aftermath of the current global financial and economic crisis they are on track to become larger than the developed countries faster than earlier projected. Their rise also raises issues of competition for markets, and natural resources; the role of population, education, and technology in global competition and relative wages between developed and developing economies; the most effective development models in terms of the mix of the state vs. the market, and authoritarian vs. democratic governance systems; the appropriate structure of global governance institutions and mechanism and their representation within them; security and the stability of the global system; and not least, global warming and sustainability in light of their large contributions to CO2 emissions and different approaches to what they consider appropriate burden sharing between developed and developing countries in dealing with this challenge. This course will begin by reviewing the history and development of these countries and the challenges to their development. It will then explore the issues highlighted above. By the end of the course students will have a good grasp of how the BRICs are impacting and being impacted by the world economy. There will be a midterm and a 25 page research paper. Open to Seniors and Juniors and Graduate Students.

title: Sr. Sem: Tech/Global/Growth
description: The acceleration in the creation and dissemination of technology in the last five decades, combined with the information revolution, has a major impact on the growth prospects of advanced and developing countries. These revolutions in information and technology are also increasing trade integration and other interdependences across countries and regions. The course starts with a brief overview of the role of technology in growth and development, as well as the basics of invention, innovation, and commercialization of technology. It explores the interplay between four critical elements of a country's readiness to take advantage of these revolutions: a demanding economic incentive and institutional regime; an educated and skilled population; a dynamic information infrastructure; and an effective innovation system. It uses measures of these elements to assess the strengths and weaknesses of the US, the main European countries, Japan, China, India, Russia, Brazil, Mexico, and major developing regions. It also explores how the technology and information revolutions affect four key issues of global concern: inequality, security, sustainability, and quality of life. With these building blocks and some basic assumptions about key political and economic trends, students will be asked to project how the world may look in 2020, and to propose actions to improve these prospective futures. The course is open to upperclassmen and graduate students with some background in economics and international relations.

title: Practice/Ethics in Global Development
description: This seminar combines an analytic reflection about international development approaches and institutions with a pragmatic exploration of contemporary global policy issues that face development institutions, practitioners, and scholars. It explores the framing and implementation of policy choices relevant for the global effort to end poverty. It also explores the core challenges of poverty and social justice are so significant today, ranging from security to political to moral. Starting from the foundation of the human rights approaches that have shaped the evolution and approach of much contemporary development thinking, the course will explore both what a rights based approach implies and its strengths and weaknesses. It will address the range of development topics and controversies and the ethical challenges they pose, taking four different lenses - the contributions of religious ideas and institutions, how far different cultures shape development paths and objectives, the rising focus of governance challenges in development thinking and practice, and emerging approaches to empowerment and âownershipâ. The course entails a combination of policy analysis and writing and research.

title: Economic Devt in SE Asia
description: The goal of the course is to create and enhance a capacity for understanding contemporary development issues and debates in Southeast Asia. Long-run and current economic issues in Cambodia, Indonesia, Lao PDR, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam will be examined; Brunei, East Timor, Myanmar, and Singapore will receive secondary attention. Lectures will cover the economic, political, and institutional context, draw on theoretic essentials in the areas of economic growth, international trade, and human capital, as well as confront national statistics and firm-level data. Students will acquire comprehensive knowledge of the economic experience in Southeast Asia, and develop an analytical framework to understand the determinants of competitiveness and growth in the region. Current debates on growth, distribution, and globalization will be addressed by utilizing the tools of economics and interpreting data in order to understand and make judgments about alternative development paths in Southeast Asia. To do this well requires a degree of formal economic analysis as well as carefulscrutiny of the empirical evidence. These are the kinds of exercises the course aims to make possible for students.

title: Economic Devt in SE Asia
description: The goal of the course is to create and enhance a capacity for understanding contemporary development issues and debates in Southeast Asia. Long-run and current economic issues in Cambodia, Indonesia, Lao PDR, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam will be examined; Brunei, East Timor, Myanmar, and Singapore will receive secondary attention. Lectures will cover the economic, political, and institutional context, draw on theoretic essentials in the areas of economic growth, international trade, and human capital, as well as confront national statistics and firm-level data. Students will acquire comprehensive knowledge of the economic experience in Southeast Asia, and develop an analytical framework to understand the determinants of competitiveness and growth in the region. Current debates on growth, distribution, and globalization will be addressed by utilizing the tools of economics and interpreting data in order to understand and make judgments about alternative development paths in Southeast Asia. To do this well requires a degree of formal economic analysis as well as carefulscrutiny of the empirical evidence. These are the kinds of exercises the course aims to make possible for students.

title: Business Politics in Africa
description: Many scholars and practitioners in the developing world once held that the private sector was peripheral, if not antithetical, to development goals, preferring instead statist or nationalist approaches. Today, a more nuanced approach to development accounts for the states need to rely on resources and information provided by a viable private sector in order to formulate and implement effective economic policies. While hardly a paragon of âmature capitalism,â the increased relevance of African private sectors â spurred in part by marked changes in democratic and economic institutions beginning in the 1990s â is today undeniable. We can now at least begin to talk about African private sectors on their own merits, rather than in terms of their parasitism vis-Ã -vis the public sector. Private sector activity in Africa is also deeply influenced by external actors and the culture of business is both more diverse and dynamic than appreciated. How have business politics and the politics of business changed in the last two decades? This course aims to address these essential questions by investigating how business in Africa has been historically misconstrued, as well as through examination of business prospects. This course will examine the changing role and perception of the private sector in African development, and the interactions of businesspeople and business organizations with the African state. Certainly, the private sector role in Africa has been both positive and negative. We will explore the various tradeoffs and the implications for development and democracy, drawing on numerous country and firm cases from around the continent.

title: International Business Ethics
description: International business helps drive globalization trends that are reshaping political, economic and socio-cultural values. Increasingly, many governments and civil society groups urge corporations to go beyond traditional business roles to help improve human rights, labor conditions, and environmental impacts in countries around the world. Such actions can pose serious policy dilemmas, involving difficult value choices. Should foreign companies try to influence a countryâs domestic political process on issues such as democratic elections, religious freedoms, the treatment of indigenous peoples, or the domestic allocation of tax revenue? Should large international retailers seek to impose âliving wageâ standards on their foreign suppliers? Do the marketing practices of international companies impose inappropriate choices on vulnerable consumers in developing countries, or enhance their free choice of products?
