The relationship of propaganda to traditional, public, and cultural diplomacy is examined in detail. The use of propaganda by totalitarian states and terrorist groups is also studied. By taking this course students should have a better understanding of propaganda and its problematical but important role in the world today.

title: Budget, Finance, and Public Policy
description: The processes of budget and financial management in the federal government have inherent public policy values and biases. These are often in conflict with broader program goals, missions, and workways. This course will explore the values inherent in the processes of budget formulation and financial management, the conflicts with programmatic values, and the mechanisms of conflict resolution.

title: Introduction to Language
description: This course is designed to provide students with a general introduction to the scientific study of language. Our main goals include familiarizing you with linguistic terminology, teaching both the methods of linguistic analysis and how to apply them (to English and to other languages, including languages that you might know, and languages that you donât know). We also discuss theories and research on how both first and other languages are learned by children and adults, and encourage students to think about the social implications of language use. In the first part of the course students will learn about the physical and mental aspects of sounds, or speech production. We then discuss how knowledge about the sound patterns of language can be applied in context, by learning about accents, dialects, and variation in patterns. This can involve topics as varied as how English is spoken on the East and West coasts of the U.S., or in Britain vs. America, the study of dialects such as x in various countries, how men and women may talk differently to single- or mixed-gender groups regardless of what language they use, or what President Obamaâs speech reveals about the way he wants to be seen by different social groups. After this discussion of sounds, we move on to an examination of the linguistic structure of words and sentences, building towards the ability to extract regularities from linguistic data in unfamiliar languages. We discuss how this can be applied by learning about how language is acquired and processed by children and adults. This can involve topics like age, and individual differences in cognition, for example, in working memory, as well as the study of how sentences might be processed in the brain. In the last part of the course we focus on understanding the ways people use and interpret language in different contexts. With understanding âmeaningâ as a foundation, we move on to exploring a variety of issues in language and culture, including language and humor and social rules of politeness. If you are majoring or minoring in linguistics, this course will provide you with the background you need to succeed in other linguistics courses. If you are studying a foreign language, this course will provide you with additional tools to facilitate the learning process. Linguistics, which is the scientific study of language, interfaces with a wide variety of other fields (e.g., anthropology, sociology, psychology, cognitive neuroscience, computer science, philosophy, politics and the law, and public policy), so you should be able to make exciting connections whatever your background and interests. This course also fulfils the social science requirement for undergraduates.

title: Linguistics and Reading
description: This theoretical and practical course provides an in-depth investigation to the application of linguistics and reading instruction. Among the topics addressed are: processing strategies and their interactions; the influences of L1 features (orthographic systems, phonology, morphology) on English (L2) literacy practices; an investigation of writing systems of English and other languages; the role of listening and reading; different approaches to phonics instruction; an investigation of an analytical reading inventory, including word lists, miscue analyses, fluency, and multi-leveled comprehension questions; and formal and informal reading assessments. The course is designed to allow the student to develop a draft of a case study by the end of the semester.

title: Sounds of Language
description: Sounds are essential to speech. We use speech for communication and sounds of speech are the fundamental elements of such a communication system. We consider sounds to be the basic components of speech, the way the letters are for writing. There will be no words without these sounds, just like there can no dictionaries of words without letters and no grammars of sentences without these words. All speech is built on these sounds just as all writing is built on letters. So sounds are essential parts of speech as letters are part of writing. The study of the sounds of language gives us a chance to see how the organs of speech produce these delicate sounds. We will look into the oral cavity, the tongue, the teeth and lips, into the pharynx, larynx and the lungs, into the nasal cavity and also at how the brain coordinates all the different parts of the human anatomical systems to produce the sounds of speech and the stream of speech. Needless to say any pathology in any of these anatomical parts affects the optimality of sound production. Speech and writing are the most common expressions of language The ability to produce sounds is not sufficient for communication. We need a vehicle of transmitting these sounds to the ears of the hearers and that is where the study of the sounds waves comes into play. It is called acoustic phonetics. Transmission takes the sounds out and far but again there is a need for a receiver to pick those sounds from the air and analyze them to hear what the speaker was saying, like we do with radio and television. This is called auditory phonetics. These three components, in a simplified manner, outline the communication cycle using speech. The course also attempts to give not only the study of the sounds of language which is called phonetics but also why languages differ in the number, variety and use of sounds. Each language is a special system. Arabic is not the same as English. We have to learn to figure out the sound system of Arabic and that of English to understand why they are different. This is called the study of phonology. Phoneticians ask questions such as: â¢ How are speech sounds made? â¢ How many different sounds do languages use? â¢ How does sound travel through the air? â¢ How is it registered decoded by the ears? â¢ How can we measure speech? Phonology studies how languages organize sounds into different patterns. Phonologists ask questions such as: â¢ How do languages organize sounds to distinguish different words? â¢ What sorts of restrictions, or constraints, do languages put on sequences of sounds? â¢ What sorts of changes (alternations) do sounds undergo if illicit sequences arise? â¢ How are sounds organized into larger constituents (syllables, stress feet, words, phrases)? â¢ Patterns relating to linear strings of segments are the concern of segmental phonology; patterns relating to larger hierarchically-organized constituents are the concern of suprasegmental phonology. The objective aim of the course is: a. begins to address each of these questions, providing a basic introduction to what the linguistic study of sounds is about. The format of the course is very hands-on, with extensive exposure to primary data and practice with data analysis and problem solving with the aim that the students will: â¢ Understand the basic anatomy and physiology of the human vocal tract, and be able to describe speech sounds in terms of place and manner of articulation, along with other descriptive variables (nasality, voicing, etc.); â¢ be familiar with the range of human phonetic capabilities, and the phonological contrasts and processes common in the languages of the world; â¢ be proficient with a number of tools for data analysis, including IPA transcription, digital acoustic analysis, and phonological rules and constraints. â¢ have gained an understanding of the central questions of phonological and phonetic theory (see above), and the various answers that have been proposed; â¢ be familiar with the names and contributions of the major contributors to the field; â¢ be prepared to continue with more specialized study. Text: Davenport, M. And S.J. Hannahs. 2010. Introducing Phonetics and Phonology, 3rd edition Hodder. Requirements: There will be a short homework assignment due at the beginning of every class. In addition, 4 or so quizzes will be spaced throughout the semester. Prerequisites: No other linguistic courses apart from interest and curiosity about speech and language are required. This course is required for all Linguistics majors.

title: Grammatical Analysis
description: In this course, we will examine the (mostly unconscious) knowledge that each native speaker has of the structure of his or her language. We will focus on the structure of words and sentences in particular, examining their properties and uncovering patterns in their distribution both within English and other languages. In our study of words, we will take the perspective of scientists encountering an unfamiliar language for the first time, learning how to analyze foreign language word data and how to understand the grammatical relationships that can be encoded cross-linguistically. In our study of sentences, we will focus on adopting and refining a formal model of sentence structure, using data from English and other languages, in the tradition of Generative Grammar (a fruitful line of research that has been the mainstay of most research in sentence structure since the 1950s). We will gain not only knowledge related to the structure of words and phrases, but also significant experience with the tools and methodology employed by researchers in linguistic theory. Requirements for the course will involve weekly homework assignments plus a midterm and a final.

title: Semantics and Pragmatics
description: This course introduces linguistic approaches to the study of meaning. We will cover both semantics (the investigation of the literal meaning of language) and pragmatics (that of meaning in context). We also consider the interface between semantics and pragmatics, the relationship of meaning to grammar, and philosophical issues in the study of meaning. The course will be of interest to majors as well as students of philosophy, languages, cognitive science, computer science, and psychology.

title: How Languages are Learned
description: This course will introduce you to central questions, theories, debates, and research findings in the areas of first, second, and bilingual language acquisition. For example: â¢ What stages do children go through in acquiring a first language? â¢ What are the roles of parental input and feedback in child language acquisition? â¢ Does exposure to more than one language in childhood cause confusion? â¢ What are some of the cognitive benefits of bilingualism? Can knowing another language affect the way you think? â¢ What can parents do to make it more likely that their children will grow up bilingual? â¢ Is it possible to attain very high levels of proficiency if you start learning a language after childhood? â¢ What are some of the differences in how children and adults acquire languages? â¢ How might a personâs knowledge of a first language influence the learning of a second? Can knowledge of a second language have an influence on oneâs native language? â¢ What are some individual differences (e.g., in aptitude, memory, motivation, learning styles) associated with language learning success across different contexts (e.g., naturalistic exposure, formal instruction, study abroad)? We will critically evaluate and synthesize classic and current work in these areas, as well as consider how theories and empirical findings can inform current educational policies and practices. The class will be interactive, with a combination of lectures, hands-on group activities, and instructor- and student-led discussions of primary sources which will deepen your understanding of the literature and develop your ability to be a critical consumer of research. Students who choose to enroll in the optional 4th-Credit Option for Social Action have the unique opportunity to apply and enhance their understanding of the course content and gain practical experience by working in a community based organization serving disadvantaged or underserved individuals in the DC area (for example, by tutoring ESL students with the DC Schools Project). Texts & Readings Saxton, M. (2010). Child language: Acquisition and development. Washington, DC: Sage. Ortega, L. (2009). Understanding second language acquisition. NY: Oxford University Press. Additional readings (journal articles and book chapters) will be made available on Blackboard. Assignments & Expectations of Students - 3 homework assignments (essays) - participation in Blackboard discussions - leading of a class discussion of empirical research reported in a journal article - final group project and presentation What Students Should Know The syllabus posted is from Fall, 2012. Several of the readings and the specific content of the homework assignments may be changed, but the basic structure of the course will remain the same.

title: How to Teach Second/Foreign Languages
description: This course is designed to prepare students with the basic skills and resources that are foundational to successful second language teaching. Our main goals include familiarizing you with the basics of instructional design, setting goals, and assessment, and current best practices. You will also be familiarized with the fundamentals of teaching speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Within each of these units we will introduce numerous teaching techniques which can be adjusted to apply to a range of teaching situations. The course emphasizes how to develop effective and engaging lessons in todayâs classrooms, for example through a unit on how to use on-line resources, such as Youtube and popular sitcoms, as the basis for appealing lessons. Current research shows that students tend to learn a second language best when language is set in context and emphasizes communication. Part of our focus will include understanding the ways people use and interpret language in different contexts. With understanding âmeaningâ as a foundation, we move on to exploring a variety of issues in language and culture, including language and social rules of politeness. We start with a brief introduction to what every second language teacher needs to know about the human mind and how language works in context. These will be recurrent themes throughout the units on the skills of speaking, listening, reading and writing in a second language. We will also address how to teach vocabulary and grammar in ways consistent with a communicative approach to language learning. You will be given numerous opportunities to apply what you learn through observing and reporting on L2 classrooms and developing lesson plans. Your last assignment involves developing a 5 day unit. We also encourage your active participation in class discussions. Another way you can be more involved is to post ideas and questions on Blackboard. Guest speakers with expertise in topics such as how to use the internet to create interactive engaging materials and effective use of task-based materials will also be a featured aspect of the course.

title: Introduction to Psycholinguistics
description: This course provides a basic overview of how the mind and the brain process language. We will cover topics such as the following: speech perception, word recognition, sentence comprehension, language production, connectionism, first language acquisition and bilingualism. There are two goals for this course. The first goal is to introduce you to the key findings and central debates within psycholinguistic research. The second goal is to introduce you to the tools and methods they need for designing psycholinguistic experiments. In this class, we will look at behavioral, neuroscientific, and computational approaches to language, mind, and brain. The class will consist of lectures, group activities and discussion, and special tutorials for learning to use psycholinguistic laboratory equipment. You will be required to lead a class discussion of two articles and conduct a small experimental study in groups. CLASS MATERIALS â¢ Readings for class will be a combination of textbook readings and chapter readings. â¢ All readings that are not from the textbook can be found on the class page on Blackboard: //campus.georgetown.edu. Required textbooks 1. Harley, T.E. (2008). The psychology of language: From data to theory (3rd ed.). Hove, UK: Psychology Press. 2. Altmann, G.T.M. (1999). The ascent of Babel: An exploration of language, mind, and understanding. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

title: Language and Society
description: Language and Society is an overview of topics about language in culturally situated uses in and across societies. Students will learn approaches to the study of social variation in language and develop practical skills at sociolinguistic analysis through practical exercises throughout the term. We will discuss methods and questions of sociolinguistics and linguistic anthropology, approaching language as a property of "speech communities" which are in part defined through the "patterns of variation" and "ways of speaking" distributed regionally and socially, and circulated through social interactions and social media. We will examine language in its performative value to construct gendered and ethnic identities, define social roles and power relations, and socialize children to the meaningful practices that make us competent members of a society. We will attend to the role of language variation in linguistic change, and the role of ideologies of language in producing and maintaining language variation. Students will participate in practical exercises where they will apply the theory and methods studied in the course to gain experience with techniques for phonetic, discourse, and conversation analysis.

title: Cross Cultural Communication
description: This course explores the nature of cross-cultural communication from the perspective of interactional sociolinguistics. We take a broad view of âcultureâ, which encompasses geographic region, ethnicity, age, socioeconomic class, and gender. We examine the relationship between language and culture by investigating aspects of language use that vary by culture, such as turn-taking norms, speech acts (e.g. compliments, apologies), silence, politeness strategoes, and nonverbal cues. In addition to considering language use in everyday conversation, we examine communication in institutional contexts (education, law, business, medicine). Class activities include lectures, discussion in whole-class and small-group formats, video presentations, hands-on data analysis, and guest lectures. Requirements include a solid attendance record (more than one unexcused absence will affect the final grade); active participation in class discussions; weekly reading responses on the Blackboard discussion forum; and two short research papers. There are no prerequisites for this course.

title: Women, Men, and Language
description: Understanding the links between language and gender can lead to a deeper understanding of the functioning of language, as well as the sociohistorical contingency of gender difference and its political implications. In this course, we will be introduced to the field of language and gender, overviewing important research and major theoretical approaches to gendered linguistic practice. Through lectures, group exercises, weekly readings, class discussions and data analysis, the course will cover topics including gender as cultural difference, power, and social practice; sexist and heterosexist language; language and gender in interaction (including turn-taking and gossip); the linguistic construction of masculine and feminine styles; cross-cultural perspectives on language and gender; and language and sexuality. Students will also learn to gather and analyze their own data, emphasizing the development of skills in linguistic analysis, critical thinking, and argumentative writing.

title: Foundations of Education
description: This course is designed to provide an overview of the historical, intellectual, social, and political foundations of American education. It introduces students to the major issues impacting schools and teachers. The following themes will be studied: 1) teachers and students, 2) schools and curriculum, 3) historical and philosophical foundations of education, 4) school finance and governance, 5) the struggle of equal education opportunities, 6) tomorrow's issues and trends.

title: Language in the U.S.A.
description: Language in the U.S.A. will address language issues in this country, with emphasis on language policy issues. We will examine various American dialects, with special attention to Ebonics (African American English), and also the non-English languages spoken here. You will find that matters of language and identity, attitude and discrimination inevitably come up. Part of the course will be about the official English question. Laws mandating the use of English for official purposes have been passed in over half of the states in the past 15 years or so. Legislation or a constitutional amendment declaring English as the official language of the United States (English is actually not official now) has been introduced into every Congress for the last 25 years or so but so far all have died in committee. Along with the official English issue, are some special related cases. Native American languages have been steadily dying out over the course of the history of the nation. Many of these languages would have succumbed regardless of policy, but U.S. policy on native languages was quite harsh for a long time. Thirteen years ago, the Ebonics controversy burst on the national scene, following a proposal by the Oakland, California School Board to make use of the language or dialect Ebonics. This happened a long time ago, but the issues involved have not changed, although they have lain dormant since then. Because this was a policy statement by a local governing body (the Oakland Unified School District Board), it was a matter of language policy. I have done research on Ebonics for years and have at times been involved with the implications of the language variety in education, so we will spend some time on this topic. More recently, the language of hiphop and rap music has come under linguistic analysis. Weâll look at this briefly. Perhaps surprisingly, language policy impacts Deaf people as well. Most Americans see Deaf people as people like everybody else, except with a physical handicap. They think that sign language is either a loose system of gestures, or is a way of speaking English with oneâs hands. Deaf people see themselves as a sociocultural group, with its own cultural norms and language, American Sign Language, which has nothing to do with English, although it is a language. Some Deaf people even feel that they would not want to be able to hear, since it would remove them from Deaf society. (I am using the capital âDâ because Deaf people who have these values use that orthographical convention). With this degree of misunderstanding, it is inevitable there will be policy conflicts and there are. We will take a look. Another policy issue that may surprise and interest you is official policy on swearing and obscene language. People have been arrested for violating state laws on public offensive language, and the issue arose in 2004 in connection with some rude signs and chants that University of Maryland basketball fans used, especially against their archrivals from Duke University. We'll look at these cases and discuss the role of government in maintaining decency in speech behavior. Before we can begin with all of this, we will have to review the linguistâs view of language, and some points about how language relates to society in general. These assumptions made by linguists have made them virtually unanimous on problems we will take up, and opposed to the opinions of the great majority of other people, even well-educated people. I share the typical linguistâs position on most of these matters (not all), but that doesnât mean that Iâor weâare correct. I have tried hard to include readings by folks who argue for contrary opinions and I hope you will consider these views carefully and not fall into the trap of just agreeing with the professor. The textbooks will be Ronald Schmidt, Sr., /Language Policy and Identity Politics in the United States/, Edward Finegan and John R. Rickford, /Language in the USA/, and other readings which will be on the course Blackboard web site.

title: Preparation and Evaluation of Language Teaching Materials
description: This course covers the preparation and evaluation of L2 instructional materials in use with a range of different teaching methods. Students learn skills for developing and analyzing materials in accordance with knowledge about how second languages are learned and taught. Practical classroom issues and linguistic and pedagogical rationales for the materials and methods are key considerations. The course utilizes a practical, hands-on approach to adapting current materials and developing new materials. The focus is on materials for the different skills in current use in communicative methods in todayâs classrooms, including (but not limited to): task- based approaches to language teaching, computer-assisted language learning, and content-based instruction. Concepts such as learning styles, different teaching contexts (including child/adolescent learners, ESL vs. EFL, etc.), culture, authenticity and classroom management are also discussed in this class. All concepts presented are applicable to foreign/second language materials design in general. Students will participate in instructor- and student-led discussions, evaluate published and develop original teaching materials, and give classroom demonstrations of original materials. Objectives: Participants will (a) gain an understanding of current issues at the core of ESL/EFL materials evaluation and preparation, and (b) develop their ability to evaluate existing and develop original ESL/EFL materials. In addition, participants will improve (a) their ability to engage in academic discussions by facilitating and participating in in-class discussions and (b) their critical thinking and writing skills by carrying out the class assignments. Check back soon for updated description.

title: How To Teach a Second/Foreign Language
description: Linguistics 359: How to Teach a Second Language Prof. Andrea Tyler and Sarah Young Course description This course is designed to prepare students with the basic skills and resources that are foundational to successful second language teaching. Our main goals include familiarizing you with the basics of instructional design, setting goals, and current best practices. You will also be familiarized with the fundamentals of teaching speaking, listening, reading, and writing. You will be given numerous opportunities to apply what you learn through observing and reporting on L2 classrooms and developing lesson plans. Your last assignment involves developing a 5 day unit. We also encourage your active participation in class discussions. Another way you can be more involved is to post ideas and questions on Blackboard. Guest speakers with expertise in topics such as how to use the internet to create interactive engaging materials and effective use of task-based materials will also be a featured aspect of the course. Learning Goals Students will demonstrate ability to : Students will be assessed by: 1 Analyze, critically evaluate, and integrate current research in SLA connected to language teaching applications Participation in BB and class discussions on readings concerning communicative and task-based approaches to language and performance on classroom observations and lesson plans. 2 Identify and assess the importance of the relationships between language and a variety of social contexts. Participation in Bb and class discussions on language in context and performance on lesson plans. 3 Assess the complexity and underlying systematicity of language. Demonstrated in participation on BB and class discussions and in their lesson plans and group project which will include some component of grammar intervention. 4 Identify and apply their knowledge of how languages are structured and how they convey meaning. Participation in BB and class discussions on language in context and on their observation reports and lesson plans which should be framed in terms of a communicative approach to language learning. 5 Demonstrate familiarity with readings and approaches related to the analysis of language. Demonstrated knowledge in lesson plans and classroom observation reports. 6 Demonstrate familiarity with how first and second languages are learned; how 1st and 2nd language learning differ; how learning in an EFL situation differs from an ESL situation. Participation in Bb and class discussions on L1 and L2 Acquisition. Lesson plans and group report. 7 Design, carry out, and report an original project which integrates information from readings and discussions. Completion of lesson plans and final project. ? Honor code We are obliged by university rules to report any apparent violations of the Honor Code to the Honor Council. According to the University Honor Code (//www.georgetown.edu/honor), âWithout regard to motive, student conduct that is academically dishonest, evidences lack of academic integrity or trustworthiness, or unfairly impinges upon the intellectual rights and privileges of others is prohibited.â Violations of the honor system include cheating on exams or assignments, committing plagiarism, using false citations, submitting work for multiple purposes without prior permission of relevant instructors, submitting false data, falsifying academic documentation, abusing library privileges, and abusing shared electronic media. The text of the honor pledge is printed below. If you have any questions at all about the honor system, please contact us immediately. In the pursuit of the high ideals and rigorous standards of academic life, I commit myself to respect and uphold the Georgetown University Honor System: To be honest in any academic endeavor, and to conduct myself honorably, as a responsible member of the Georgetown community, as we live and work together. Special accommodations If you believe you have a disability that will affect your performance in this class, then you should contact the Academic Resource Center (arc@georgetown.edu) for further information. The Center is located in the Leavey Center, Suite 335. The Academic Resource Center is the campus office responsible for reviewing documentation provided by students with disabilities and for determining reasonable accommodations in accordance with the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) and University policies.

title: Computational Corpus Linguistics
description: This course is designed to increase awareness of computational tools and their applications for linguistic research. In other words, it will provide ways to learn about various tools to at least partially automate or accelerate linguistic analysis. We cannot replace linguistic intuition, but we provide students with a greater understanding of how and when to use empirical approaches to linguistic analysis. A large emphasis of the course is in how to deal with large amounts of language data and to understand practical issues in dealing with corpora, annotation and multi-lingual data.

title: Language and Writing Systems
description: Language and Writing Systems. Ling. 382 fall 2012 Prof. Solomon Sara, S.J. Class: MW 5:00-6:15 Office: 250 Poulton Room: TBD Tel: (202)687-6226/4004 Office hours: E-mail: Saras@georgetown.edu Writing : Just as sound represents language in an audible form we call speech, so does writing represent language in a visual form we call writing. Sound representation is quite uniform and a [b] will be similarly pronounced as a [b] in every language. This is not true of writing. A [b] may have different visual representations in different languages, e.g. take the representation of [b] in English and Arabic [? ] , or Chinese and ancient Egyptian or all of them together. The Course:. One can argue that the single greatest achievement in the history of human intellectual development is writing. Societies throughout history and around the globe, by different visual means, have come to similar solutions to similar problems in how to preserve a permanent or a quasi permanent record of spoken languages. This course will take a historical and systematic approach to the study of writing. It will begin with the precursors to writing and proceed to the earliest writing systems, say the cuneiform of the Sumerians, and continue on to the other forms of writing, whether logographic, syllabic, alphabetic or a mixture of these. It will consider their origins, their developments, their adaptations to other languages and their stability over time. Since writing is the preservation of language in a visible form, the course will look at the principles of invention of writing, the adequacy of writing and its usefulness. Though not much discussed among linguists, writing systems are every bit as challenging to comprehend as the language systems they represent. Most likely, everyone of us knows and uses more than one writing system. So this is a linguistics adventure into the writing systems. To appreciate the novelty of a writing system is to use it. It is useful to learn few of the writing systems and attempt to write them in an elementary fashion. Since the topic is immense and the time is short, only a limited sample may be attempted. Requirements: The course has no specific requirements apart from interest in writing systems, their origins, their complexities, their varieties, their decipherment, their future development and a fascination in understanding their unique characteristics. There are between 4000-8000 thousand languages spoken in the world. Though not each has a unique writing system, many do and so the number of writing systems is also great. One need to be selective on focusing on what is unique and original and, in certain cases, on what is necessary to be familiar with at this time and the interests of the class. Text: Rogers, Henry, 2005. Writing Systems: A Linguistic Approach. New York: Blackwell Publishing.. Projects/Exams: The nature of the course calls for linguistic projects that have to do with writing. Useful ones would be writing up a profile of the writing system of a language one knows or one for a language system one does not know. To do this, one can explore the literature and the internet for relevant data according to procedures we will develop in the course. Method of grading Homework: Needless to say, the new terminology will have to be mastered if one is to become conversant in the topic of writing. All such queries can be done at home instead of in class. Below is a tentative outline of the course.

title: Developmental Reading
description: LING 398, Developmental Reading, provides an introduction to the reading/writing process in first and second languages, research on reading comprehension, and effective teaching and assessment approaches for students from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds. Among the topics addressed are: the role of prior knowledge; interaction between reading and writing; what the research says about teaching reading and writing strategies; effective instructional practices for English language learners and struggling readers; the five areas of focusâphonics, phonetic awareness, vocabulary, fluency and comprehension; oral language development and literacy development; emergent literacy; reading and writing in the content areas; and developing authentic, performance-based assessments of reading and writing.

title: Senior Honors Thesis Seminar
description: Ling 400/500: Thesis Seminar AY 2012 - 2013 Class meetings: every other M 3:30 â 6:00 Fall: Professor Elizabeth Zsiga Spring: Professor Ruth Kramer Course description: Ling 400 is required for all linguistics majors writing a senior honors thesis. Master's-level students writing a thesis are invited to join the seminar by registering for Ling 500. The goal of this course is to provide additional support and guidance as you write your thesis. Your thesis mentor will be responsible for overseeing the content of your research and for evaluating your final paper. In this class, the instructor and your peers will provide guidance and feedback on the research and writing process, helping you to get organized, break down the project into manageable stages, avoid mistakes, present your work clearly, and keep on schedule as the year progresses. Using a textbook, published models, peer review, and workshop-format discussions of preliminary work, we will discuss both progress and problems at each stage. The seminar will meet "half-time" (approximately every other week) during the fall and spring semesters (see provisional schedule below). Undergraduate students will receive 3 credits for Ling400, with an IP grade for the fall to be replaced with a letter grade upon successful completion of the all course requirements in the spring. Credit for Ling400 will count toward Linguistics major requirements. Ling500 is a no-fee, noncredit course for graduate students. It will appear on your transcript, with a grade of either Satisfactory or Not Satisfactory, but will not count for your GPA nor accrue credits toward the degree. Readings: Textbook (BCW): Booth, Wayne C., Gregory G. Colomb, and Joseph M. Williams. 2008. The Craft of Research, 3rd Edition. University of Chicago Press. In addition to the text, model (published) research papers on topics similar to those being pursued in this class will be posted on the BB website. You will be expected to read relevant portions each week, and come to class prepared to discuss them. You will also participate in reading and commenting on the work of other students. Assignments and grading: You will not be graded on the final content of your thesis for this course. At the end of the semester, your mentor will decide whether or not your work meets the requirements for an acceptable thesis. In this course, your grade will be based on participation, ability to give and receive constructive feedback, presentation of your work, and handing in drafts on schedule. 1. Attendance and participation in class discussions (20%). Come to each class having completed the required reading and prepared to discuss the day's topic. If you must miss class due to an illness or other emergency, please contact me, in advance if possible. 2. CITI training course (10%). 3. Handing in drafts on schedule (30%). You are expected to in hand in substantive working drafts of the different parts of your thesis by the dates specified. Failure to do so will not only lower your course grade, but will also preclude any benefit you would have received from peer review and discussion. 4. Peer review (20%). You are expected to make timely and constructive comments on the work of the other students in the course. 5. Final presentations (20%). You will make a final 20-minute presentation of your research â invite your mentors, fellow students, and friends to hear and celebrate what you've been doing! TOPICS TO BE COVERED SEMESTER 1: Planning Your Research Becoming a Researcher Defining the research problem Positioning the problem: the literature review Ethics of research; Working with human subjects Arguments and evidence; Making a claim and supporting it; Hypothesis testing Data analysis strategies STER 2: Reporting Your Results Organizing and reporting your findings Creating informative tables and graphs Writing the conclusion: what have we learned? where do we go from here? Writing abstracts Giving and receiving constructive feedback Effective presentations

title: General Linguistics
description: Phonetics, phonology, morphology, syntax are viewed within the framework of contemporary linguistics. Students are expected to work on problems dealing with phonology, morphology, and syntax. The course ends with the discussion of semantics, historical linguistics, and language and culture.

title: Forensic Linguistics
description: This course provides students with an overview of the broad field of Forensic Linguistics â namely, linguistic science applied in legal settings. Topics covered include the language of police and suspects (e.g. the language of âconsensualâ searches, interrogations, confessions, advising of/requesting right to counsel); language crimes (e.g. solicitation, conspiracy, bribery, threats, perjury, plagiarism); and linguistics as evidence (e.g. identifying anonymous authors of documents associated with criminal activity, identifying anonymous voices). We will also discuss the status of linguistic evidence in court and how best to disseminate relevant knowledge about linguistics to law enforcement practitioners, judges, lawyers, juries, etc. The course will incorporate lecture, class discussion, demonstrations and exercises pertaining to language crimes and linguistic evidence, and presentations by practitioners in the field. Students will conduct a term research project and present their results in (1) a final presentation to the class at the end of the term, and (2) a final paper suitable for presentation at a professional linguistics or forensic linguistics conference. Students will also be expected to participate actively in class and complete all in-class exercises.

title: Language and Social Media
description: Drawing from Interactional Sociolinguistics, Ethnography, and Visual Analysis, this course is designed to investigate how language shapes and is shaped by society, with a focus on data taken from New Media contexts. We will examine the structure and use of language in electronic social media such as e-mail, Instant Messaging, SMS, texting, Facebook, Twitter, personal blogging, etc. The goals of the course are 1) to examine and analyze the structure and use of digital social media discourse and 2) to investigate the ways that communicating via these media shapes and changes interpersonal relationships. The first half of each class will be devoted to lecture and discussion of the readings. The second half will be devoted to analysis and discussion of student-gathered discourse. The class will survey prior research on this topic, and students will conduct original research by gathering and analyzing naturally-occurring discourse in one or more of these media. Each student will choose a genre in which to specialize for the duration of the course, presenting the findings of their research in small groups and to the entire class. Required texts: Digital Discourse: Language in the New Media (Oxford Studies in Sociolinguistics) Crispin Thurlow (Editor), Kristine Mroczek (Editor) Publication Date: October 26, 2011 | ISBN-10: 0199795444 | ISBN-13: 978-0199795444 Publisher: Oxford University Press, USA (October 26, 2011) Language and Media: A Resource Book for Students (Routledge English Language Introductions) Alan Durant (Author), Marina Lambrou (Author) Publication Date: July 15, 2009 | ISBN-10: 0415475740 | ISBN-13: 978-0415475747 | Edition: 1?? Publisher: Routledge; 1 edition (July 15, 2009) Prerequisite: (1) At least one previous course in analyzing discourse, such as Cross-Cultural Communication; Women, Men and Language; Text and Talk; Discourse Analysis: Conversation; or Discourse Analysis: Narrative; Language and Society or (2) permission of instructor. Course Requirements 1. Attendance in class 2. Assigned readings 3. 1weekly blog post summary/response for each week's readings 4. 10 field notes 5. 2 analytic papers: one 5 and one 10 pages long

title: Phonology and Phonetics I
description: This course is designed as a one-semester graduate-level introduction to the basics of phonetics and phonology, that is, to the linguistic analysis of the sound patterns of the languages of the world. It is divided into four sections: 1. Articulatory phonetics (how speech sounds are made) 2. Acoustic phonetics (how sound travels and how sound is measured) 3. Segmental phonology (how sounds contrast and alternate) 4. Suprasegmental phonology (how sounds are grouped into hierarchical constituents) At the end of the class, students will have learned the terms and concepts central to phonology and phonetics, and will have acquired basic data analysis skills (including IPA transcription, recording and basic acoustic analysis, and approaches to solving phonology problems), and will be prepared to move on to more advanced courses. In order to accomplish these goals, students will participate in daily production and transcription drills and complete daily homework assignments to reinforce concepts and skills. There will be four (non-cumulative) quizzes, one for each quarter of the course. Prerequisites and course sequencing: This course has no prerequisites, but is itself prerequisite for all other graduate-level courses in phonology and phonetics. Ling410, Phonology and Phonetics I, is the first in a three-course sequence.

title: Phonetics and Phonology II
description: This course continues the introduction to phonetics and phonology begun in Ling 410 (for graduate students) or Ling 215 (for undergrads). The four topic areas introduced in these courses are explored in greater depth in 411. These are: 1. Articulatory phonetics (how speech sounds are made) 2. Acoustic phonetics (how sound travels and how sound is measured) 3. Segmental phonology (how sounds contrast and alternate) 4. Suprasegmental phonology (prosody, tone, and stress) Students will complete transcription assignments for every class, weekly homework assignments that reinforce concepts and skills, two reading reports that will introduce students to the primary literature in this area, and one final project that will allow students to demonstrate analytical skills in both phonology and phonetics. At the end of the class, students will have acquired the skills necessary to: 1. Understand the major points of current phonological and phonetic theories 2. Follow a talk on phonology or phonetics with critical understanding 3. Record, transcribe, and analyze speech for their own linguistic investigations 4. Be prepared to move on to more advanced courses that focus on current theoretical problems and research Prerequisite: Ling 410 (Phonetics and Phonology I), Ling 215 (Sounds of Speech) or permission of the instructor Textbook: Zsiga, Elizabeth 2012. The Sounds of Language: An Introduction to Phonetics and Phonology. Wiley-Blackwell. Assignments and Grading: 1. Transcription. 10%. 2. Homework. 40%. 3. Reading reports: 2 at 10% each. 4. Final project. 30%.

title: Language and Education
description: What role does dialect play in educational outcomes? How can the tools of sociolinguistic analysis provide insight into the public discourse on education? What can concerned sociolinguists do to advance the cause of educational equity? Through a highly participatory seminar, students explore these questions to develop a more informed perspective on addressing language variation in educational contexts, deepen their understanding of contemporary issues in education policy and practice, and gain skills in developing and implementing sociolinguistic educational interventions. The primary focus of course content is on first-language education in the US context, with particular emphasis on issues related to African American Vernacular English.

title: Sociophonetics
description: This course examines the phonetic aspects of sociolinguistic variation and the social significance of phonetic variation, from the perspectives of both theory and methodology. Our goals will be to (a) acquire the phonetic foundation necessary to conduct sociophonetic research, pursuing social lines of inquiry with proper techniques for phonetic analysis; (b) locate and explain phonetic variation in its full social context while drawing on current approaches to the relationship between language and society; and (c) survey new research that addresses the intersection of the social and the phonetic. We will address all domains of sound, including vowels, consonants, prosody, and voice quality. Class time will consist of lectures, discussions, and data workshops. Prerequisites: (a) Ling 213 or Ling 510 (or equivalent) and (b) at least one previous sociolinguistics course

title: Morphology
description: Morphology is quite literally the âstudy of formââin language, the formal means of expressing grammatical and (grammaticalized) conceptual categories encoded in actual and potential word-signs. This graduate-level course provides a basic introduction to traditional and more contemporary approaches to word formation and grammatical categorization. We will consider previous attempts to define word and morpheme, the notion of paradigm, the nature of lexical entries, the characterization of morphological processes as derivational/lexical vs. inflectional/syntactic, the place of morphology in the overall architecture of a grammar, and other related topics. We will also devote a considerable amount of time to working through language data in order to increase your familiarity with some of the more interesting morphological phenomena found among the worldâs languages.

title: Generative Syntax I
description: Section 01: Hector Campos Section 02: Ruth Kramer After a brief introduction to the philosophy and methods of generative grammar, this course examines some of the basic aspects of modern syntactic theory. It starts with an in depth discussion of the need for postulating syntactic structures and then moves on to examine how best to account for phenomena such as word order variation, the distribution and referential properties of noun phrases, and the differences among clausal types. These phenomena will be analyzed mainly through the insights offered by the Principles and Parameters framework. The course consists of lecture presentations, class discussion and intensive problem solving and argumentation on the part of the students. In addition to the Tuesday-Thursday lectures, it comprises a weekly discussion session led by the Teaching Asssistant; this provides an opportunity for students to ask further clarification questions, go over issues that one might feel were not covered in sufficient detail in class, and discuss the weekly homework assignments. LING/SPAN 527 is the first segment of a two-semester sequence which aims to familiarize the students with the assumptions, goals and methodology of generative syntax, while also providing them the opportunity to explore a few topics in some depth through readings from the primary literature. The second semester course will discuss the relation between the Principles & Parameters and the Minimalist framework.

title: Structure of Semitic Languages
description: The Semitic language family is one of the oldest attested language families which is still spoken today. It includes languages spoken in Africa, Middle East and Western Asia, languages which figure prominently in the religious, political and cultural issues of our time and of history. This course aims at familiarizing students with the grammatical structure of a number of ancient and modern Semitic languages (e.g., Hebrew, Standard Arabic and dialects, Amharic, Akkadian, Aramaic and others). Topics to be covered include (but are not limited to) root and pattern morphology, the construct state, the derivation of Semitic word order, as well as aspect and modality. By the end of the class, students will have a basic understanding of the Semitic family and its special characteristics. The course is driven by class discussion, homework assignments and readings. Homework assignments will possibly include fieldwork with native speakers of Semitic languages in the D.C. area. Previous study of a Semitic language is encouraged, but not required.

title: Ethnography of Poetry
description: The Text: A reading package of collection 100 poems from various cultures. Other materials: T. S. Eliot, Selected Essays (1932); S. Langer, Feeling and Form (1933); Paul Valery, Selected Writings (1964). 1. Introduction: Ethnography of poetry: Eliot on Dante. 2. Poetry as ethnography of life: (the life of emotions and desires; an instrument of knowledge, an art in the service of culture and civilization; a collection of words that bears in itself the substance of culture: Eliot on Shakespeare; , Langer, Chapters 1, 2, 3; Valery, Essays on various subjects. 3. Poetry and feeling: Langer, Poesis; Valery, On poetry 4. Readings, analysis, and discussion: From the collection of 100 poems from various cultures.

title: Diachronic Syntax
description: LING440 Diachronic Syntax David Lightfoot Description: Languages change and seeking to understand why and how constituted the beginnings of linguistics 200 years ago. Asking how a language came to be what it is was really the only research question in the 19th century. We have learned much about language and now we can address those questions differently and more successfully. We will address the questions about change through time by considering how a child acquires her mature language capacity. When children develop their native language capacity, they acquire properties for which there is no evidence in the speech they encounter; these are so-called âemergent phenomena.â Furthermore, children sometimes acquire systems that are significantly different from those of their parents and the language undergoes rapid structural change, a âphase transition.â We can understand emergent phenomena and phase transitions through a principled approach to language acquisition by young children. We will consider the methods, successes and failures of 19th-century work on language change, which was the foundation for a new discipline of linguistics and of great interest to thinkers in evolutionary biology, beginning with Darwin, and early researchers in what we now call political science, including Marx. We will then move to language acquisition, arguments from the poverty of the stimulus and cue-based acquisition. We will investigate how historical change can be understood (within limits) through childrenâs acquisition and how it enables us to understand the birth and death of languages more generally. This will involve a crucial distinction between external and internal languages and new methods for historical linguistics. Throughout the course we will see how linguistics is a lead science in understanding change and complex dynamics quite generally. Required text: Lightfoot 2006 How New Languages Emerge. Cambridge UP. Prerequisites: students need at least one course in generative syntax. Syllabus: Week 1 The beginnings: 19th-century work on language change: methods, successes and failures. Reading: Lightfoot chs 1, 2. Week 2 Syntax as a complex system, poverty of stimulus reasoning. What is learned and how. Reading: L ch 3 Week 3 Models of acquisition: learning algorithms, cue-based acquisition. Reading: L ch 4 Week 4 Phenomena of syntactic change in English: Modal auxiliaries, verb movement, be+part, change in meaning of like, repent, ail, etc. Methodologies. Reading: L ch 5, Heycock et al 2012 Week 5 Explanations for historical changes through acquisition (thresholds and phase transitions). E-language vs. I-language. Week 6 Other explanations for change: grammar evaluation, principles of change (grammaticalization, UG biases). Gradualism. Reading: L ch 6 Week 7 Midterm exam Week 8 The apparent gradualness of change; corpus work. Reading: Kroch 1989, 1994 Week 9 The sudden emergence of languages ex nihilo, creoles. Reading: McWhorter, DeGraff Week 10 More case studies: lexical passives, quantifiers, group genitives, infinitives. Other languages: verb movement, null subjects. Reading: L ch 7 Week 11 Reconstruction of proto-syntax. Reading: Journal of Linguistics debate Week 12 Evolution of the language faculty: mutations and natural selection. Reading: TBA Week 13 How diachronic syntax differs from synchronic work and how it illuminates some of the idealizations of synchronic work. Reading: L ch 8 Week 14 New methods for historical linguistics, working with corpora. Research topics: microvariation and abstract categories. Linguistics as a lead science in complex dynamics.

title: Language Contact
description: Course Goals & Description Studies of Language Contact consider how language structure bears witness to the contact situations, inter-cultural interactions, and multilingual practices that shaped the ways the contributing languages changed through time. While languages have arguably been in contact with each other for as long as linguistic interaction has been a part of human sociality, the linguistic effects of world politics, globalization and economically driven migrations make this study especially relevant for contemporary linguistics and social theory. We will examine numerous case-studies of contact-induced language change involving the borrowing of vocabulary and/or the transfer of grammatical features from one language to another during second-language acquisition and consider how these two processes create different kinds linguistic effects. We will consider institutionalized multilingualism, education, and code switching practices and also study how the beliefs, behavior and attitudes of speakers can influence the linguistic outcomes of language contact situations. We will study the emergence of pidgin and creole "mixed" languages, language endangerment and language loss, media of language contact, and computational methods for analyzing language contact in prehistory. Through our focus on language contact this course will shed light on a variety of social, cultural, and cognitive universals of human experience as well as detail the variation of particular histories and social responses to colonial and state driven interactions between cultures. Texts & Readings Thomason, Sarah. 2001. Language Contact: An Introduction. Georgetown University Press. Plus additional articles. Assignments & Expectations of Students The course will consist primarily of classroom discussions around topics of assigned readings. Students will submit weekly written responses to the readings for peer review and lead two classroom discussions on the readings. Students will also conduct individualized research for a term paper and give an oral presentation of their results. What Students Should Know This class welcomes advanced undergraduates and graduate students into the same classroom to create an environment for engaged discussions. Undergraduates should have completed LING 001 âIntroduction to Language,â and two courses from LING-215, 224, 252, 283(Sounds of Language, Grammatical Analysis, How Languages Are Learned, Language and Society). If you have a different background please contact the instructor to talk about the course: Dr. Mark A. Sicoli, mas498@georgetown.edu

title: Historical Linguistics
description: The Textbook: W. P. Lehmann, Historical Linguistics (3rd edition). Other books: Raimo Anttila, And Introduction to Historical and Comparative Linguistics (1972); L. Bloomfield, Language (1933); L. Campbell, Historical Linguistics (1998); R. A. Hall, Introductory Linguistics (1964); C. F. Hockett, A Course in Modern Linguistics (1958); E. Sapir, Language (1921), Selected Writings (1949, David Mandelbaum, ed.). 1. Introduction: synchronic vs. diachronic linguistics; history and nature of historical research; beginning of historical research in language. Anttila Chapter1; Bloomfield Chapter 1, Lehmann, Chapter 2. 2. Writing and language: written records. Anttila 1, 2, 3; Bloomfield 17; Hall 44, 45; Hockett 42, 43, 62; Lehmann 4. 3. The nature of linguistic change: kinds and causes of linguistic change; lineal and collateral relationships; the discovery of linguistic relationships and groupings. Anttila 4-9; Bloomfield 17; Hall 44, 45; Hockett 42, 43, 62; Lehmann 10-15. 4. Classification of languages: genealogical and typological; major language families of the world. Lehmann 2, 3; Bloomfield 4; Hall 50, 51; Hockett 5-60. 5. Methods in comparative linguistics: (a) The comparative method: Anttila 10, 11, 13; Lehmann 7; Bloomfield 4; Hall 50, 51; Hockett 5-60. (b) Internal reconstruction: Anttila 12; Lehmann 8; Bloomfield 4; Hall 50, 51; Hockett 5-60. (c) On the nature of linguistic grouping: Lehmann 14. 6. Linguistic change and change in culture: Sapir, Selected writings, pp. 432-460.

title: Language Acquisition
description: In this introductory graduate-credit course we survey some of the important issues and ideas surrounding the accomplishment of an extraordinary featââthe near-effortless intellectual mastery by young children of systems so complex that they have so far defied precise description and modeling by computer scientists, linguists, philosophers and psychologists. We will consider at what point children are thought to know certain things about the language theyâre acquiring (and how we think we know this); how children learn to segment the speech stream (and why this may be much harder for adult language learners); the use of special kinds of input modification (such as âmothereseâ) and whether/how this is helpful or necessary for language development; how childrenâs phonological, grammatical, lexical-semantic, and pragmatic communicative competence systems develop; the critical period hypothesis, with implications for adult L2 acquisition and L1 acquisition by Deaf children; and possibly other topics (as time and interest allow) such as L1 attrition and specific language impairment. There will be some emphasis on close reading and critical analysis of primary-source literature (i.e. actual acquisition studies), along with some data analysis so that you will have an opportunity to become more familiar with some of the design and experimental methods employed in language acquisition research. This course satisfies the departmental PhD distributional requirement in language acquisition.

title: Linguistics and Reading
description: This theoretical and practical course provides an in-depth investigation to the application of linguistics and reading instruction. Among the topics addressed are: processing strategies and their interactions; the influences of L1 features (orthographic systems, phonology, morphology) on English (L2) literacy practices; an investigation of writing systems of English and other languages; the role of listening and reading; different approaches to phonics instruction; an investigation of an analytical reading inventory, including word lists, miscue analyses, fluency, and multi-leveled comprehension questions; and formal and informal reading assessments. The course is designed to allow the student to develop a draft of a case study by the end of the semester.

title: Second Language Writing
description: This course offers a critical overview of what is known about the value of instruction, curriculum, and assessment for learning to write in a second/foreign/heritage language (all implied in the term âsecond languageâ or âL2â). It also provides an introduction to the burgeoning field of L2 writing (Leki et al., 2008). By balancing both pedagogical and research issues, the course seeks to appeal to graduate students who want to develop cutting-edge professional expertise in the teaching of L2 writing as well as to those who want to explore possibilities for conducting research on L2 writing. The course exploration of topics will reflect three distinct albeit intersecting conceptualizations of L2 writing (ManchÃ³n, 2011). First, writing can be used in instruction as a site to support development of proficiency in the L2. This is a concern that is prevalent in writing instructional practices found in foreign language contexts (ManchÃ³n, 2009; Reichelt et al., 2012), and it is investigated in research at the interfaces between L2 writing and second language acquisition (Ortega, 2013) and between writing and cognitive psychology. Second, writing can be the focus of instruction with the goal to learn to become a good writer. This is a concern typical of genre-based writing approaches (Tardy, 2011) and English for Academic or Specific Purposes approaches (Belcher et al., 2011; Tang, 2012) in applied linguistics, and it is related to the traditions in first-language writing associated with freshman composition and creative and technical writing. Third, writing can be used to support the learning of content, a concern typical of programs serving international students in English-medium universities (Leki, 2007; Roberge et al., 2009) and of writing that is integrated into task-based and content-based approaches in applied linguistics, and it relates to Writing Across the Curriculum initiatives in college first-language writing in the United States. Specific issues examined in the course will include: Â· What is âgoodâ writing? Â· Developing tasks and assignments for writing Â· Error correction in writing Â· Responding to student writing Â· Assessing L2 writing Â· Teaching the process of writing Â· Teaching of genres Â· Contrastive rhetoric, authorial voice, writerly identities Â· The role of new technologies in L2 writing Â· Measuring language development in writing Depending on the course participantsâ interests, we may include readings addressing questions for L2 writing instruction, curriculum, and assessment in contexts that serve college-level minority and majority language learners; young learners; heritage language learners; or second language users in the workplace and in academia. Course Assessment Studentsâ learning will be supported and evaluated through (1) the writing of responses to the readings (at home) and responses to peers (in class) (25%); (2) participation in class and blackboard discussions (10%); and (3) a capstone project (65%) to be chosen among the following: Â· a personal review essay on an L2 writing topic that could appear in a professional newsletter (e.g., VATESOL,//www.vatesol.cloverpad.org/Resources/Documents/VATESOL%20Newsletter%20Summer%202012.pdf; MDTESOL, //mtesol.memberclicks.net/assets/Newsletters/tesol-fall2012-r1.pdf) Â· a professional teacher development workshop on an L2 writing topic that could be conducted at a teacher conference (e.g., a âPractice-Oriented Presentationâ or a âWorkshopâ to propose for TESOL 2014 on Portland OR or ACTFL 2014 in San Antonio TX) Â· a book review on a recently published book on writing that can be submitted for publication to a professional newsletter or a journal Â· a proposal for an L2 writing research project that could be presented at a conference (e.g., the next Symposium on Second Language Writing, November 12-14 2013 in Shandong University, Jinan, China, //sslw.asu.edu/ ) Cross-Institutional Interactions A special feature of LING 456 in the spring of 2013 is that we will plan some exchanges and interactions with students enrolled in a course on L2 writing (CTL 3020) offered at the same time at the University of Toronto by Professor Alister Cumming: //www.oise.utoronto.ca/ctl/Faculty_Staff/Faculty_Profiles/1213/Alister_Cumming.html References cited: Belcher, D., Johns, A. title: Supervised Teaching Practicum
description: This class provides an intensive supervised student teaching experience in language classrooms. Participants are required to be at their assigned site for a minimum of 120 hours for 6 credit hours or 60 hours for 3 credit hours. This practicum provides opportunities for extended practice under the guidance of experienced professionals. Permission of instructor required.

title: Machine Translation
description: Course: Machine Translation - Lingustics 466 Instructor: Dr. George V. Wilson Course Text: "Statistical Machine Translation", by Philipp Koehn Grading: Assignments 30% Midterm 30% Term Paper 40% Major Topics: 1. Introduction 2. Why is Machine Translation difficult? 3. Transfer Systems - Taxonomy of transfer systems 4. Grammars and Parsers - Class develops a grammar and rule-based MT system 5. Extended Study of Syntactic Transfer Systems 6. Statistical MT - Parallel Corpora - Language Modeling - Alignment Models 7. Evaluation of MT systems 8. Internationalization

title: Information Extraction and Retrieval
description: This course focuses on linguistic aspects of finding information in collections of documents. We'll investigate various tasks connected with this theme, including document categorization, topic modeling and detection, information extraction, and term-based search. We'll examine linguistic and statistical methods involved in each of these; some familiarity or comfort with probability and statistics, in addition to linguistic concepts, is therefore desirable. There will be several small-scale assignments, and students will jointly complete a project to be decided on once the class starts.

title: Discourse Analysis for Language Teachers
description: Spring 2013 Time: T/Th 2:40-4:05 Prof. Andrea Tyler 254 Poulton 687-5755 All readings are available through Blackboard. Recent trends in language learning research show that understanding how language is used in context is crucial to an accurate description of language itself and to effective language learning. Using insights from several approaches to discourse analysis, such as Interactional Sociolinguistics, Speech Act theory, and Conversational Analysis, this course focuses on the contextualized nature of spoken communication with special emphasis on how discourse analysis can be effectively used in L2 learning and teaching. In each class, we will analyze video and audiotaped conversations, classroom interactions, and other speech events, such as Oral Proficiency Interviews. We will consider how various discourse analytic approaches can clarify our understanding of L2 communication patterns, including cross-cultural communication breakdowns, as well as inform daily classroom teaching practices, assessment measures, and materials development. A primary goal is to develop analytical skills for purposes of teaching and research. Requirements: In small groups, students will gather and analyze L2 classroom data. Students will also undertake two mini-projects that will give them the opportunity to apply a range of discourse approaches to language learning and teaching situations. Students will carry out a final research project which involves application of discourse analysis to an issue in second language learning, cross-cultural communication, cross-cultural pragmatics, or second language pedagogy.
