description: Real estate companies, banks and investment firms face strategic challenges during rapidly changing economic cycles. This course will focus on strategies in analyzing markets, repositioning troubled assets/debt, cost control techniques and managing internal change and conflict during uncertain times. The course will incorporate lectures, class discussion, case studies and guest lecturers. The guest lecturers will include; real estate transaction workout/bankruptcy attorneys, bankers, finance executives, real estate developers, market analysts and strategic planners. Topics include: â¢ Evaluating financing sources and structures â¢ Monetary and non-monetary loan default resolution â¢ Developing an asset or portfolio plan â¢ Purchasing and selling real estate in a credit constrained market â¢ Bankruptcy and consensual workouts â¢ Developing and implementing workout plans and alternative strategies â¢ Evaluating your overall position within the market â¢ Strategic planning in a rapidly changing economic environment

title: Private Equity
description: The courseâs objective is to introduce the world of real estate private equity investment to the student by reviewing the role of private real estate in a portfolio, as well as reviewing various strategies for gaining exposure. We will look at the private equity investment platform from the perspective of 1) an investor, 2) the general partner of an institutional style fund, and 3) the developers who access this capital. At completion, the student should be conversant in different types of offerings, risks and rewards, and how they may fit together as part of an overall real estate investment fund strategy. From the perspective of an investor, we will examine the justification for real estate in the context of a portfolio of varied alternatives. Then, we will consider the role of real estate private equity in satisfying a real estate investment allocation requirement. We will also consider the varied strategies available for accessing real estate exposure. And, lastly, we will review the due diligence appropriate for selecting a real estate investment sponsor (or âGPâ). From the perspective of an institutional fund sponsor (or GP), we will consider the challenges, and opportunities, associated with capitalizing an investment platform with investors organized via a private equity style fund. We will also look at the unique regulatory and tax issues associated with such a fund. And, review acquisition and asset management issues critical to a successful result. From the perspective of a developer, seeking equity for a real estate project, we will consider the benefits and risks associated with raising equity from an institutional fund. The capstone of the course will be the organization, by students, in groups of three to four, of a real estate private equity investment proposal suitable for an institutional investor. Each group will their proposal to a committee comprised of the course instructor and other industry practioners. This proposal will be accompanied by a full business plan for the fundâs general partner sponsor.

title: Real Estate Private Equity
description: The courseâs objective is to introduce the world of private real estate investment to the student by reviewing the role of private real estate in a portfolio as well as reviewing various strategies for gaining exposure. We will look at the alternatives from the perspective of both a general partner of a fund, but also from the perspective of an investor in a real estate private equity fund operator. At completion, the student should be conversant in different types of offerings, risks and rewards, and how they may fit together as part of an overall real estate investment fund strategy. First, we will examine the rationale for private versus public real estate investment in the context of an overall real estate portfolio strategy. Once the theoretical justification is made, we will examine various strategies for gaining private real estate exposure, including reviewing private funds, fund of funds, property and/or geographic specific funds as well as private REITâs. As part of this course, we will examine different fund structures, review fund governance, how the different vehicles are marketed as well as tax issues affecting each of the different investment structures. Furthermore, we will review best in practice due diligence procedures for various investments. Then we will review the implementation of the investment strategy including a review of the investment and asset management processes. Given the current state of the markets, we will examine the investment process in the context of evaluating distressed property opportunities. In addition, we will cover negotiating joint ventures, execution of venture business plans, and troubleshooting when plans go awry. Course Description for MPRE 728-01, Spring Semester 2013, Instructor, Roger Staiger: A look at how Private Equity underwrites real estate transactions and structures project returns. Financial Engineer of real estate returns for equity investors will be demonstrated and modeled.

title: Corporate Real Estate
description: Real Estate enthusiasm and education often default to the supply side. When focus shifts to the demand side it is usually from a macro or aggregated sub-market perspective. This course concentrates on the demand side in granular detail, but with the dynamics of a dual viewpoint: A. The Corporate Real Estate Professional (CRE) and his/her objectives and the constraints. B. The Owner, Developer, Investor who negotiates with the CRE to develop or lease his project and must understand these issues for successful design, marketing and deal making. Corporate Real Estate is one of the top two or three cost centers in all major corporations, but it is also the source of stabilized net operating income for any successful commercial property, and ultimately, the basis of all capitalized value. Hence the dual viewpoints. But corporate real estate is not always well managed strategically; matching operational planning with market opportunities. This presents an opportunity to expand upon current practices within the corporation or, conversely, for developers to leverage these inefficiencies to their benefit. A broader understanding of the CRE function, essentially the in-house developer, should also open up a career avenue with an alternate risk/return profile. Coursework will include case study and group project work, with an emphasis on interactive participation. We will consider a broad range of property types and transaction mechanisms both in the US and abroad, with an underlying theme of design, location (urban design) and configuration. â¢ Site Selection and Competition at different scales and across different markets â¢ Headcount driven Government Incentives and the Public/Private Partnership â¢ Structured Finance and alternative delivery mechanisms â¢ Headquarters Campus development drivers & constraints â¢ Build-to-Suite, Sale/Leaseback and Development Partnerships â¢ Alternative Workplace Design and the impact on demand and configuration â¢ Corporate Environmental demands and the Green Lease â¢ Corporate Agility, product & business cycle impact of lease term and flexibility â¢ Portfolio Management and Decision Metrics, including M&A activity â¢ Disposition, Consolidation, Reorganization and Lease Restructuring

title: Real Estate Economics
description: This course applies economic analysis to the study of urban real estate markets. The goal of the course is for students to understand and apply economic principles to the analysis of real estate. The course provides an overview of real estate in the U.S. relative to other asset categories, and examines unique aspects of both residential and nonresidential property in urban markets. Topics covered include growth of the urban core, determination of rent and vacancy, loan-product choice, property price indexes, and economics of the real estate cycle. Previous coursework in economics is expected, preferably through intermediate economics, and familiarity with basic terms and concepts in economics. Calculus is not required, but a good understanding of algebra and comfort in interpreting graphs is important. Format is lecture with occasional guest speakers. Required texts for this course are: Urban Economics and Real Estate Markets by Denise DiPasquale and William C. Wheaton (Prentice-Hall); A Primer on U.S. Housing Markets and Housing Policy by Richard K. Green and Stephen Malpezzi (Urban Institute Press).

Green and Stephen Malpezzi (Urban Institute Press).

title: Structured Finance
description: The Structured Finance Course examines how large commercial real estate financings are put together. The course will explore the evolution of the real estate finance transaction. Who the players were and who they are now. How have structures changed with the influence of Wall Street as a major player having entered the market in the early 90âs and the issues they faced during the credit crisis of late 2007? We will examine the options available and the problems currently facing both Borrowers and Lenders. The course will give a practical overview of how we finance the entire capital structure from senior loans to mezzanine tranches to equity. Guest lecturers, who are major forces in the business, will tell you their stories and impart their wisdom in this challenging time for structured finance. Upon completion of this course, students should have a full grasp of how deals are financed. As a result, the student should be an asset to their employer in this field. The course will include some lecturing, discussion and a significant amount of team projects designed to teach the student the anatomy of âthe deal.â Class participation (20%), significant team projects including the creation of a finance memorandum, as well as specific research assignments to help train the real estate mind (30%), how well the professor feels you grasp the knowledge (20%), and a final examination (30%) will serve as the learning and assessment tools.

title: Real Estate Statistics and Modeling
description: The goal of this course is to allow students to develop a firm foundation in statistical analysis techniques to include linear and multiple regression analysis. Students will be able to construct computer models to predict and understand relationships between variables with specific emphasis on real estate. Students will understand two methods for modeling and forecasting: stochastic and multivariate regression. Students will understand the strengths and weaknesses of statistics and learn to recognize pitfalls in existing statistical models. Most importantly, students will be able to discuss and understand statistics in greater depth than would result from an introductory course, and they will learn how to best use statistics in real estate forecasting. In addition, multiple types of pro forma models will be constructed. Students will construct a multifamily model and add functionality to the model throughout the semester. At the end of the class, students will have a scalable multi-family model with stochastic modeling incorporated and a waterfall distribution of equity returns on the back-end. Further, students will be exposed to portfolio management and understand the difference in project and portfolio variances as well as how to construct a simple 4-asset portfolio model. Prerequisites: None! General knowledge of basic mathematics, algebra, finance, and MS Excel. Objective: Gain a very solid understanding of the statistics and how they are used in real estate analysis. An understanding of distribution types and confidence intervals and how they are used and must be used in all fundamental analyses. Students will have a solid understanding of how to construct forecasting models including multivariate regression models and medium-complexity pro form as with stochastic modeling, i.e. Monte Carlo analysis, to analyze future risk expectations.

title: Real Estate Statistics and Modeling
description: Course Description: Develop a firm foundation of statistical analysis techniques to include linear and multiple regression analysis. Students will be able to construct computer models to predict and understand relationships between variables with specific emphasis on real estate. Students will understand two methods for modeling and forecasting, stochastic and multivariate regression. The strengths and weaknesses of statistics will be understood along with the ability to recognize pitfalls in existing statistical models. Most importantly, students will be able to discuss and understand statistics in greater depth than would result from an introductory course and how to best use statistics in real estate forecasting. In addition, multiple types of pro forma models will be constructed. Students will construct a multifamily model and add functionality to the model throughout the semester. At the end of the class, student will have a scalable multi-family model with stochastic modeling incorporated and a waterfall distribution of equity returns on the back-end. Further, students will be exposed to portfolio management and understand the difference in project and portfolio variances as well as how to construct a simple 4-asset portfolio model. Prerequisites: None! General knowledge of basic mathematics, algebra, finance, and MSExcel. Objective: Gain a very solid understanding of the statistics and how they are used in real estate analyses. An understanding of distribution types and confidence intervals and how they are used and must be used in all fundamental analyses. Students will have a solid understanding of how to construct forecasting models including multivariate regression models and medium-complexity pro formas with stochastic modeling, i.e. monte carlo analysis, to analyze future risk expectations.

title: Asset Management for Commercial Real Estate
description: Course Objectives: 1. To introduce students to the many facets of asset and property management for commercial real estate. 2. To have an understanding of the many disciplines of commercial real estate management including asset and property management, leasing, accounting, lease administration, construction management, marketing and physical operations. 3. To work in a team environment to produce a âManagement Planâ for a specific asset and present the completed product to a review committee of professionals in the industry. 4. To develop and enhance students thought process and presentation skills. 5. To provide a forum for candid discussion and development of ethical standards on which to base decisions.

title: Construction Management
description: The core business of real estate development is the design, construction and delivery of buildings and improvements to real property. This course presents the fundamentals of (1) project delivery methods, (2) preconstruction services and contractor procurement, (3) field and project management processes, (4) construction materials, equipment and systems, (5) design and construction contracts, (6) technology tools, sustainability and trends in construction, and (7) ethical considerations.

title: Green/Sustainable Construction
description: In todayâs real estate environment owners, tenants, and jurisdictions are all asking how sustainability can be incorporated into new and existing buildings. Sustainable Construction and the impact on building operation is a graduate level course which builds on the foundation of the Green Building and Construction Management courses, and allows students to understand the link between design, construction and ultimately building operations. The course will present how the design impacts the construction and operation of the building as they relate to sustainable practices. The course will cover, the impact of design decisions upon construction and operations, including cost benefit analysis of design decisions. We will examine sustainable construction practice and how it impacts on owners and tenants and leases. What state and local legislation is driving sustainable building construction and operations. Ethical considerations of sustainability practice in construction and operations.

title: Introduction to International Real Estate Investment
description: The primary purpose of this course is to learn how to participate in international real estate transactions. This translates into working on investments involving foreign investors in the United States, as well getting a clear understanding of the motivations and subsequently the cultural differences encountered of US investors investing overseas, taking a close look at development, real estate brokerage, architecture and corporate users. The course will bring in speakers with experiences of working with different nationalities to relate case studies and work in their sectors. The schedule of the speakers, especially those from out of town, will determine which class they speak at and this schedule will be constantly updated. Readings preparing for the next class will follow the sessions, topic and speakers, as revised. Class participants will be given $100 million to invest in the US, and on the last day of the class present orally for no more than 12 minutes and provide a written report on how, why and where they invested the monies, which will represent 70% of their grade. They will be asked to also present investing $100 million in their chosen country from the US, which will contribute 15% to their grade. They will also be asked to write 500 words on their favorite course speaker. Other contributors to their grade will be their class participation and attendance.

Participants will be asked to provide an outline by March 14th, 2011 of their presentations. They will also be asked to write 500 words on their favorite course speaker. Other contributors to their grade will be their class participation and attendance.

title: Selected Regions in International Real Estate: India
description: India is widely regarded as a land of great business opportunity and significant growth. It is a subcontinent with a highly diverse culture and a history steeped in tradition (as well as connectivity to the western world). Indiaâs growth over the past 20 years has been well documented, and has coincided with its push to privatize large sectors of the economy (and promote policies to encourage trade and investment). The Indian real estate sector presents both formidable challenges and lucrative opportunities. This course provides a unique perspective on the dynamic, intense and complex real estate industry in India. The instructor has had over 25 years of experience in construction management, real estate development and project leadership. He brings the perspective of an Indian-American who is sensitive to the academic requirements of the course and is familiar with the real estate landscape in India. This course presents an in-depth look at the Indian real estate market from a development and investment viewpoint. It is geared towards preparing students who have an interest in international real estate consulting and / or private equity investment. A multi-faceted approach is employed; this leverages relationships to Indian RE developers, investors and analysts. Emphasis is placed on enriching the learning experience for students by balancing primary information from Indian real estate leaders with case studies, theory, exercises, group discussions and group research projects. The purpose of this course is to provide an understanding of the Indian real estate market, illustrate how it functions and describe the framework of real estate investment and development in India. It explores financing, investment criteria, trends, legal issues, land economics, construction methods, deal structures, disposition, leasing and risk/return profiles. This includes typical expected returns and the implications of inflation and taxation. Transactions are studied across the various real estate types.

title: Real Estate Investments
description: Course Description: The Real Estate Investments course will prepare students for all aspects of making a leveraged income-producing real estate acquisition. Students will learn how to evaluate a projectâs viability by learning how to perform a market analysis, determine economic feasibility, structure debt, raise and structure equity, and perform tax analyses. Topics will include discounted cash flows, net present values, internal rate of return analyses, sensitivity analyses and before- and after-tax cash flow analyses. Distressed debt and equity restructures will be introduced. The class structure will include lectures, class discussion, readings, guest speakers, preparation & discussion of case studies, and a team project. Students will engage in actual, current investment and development projects. Methodology: Lectures, class discussion, readings, ARGUS, guest speakers and preparation and discussion of case studies and term project. Instructors will discuss actual investment and development projects as well as stressed debt workouts.

title: Negotiations
description: Overview: This course will cover approaches for managing differences between individuals and groups as seen through the lens of negotiation. Students will investigate models of negotiation decision-making. The goal of the course is to provide students both a conceptual and practical grasp of negotiating concepts which are applicable in their professional and personal lives. Principal focus will be on interest-based negotiation with consideration given to the psychological underpinnings of difficult conversations. This approach is now taught in business schools, law schools and school of public policy and widely taught as better business practice to profit and non-profit groups. It has also been the basis for resolving violent conflicts. Students will explore, through role exercises, two and multi-party negotiations. Focus will be both on the importance of substance and procedural considerations in complex negotiation. The conceptual framework is applicable in real estate negotiations as well as in other public policy and personal settings. Please Note: This course is based upon experiential learning. Thus a substantial amount of personal learning is accomplished through participation in and critique of class negotiation exercises. The value of the exercise is often dependent on the participation of your fellow students. Therefore, students should make every effort to attend all classes.

title: Independent Study
description: The Independent Study course, MPRE 901, is designed to accommodate students who wish to pursue real estate-related academic endeavors which, in some manner, cannot readily covered by conventional MPS/RE courses. In appropriate circumstances, students doing internships may receive Independent Study credit for deliverables created in the course of their internship. Independent Study also provides flexibility for students who have been detailed to another city, are pursuing international real estate research, have a prolonged illness, and in similar situations. A student who wishes to take MPRE 901 should approach the MPS/RE Associate Dean as early as possible prior to the semester in which Independent Study is contemplated. The student must submit a brief proposal for the studentâs Independent Study project, which the MPS/RE Associate Dean must approve. In conjunction with other MPS/RE Faculty members as appropriate, the MPS/RE Dean will determine the appropriate deliverables from the student, and will assign the most appropriate MPS/RE Faculty member for the project that the student proposes. A student may only take MPRE 901 once.

title: Independent Study
description: Spring Independent Study: The NAIOP Independent Study Real Estate Case Competition, NAIOP Maryland/DC Capital Challenge course offers MPS/RE students the opportunity to participate in a real estate case competition generously sponsored by the local chapter of one of the leading commercial real estate trade associations. In addition to the Georgetown MPS/RE program, teams from the graduate real estate programs of the University of Maryland and George Washington University are participating. Working in teams, students on the Georgetown MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team visit the project Site, analyze its current utilization, assess competitive sites in the regional submarket, and develop alternative plans for putting the Site to its highest and best commercial real estate use. Students conduct substantial research, write reports on their research findings, and apply that research to developing such plans. A group of top industry advisors who are provided by NAIOP Maryland/DC advises and assists the MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team in developing its Case. Industry advisors include architects, developers, financiers, engineers, and land use attorneys. This is an incredible opportunity for students to work closely with some of the top real estate professionals in Greater Washington. The MPS/RE Capital Challenge Team presents its ultimate findings to the panel of NAIOP Capital Challenge judges, hopefully emerging victorious. Go Hoya Capital Challenge Team!!!! The subject of the upcoming Case Competition is forthcoming. Additional Independent Study Course options: The Independent Study course, MPRE 901, is designed to accommodate students who wish to pursue real estate-related academic endeavors which in some manner cannot readily covered by conventional MPS/RE courses. In appropriate circumstances, students doing internships may receive Independent Study credit for deliverables created in the course of their internship. Independent Study also provides flexibility for students who have been detailed to another city, are pursuing international real estate research, have a prolonged illness, and in similar situations. A student who wishes to take MPRE 901 should approach the MPS/RE Associate Dean as early as possible prior to the semester in which Independent Study is contemplated. The student must submit a brief proposal for the studentâs Independent Study project, which the MPS/RE Associate Dean must approve. In conjunction with other MPS/RE Faculty members as appropriate, the MPS/RE Dean will determine the appropriate deliverables from the student, and will assign the most appropriate MPS/RE Faculty member for the project that the student proposes. A student may only take MPRE 901 once.

title: Capstone
description: The required Capstone Course marks the culmination of a studentâs career in the Master of Professional Studies in Real Estate program, and the studentâs transition from journeyman to advanced real estate professional. The Capstone Course offers the student the opportunity to explore a major real estate topic in significant depth and make a substantial contribution to knowledge in a selected sector of the real estate profession. Based upon previous courses and professional experience, the student formulates a Capstone Project topic. Under the tutelage of a senior real estate professional from Metropolitan Washington or beyond, the student researches the chosen topic, participates in any appropriate activities related to the topic, and produces a substantial work which constitutes a significant contribution to real estate knowledge. Because MPS/RE encompasses institutional, financial, legal, marketing, accounting, environmental, planning, design, engineering, and other disciplines, it is impossible to specify a single genre of Capstone Project, and the Capstone Project is thus defined chiefly by quality. With the fullest support of the entire MPS/RE program, the student has maximum freedom to pursue a real estate topic that embodies the very essence of the studentâs professionalism, and should be one of the best pieces of work the student ever does. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Capstone course to qualify for graduation.

title: Applied Ethics in Sports
description: A course that allows students to explore their own values and challenges them to codify and commit to their own code of ethics in relation to professional codes of conduct and best practices. The Ethics course is required of all MPS students and should be taken in the first semester of study. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Ethics course.

title: Sports Leadership and Management
description: An examination of management issues and organizational behavior in the industry, relating mostly to staffing, motivation, and communication. In application, it introduces theories on leadership and the tools and techniques of how to effectively lead a sports organization on a daily basis.

title: Diversity and Social Responsibliity in Sports
description: A thorough introduction to the history and socioeconomic underpinnings of sports. This course examines the issues of ethnicity, gender, and international scope in relation to the modern sports world. Through readings, discussion, and role-playing, this course explores the stereotypes, misconceptions, and social elements of a changing sports landscape.

title: Sports Marketing Strategy
description: An examination of marketing issues specific to the sports industry, based on the application of basic marketing principles to a range of sports organizations. This course will analyze the field of sports and lifestyle marketing with the practices, applications, and strategy of mainstream marketing. Course content will include a historical overview of sports marketing, delving into areas such as the team-side versus agency-side of the industry, including sponsors and corporations, sporting goods, special events, and sports enterprises in general.

title: Sports Communications and Public Relations
description: An analysis of the role of public relations and mass media in sports communication. Hands-on training in the tools and technology of sports PR is combined with an examination of the publicâs relationship with athletes and sports. This course explores the progressive techniques and activities used to promote sporting events. Discussion topics include brand awareness, media management, message recall, and the examination of four news mediums: television, internet, print, and radio.

title: Sales Promotion, Licensing and Sponsorship
description: An exploration of techniques used to sell and generate revenue in the business cycle. Topics include sales proposal development, sponsor solicitation, licensing rights, new business development, endorsements and merchandising, commercialization of technology, and corporate partnerships.

title: Sports Digital Media and Consumer Engagement
description: A review of how to develop and apply essential data in marketing decision-making in the sports industry, including database design, website development and management, privacy issues, and social media. This course considers the application of marketing principles, research methodologies, design implementation, and the analysis and interpretation of data. Incorporating horizontal and vertical distribution systems that integrate with the digital marketplace, the role of technology is explored in relationship to providing management the necessary support and maintenance of customer profiles. Students will gain an understanding of distribution channels, segmentation, demographic characteristics, and e-marketing methods and their applications.

title: Sports Business and Finance
description: This course provides a broad overview of the sports business marketplace, including the financial and accounting acumen necessary for executives to succeed in sports organizations. The goal is to provide hands-on and practical experience to real-time business challenges. Topics as they relate to these sectors include fiscal and budgetary control, ownership, and the skills used in the day-to-day operations of professional and amateur sports organizations.

title: Sports Law, Contracts, and Negotiation
description: This course provides an in-depth overview and analysis of legal principles, ethical issues, and salary cap negotiations in professional sports. Issues that are identified, analyzed, and explored include athlete representation, labor law, antitrust exemption, arbitration, collective bargaining, salary cap administration, free agency and the application of ethics as a method in the decision making and problem solving process.

title: Sports Event Planning and Facility Management
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the complexity involved in major sports events and facilities in relation to their social, cultural, and global environment. Featured topics include the analysis of goals and resources, the development of the revenue streams, design and planning, scheduling and operations, staff management, and the implementation of action deadlines.

title: Economics, Brand Management and Under Armour
description: An economic analysis of a variety of issues affecting the sports industry, including ticket pricing, luxury taxes, competitive balance, apparel and retail strategies, revenue sharing, the impacts and rationale of government subsidies, and the global currency marketplace.

title: Internship I
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry.

title: Internship II
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry.

title: NCAA Tournament in DC with Georgetown
description: The Business and Operations of the NCAA Basketball Tournament This course will provide students with an understanding of the requirements and complexities involved in planning, managing and executing an annual sporting event. Featured topics and areas of event day responsibility include sales & marketing, ticketing / admissions, operations, communications, tournament services, staff management and the implementation of action plans. This interdisciplinary course will focus on the business and operational scope of putting on a national sporting event from the competition committee, media relations, pre-game and game-day execution and fan engagement. Georgetown University will serve as host of the 2013 NCAA East Regional will be held on Wednesday, March 28 through Saturday, March 30 at Verizon Center. Students will serve event operations roles during that period concluding with a final project on their areas of responsibility. This is the sixth time the NCAA Tournament will return to the Nation's Capital since 1998 and it will be the fifth time Georgetown has served as a host at Verizon Center. Georgetown has served as a host for first and second round events in 2001, 2008 and 2010 and served as a host for the regional finals in 2006.

title: Capstone
description: A semester-long opportunity for students to apply what they have learned in the program to produce one substantial piece of work under the tutelage of an industry sponsor and the program faculty. The subject will be appropriate to the students chosen interest. Board members and industry guest lecturers will visit and students will have the opportunity to present their work for the purpose of review and advice. Each student will be assisted in devising a strategy to support the topic of interest, appropriate to the course goals, by semesters end. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Capstone course to qualify for graduation.

title: Ethics in Technology Management
description: Provides a firm foundation of essential concepts, skills, and strategies to allow for informed decision-making and effective leadership. The course emphasizes ethical responsibilities and core values endemic to the professional world, cutting across several disciplines but focusing particularly on the connections between applied ethics and areas such as technology, business management, and law. You will explore real-world dilemmas and the framework for reaching ethical decisions. Topics could include intellectual property rights, government regulations, privacy, and others. Professional guests from various fieldsâsuch as business, law, and governmentâwill present case studies representative of their experiences. The Ethics course is required of all MPS students and should be taken in the first semester of study.

title: Ethics in Technology Management
description: Provides a firm foundation of essential concepts, skills, and strategies to allow for informed decision-making and effective leadership. The course emphasizes ethical responsibilities and core values endemic to the professional world, cutting across several disciplines but focusing particularly on the connections between applied ethics and areas such as technology, business management, and law. You will explore real-world dilemmas and the framework for reaching ethical decisions. Topics could include intellectual property rights, government regulations, privacy, and others. Professional guests from various fieldsâsuch as business, law, and governmentâwill present case studies representative of their experiences. The Ethics course is required of all MPS students and should be taken in the first semester of study. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Ethics course.

title: Management of Technology
description: The Management of Technology course provides theoretical and practical experience in using information technology to support organizational decision-making processes. You will examine the requirements of an organization to optimize its competitive strategy and core competencies. You will learn different tactical, strategic, and organizational factors relative to various information systems, as well as how to gauge the effectiveness of an organizationâs information system. The course addresses some of the unique issues in managing different types of IT professionals, from senior-level architects to junior-level infrastructure support personnel. You will explore tools and techniques related to hiring, developing, assessing, and retaining staffâas well as theories/methods of managing remote workers and virtual teams.

title: Project Mgmt for IT Professionals
description: Project Management for IT Professionals will master both the practical and theoretical sides of project management for IT professionals. In keeping with the ANSI standard for project management, this comprehensive course covers such core concepts as triple-constant theory, critical path method, and tracking earned value. Youâll follow the project lifecycleâfrom planning to monitoring to controlâand learn how to avoid the most common challenges of successful project management.

title: Technology Entrepreneurship
description: This course examines the startup process from both sidesâexploring the perspective of the entrepreneur as well as the corporation pursuing a model of open innovation. Based largely on case study discussions, the course focuses on intellectual property, high-tech product development, venture finance, high-tech market strategy, strategic alliances, and entrepreneurial leadership skills.

title: e-Business/Social Media Technology Trends
description: Effective corporate e-business strategies demand on an in-depth understanding of the various technologies that enable electronic business. In this course, youâll examine how emerging trendsâin telecommunications, search engines, security, portals, website and user interface design, electronic payment systems, e-publishing and digital download features, mobile commerce, and pervasive computingâimpact industry, corporate strategy, and competitive dynamics. Specific topics covered include: Web 2.0, Service Oriented Architecture, On-Demand Computing, and Open Source Software.

title: Enterprise Modernization & Technology Assertion
description: Enterprise modernization changes every dimension of an organizationâhow it views itself, responds to its needs, and conducts its business. In this course, youâll focus on the management challenges posed by emerging technologies and learn how to overcome these obstacles. Youâll explore how these technologies affect (and are affected by) political, social, economic, and cultural factors. Specific topics include emerging standards, nanotechnologies, artificial intelligence, artificial life, and expansion of the digital world.

title: Software Life Cycle Management
description: Gain a deeper understanding of the process of analyzing and designing enterprise-wide systems. This course focuses on the software engineering principles that can be leveraged for software application development. Youâll focus on object-oriented analysis and design concepts using Unified Modeling Language methodology use cases, classes and object diagrams, inheritance,behavioral modeling, data abstraction, and information hiding. Additional topics include overloading, polymorphism, and programming languages such as .NET and Java.

title: Managing Information Security
description: The Managing Information Security course is designed for managers charged with protecting IT systems, this course prepares you to anticipate the threat of Internet attacksâand develop effective strategies to defend against them. Learn the core concepts of data security, signature algorithms, key distribution, hash functions, and identification schemes. Youâll even explore public/private key cryptography, secret key schemes (DES and IDEA), public key schemes (RSA and ElGamal), and systems based on elliptic curves. The pertinent implementation aspects and current security estimations will be covered for all schemes.

title: Cloud Computing & Virtual Data Centers
description: The Cloud Computing and Virtual Data Centers course explores the emergence of Cloud Computing and Virtual Data Centers in Information Technology. The course provides a complete introduction to cloud computing, virtualization, legacy hardware and software considerations and approaches to transitioning Information Technology from legacy systems to a shared managed service model, Various Cloud Computing models will be covered including Private, Public, Hybrid and Community Clouds. Management of Cloud Computing Providers using automated tools and Service Level Agreements will be discussed, along with security and privacy considerations. Students will learn how to effectively plan, implement and manage Cloud Computing in virtual data centers and complete introductory coursework in VMware Virtualization software.

title: IT Security Compliance/Forensics
description: This course examines the various laws related to information security and protection of data focusing on private and government sectors and contemporary policy issues related to forensics. Also covered in the course are information security operations that may be at issue in legal proceedings such as the identification, preservation, collection and analysis of digital evidence for legal purposes. Legal and ethical issues will be covered. Best practices and tips for collecting forensic data will be discussed.

title: Information Assurance & Risk Assessment
description: This is an overview of enterprise security, privacy and information security assessment and management, and cyber security. This course intends to concentrate on tangible and intangible costs and examines concepts of information assurance and security risk assessment security. This course will help students understand how to assess information security risks and develop solutions. Topics covered include social engineering, risk assessment, recovery and response, enterprise security, and formal techniques and policies.

title: Cryptography & Network Security
description: This fundamental course covers the theory of encryption and standard protocols for data communications and network security. Students will learn the goals of cryptography such as data privacy, authenticity and integrity. Topics that may be covered include PKI, digital signatures, message authentication codes, hash functions, etc. An examination of network security defenses and countermeasures are also covered.

title: Threats/Vulnerabilities
description: Focused on threats, vulnerabilities, patch management, incident response, and security operations to identify and protect against internal and external threats. Various security threats will be covered including hacker attacks, e-mail borne viruses, backdoor problems, and internal sabotage. Cybercrime, cyber security, and global information security will be discussed. Students will learn how to prevent and respond to such types of attacks using various assessment tools, intrusion detection systems, etc.

title: Intelligence Analysis for Information Security
description: The Intelligence Analysis for Information Security course examines principles, tools, and techniques to analyze and reason over data in the information security domain. Based on exercises and case study discussions, the course focuses on structured analytic techniques, common pitfalls, big data issues, visualization, data collection, and counterintelligence.

title: Healthcare Information Systems and Big Data Analytics
description: This course focuses on a managerial perspective of the effective use of data and information technology to improve organizational performance in healthcare settings. The Nationwide Healthcare Information Network (NHIN), BioPharma and other health information ecosystems will be surveyed. Information systems and data management fundamentals will be reviewed. The use of databases and other analytical tools to structure, analyze and present information related to complex organizational problems will also be examined. The course includes an examination of electronic medical records (EMRs) that are used in hospitals, physician offices, integrated health maintenance organizations and other generators and users of healthcare information. Meaningful use, legal and ethical issues of Big Data Analytics will be explored as will the use of the Internet in healthcare settings.

title: Healthcare Informatics
description: This course explores concepts and activities in medical informatics, including an introduction to the applications of information technology in the areas of knowledge-based information informatics; research informatics in public health care, integrated hospital information systems and patient-specific information; nursing, radiology, pathology, and pharmacy services; clinical decision support; telehealth; and medical education. Also included are concepts related to applications of informatics in health care financing; legal, ethical, and philosophical issues in medical informatics.

title: Systems Requirements & Analysis
description: Gives you a strong understanding of the system development life cycle (SDLC). You will learn methods of gathering, analyzing, and prioritizing business requirements with a focus on the ease of use for IT staff and end-users alike. The course covers many of the specifics of gap analysis through correctness and completeness methods, the use of case models, process and data modeling, database design principles, and more. In addition, the course covers SDLC models, Unified Process and CASE tools, Rapid Application Development methodologies, Package Evaluation and Selection, and more. Finally, the course will be well-grounded in practice through the coverage of relevant requirements standards (IEEE Standard 830) and current government practices for requirements management (i.e., System Requirements Specifications (SRS).

title: Practicum: Enterprise Community
description: This course is designed as a practicum in collaboration with the Enterprise* Community Based Learning Organization. Georgetown University and Enterprise will work with students to create a valuable learning experience that merges classroom instruction and hands-on application. The course will provide a common learning âthreadâ that is intended to enhance both the educational value and community based contribution of the internship program. The practicum will use a combination of panel presentation, discussion, guest lecturers and research to understand the challenges of successfully developing and managing in the areas of Land Development, Organizational Change, Training and Education, and Program Evaluation with a Faith Based Community Development Initiative that is dedicated to growing needs of the faith community in Washington D.C. and the greater Metropolitan area.

title: Financial Analysis for Managers
description: Provides theories and practical techniques related to acquiring, accounting for, and allocating an organizationâs financial assets. It analyzes basic business problems that managers face as they make technology decisions for their organizations. Some of the topics include return on investment, theories of portfolio management, financial statement and discounted cash flow analysis, interest rate determination, capital budgeting methodologies, concepts of risk and return, asset pricing, and valuation models. You will apply the knowledge you gain to issues you face within your work environment and to real-world examples through business cases and case studies.

title: Capstone
description: The Capstone Course is the culmination of the student's academic and professional experience in the Technology Management program. Over the course of the semester, students will be asked to apply the knowledge gained during the program to a project. With a focus on technology for social good, students will incorporate the skills necessary for analyzing key issues, thinking creatively, and making sound decisions in order to develop and execute plans. Projects will address global and local challenges. During the semester in which students are enrolled in the Capstone Course, it is strongly recommended that they only take one additional course along with it. Students must earn a grade of B or better in the Capstone Course in order to graduate.

title: Globalization of Intersocietal Relations
description: The purpose of this course is to track and understand the evolution of the political, economic, social, and cultural relations of the world's people. The course will examine the establishment of national states in Europe and its subsequent global expansion, and the impact of that expansion on peoples with different political and social traditions. It will also include the impact of the world wars and twentieth-century ideological conflicts on inter- and intra-societal relations; the post-1945 transformation of Latin American, African, and Asian societies and their relations to the international system; the growing influence of a dynamic and pervasive global economy; the expanding roles of regional and transnational organizations and actors; and the rise transnational actors and problems. Extrapolating from past and current trends, the course will conclude with some speculation on the future of international and intersocietal relations.(Not required for MSFS/MA in History majors).

title: International Relations: Theory and Practice
description: The first part of the course offers a brief historical background to the theory of international relations. Part II examines in detail mainstream theoretical arguments (i.e., structural, unit-level, cognitive and world society) about the nature of international relations and seeks to demonstrate the utility of theory for practitioners. The third part of the course applies these theories to a variety of important issues such as nationalism, ethics, environmental change, security, North-South relations, and trade and development. The course concludes with a discussion of several scenarios of the future that emphasize different trends in international relations.The main objectives of the course are to provide students with a useful set of analytical tools, an appreciation of the complexity of the field and of the value of different approaches to it, and an understanding of some of the major issues facing contemporary policymakers. In addition, the course offers students an opportunity to refine presentation and discussion skills.

title: Development Orthodoxies
description: âInternational Developmentâ as a field of academic inquiry and field operations is both relatively recent and constantly changing. Successive theories have been promoted vigorously, and then discredited as incomplete, ineffective or even pernicious. The âbest practicesâ associated with each of these theories have been first widely publicized and then gradually abandoned as outdated and flawed. This course will examine the most significant theories (or âorthodoxiesâ) of international development over the last 50 years. These include classical development theories (such as Rostowian âtake-offâ and modernization theories), critiques from the left (such as dependency theory), human capital and basic human needs approaches, neoclassical theories (such as the Washington Consensus) and more recent theories of social capital and institutional reform. For each of these orthodoxies, the course will explore how development theory has shaped development practice. It will systematically focus on four distinct aspects or questions: â¢ key dimensions or definitions of development (that is, âwhat is development?â); â¢ theories of social change underlying these definitions (that is, âwhat drives development?â); â¢ critical assumptions of these theories (that is, âwhat are the limitations of this perspective of development?â); â¢ practical implications of these theories, including the bureaucratic structures, professional vocabularies, programmatic approaches, operational tools and instruments to manage international development (that is, âhow can we shape and manage development?â). Students will be expected to participate actively in class seminars, contributing one Issues Paper to guide seminar discussions during the term. Additional written assignments (including a country development assessment paper and take-home examine) will also be required.

title: Development Economics: Theory, Evidence, & Policy
description: The principal objective of this course is to familiarize students with the basic tools and theoretical concepts that development economists most frequently use both to analyze and understand the leading economic development issues and to give relevant policy advice to decision makers. Related empirical evidence is also examined. Students are expected to attain a level of proficiency in development economics whereby they can hold their own and feel comfortable discussing economic development issues, particularly policy-related issues, with development economists and economic policymakers. The course starts with the basics: the meaning and measurement of development, including how to access and use important databases, the relationship between economic growth and development, and the overall growth and development experience of countries and regions in the developing world. In addition, the course offers a general introduction to multivariate regression analysis through a workshop with hands-on computer lab experience, aimed at giving students access to the vast empirical literature that now uses these methods. The course then turns to the major subject areas, including: (i) theories of economic growth and their relevance for developing countries; (ii) growth accounting or growth empirics; (iii) the underlying long term determinants of economic growthâinitial conditions, economic policies, institutions; human capital, exogenous factors; (iv) theories of structural change, including the policies, institutions and conditions at both local and national levels that enable countries to progress from rural agrarian societies to developed industrial economies; (v) poverty and inequality and the linkages between them and economic growth and development; (vi) policymaking, the role and functions of the state, public goods and externalities and macroeconomic management including the role of central banks and exchange rate regimes; (vii) foreign trade, finance and investment in the developing world; and (viii) the role and effectiveness of foreign aid and debt relief. The course ends with an overview of the current state and frontiers of development economics. The format of the classes is a mix of lectures and seminar-style discussions. Students are encouraged and expected to actively participate.

title: Foundations of Corporate Finance
description: The course objective is to provide a foundation in the fundamentals of financial accounting, analysis and decision making and to gain practical experience in applying analytical tools to practical situations. Considerable preparation and classroom time will be devoted to exercises that will challenge the studentâs grasp of what is being learned. A two-session Excel workshop will integrate accounting and financial analysis precepts with basic instruction on spreadsheet modeling and analysis. By the end of the program, students should have developed competencies in the following area, with specific skill sets in the areas highlighted: â¢ Understand the principles of accrual accounting and how to critically evaluate the financial statements of an enterprise to make an informed assessment of the viability and strength of that enterprise. â¢ Understand the capital budgeting process, how to model the financial performance of an enterprise in Excel (or comparable) spreadsheet format, how to apply the analytical tools and decision criteria that will be introduced throughout the course to analysis of specific investment situations. â¢ Understand how different forms of risk impact enterprise and securities valuation, and corporate decision making. Understand basic decision theory and risk assessment and mitigation strategies. â¢ Understand the fundamentals of stock and bond valuations and principles of long term financing. Understand options for raising debt and equity on capital markets and the securities issuance process.

title: Investment in Emerging Markets
description: This course will offer a unique investors point of view of Emerging Markets emphasizing different characteristics of frontier, emerging and developed country indices. Analyzing asset allocation decisions within Emerging Market countries will be a key theme of this course. Economic theory and accompanying evidence will be extensively employed to identify and explain sources of relative returns between Emerging Market countries. Relative performance trends within a country, at sector or individual stock level, will also be discussed in detail. Investment vehicles available to todayâs global investors will be presented with real live examples including ETFs, Closed End Funds, ADRs or investments in locally listed stocks/bond and OTC derivative instruments. Limits to market efficiency, restrictive or unpredictable regulatory frameworks, transaction costs and ownership limitations still create headwinds to investors in emerging markets, but improvements in fiscal & monetary policies and relative political stability offer unique investment opportunities for the next decade or the next century for some strong believers of Emerging Markets story. While keeping the main focus on rationale of portfolio investments in emerging markets, the course will also cover theoretical foundations of economic growth, business cycles, and economic policy in Emerging Market economies. Recommended to: Public officers covering international markets (Treasury, FED, State Department etc..), Consultants, Country Analysts, Portfolio Managers, Traders, Asset Allocators and to anyone who is interested in international finance with Emerging Markets focus. Course Organization Each participant of the course will be assigned to one emerging market country as a Country Analyst. Country Analysts are responsible to closely follow macroeconomic, political and major market driven events of the country they are covering. Active participation in information sharing between country analysts via group e-mails is required during the course of this class. At the end of the semester, analysts are expected to be able to explain sources of relative performance of the country they covered and also show ability to provide actionable investment ideas utilizing theoretical and practical tools they acquired during the semester.

title: International Mediation: Strategy and Methods
description: This seminar explores the role of mediation as an instrument of conflict management and a foreign policy technique. Students will consult both theoretical and case study materials, and become adept at analyzing the suitability of diverse mediatory approaches and actors to concrete conflict situations at diverse stages of the conflict life cycle. Adopting the mediatorâs perspective, they will address the challenge of how to design and conduct mediation as a form of third party intervention in violent international conflict. The course will identify and explore strategies and techniques used to overcome obstacles to a mediated settlement in intractable conflicts. Simulation and mediation analysis exercises will be used to illustrate the perspectives of mediators and conflict parties, and to introduce elements of mediation strategy.

title: Analytical and Statistical Skills
description: This course is designed as an introduction to the use of quantitative methods for graduate students. The progression of this course includes the theory and rubric of scientific research designs and the practice in generating, interpreting, and ultimately employing quantitative methods to critically assess empirical works as well as informing oneâs own research questions. The goals of this course are to develop statistical literacy coupled with analytical and research abilities. We will cover topics such as descriptive statistics, including measures of central tendency, dispersion, and association; inferential statistics, including probability theory, Normal distributions, and statistical significance; and OLS regression, including the use of dummy variables, interactions, factor analysis, and extensions to regression for categorical and limited dependent variables. The course is designed to enable students to critically assess and contribute to a broad range of quantitative work in the policy setting (e.g. risk assessment, policy analysis), applied fields (e.g. development, political organization), and applied theoretical work in international affairs (e.g. academic and professional works such as Foreign Affairs). The course will include weekly exercises in the discussed analytical techniques (including independent lab work) and three policy papers that include presentations. It will include a final exam and a participation evaluation.

title: Managing International Public Affairs
description: This course is designed for students who aspire to a leadership position in any organization â business, government, or non-profit. The operating premise of the course is that understanding and appreciating the role of public affairs is as important to organizational leadership as having financial, technical, or operating skills. Public Affairs is a discipline that monitors, interprets and âmanagesâ an organizationâs social and political environment to further the organizationâs strategic objectives while maintaining its legitimacy or âlicense to operateâ among its stakeholders. Public affairs is global in its reach, but always local in its application. The course will be in two parts. Part I will cover the various sub-functions that make up public affairs: stakeholder relations; issue management; reputation management; crisis management; government relations; and community relations, including corporate social responsibility. The international dimension of public affairs will be emphasized throughout. Part II will apply the framework and concepts explored in Part I to the practice of public affairs in the United States, Australia, China, and Mexico. Cases will be used to illustrate the practice across geographies and sectors. Its focus is practical and current, taking into account the impact of technology, money, and organizational behavior

title: The UN & Emerging Institutions
description: This course is the MSFS Programâs foundational class on Global Institutions and Partnerships. Its premise is that the UN and multilateral institutions are animated by a particular form of âpolitics,â in which learning how âthe gameâ is played will benefit future professionals in international organizations themselves, governments, nonprofits, and business. The course begins with the primary locus of multilateral institutions â the United Nations â as a system any professional in international affairs should grasp. It addresses the role and reform of the UN Security Council and UN General Assembly; the politics of development, human rights and the Responsibility to Protect against atrocities, and of reform in the UN; and the UN's major agencies and programs, from UNDP to the WHO to UNICEF. It examines the impact of equal legitimacy for all governments, of blocs and caucuses in the UN, and of funding arrangements. The last 40 percent of the course addresses other institutions of global governance which are crucial partners and alternatives to the UN. Building critical thinking about the comparative advantages of various arrangements to solve particular problems, the course will treat emerging forums (e.g., the G-20), other economic, functional, and regional institutions (from the IMF and World Bank to the Arab League and OSCE), as well as hybrid and public-private partnerships (such as the UN-African Union peacekeeping operations or the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria). The course will intensively hone oral presentation and writing skills useful in professional settings. It will build familiarity with fundamental topics and concepts in global governance (treated in a take-home final exam), but allows focus in areas of student interest in UN and multilateral affairs, especially through short papers.

title: Trade Negotiations & Commercial Diplomacy
description: Trade Negotiations and Commercial Diplomacy (TNCD) builds practical analytical and negotiation skills for diplomacy that is aimed at influencing domestic and foreign government policy and at regulatory decisions affecting global trade and investment. Commercial diplomacy is the art of building consensus among domestic and international stakeholders when developing national policies that affect trade and when negotiating and implementing international trade agreements. Trade negotiations are no longer just about tariffs and quotas. Todayâs interdependent world demands skills and knowledge over a wide range of governmental activity affecting international commerce âin areas such as health, safety, and environment standards; consumer protection; competition policy; bribery and corruption laws: agricultural support programs; industrial subsidies; and regulations covering services such as banking, telecommunications and accounting. TNCD focuses on the analysis, advocacy, and negotiating in a public policy arena. These skills are built in the context of international agreements on investment and trade -related issues: in-depth analysis of the factors that bear on the policy process at home and abroad, political advocacy tools and techniques to assure support for desired outcomes, and the skills needed to complete successful negotiations.

title: Practical Evaluation
description: For each of the elements listed below, we will introduce the main concepts, discuss tools, and then engage the students in applying the tools through a âliveâ project.â 1. What is evaluation? -Vocabulary of evaluation -Challenges of evaluation 2. Evaluation design: quantitative and qualitative approaches 3. Evaluation tools and methods 4. Practical data collection procedures 5. Practical data analysis 6. Engaging stakeholders in the design and implementation 7. Planning and managing evaluation for maximum effectiveness 8. Budgeting the evaluation 9. Assessing the feasibility and usefulness of evaluation 10. Communication of findings By the end of this course students will be conversant in evaluation methodology and procedures, become discerning users of existing data, differentiate between types of methods and tools for practical evaluation, be able to discuss why evaluation techniques are appropriate to a country setting, apply the appropriate techniques to various projects, and be able to plan and carry out an evaluation of a project of their choice.

title: Small and Medium Enterprise Development
description: Pre-Requisite: For this course, microenterprise development coursework or relevant experience is desirable but not required. Statistics show that small and medium enterprises represent a high share of developing countriesâ private sectors. Recently, a new dynamic has started that integrates business management approaches related to organization, process, or service models into human development efforts. Many of these new ideas are tested and promoted by small enterprises. Thus, the course will look at private sector and SME development as an objective, the private sector as an actor for development, and business management as a toolbox for innovative development ideas. The course will begin with an introduction to the economics and dynamics of the sector as well as basic theory, and then it will focus on foundations of, and obstacles to growth, including the regulatory regime, finance, training/technical assistance, market linkages and business development services. Through case studies, exposure to practitionersâ best practices, and an operational approach, the course will analyze what works and why in small and medium enterprise development. It particularly will evaluate alternative solutions regarding their scalability, sustainability, and their return on investment.

title: Economic Dimensions of International Security
description: This class will help international affairs professionals better understand and work within a traditional gap between international security and economic affairs, focusing on economic constraints and opportunities affecting security. While security and economics too often exist in separate worlds or are compartmentalized-in both academia and government-we will focus on their intersections, constitutive effects, and costs and benefits that economics has on international security. We will draw upon the alternative schools of thought to analyze recent historical and contemporary global cases, examining the motivations, ends, and means of international actors as well as the strategic environment shaping their choices. Designed primarily for those interested in economics but working in political or security fields, this course will also directly address tools and lessons for policymakers and practitioners.

title: InformationTechnologies - Innovation & Society
description: Description. This course explores digital information and communication technologies (ICTs) in their âhumanâ and âglobalâ dimensions, paying particular attention to the mutual, constant co-shaping of the technical and the social in ICT-related innovation contexts. It draws from an interdisciplinary perspective â including elements of science and technology studies, sociology of socio-technical controversies and contentious politics, sociology of media, international communication, and international law. Objectives. The course aims at providing students with a postgraduate-level understanding of: the creation and production of digital ICTs and new media; the social, economic, and cultural forces that shape ICTs; information policy, and the governance of digital content and infrastructure; the impact of ICTs on the economic organization of societies, and on social inequality; the relation between users and ICT/new media content; how social movements and organized civil society use ICTs for social change. Expected outcomes. Learning outcomes of the course include: developing an understanding of the current debates on technology and society; applying academic concepts and case-specific knowledge on ICTs and new media to the analysis of current debates and issues; critically evaluating the impact and role of ICTs and new media on the social, economic and political spheres; identifying key actors, historical moments, and processes in the analysis of technological development, production and governance of digital technology and infrastructure; discussing the (inter)relationship between national, local and global ICT-related innovation processes. Requirements. The course includes a mix of class discussions on the basis of the reading list, and student presentations. Students will be expected to work on: weekly discussion questions, to be submitted the day before each class; case studies addressed in small teams, whose topics will be discussed with the instructor in the first weeks of the course, and will be presented to the class at its end; a mid-term, take-home 1,500 word exam; a final essay of about 3,500 words, on a topic of the studentâs choice.

title: Petroleum in the Global Economy
description: This course aims to present students with an introduction to the key issues and challenges for business leaders in the petroleum industry - and those who seek to regulate or interact with them. The oil and gas industry is arguably the most genuinely global in operation today. Companies in the industry span the full range from ânational championâ state owned companies and the largest multinationals to tiny independents. Its proponents argue its products are essential to modern life; yet companies in the industry are also often controversial and at times despised. The course will deal with major policy questions such as pricing, resource exploitation in less developed countries, climate change and energy security. It will approach these issues through the lens of the business participantsâ concerns and priorities, via an in depth understanding of their approach to investment, portfolio design and risk management, as well as the external environment in which the industry operates. The first half of this program will explore the context of the industry, the business approach of those involved, and how it is grappling with the changes brought about by globalization, new technologies, environmental regulation and a volatile commercial environment. The second half will explore some major issues of geopolitics, environment and security as they impact the industry Course Learning Objectives and Outcomes: The course is geared toward non-technical specialists, i.e., students are not required to have substantial knowledge of engineering, chemistry, economics, finance, etc. although aspects of these disciplines will be woven into the work program. Generally, at the end of this course students should have increased understanding of the realities of the energy business and be comfortable advising senior decision-makers and leaders in business and government on a variety of policy and strategic questions related to the industry. They will have gained an understanding of decision making processes and factors for major players in the industry and how they impact on (and are impacted by) the external environment.

title: Emerging Issues in African Development
description: The course will survey the development of the Sub-Saharan African economies since independence. The focus will be on economic development, but aims to place the subject in its broader political, social and cultural context. The approach will be historical: starting with a brief review of the colonial inheritance, then covering the largely optimistic period to the early 1970s, moving on to the problems and stagnation of the 1980s, followed by the slow recovery of the 1990s which has accelerated in the first half of this decade. The survey will conclude by looking at where Africa is today and the prospects going forward. The course will explore key issues and themes that marked each period and try to piece together an overall story concerning where African economies have been and where they are going.

title: The Political Economy of Global Climate Change
description: The Climate Transition is the process by which humanity must reduce emissions of greenhouse gases and adapt to already changing climates. The success of this Transition will hinge on new institutions, or rules of the game, that will apportion access to the atmospheric commons. These arrangements are inherently distributional in nature, affecting contemporary actors and current and future generations. Progress further depends on new and disruptive technologies and incentives that foster the innovation and financing needed to deliver carbon neutral tools and processes. This course begins with a focus on areas of consensus within climate science, and proceeds upon this foundation to explore the following four interconnected but distinct themes, or questions: (a) the basis and evolution of climate policy (or âhow do we conceptually frame this threat?â) (b) the role of climate change in development (or, âhow do the âNorthâ and âSouthâ respond to this dilemma together?â) (c) the emergence of economic opportunities and new climate technologies, including geoengineering schemes (or, âcan the profit motive and technical âfixesâ help us avoid calamity?â), and finally, (d) the political economy of climate-induced conflict (or, âwill climate stress on resources lead to instability in parts of the globe?â). Special topics may also include the impact of climate change on public health, and religious and ethical perspectives on this challenge. Students are expected to be prepared for each class and to participate in discussions. Written assignments and a take home exam will be required.

title: Managerial Economics
description: The decisions made by consumers and the decisions made by firms are constantly evolving with the changes in the global economy. This course introduces microeconomic concepts and relates the concepts to the firmâs balance sheet and the global economy. The first half of the course introduces the economic concepts useful to the firmâs decision-making process. The second half of the course analyzes the strategies of the firm in the context of different risks â operational risks, liquidity risks, currency risks, business cycle risks and counterparty risks.
