Throughout the course, a variety of media-reported economic data will be introduced and discussed to provide the students with a useful and real-time application of the analytical tools developed in this course. The economic reports will be discussed in class during the week of their public release. Upon the completion of the course, students will be able to: â¢ Understand economic concepts such as pricing, costs, consumer demand, market power, competitive markets, and strategic interactions. â¢ The relationship between these concepts and the firmâs balance sheet. â¢ The analytical tools required to evaluate projects and firms in a global economy. â¢ The tradeoffs made by managers between revenue growth and cost cutting through the use of case studies. â¢ The impact of economic news (e.g. inflation, employment, consumer confidence announcements) on the decisions of the firm. â¢ Identify and analyze risks to the firm (e.g., operational risks, liquidity risks, currency risks, business cycle risks, and counterparty risks).

title: Business Operations in Emerging Markets
description: The purpose of this course is to help the student transition from the consideration of high-level global business strategies to a focus on more âon-the-groundâ implementation of these strategies in emerging markets, defined by the local conditions faced by the operating âsubsidiaryâ of the global firm. It will commence with a presentation of concepts, in which we will look at the operational, marketing, strategic and investment challenges of subsidiaries of multinational corporations, as well as some large-scale local companies, confronting local market and non-market conditions. The next phase will use case studies of multinational corporations and some local corporations operating in the Middle East, China, India, Africa, Russia, Central Asia, the Caucasus, and Latin America, to assist in an understanding of the challenges of operating businesses in developing countries. The courseâs last phase will involve a simulation exercise in which students will develop an operational strategy for a business unit operating in a developing market. Students will build the business skill-sets necessary for conducting situational analyses, making business recommendations, and developing an operation plan for firms doing business in developing country environments.

title: Public Diplomacy
description: This course examines concepts, practices, institutions, and critical issues in public diplomacy. It is intended for students considering careers in government, international organizations, the private sector, and civil society. The course assumes the central importance of a public dimension in today's diplomacy and that diplomatic practice is changing in fundamental ways. There are more diplomatic actors dealing with a larger number of issues. More diplomatic engagement occurs in public spaces. Information technologies are accelerating the speed of diplomacy and transforming its forms and core functions. Diplomats need street smarts and media skills. Diplomacy increasingly is âwhole of governmentâ in concept and practice. Public diplomacy is an instrument used by states and non-state actors to understand cultures, attitudes, and behavior; build and manage relationships; and influence thoughts and actions to advance their interests and values. Drawing on the experiences of diplomats and a growing academic literature, we will explore what this means for the changing actors, issues, methods, and mediated environments of diplomacy in the 21st century. The course will focus on five themes: 1. Perspective on public opinion, global public engagement, strategic communication, soft power, nation branding, and propaganda; 2. Diplomacy's context: networks, global governance, non-state actors, and social media; 3. Comparative public diplomacy of large and small states, and regional entities; 4. US multi-stakeholder diplomacy and the roles of the White House, the Departments of State and Defense, USAID, embassies, cultural diplomats, international broadcasters and civil society; and 5. Issues in academic study and future diplomatic practice. Students will be expected to do readings in advance and discuss them critically in class. Course requirements include written public diplomacy products (a briefing memo, speech, and strategy paper for a public diplomacy actor) and oral work (class participation, short presentations, role playing in a mock press conference, and a participatory case study).

title: Economic Diplomacy & Tradecraft
description: In the past twenty years, with the advent of an increasingly integrated global environment, policymakers, scholars, and pundits have renewed focus on the role of economic diplomacy, or economic statecraft, as a means to influence and change state behavior or to enhance national power. Economic statecraft is certainly not a new concept: sanctions were used by the League of Nations prior to the Second World War and the 1970s Arab Oil Embargo signaled a major shift in the policy environment. With Secretary Clintonâs announcement last year that the U.S. Government would redouble its efforts to support foreign policy goals through the vigorous application of economic statecraft, however, the term has gained renewed currency - and highlighted the confusing multiplicity of definitions, goals and intentions that the term embodies. This course seeks to define economic diplomacy, or âeconomic statecraftâ as is often used today, as a practical concept for the student and place it in the appropriate foreign policy context. It will examine and assess the means of exerting international political influence, both positively and negatively, through the application of key economic instruments and undertakings. We will push back against the current tendency of some practitioners to use the term âeconomic statecraftâ as shorthand for âeconomic sanctionsâ by demonstrating that a broad variety of economic measures have a role in a nationâs economic toolkit. We will examine ways that trade and commercial policies, financial regulations and regimes, export controls on technology transfer and military equipment, and aid and development policies, can be used to promote national interests â or to influence partners and adversaries. We will discuss and assess the economic tradecraft necessary to promote a nationâs interests abroad and meet with practitioners of economic statecraft from the U.S. Government. Although U.S. policy is our primary focus, we will also analyze the role of international states, partners and international financial institutions in the U.S. economic statecraft policy formulation and implementation process. In addition to discussion of trade, finance, aid, and export controls, we will look at economic sanctions, both unilateral and multilateral, focusing on the critical cases of Iran, Libya, Yugoslavia, and Iraq, and examine the practicality and efficacy of so-called âsmart sanctions.â We will consider the political, legislative, and institutional setting which affects economic statecraft decisions and seek to identify the conditions, limitations and circumstances influences its use. We will address the practical aspects of using such measures, including how best to determine their appropriateness, efficacy and cost, what trade-offs are necessary when they are employed, and if/when moral considerations should factored into the policy equation. At the end of the course, students should be able to better assess the impact and effectiveness of economic diplomacy on a nationâs foreign policy, what tools may be brought to bear, and how a nationâs diplomats and officials support economic interests and broader foreign policy goals with application of economic statecraft. They should also be able to evaluate which measures are the most appropriate and effective means to achieve specific foreign policy goals and how they fit into our broader policy. This seminar explores the role of economic diplomacy as a means to promote national interests and key foreign policy goals, as well as influence partners and adversaries. It provides students with both a theoretical and practical approach to economic statecraft, and the tradecraft necessary for its successful application, through the use of case studies, discussion and current readings. Students will evaluate the use of economic diplomacy in todayâs international environment, develop a practical understanding of the current policy, economic and institutional setting in which economic diplomacy is , and assess the use of economic instruments, including trade, aid, finance, and sanctions, to influence international partners and adversaries. In addition to class participation and contributions to case study discussions, students will be graded on 2 short (2-3 page) policy recommendation memos and a take-home final exam.

title: Corporate Responsibility in the 21st Century
description: Corporate Responsibility (CR) emerged as a major business driver over the past decade as businesses saw both the benefits and the necessity of a well developed and relevant CR strategy. The motivations differ depending on the sector, the business and the communities in which they operate and can be a combination of the following: ensuring more acceptance in a community for the business, mitigation of risks and/or serving as a driver of a growth strategy. When it becomes integral to the business strategy it has the greatest potential to have a significant impact beyond commercial interests. Corporate responsibility emerged as a concept and a field of study as civil society groups became aware of the impact that businesses were having and could have on the challenges they were facing and how the civil society groups might shape the corporate response. Civil society organizations increased the spotlight they put on businesses and demanded changes in operations and policies but in some cases they also became partners and collaborators. It changed how business, non-profits, government and international organizations interacted. This course aims to give students an understanding of what Corporate Responsibility is (and related concepts such as sustainability, corporate citizenship, âgreenâ corporations), its origins, how it fits into a business strategy, steps in defining a CR strategy, criteria for evaluating different strategies and identifying the success components in a strong and effective CR strategy.

title: Identity and Conflict
description: This course will introduce students to the concept of identity and its various components â racial, ethnic, religious, national â and explore how and why issues of identity often are at the core of contemporary international conflicts. We also will investigate the unique challenges that face negotiators and mediators when trying to manage identity-based conflicts. We will define conflict very broadly, looking not only at the role of identity in violent or potentially violent conflicts (such as the Balkans, India-Pakistan, Cyprus, and Israeli-Palestinian situations) but also in other types of international interaction (such as the debate over Turkeyâs accession to the EU, immigration and migration, and even international commercial transactions). The goal will be for the student to come away with a better understanding of the role that issues of identity play, even if indirectly, in many contemporary international conflicts, and to be better equipped to recognize and assess identity issues when they are present. Although parts of the course will be theoretical, the thrust is practical, reflecting the fact that identity issues are real, volatile, and have a pronounced impact on the way in which international conflicts unfold. The role of identity in international interaction shows no signs of lessening.

title: Global Marketing Strategies
description: Marketing strategy is increasingly impacted by the international context at every level. As global corporations grow ever more dominant within the world economy their role in shaping the future, for good or ill, hardly can be exaggerated. At the same time, competition between these leviathans has never been fiercer. Yet on top of the traditional managerial challenges implicit in balancing the conflicting demands of globalization and localization a whole new set of trends is confronting marketers in their quest for competitive advantage. This course is designed to fully acquaint students with these issues, to impart the most current theory and knowledge of the subject, and equip them with the abilities necessary to make the most relevant and effective decisions in this most dynamic of business arenas. In short its purpose is to build the skills needed to analyze, develop and evaluate international marketing strategies. It will be applicable both to those who plan to use this expertise directly in their careers and those who are seeking an understanding from an outside perspective.

title: International Trade & Investment
description: This course focuses on the practice of international trade and investment policy from the viewpoint of a practitioner (government, business, labor union, environmental organization, media, etc.) The course covers the basic elements of modern-day trade policy, including domestic policy mechanisms, regional or bilateral free trade agreements, and the World Trade Organization, as these address trade in goods, trade in services, private direct cross-border investment, the protection of intellectual property rights, and linkage between trade and social issues. The course is designed to help the student develop practical skills in solving problems presented in international trade and investment policy; gain specific experience in collaborative problem solving through classroom simulations and other exercises; and refine oral and written briefing skills critical to the success of a practitioner in this area. The first part of the semester will focus on the rules that emerge from international trade and investment and that increasingly govern global trade policy making. The second part of the semester will address specific problems in policy making that arise in interagency and international negotiations.

title: The Art of Advocacy Inside & Outside of Government
description: This course examines how advocacy influences political leaders and other stakeholders responsible for shaping international policy for managing global crisis and conflict. The course uses a case format to study examples of advocacy in five real world situations. The objective is to build a pragmatic understanding of the impact of advocacy on policy and the management of complex global problems, including famine, health pandemics, war, and acts of terrorism. The class will include interactive class discussions with policymakers and advocates who played principal roles in each case. The course will build practical skills, including: the ability to identify, evaluate, and formulate effective advocacy strategies and tactics; and the ability to develop common practitioner tools, such as concise briefing and messaging materials used in advocacy initiatives. Key requirements include readings for each case, leading a class discussion with a guest subject matter expert (to be completed in teams), a concise briefing memo for each case, and a short final paper.

title: Religion & International Affairs
description: Religion continues to factor significantly in regional and intra-state conflicts, spontaneous outbreaks of grassroots violence, and foreign policy-making of various countries, thus making it central to security, development, and diplomatic calculations. Liberal and illiberal faith-based actors â including terrorist groups, faith-based political parties, and international humanitarian and conflict-resolution organizations â are playing an increasingly more impactful role on the international stage, particularly in light of the changing role of nation-states. Studies show that global religiosity is growing, which indicates that religion will continue to factor into citizensâ demands on government and society. At the same time, three-quarters of the world, as of mid-2010, lives in countries where governments, social groups or individuals restrict peopleâs ability to freely practice their faith, according to a 2012 Pew Research Center study. Therefore, understanding the role of religion is essential for international affairs professionals in all sectors. This course will look at the role that religion plays in shaping citizensâ and leadersâ views of the international sphere, the actions of faith-motivated actors, and the role of religion as both a source of and solution to conflict. Assignments will be practically-oriented, challenging students to develop real-world policy responses to current events, grapple with challenging questions of working with faith-based actors, and assess how to factor religion and religious actors into the causes and solutions of policy matters, and, hence, develop policy prescriptions.

title: Global Political Economy
description: This course explores current issues and contemporary debates in the field of global political economy. Students will gain familiarity with debates regarding globalization and the role of the state in the market, the impact of societal groups and preferences on national economic behavior, the political aspects of international finance and financial crises, the political economy of development, the management of environmental issues, the political economy of security and the nature of economic statecraft.

title: International Financial Institutions
description: The class will cover global economic governance and assess how it has evolved in the post war period that has seen growing trade and financial linkages between advanced, emerging market, and low income countries. The main focus will be on international financial institutions and how they have conducted economic surveillance, crisis prevention, and crisis management to improve economic security. Students will become conversant in the role and structure of these organizations, their cooperation with bilateral and other multilateral standing groups like the G-7, Paris Club, the Financial Stability Forum (FSF), and the G20 that steer international financial and economic policy, and the relationships between the main actors shaping international financial and macroeconomic policy. They will gain an understanding of how global governance is changing in response to the weaknesses triggered by the recent financial crisis, and the key challenges going forward. Although the main focus of the course will be on emerging market and industrial economies, the course will also review development issues facing low income countries.

title: Strategy: Security/Development/Business
description: In an international environment characterized by scarce resources with which to confront complex problems, strategic decision-making is a necessity. Students planning to enter the field of international affairs, therefore, must be nimble in terms of strategic thought and action. This course will focus on building this strategic capacity. The course is interdisciplinary and is designed for students from all 3 MSFS concentrations. It will address the strategy-making process in the national security, development and business worlds, and will use cases from all 3 of these sectors. The course will start with the question of how to define strategy, what are the logics of strategy and why strategy is important. From there the course will deconstruct the strategy-making process into 4 parts: setting strategic goals, assessing strategic resources, mapping strategic environments, and choosing among strategic options. In order to expose students to how strategy is actually conducted, practitioners from each of the sectors will visit the class to review actual strategic successes and failures. The course will then transition from the âanalyzing strategyâ phase to the âmaking strategyâ phase. In this section students will confront current security, development and business scenarios, and will be required to establish strategic goals, present their environmental analyses, and then come up with strategy proposals. Strategic scenarios will include the U.S. strategy towards Iran, the surge strategy for Iraq, poverty reduction strategies, and Apple Computerâs strategy. The course will culminate in a strategy simulation, involving governments, development organizations and businesses.

title: Science, Technology & Development
description: This course introduces students to the transformational nature of innovation across different economic sectors, at the national and international level. Technology is the study and use of art, skills and craft to solve a problem. In that regard, technology is a means for innovation. Etymologically, innovation does not equate with improvement. However, innovation implies the creation of value added. To that extent, innovation is the main engine of development as it is mostly through innovation that a group of people would design new means to meet their needs and improve their standard of living. The first part of the course lays down the foundations to understand the emergence and potential transformative impact of innovation. The second part of the course focuses on sectoral analysis of technology (agriculture, manufacturing and services). The third part of the course studies technology in the context of globalization. It sheds light on how government policies could be impacted by innovation, the international diffusion of innovation, the global market for innovation, innovation as it relates to sustainable development and the ethical dimension of open source innovation. The teaching methodology of the class includes the use of case studies and discussions with seasons innovation practitioners.

title: Ethics in International Relations
description: Limited to and required for International Relations and Security Concentrators in the MSFS Program, this course addresses ethical dilemmas arising in international affairs careers in the public, private, and nonprofit sectors. It will offer frameworks as decision-making tools, including those drawn from natural, national, and international law, while emphasizing discussion of specific issues and cases. It will hone decision-making judgment on key problems as mass atrocities, dealing with illiberal governments, forms of slavery, gender justice and opportunity, poverty and hunger, corporate responsibility, and public health. It will explore choice of means, such as military force, counterterrorism measures, economic sanctions, use of contractors rather than public institutions, compromise, and nonpartisanship, as well as dissent and resignation in professional settings. The course will build skills of concise and effective written and oral argumentation based on ethical reasoning.

title: Development and Demography in Asia
description: In this class you will learn skills that are critical for specialists in a data-driven world: Be able to interpret regression results, and other statistical tables. Compute standard demographic measures of mortality, fertility, and migration. Prepare statistical and demographic graphs and tables, such as a population pyramid and time series data. â¢ Have a basic understanding of geographic information systems. â¢ Be able to locate and determine the reliability of demographic data. â¢ Develop research methods skills relating to questionnaire design and sampling. This course is a seminar and students should participate actively in class. That can be best accomplished by reading carefully each assignment prior to class, posting to the discussion site, and being respectful of your classmatesâ views and opinions. Students are expected to attend every class meeting and must read all of the required materials prior to class. Each student must post on a weekly basis to the on-line class site. Required readings are available on-line. Complete the five two-page policy briefs on the dates they are due. Each class will be in three parts: 1) a case study; 2) discussion of the assigned readings; 3) a lab relating on demographic data, statistical methods, and/or research methods. We will discuss demographic issues across Asia to give you breadth in the topics, and use country examples to give you depth in specific subareas/regions. Topics will include: Mortality and Fertility Transitions in Asia; Chinaâs Policy to Limit Births; Implications of Imbalanced Sex Ratios; Family Systems; Marriage and Marriage Markets; Ageing; Diet, Nutrition, and Famine; Smoking, public health and mortality; Immigration; Urbanization

title: Comparative Regional Economic Development (CRED)
description: The main objective of this course is to help develop and hone the skills needed to diagnose a countryâs major development constraints or challenges and provide realistic policy advice. The course seeks to achieve this goal by providing students with an international comparative perspective on the key development issues facing countries throughout the developing world. The course is organized around five regional segments or modules corresponding to the worldâs major developing areas or regions: (i) East Asia; (ii) South Asia; (iii) Sub-Saharan Africa; (iv) Latin America; and (v) the Middle East and North Africa. Under each module, a set of two or three leading development issues are analyzed. These issues are selected with a view to analyzing a particular issue in the context of a region in which that issue is most prominent or challenging. The intention, therefore, is to present each major development issue in stark relief and encourage comparisons and contrasts across regions and countries where appropriate. For example, equity and distributional issues and macroeconomic stabilization are examined against the backdrop of Latin America where these issues have been particularly acute, while economic stagnation, how to initiate and sustain economic growth and aid effectiveness are analyzed in the context of Sub-Saharan Africa. Similarly, how to make growth more pro-poor is studied in South Asia, and the challenges of rebalancing growth, raising productivity and moving up the global value chain are examined in East Asia. Finally, resource management and governance issues related to changing the role and functions of the state are investigated in the oil-rich countries of the Middle East and North Africa.

title: Health, Governance & Diplomacy
description: This course will introduce students to the fundamentals of global health governance, and how policies are made, argued, negotiated and managed in an increasingly complex global environment. The course will emphasize (i) the use of evidence to analyze some of the major health challenges of today (i.e., services delivery approaches; financing mechanisms), and how these are addressed by different players and at different levels of governance; (ii) the application of analytical skills to explaining the role of state and non-state actors in the current global health architecture, and their significance; and (iii) the utilization of diplomatic tools for improved governance and negotiation as it relates to health issues that transcend national boundaries. Global health is all about purposeful global action to improve peopleâs lives. It is about agreeing on global health challenges, shaping and implementing sound policies, involving diverse actors at many levels of governance, and applying diplomatic negotiation processes to address health issues effectively. The global architecture that has emerged in the last decade is one where issues of health, governance, and diplomacy come together for effective collective action.

title: International Project Finance and Investment
description: The course will focus on design, development, structuring and execution of international project finance transactions. The semester will commence with the building blocks of structured finance including discussions of the differences between corporate and project financing and reviewing the interests of the various participants. We will then move quickly into the discussion of risk identification, allocation and mitigation, which is the foundation of creative financial solutions. The second half of the course will commence with a detailed review of the concepts and use of financial analysis and financial modeling followed by a discussion of the role of contracts in mitigating risk. The second half will also include a series of case studies as well as guest speakers that will discuss topics such as financial documentation, syndication, re-structuring troubled projects and the increasing emphasis on environmental and social issues in successful project implementation.

title: International Negotiation
description: The objectives of this course are to explore and discuss the various theories of and approaches to negotiation analysis; develop an understanding of the many factors that influence the international negotiation process (including power, domestic politics, culture); and integrate theory and reality by analyzing case studies. Students also will participate in a negotiation simulation over two class periods. The ultimate goal of the course is to enhance our ability to understand the factors that affect the process and outcome of international negotiations, and thereby enhance our ability to identify conflicts that are ripe for negotiation as well as assess the dynamics of on-going negotiations. A better understanding of the negotiation process and the factors that determine may even make us more effective negotiators.

title: Congressional Decision-Making and U.S. Foreign Policy
description: This seminar will examine how Congress participates â or does not participate â in U.S. foreign policy, and look at the ways in which others attempt to work with and influence Congressional decision-making. It will examine the basic premise that â in any democracy â politics is closely connected to the making and conduct of foreign policy and that in the United States a great deal of the political process plays out in Congress. The first part of the course looks at Congressâ role in foreign policy decision-making historically and in the present, the second examines how Congress is organized to make foreign policy decisions and assert its influence, and the third addresses the interplay between Congress and other political actors in foreign policy decision-making. The instructor was a Foreign Service Officer who also worked on Capitol Hill and had extensive dealing with Congress on foreign policy.

title: The BRICs & the World Economy
description: Although Brazil, Russia, India and China are often lumped together since a report in 2001 by Goldman Sachs famously grouped them as the BRICs and projected that they would be larger economically than the developed countries by 2050, they are very different economically, politically and structurally. The growth and development of these emerging economies raises many challenges and opportunities, not just to their own development strategy, but also for the rest of the world. In the aftermath of the current global financial and economic crisis they are on track to become larger than the developed countries faster than earlier projected. Their rise also raises issues of competition for markets, and natural resources; the role of population, education, and technology in global competition and relative wages between developed and developing economies; the most effective development models in terms of the mix of the state vs. the market, and authoritarian vs. democratic governance systems; the appropriate structure of global governance institutions and mechanism and their representation within them; security and the stability of the global system; and not least, global warming and sustainability in light of their large contributions to CO2 emissions and different approaches to what they consider appropriate burden sharing between developed and developing countries in dealing with this challenge. This course will begin by reviewing the history and development of these countries and the challenges to their development. It will then explore the issues highlighted above. Students will be asked to write position papers on some of the above issues from the perspective of these countries, as well as from the US, the EC, and the rest of the developing world. By the end of the course students will have a good grasp of how the BRICs are impacting and being impacted by the world economy.

title: Conflict Management and International Security
description: The seminar's central focus is the challenge of creating security and building peace in the 21st Century. It will introduce students to the "intellectual map" of the peace-maker by exploring a wide range of literatures and cases, exploring the roots and sources of conflict, and illustrating the varieties of third party intervention for conflict management. Drawing upon readings and discussion, students will examine changing patterns of conflict and explore a range of institutions, techniques and strategies for responding to the challenge of disorder.

title: Energy in a Low-Carbon World
description: This course is designed to present students with an introduction to the key issues and challenges confronting government policy-makers and business leaders in global energy. The course focuses on the impacts and intersection of emerging but potentially transformational events: 1. Forecasts of rising global energy demand 2. Growing concern about the dangers posed by global climate change (GCC), especially the role and impact of CO2 emissions from fossil fuel use in the energy sector 3. Challenges in moving to low-carbon sources for power generation and transportation 4. Demand-side challenges, including achieving greater energy efficiency 5. The changing dynamics of international oil and gas markets 6. The rise of electric transportation 7. Importance of non-OECD countries, and addressing energy access in the poorest countries Course Learning Objectives and Outcomes: The course is geared toward non-technical specialists, i.e., students are not required to have a background in engineering, economics, finance, etc, although these disciplines are integrated into the discussion. The emphasis is on the difficult choices facing policy makers in balancing economic growth, environmental challenges, and national and global security, as well as confronting business leaders in meeting the needs of changing energy markets. Generally, at the end of this course students should be comfortable advising senior decision-makers and leaders in government and business on a variety of policy and strategic options, demonstrating an understanding of the complexities and trade-offs involved and, most importantly, the ability to synthesize this information into actionable policies and plans. Within the realm of policy and strategy, students will be guided by an analytical framework that requires consideration of (among others): - Short-term and long-term objectives, often at odds - Government and private sector roles, and their inter-relationships - Regulatory and governance implications - Geopolitical factors - Economic and technology incentives and constraints By the end of the course, students should be able to take any issue and prioritize the critical factors within this framework, weeding through peripheral matters, identifying the most important linkages and trade-offs, and arguing for well-targeted solutions in a government, business or other context. Specific skills students will attain are: Understanding of critical issues in each of the major topical areas addressed Analytical capabilities â the ability to assess various policy and strategic options and understand the inter-linkages and complexities involved in each; identify objectives to be achieved, focus on most important analytical framework considerations in implementing the policy or strategy, and defining what needs to be done to achieve the objectives Presentational and communication skills â the ability â to verbally and in written form â work with senior government and business decision-makers, in making powerful, concise and convincing arguments for policy/strategic directions

title: Media & World Affairs
description: This course examines the relationship between the media and the conduct of international relations, beginning with Julius Caesar's self-serving account of the Gallic Wars and continuing through the media coverage of the Bush administration's case for pre-emptive war on Iraq, the global financial crisis and environmental issues. It also traces the evolution of the American news media from two developments in the 15th and 16th Centuries: the invention of moveable type and the translation of the Bible into English and German. The course includes a number of weekly writing assignments, and it places a heavy emphasis on the distillation and organization of large amounts of material into clear, concise prose, the kind that's valued not only in journalism, but also in industry, government and NGOs. In addition, there is a final paper of 2,000-2,500 words on a topic to be chosen by the student and approved by the professor.

title: Fragile States in Africa
description: This course is for students interested in some of the most important, challenging, complex, and difficult issues facing professionals working on diplomacy and economic development (including the role of the private sector) in Africa today. Focusing on the set of countries in Africa referred to as âweakâ or âfragileâ states, the course will analyze causes of state weakness, examining and critiquing various strategic approaches intended to help such states progress towards greater stability and development. From the Democratic Republic of the Congo to Ivory Coast, Somalia, Zimbabwe, and other countries, this course will first examine the concept of state fragility: what is it, why is the concept important, which countries can be considered fragile, how are they different from other developing countries, how do states move out of fragility? A fundamental course goal is ultimately to identify and discuss the most promising approaches to reduce conflict and fragility while fostering democratic development. The course will both examine specific country case studies and also discuss broad issues across countries, focusing on sectors and issues such as economic growth, education, health, justice, and democracy promotion. The course will compare strategies for fragile states with other approaches for more stable countries. Are strategies for fragile states radically different than those for other states? If so, why? Is this distinction regularly applied? If not, why not, and why does it matter? The point of view generally adopted will be of a person working in an Embassy, for a bilateral/multilateral donor, or for a UN operating unit in a developing country, but the course also will examine alternative points of view, including host country, business, and nongovernmental actors.

title: Global Business and Public Policy Practicum
description: Students aspiring to leadership in the public and private sectors need to understand the increasingly important role global companies play in shaping public policy on international trade and related issues. This course focuses on the practice of business-government relations, specifically the role of the global company as advocate on international economic issues. Last Fallâs course introduced students to the government relations programs of companies such as Proctor & Gamble, Coca Cola, Hewlett-Packard, Caterpillar, Bayer, McDonaldâs, Google, Boeing, Novartis and IBM. Students will gain a greater understanding of the ways in which corporations advocate in the executive and legislative branches of government. In addition, they will learn that advocacy takes many forms and that strategies differ from case to case. With reference to key issues, students will learn how major corporations identify priorities and develop and advocate policy positions. For 2012, students will explore government relations messaging and communications, including the shaping of public opinionânow a vital component of successful public policy programs. Students will evaluate the advent of social media in communications campaigns, studying the Capitol Hill debate over the Stop Online Piracy Act (SOPA) and its potential to redefine âgrassrootsâ advocacy. The course will also feature government relations crisis management, with particular reference to Googleâs decision to shut down its search engine in China rather than comply with Chinese law requiring self-censorship of Internet searches. Weâll contrast the business case for complying with Chinese law and Googleâs commitment to the principle of Internet freedom. To the extent practicable, the course will also consider what the 2012 elections might mean for global company government relations practices. To create a personal experience of the practice of government relations each student is teamed with a Washington-based corporate government relations executive involved in international business policy. A course highlight, the executive mentor assigns an issue project important to his/her company and the student has an opportunity to study and make a presentation on the issue for the companyâs Washington office. The course emphasizes developing internal and external business communication skills, including meeting-leadership skills, for effective Washington representation. It also focuses on advocacy, networking, decision-making processes and the respective roles of Capitol Hill and Executive Branch agencies. The course contributes to studentsâ ability to be effective participants in the development, implementation, and assessment of sound public policy, government affairs, and advocacy strategies for their current or future organizations.

title: International Law
description: This course will provide a detailed examination of the fundamentals of public international law. Taking a case-method approach, the course will discuss the sources of international law, the relationship of international law to domestic law, international personality, jurisdiction under international law-- including general principles of jurisdiction, extradition, the law of the sea, and air and space law, immunity from jurisdiction, state responsibility for injury to aliens, international human rights law, the law relating to the recourse to force, and the law concerning the conduct of hostilities. The primary text of the course will be: *International Law: Cases and Materials, 5th ed. 2009*, by Lori Damrosch, et al. Students will be evaluated based on class participation and *four* short legal memoranda.

title: Good Governance and Capable
description: Over the last decade, the effectiveness of the state and how states are governed have emerged as central topics among donors and the aid policy community. There is now increased consensus that well functioning and accountable public sector institutions that make sound policies, regulate markets, mobilize public resources, and deliver services are the foundation for sustainable and broad-based socioeconomic development. Whether it is responding to natural disasters, curtailing humanitarian crises, mitigating conflict, or effectively implementing development policy â a capable state and its institutions of governance must lead the way. Increased interest in state capacity building and good governance has also been fueled by the on-going debate on aid effectiveness. After decades of post-war development assistance, there is growing concern that a lack of attention to state capacity and good governance has led to an unsustainable addiction to foreign assistance. The preferred prescription has been increased âcountry ownershipâ and use of host country systems. This presupposes capable state institutions that can effectively plan, design, execute and evaluate development programs in an accountable and transparent manner. Yet, daily headlines more often highlight governance failings than they do success, suggesting a continued need for reflection and improvements in how the foreign aid community promotes good governance and builds capable states. This class explores the relationship between good governance, capable states, and sustainable development. The emphasis is not on why the problem of governance exists, although we will establish conceptual foundations about the nature and scope of the state. Rather, our focus is on how the development community tackles the challenge, and what aspiring development managers can learn from collective experience in this field. Working both individually and in teams, students will gain a practical understanding of approaches, methods and solutions that are used today to tackle the problem of governance and state building. Students are encouraged to challenge assumptions, and openly examine the successes, failures and track-record of a unique type of development assistance. Class discussion will center on practical experience of development managers and real-world reading material â case studies, toolkits, operational handbooks, and program evaluations. Students will gain practical skills to analyze arguments, assess governance challenges, and make recommendations on program design, focus and priority.

title: Introduction to Microfinance
description: Microfinance is usually viewed as a development success in that it has demonstrated the capacity to improve standards of living among the worldâs poorest in a sustainable way. Microfinance development programs target their financial services to low-income micro entrepreneurs with fewer than 10 employees. The purpose of this class is to provide an introduction to best practices in the design, management, and evaluation of microfinance programs. At the end of the course, students will be able to describe the role of microfinance in the financial services sector; the profile of the average microfinance client; the range of providers of microfinance services; the role of government in developing microfinance; and the principles of microfinance performance management.

title: Econometrics for Policy-Makers
description: Econometrics is the quantitative analysis of economic phenomena using the tools of mathematics and statistics. This course is tailored for practitioners and policy makers who need to develop skills and stools to be effective in using economic analysis in policy formulation and analysis. The skills developed in this course allow policy makers not only to be critical consumers of policy research but also to understand the process that underlies policy recommendations. The course takes as given some basic familiarity with descriptive statistics, probability theory, random variables and probability distributions and inference statistics both in an univariate and multivariate setting. The first part of the course focuses on the practice of regression analysis for cross-sectional data. The second part builds on the first part to provide a solid treatment of regression analysis with panel data and the third part covers discrete modeling, experimental and quasi-experimental methods.

title: Foreign Policy Making in Developing Nations
description: This course is aimed at four objectives: (1) to give students a broad understanding of the foreign policy process in developing nations in the major developing areas, dealing specifically with each area both as a region, and with a closer examination of the foreign policy â outcomes and process â of several of the major states; (2) to present for criticism and discussion some of the major theoretical approaches to the analysis and comparison of foreign policy and the processes by which it is made; (3) to allow students to deepen their expertise in the region of their interest through the research and presentation of a foreign policy study of a state in this region; and (4) to sharpen students' analytic skills through creation of a common research design so that the students' research will fit together into a collaborative framework. This will facilitate both a specific understanding of the foreign policy in individual nations as well as an ability to make comparisons between states and generalizations about the topic as a whole.

title: China-US Great Power Rivalry
description: The bilateral relationship between the U.S. and China has emerged as the most important such interaction in current world affairs. While it is unique and complex, with an undefined trajectory and an unknown outcome, it has a context among previous relationships between great powers. This course will examine the U.S. â China relationship as a great power rivalry, following primarily a structural-historical approach. After exploring the concept of great power rivalry and examining several previous such relationships, the seminar will then turn to an examination of the U.S. and China, isolating such factors as the security, economic, cultural and ideological dimensions. The students will be expected to draw upon the discussion of these factors in the earlier case studies to establish analogies to their current and future roles in relations between Beijing and Washington. In addition to an appreciation of the phenomenon of Great Power rivalry and an in-depth understanding of the U.S. â China relationship in particular, students will be expected to develop and utilize presentation and briefing, interview, serving as a discussant for reviewing peer presentations, and policy-oriented writing skills.

title: Fin Reg & Reform in Dev Countries
description: Financial markets have grown in size and sophistication over the last decade in the developing, newly industrializing, and post-Communist world. Financial liberalization in these countries has often involved contrasting polices. On the one hand, governments in capital-scarce economies have retreated from direct involvement in the allocation of financial resources. On the other hand, government authorities have also intervened in financial markets to protect consumers and investors, to promote fairness and transparency in financial transactions, and to maintain stability. Financial crises in emerging markets throughout the 1990s, however, raise several questions regarding the effectiveness of the financial reforms undertaken in those countries. Meanwhile, more recent challenges to the global financial system have required governments and international financial institutions to rethink regulatory standards. This seminar explores how and why governments intervene in financial markets. Four questions are of special importance: (1) What impact do financial markets have on the economic performance of developing countries? (2) What are the main dimensions along which financial systems vary across countries, and how do these differences matter? (3) Why are financial systems âfragileâ and what can governments do to ensure their stability? (4) How do financial markets recover, and what can governments do to speed restructuring? These questions are explored through a number of articles, books, reports, and case studies taken from different disciplinesâfinance, economics, public policy, and administrative studiesâand from different international settings.

title: Human Rights and War
description: This course will examine the increasingly complex link between human rights and war. War has always posed a threat to human rights, but the threat has evolved as wars have evolved from traditional inter-state conflicts to ânew warsâ in countries like Bosnia, which deliberately target civilians. More recently,, we have seen the emergence of the âhybridâ war against terror. Looking ahead, we see new challenges in the form of cyber-warfare, new forms of weaponry like drones, and even climate change. The nature of war is changing before our eyes. What are the implications of these developments for human rights? Do they make it harder or easier to protect the vulnerable and disempowered? And is the traditional approach of international human rights â developing and implementing legal standards (conventions) through the UN and regional organizations - equal to the challenge? If not, how could the machinery of international human rights be adapted, to better respond? Is there still a role for traditional theories like Just War and even pacifism? How do we assess emerging doctrines, like R2P? The course will address these key questions through a series of major case studies: World War 2; Iraq; Afghanistan; Cambodia; Bosnia; Rwanda and the Great Lakes; Sierra Leone; Uganda/LRA; the Middle East (Gaza); and Post 9/11. The course will mix theory and practice, and focus in particular on the role of community organizations as agents of peace. Each participant will be assigned a case study and make a class presentation, working together or in teams. There will be one final paper, but no exams. A full syllabus and reading list will be posted in July. In the meantime, students will find a good overview in âHUMAN RIGHTS AND CONFLICT - Exploring the Links between Rights, Law, and Peace-building,â Edited by Julie Mertus and Jeffrey Helsing (US Institute of Peace).

title: Nuclear Policy & International Security
description: This seminar will be a real time study of the development of the overarching national security strategy of the Obama administration. There will be a heavy emphasis on nuclear weapons policy and nonproliferation strategy, and we will review the 2010 debate over Senate ratification of the new START treaty and track Senate consideration of the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty (CTBT). The course explores, in addition, a brief overview of the successes and failures of the previous administrationâs strategy. We will also discuss the practical aspects of government foreign policy decision-making, including the role of Congress and the national security bureaucracy. We will not spend much time discussing Libya, Iraq and Afghanistan, as these are amply covered by other courses, but there will be discussion of Pakistan, India, Iran and North Korea. There will be a heavy emphasis on learning the necessary skills of concise oral presentation and impact writing, such as the art of the one-page government memo and the two-minute oral presentation. Students will be expected to follow major diplomatic and regional developments in the daily and weekly media and specialized journals.

title: Workshop: Coordinating US State-Building Programs
description: This workshop draws upon the faculty's experience as the U.S. Department of Defense's coordinator for Afghanistan reconstruction as well as of funding and troop raiser for Iraq, working not only the U.S. inter-agency process, but also jointly with the State Department, USAID and the NSC in collaborating internationally, with allies and friends around the globe as well as with international organizations (e.g., the UN and EU), NGOs (e.g., Catholic Charities, CARE, Save the Children, and Oxfam) and the international financial institutions. The workshop provides an understanding of the inter-agency process; of the challenges posed by America's proclivity toward state-building and how those challenges have been met, with lesser or greater success, from the aftermath of the Spanish-American War (Philippines, Cuba), to post-World War II cases (Germany, Japan), to Iraq and Afghanistan (e.g., Provincial Reconstruction Teams). The course will also examine the interactions in the field between embassies and combatant commanders, the Defense Department, State Department, USAID and other agencies, and the changing role of the Department of the Treasury. The workshop will address the need for multi-stakeholder coordination in the specific case of state-building, and more broadly in foreign policy. In class and assignment, students will develop skills pertinent to working in such institutions and bringing about better coordination.

title: WKSHP: Managing Risk
description: International development is all about managed change; about self-conscious, deliberate, organized and purposeful efforts to change social, economic or political systems. Development theories and programs most commonly focus on the technical rationale for these changes, emphasizing the benefits that will be realized if they are implemented. Accordingly, development programs generally focus on technical assistance, training and capacity building, and institutional development. These are all intended to promote and encourage the adoption of new techniques in agriculture, new health protocols for infants, new approaches to food security, or new economic opportunities for women. The core objective of this course is to articulate, develop, test, and refine the analytic frameworks and practical tools to understand and manage risk as an integral part of the development process. This will include the ability to: â¢ Identify risk â understand the different kinds, modes, and varieties of risk; â¢ Assess risk â be able to examine risks relative to overall objective and other considerations; â¢ Manage risk â develop practical measures to mitigate and reduce risk By the end of the course, students will acquire professional skills in three distinct areas. First, students will develop and use key field research skills, such as research design, interview protocols and techniques, and case study methods. Second, students will be familiar with and practice using important technical skills. These will include risk management tools (such as risk mapping and risk indices), and change management tools (such as stakeholder analysis, lobbying and advocacy strategies). Finally, students will be introduced to and coached in using practical consulting skills (such as negotiating a Scope of Work, developing and managing a work plan, preparing and delivering interim briefings for senior managers, and client management strategies).

title: WKSHP: Engaging Change: Middle East and North Africa
description: In the Middle East and North Africa, international affairs professionals face regional upheaval, shifting regimes, non-state actors morphing into political parties, social unrest, increased demands from constituents for more accountable governments and more assertive foreign policies, as well as a 24-hour media cycle that includes new forms of communication. These dynamic factors have combined to make for the most profound transformations in the regionâs contemporary history. Professionals seeking to understand and adapt to these fast-paced developments will require appreciating the perspectives of the various regional and international actors; and require acquiring the skills necessary to gather and process information as well as assess and present policy options, whether in a government, an international organization, an NGO, or a business. This workshop will focus on short presentations based on written sources and interviews with practitioners, role-plays/simulation, and written work, to help students prepare for professional engagement with this specific regionâs dynamics, and with those of other regions as well.

title: WKSHP: Policy Innovation & Coalition Management
description: Regardless of which sector students enter after graduation, many will be responsible for the management of innovative policy initiatives and of complex diverse coalitions to advance such initiatives. This workshop will focus comprehensively on how to manage such multi-stakeholder policy innovations. Students will work in teams, each working for a specific private, public or NGO entity and each with a multifaceted policy issue, such as Pakistani madrassa reform, regulation of conflict minerals, or corporate social responsibility initiatives to end the use of slave labor in agricultural products. Students will learn key skills needed in navigating policy making, including issue analysis, risk and stakeholder assessment, coalition development and management, advocacy, and oral and written presentation skills. Each team will research and develop its policy initiative and strategies for working with key stakeholders in all sectors -- directly conferring with professionals in them, and present key proposals and an implementation plan to a panel of external experts.

title: Workshop: Global Finance
description: This workshop will cover a wide range of material designed to give students a solid background in global business and finance, particularly in the context of the global capital markets and the crises of the last few years. The workshop will develop existing functional skills as well as introducing new topics in order to enhance an overall understanding of the context, players, practice and relevant techniques of global business and finance. Sessions will largely be in-class work on cases in order to emphasize real world application and complexity and are intended to be inter-active sessions. At the end of the workshop students will have a critical understanding of the complexity and interconnectedness of many aspects of finance, and will have developed some key analytic tools in viewing these. The case process students will be employed to give students the opportunity of using this newly acquired knowledge and skill set. The workshop will benefit students who are intent upon obtaining basic corporate and capital market financial literacy and are interested in both the theory and practice of finance. Students should be curious as to the unfolding dramas contained in the financial press and wish to enhance their ability to understand and debate the salient issues. Students will become better equipped to understand these issues and the tradeoffs and uncertainties that different stakeholders might typically face. Students will also better understand the financial crisis and some of the lessons that must continue to emanate from it. The workshop will appeal to those who wish to develop careers that touch finance and the financial markets, be that in the public or private sectors as participants, regulators or observers. At the end of the workshop students will have enhanced their functional financial knowledge, and obtained a better perspective as to how the functional parts fit together. Finally students will have obtained sufficient tools and practice in applying them so as to be able to make intelligent judgments and contributions in real life situations.

title: WKSHP: Resource Management In International Relations
description: This workshop prepares students for dealing with the critical role of resources in international relations (IR). There is a vast global infrastructure that supports the conduct of IR. The requirements are infinite and expensive. The workshop will provide students with knowledge and skills that are necessary for effective IR resource management, including: budget formulation, analysis and evaluation; grant writing and evaluation; budget negotiations; program execution; the audit process; the mechanics of the U.S. foreign affairs budget, including the appropriations process and the U.S. defense budget, offered as an example of how a major world power funds its ambitious and sometimes controversial foreign affairs agenda; how multilateral institutions manage resources; and the often poorly-understood role that private corporations play as resource-mobilizing partners. The workshop utilizes the case method, in-class simulations, and practical exercises and will feature high-level guest speakers from the U.S. Government, NGOs, foundations, and private contractors.

title: Workshop: Strategy & Management Problem Solving
description: This workshop will provide students with working familiarity with the strategic, analytic and communications methods used by strategic planners, problem intervention teams, general managers/profit center managers and outside consultants. The first part of the course (weeks 1-6) will deal with high level strategy and will address issues and challenges of the large business firm that operates globally or is âglobalizingâ its scope. We will begin with a survey of general business strategy literature and concepts covering topics such as industry structure, competitive advantage, core competencies, and product differentiation. With this as a foundation, the course will engage a variety of strategic issues that accompany international or global business operations. These include country market selection, brand management and other marketing challenges, intellectual property protection, alliances and joint ventures, sourcing and supply chain management, organization design, and cross-cultural management. The second part of the course (weeks 7-14) will focus on industry analysis and corresponding corporate strategies for operating within these globally-organized industries. Industries to be covered include autos, consumer products, retailing, financial services, information technology, telecommunications, airlines, aerospace, energy, mining, pharmaceuticals and biotech, and media and entertainment. Among the practical skills to be practiced in the course is industry structure analysis. The entire course will operate at two levels reflecting, to some extent, the scope of work done by leading consulting firms for large corporates. The course will treat 1) high level strategyâglobal, regional and major market strategy. At the same time the course will provide experience in specific 2) problem diagnosis and solution strategies/tacticsâa more ground level kind of assignment. This aspect of the course will have four intersecting thrusts: business and organizational problemsâ¦across a variety of industriesâ¦in a range of geographic contextsâ¦requiring a spectrum of applicable diagnostic and analytical methods.

title: WKSHP Business of Development
description: This workshop focuses on practical skills relevant to the roles and job responsibilities of most development professionals - whether they work for multilateral organizations, government agencies, private sector firms, or NGOs. The course is designed to build knowledge and skills that match the complex, interdisciplinary reality of development management. Successful development practice depends on the capacity of program and project managers to integrate different disciplines and interact effectively with numerous stakeholders, both inside and outside their own organization. This capacity is likely to become more important, and more highly valued, as the global development ecosystem continues to evolve and the concept of âDevelopment 2.0â moves from rhetoric to reality. The workshop blends training in core skills and practices with exposure to the functions and characteristics of key actors in the ecosystem. The workshop is divided into three modules featuring exercises and presentations by student teams, and concludes with an individual project assignment that integrates material learned during the course. Module 1 will begin with an overview of the global development ecosystem, focusing on the major donor agencies and newer, nontraditional actors who are introducing important changes to the way that development projects are designed, financed, and implemented. Module 2 will examine the different types of organizations in the private and NGO sectors that implement development projects on behalf of project sponsors and funding agencies. Module 3 will focus on the skills and tools necessary to launch and implement a project, once a contract or grant agreement has been negotiated with the donor agency. The workshop will close with an individual written assignment. Students will respond to a case with a scenario describing a development problem in need of resolution, and will be asked to prepare a plan of action in response to this case.

title: Pre-Calculus
description: This course is designed to assist students whose high school mathematics background is insufficient for the standard first-year mathematics courses. It is primarily intended as a preparation for MATH-035. Topics include: algebraic operations, factoring, exponents and logarithms, polynomials, rational functions, trigonometric functions, and the logarithmic and exponential functions. Graphing and word problems will be stressed. This course is not intended to complete the math/science requirement in the College. Fall.

title: Mathematics in Society
description: The course is concerned with mathematical approaches that can help resolve conflicts, make decisions under adverse or uncertain conditions, or extract social choices from the preferences of individuals. Topics will be chosen from elementary game theory (arms race, Prisoner's Dilemma), fair division schemes, probability models for chance events and risk (lottery, poker, life insurance), voting models (voting paradoxes, building coalitions), and statistical methods (opinion polls, clinical trials). Mathematical prerequisites are algebra, analytic geometry, and elementary probability as taught in high school. This course may be used toward completion of the College math/science requirement.

title: Statistics with Exploratory Data Analysis
description: The primary objective of this beginning course in statistics is to have students learn and understand statistical concepts without being overwhelmed by cumbersome formulae and computations. The emphasis will be on data exploration and graphical techniques. Topics to be covered will include descriptive statistics, measures of center and spread, linear regression, probability theory, sampling, random variables and probability distributions. Uniform, discrete, binomial, normal, t and chi-square distributions will be among those used to introduce statistical inference, including estimation and hypothesis testing. Considerable use will be made of video tapes and computers. All classes will be held in the computer lab where the statistical software MINITAB will be taught and used to simplify computation and enhance graphical presentations. A computer tutorial will also be used. Minimum computer ability is recommended (but not required). This course is regarded as a core course (or SONY core course, as appropriate) for completion of the math/science requirement in the College. Fall and Spring.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Modeling
description: This course if for students who want to see how the math they have previously learned can be applied to personal issues related to health and finance as well as in policy decisions from many different fields. In the first part of the course, personal issues, such as weight gain or loss, alcohol abuse, proper drug dosage, mortgages and annuities are primarily considered. In the second part of the course, we will consider more global models that give insight into sustainable management of the worlds resources or issues related to genetics and genetic engineering. Often the results will be counterintuitive, such as finding that an increase in the rate of wild-life harvesting may actually decrease the long-term harvest. Fall and Spring.

title: Calculus I
description: This is the first part of the four semester calculus sequence (Math-035-036 and 137-150) for mathematics and science majors. Topics include limits, derivatives, techniques of differentiation, applications of the derivative, the Riemann integral, the trigonometric and inverse trigonometric functions, and the logarithmic and exponential functions. Fall and Spring.

title: Calculus II
description: A continuation of MATH-035. Topics include techniques of integration, applications of the definite integral, improper integrals, Newton's method and numerical integration, sequences and series including Taylor's theorem and power series, and elementary separable and first and second order linear differential equations. Fall and Spring.

title: Probability and Statistics
description: Topics include graphical and numerical methods for describing data, probability and sampling distributions, estimation, hypothesis testing, and simple linear regression with inference. This course has two lectures and one recitation section. Fall and Spring.

title: Multivariable Calculus
description: A continuation of MATH-036. This is a first course in vector analysis and the differential and integral calculus of functions of many variables. Topics include vector analysis in n-space, differentiation of real and vector valued functions of many variables, the chain rule, extrema of real valued functions, constrained extrema and Lagrange multipliers, vector fields in 3-space, the divergence and curl of a vector field, conservative fields, double and triple integrals, change of variables in multiple integrals, path and surface integrals, and the theorems of Green, Gauss, and Stokes. Fall and Spring.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Statistics
description: This course provides an introduction to basic concepts in probability theory and applied statistics. Topics to be covered include methods of enumeration, properties of random variables, common discrete and continuous distributions, expectations, the central limit theorem, parameter estimation, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and linear regression. Concepts will be illustrated and applied to data using the statistical package Minitab.

title: Linear Algebra
description: Normally taken after MATH-137. This course presents the basic theory and methods of finite dimensional vector spaces and linear transformations on them. Topics include: matrices and systems of linear equations; vector spaces, bases, and dimension; linear transformations, kernel, image, matrix representation, basis change, and rank; scalar products and orthogonality; determinants; eigenvalues, eigenvectors, diagonalization of symmetric matrices, positive definite matrices. Fall and Spring.

title: Introduction to Proof and Problem Solving
description: Formerly called Foundations of Mathematics, this course will introduce different methods for constructing simple proofs, including forwards/backwards proofs, contradiction, contraposition, and induction. The students will apply these methods to a variety of areas of mathematics, including simple number theory, relations, calculus concepts, and a study of infinity. This course is required for Math Majors and is a prerequisite for many upper level courses. Fall and Spring.

title: Ordinary Differential Equations
description: This course provides an introduction to the theory, techniques, and applications of ordinary differential equations. Topics include first order equations, second order linear equations, series solutions, the method of Laplace transforms, systems of equations, and an introduction to nonlinear equations and stability theory. Fall.

title: Discrete Dynamical Systems
description: The primary focus of the course is learning how mathematics is used to develop general principles applied to other fields of study. In particular, we will use mathematics to model and analyze dynamic real world phenomena, that is, phenomena that change over time. Among the situations we will consider are, gambling (your money changes over time), drug and alcohol elimination, genetic makeup of a population, change in the value of an annuity or mortgage, and the sustainable harvesting of a species. Once models have been developed, we will analyze the models to answer questions of interest, such as, at what rate does a population grow and how can it be harvested in a sustainable manner? How do chemicals stabilize in our bodies? How much do we need to save to have an annuity we can live with? How is the genetic makeup of a population affected by environmental stresses? We will also consider the relatively new mathematical topics of chaos and fractals. The course will use pdf materials written by the professor.

title: Introduction to Cryptography
description: In this introductory course we begin by looking at how the ancient Roman military send secret messages. We then survey cryptography from Roman times up to today's high tech world. Students will learn how to encrypt messages and how to attempt to break codes. We will discuss the efficiency and security level of different encryption methods. To make the discussion rigorous, we will need tools/concepts/results from mathematics. Mathematical content: mappings and inverse mappings, modular arithmetic, the additive group Z/n, the multiplicative group Z*/n, Euler's phi function, Fermat's Little theorem and Euler's generalization, primitve roots, discrete logarithms. Cryptographic content: classical ciphers and their decryption (shift, affine, and Vigenere ciphers), key exchange protocols (main example: Diffie-Hellman), public key ciphers (main example: RSA). An important mathematical thrust will be to show students that there are alternative arithmetic systems in which familiar objects such as inverses, products, and logarithms have strange properties, and that these are the right tools for cryptography.

title: Abstract Algebra
description: This is a rigorous introduction to algebraic structures and their homomorphisms with emphasis on proofs. Topics from group theory will include permutation groups and Sylow theory. Topics from ring theory will include integral domains, unique factorization domains, and polynomial rings. Spring. Prerequisite: MATH-150 and MATH-200.

title: Applied Statistical Methods
description: This course, a follow-up for Math-040 or Math-140, is an applied statistics course. After a brief review of the normal distribution and probability plots, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, goodness of fit tests, and simple linear regression, we will study the following topics: ANOVA, MANOVA, inference for linear regression, multiple regression, logistic regression, and nonparametric methods. Classroom material will be accompanied by hands-on experience using the statistical software package Minitab. Also, the instructor will consider advanced topics such as missing data, introduction to time series, experimental design, etc. as time permits.

title: Topics in Neural Networks
description: A neural network is a directed graph which carries out some desired computations in a distributed fashion by assigning numeric and algorithmic attributes to its nodes and arcs. These networks have a variety of antecedents including neuroscience and physics, but we concentrate on their role within mathematics. Applications of the theory of neural networks are currently important in the field of pattern recognition and so-called "artificial neural networks" are considered in computer science. This is a first course in the area so no prerequisites are needed other than sufficient mathematical aptitude to follow the arguments. We will utilize Mathematica to study some toy examples, so that students can see what the network is doing. While the software is not as fast as some other choices, it is much easier to modify and manipulate, and students will be encouraged to use it. Mathematical topics to be covered include: DAGs, Boolean networks, vectors and matrices, convexity, affine recursion, topological density, poles of meromorphic functions, the Hahn-Banach Theorem, and reproducing kernels. These have arisen in papers by the lecturer and his colleagues in which the theory of neural networks is being explored.
