title: Ethics: Quality of Life
description: We all recognize that whether a state policy or individual choice benefits or harms people has tremendous ethical importance, but what is it to benefit people and how should the benefits of policies and choices be assessed? We know that we should be âmen and women for others,â caring for our one another and advocating for policies that are in the best interests of people, but what does that mean to be âfor othersâ and how can we tell if the policies we advocate are really are in peopleâs best interest? To start with the political side of the question, GDP per capita is often used to assess quality of life in a country, but GDP figures do not take into account how resources are distributed, whether basic rights are being respected, how satisfied people are with their lives, or what sorts of opportunities are genuinely open to people. So it is questionable whether per capita economic production ought to be the focus of community decision making or whether the focus ought to be elsewhere. The âHuman Developmentâ or âCapabilitiesâ approach to quality of life and community responsibility represents one major alternative to the GDP approach, and we will be looking at the Capabilities approach in depth while considering some of its rivals along the way. What we think about the political questions of quality of life will depend, on the one hand, on what we think about the nature, responsibilities, and limits of government, but on the other hand, it will depend on how we answer some basic ethical questions which have application in our individual as well as our communal lives. For example, what is it for an individual to be better off? Should we be trying to maximize total well being, regardless of distribution or does equality and inequality matter for its own sake as well? Should we being concerned with whether people get what they deserve? Should we be concerned with making people actually better off, with ensuring that they have the genuine opportunity to become better off, or just with living and letting live? In the context of international development, the question also arises whether quality of life means the same thing around the world, or whether varies based on cultural standards and expectations. This question takes on a special urgency where womenâs quality of life is concerned. Does quality of life mean something different for women and men? Does the meaning of âquality of lifeâ for a woman depend on what her culture thinks a good life for a woman looks like? Required Texts: Shelly Kagan. 1997. Normative Ethics. Westview Press. ISBN: 0813308461 Martha C. Nussbaum. 2011. Creating Capabilities: The Human Development Approach. Belknap Press of Harvard University Press. ISBN: 0198287976 Martha Nussbaum and Amartya Sen, eds. 1993. The Quality of Life. Oxford University Press, USA. ISBN: 0674050541

title: Ethics: Just War Theory
description: When is it right for a nation to go to war? Once at war, what sort of behavior becomes permissible for soldiers and commanders? And what obligations do soldiers, sovereigns and states have in the aftermath of wars? Throughout the semester, we will tackle these questions (and many tangential and subsidiary ones) through the lens of contemporary just wary theory. We'll begin with a brief unit situating the just war tradition among other historically prominent perspectives on warârealism, pacifism, militarism and crusadism. We will subsequently turn our attention to issues of jus ad bellum, or moral issues surrounding the initiation of war. Subtopics will include aggression, preemptions, preventive wars, interventions, secessions, reprisals. We shall then turn our focus to conduct within war (jus in bello), and take a close look at the Doctrine of Double Effect, noncombatant immunity, the moral responsibility of soldiers fighting unjust wars, and conditions of "supreme emergency". Finally, weâll discuss a recently popular movement in just war theory that focus on justice in the conclusion of war (jus post bellum). While our readings will come primarily from contemporary western just war theory, we will also examine the historical roots of the western just war tradition, as well as the ethics of war in other cultures. In our final unit, we will briefly explore the Islamic, Jewish and Chinese traditions of just war.

title: Ethics:  Gender & Justice
description: Ethics: Gender & Justice Should gender make a difference in how people are treated? This course will explore both classical and contemporary theories of justice and ethics. These theories are centrally concerned with how people should be treated, and whether, how, and why people should be treated differently from one another. Historically, gender difference has been used to justify different social and political roles for men and women. We will read classic texts on theories of justice, and will explore the role gender has played in structuring these theories. We will then turn to contemporary philosophers who are concerned with issues of oppression and exclusion. Today, people have largely rejected the notion that gender difference justifies different political rights or liberties, but social practices still work powerfully to reinforce normative gender roles. By drawing on texts from classical and contemporary philosophy, we will work to better understand the way gender can intersect with race, class, and sexuality to differently privilege people. We will read texts by Kant, Mill, Hobbes, Frye, Young, and Bartky, amongst others.

title: Ethics:  Integrity & Commitment
description: "To lack integrity is to fail at life in a major way. Or so we tend to suppose. But if lacking integrity really is such a terrible failure, why is this so? What kind of a failure is it, exactly? What does one lack when one lacks integrity? Has one who lacks integrity lost something they formerly had? If so, where can it be found? Is integrity something we must earn? Is it granted by others? Raising these questions leads us to ask other, more general questions about what we should value and how we should live. For instance, having integrity seems to have something fundamentally to do with having commitments. So what is a commitment, anyway? Are all of our commitments chosen? Or are they inheritable, like problems? Whatever they are, they can impose demanding limitations on what we should do. So can we live without them? Should we want to? Finally, even those who are indifferent to things like familial obligations and social justice have loyalties to specific people or commitments to goals or personal projects which they value. If there is no honor amongst thieves, can there be any integrity? Or is integrity not merely a private matter, after all? Does living with integrity inevitably commit us to social virtues like honesty and fairness? In this course we will explore these questions with an eye to enriching our understanding of the relationship between integrity and commitment, and of the role they can and should play in our lives. We will seek help from a range of thoughtful voices, including those of Bernard Williams, Harry Frankfurt, Christine Korsgaard, Charles Guignon and Friedrich Nietzsche. The courseâs graded content will include one short and two medium-length written assignments as well as class participation, of which contributions to in-class discussions of the assigned material will form a part."

title: Ethics: Business Responsibility
description: What are the moral obligations of businesses? Are for-profit companies solely obligated to maximize profit or do they have responsibilities beyond this? Many top companies today have policies regarding "Corporate Social Responsibility", "Global Citizenship", "Sustainability", and other nice-sounding buzz words. And vocal groups around the world similarly call for "corporate accountability" and for businesses to "give back" to their communities. But what do these terms mean and what should businesses do about them, if anything? We will consider topics including the philosophical nature of business, the nature of money, whether and how businesses can have moral obligations, means of defining the responsibilities of a business, whether businesses can have virtuous character, and the role that personal responsibilities play in business, amongst others. By drawing on classic and contemporary philosophy, this course will work to clarify the meaning of corporate responsibility and the origins of business ethics. Readings will focus on contemporary literature in business ethics, but also incorporate Aristotle, Plato, John Locke, Adam Smith, Immanuel Kant, John Stuart Mill, and John Rawls.

title: Ethics: International Law
description: In this course on the philosophy of international law we will consider different approaches to and the different ethical, legal, and political values relevant to developing and evaluating systems of laws and institutions for the global context. The course will begin with a careful consideration of John Rawlsâs The Law of Peoples followed by an assessment of his view through the arguments raised by his critics. We will then turn to Samantha Besson and John Tasioulasâs anthology The Philosophy of International Law. Our class will cover selections on international legitimacy, international democracy, sovereignty, just war, humanitarian intervention, and responsibility, along with relevant areas of law including international criminal law, human rights law, and environmental law. The course requirements include two exams and a term paper on one of the contemporary issues discussed in class. This course will give students a background in legal theory as applied to the global context and provide students an opportunity to think and write on some of the most pressing issues facing the world today.

title: Ethics: Global Justice
description: Throughout the world there are massive social and resource inequalities between countries. Over 2,600,000 people live with less than $2 per day. Millions die from avoidable poverty-related causes each year. In this course, we will explore a number of questions related to justice at the global scale, including: â¢ Is there a universal standard of justice that we can apply in different cultures? â¢ Is the plight of the global poor merely unfortunate or is it unjust, and if so, why? â¢ How should the responsibility to alleviate global poverty be distributed between individuals, states, and international institutions? â¢ Is there a human right to health and to freedom from poverty? â¢ Who should own natural resources? â¢ How should the rules of international trade be set up? â¢ How should the burden of climate change be shared? â¢ Are restrictive immigration policies unjust? In this class we will read a number of contemporary moral and political philosophers who propose various answers to these questions. Students will evaluate these answers via class discussion and written assignments. Textbook: Chris Armstrong. 2012. Global Distributive Justice. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

title: Ethics: Justice
description: Ethics: Justice How should benefits and burdens be distributed within a political community? This question of distributive justice is at issue in current debates about income inequality, the proper level of taxation, access to health care, the financing of education, affirmative action, gender inequality, religious liberty etc. Most of us agree that justice is an important, if not the most important, standard for political and social life. But we disagree about the criteria for distributing benefits and burdens in society justly. In this class, we will examine contemporary conceptions of justice that propose answers to this question: Utilitarianism, Liberal Egalitarianism, Libertarianism, Marxism, and Communitarianism. We will consider the connection between justice and other political ideals such as fairness, equality, liberty, wellbeing, inclusion, and recognition. We will have a look at feminist critique of classical conceptions of justice, and consider whether justice is best thought of in terms of distribution at all. The readings will include texts by John Rawls, Robert Nozick, Gerald A. Cohen, Michael Sandel, Susan Okin, and Iris M. Young. We will assess the strengths and weaknesses of these conceptions, and discuss their0 implications0for some0of the current political debates0mentioned above.0Throughout the class, you will be developing the skills to interpret arguments accurately and criticize them cogently. The overall goal of the class is to help you become a thoughtful and critical participant in political and social debates. Assignments will include a midterm exam, a group presentation, a final paper, and regular reading questions.

title: Ethics: The Untold War
description: In this course, we examine issues in the moral psychology and moral philosophy of war. While just war theory has long been a central focus in understanding war from a moral and legal point of view, and traumatic illness (posttraumatic stress disorder) a focus in the clinical treatment of soldiers, little attention has been given to the moral psychology of soldiering itself. How do civilians take up the role of soldier? How do they leave that role behind? What sort of role duties are involved in being a citizen soldier? What moral quandaries and emotional conflicts do individual soldiers face as they rev up for war, deploy, and return home? Are feelings of revenge morally justified in war? Is survivor guilt or luck guilt a rational response to loss? In short, how do we conceive the interior moral landscape of soldiering? By listening to soldiers own narratives and exploring a wide range of readings in the moral philosophy and psychology of war, we shall try to understand the untold internal war that soldiers often fight.

title: Moral and Political Equality
description: How are we to understand the political commitment to equality, so central to much post-Enlightenment moral and political theory? If we are, as persons, to be regarded as equals, in what respects are we to be so regarded, and with respect to what goods? How is the commitment to equality best realized? As actual people in real times and places, we are marked by a vast array of differences (e.g., of power, vulnerability, need, gender, culture, ethnicity). Which differences, if any, should make a difference, and what difference should they make, to the design of our social and political arrangements? Does recognizing and responding to "facts" of difference jeopardize the commitment to equality, or is it Â­â to the contrary â a requirement of this commitment, properly understood? What is the status of these "facts," and are our categories of "difference" themselves sometimes normatively loaded in ways that are problematic â that support rather than subvert social and political inequality insofar as they are invoked? We will study these and other questions through the examination of an array of particular issues, including alternative accounts of the nature of equality, theories of justice and oppression, concrete measures of inequality around the world, the problem of "androcentrism," the nature of racism, the "dilemma of difference," and the moral and political viability of affirmative action.

title: Ethics: Global Warming
description: ETHICS: GLOBAL WARMING This is also an age of environmental tragedy. And it is a tragedy that implicates our institutions, our ethical frameworks, and even ourselves. Yet environmentalism is, nonetheless, entering a golden age: conservation, recycling and stewardship are more prominent than ever in daily life. Issues such as global warming, pollution and consumption have received sustained public attention. In many cases, the reasoning behind altering human behavior to avoid certain environmental consequences has been enshrined in moral language. In order to form a better understanding of this our Golden Age of Tragedy, we will use the case study of the "Perfect Moral Storm" of climate change to explore three different types of questions. 1. Value questions: Do non-human animals exert moral claims on us? Do ecosystems (or the earth as a whole) have non-instrumental moral value? Or, in the end, is it only human interests that are morally relevant? 2. Questions of collective and communal responsibility: How can we understand individual accountability for our actions when they only make a negligible contribution to a greater harm? 3. Public policy concerns: Global warming, pollution, cap and trade, environmental justice, endangered species, etc. What ethical considerations should influence public policy? This class is part of the University's and the Kennedy Center's Engaged Ethics Initiative - so it will include trips, projects, and films outside of class. Enrollment will be by approval of the instructor. If you are interested in enrolling, please send a brief (100-300 word) description of why you want to be part of such a course and what you could bring to it. Please send these NO LATER THAN November 15 to the Instructor Marcus Hedahl at moh6@georgetown.edu

title: Ethics: Technologies of the Self
description: Nothing would seem more natural than our bodies; they are, after all, the biological products of processes beyond our control. But are our bodies merely biological products? It would seem not; at every turn, we can find ways of intervening on the body to make it "better, stronger, faster." In this course, we'll look at the philosophical and ethical implications of various "technologies of the self"--that is, the various methods of intervening on a body so as to make or enhance a person. In the first half of the course, we'll examine the extent to which the self that we seem to discover is the product of technological interventions (in a broad sense). In the second part of the course, we will cover topics such as the ethics of doping in athletics, cognitive enhancement technologies, and trans/posthumanism--with an eye toward understanding these debates in light of the discussion in the first part of the course.

title: Ethics: Rights
description: What is a ârightâ? What rights do we have? In this course, we will consider these and other questions as we study the nature, content, scope, and function of rights in our political discourse. Each semester we will focus on a particular topic; the topic for this semester is environmental justice. We will start by considering important philosophical debates and theoretical approaches. We will look closely at Aquinasâs discussion of ârightâ as the object of justice and consider whether Aquinasâs theory is consistent with the notion of personal or subjective rights. Do we have any rights? We also will consider alternative justifications for natural rights based on Kantian and utilitarian ethical theories. Assuming we do have rights, we will also look at other contemporary theoretical approaches, especially as developed in the international context. After doing the hard theoretical work, we will turn to the question of what rights should be protected by law and society? We will consider several contemporary issues related to environmental justice. Do animals have rights? Do other parts of nature have rights? Do future generations have a right against present generations to a planet with a similar quality and diversity? Do the global poor have rights against the governments of affluent states? The objective of this course is not to answer these questions definitively, but to invite careful consideration of different views and thoughtful and well-reasoned argument when presenting oneâs own view.

title: Ethics: Human Rights, Global Justice
description: Course Goals & Description In this course we examine three sets of issues. (i) As a prelude to discussions of global justice we examine two leading types of theories of domestic justice â an egalitarian theory developed by John Rawls and a libertarian theory developed by Robert Nozick. From this basis we examine the reasoning behind âstrong statistâ conceptions that claim that whatever duties of egalitarian justice there may be within a purely domestic context, there are no such duties to non-citizens. (ii) We then examine various âcosmopolitanâ theories of human rights that endorse some universal rights pertaining to social and economic well-being. We consider charges (often made by libertarians) that such theories lack clear accounts of the basis for such entitlements, imply incoherent theories of obligation, rest upon Western, non-universal values, and lack the institutional basis necessary for them to be plausible. (iii) Finally, we consider intermediate theories of justice that claim that even if some duties of distributive justice are applicable only within the domestic context, there are global duties of justice that limit the kinds of harms, inequalities, or disadvantages that may be imposed on the global poor by the operation of the international economic order. Required Texts & Readings There are no required texts. All readings will be posted on Blackboard. Assignments & Expectations of Students Course requirements (and percentage of final grade) include: 3 papers of approximately 8-10 pages, (20% each); active class participation (20%); and short, (roughly 2-pages) summaries of the daily assigned readings for 6 class sessions - 2 from within each of the 3 segments of the course (20%).

title: Ethics: Public Policy
description: This course is an introduction to the study of ethics, from a theoretical and practical perspective, as it relates to public policy. The course will begin with a close reading of classic texts including works of Aristotle, Kant, and Mill, which try to make sense of what makes human lives and actions good, happy, and meaningful. We will subject these views to contemporary reflections on what it means to be a moral actor, considering pointed critiques of traditional western thinking about ethics. Throughout, we will be asking ourselves about the constraints that ethical considerations might ask us to impose on our own freedom, and whether there are elements of the ethical life that are not within our control. Finally, we will apply these ethical frameworks to public policy asking how ethical considerations inform (or fail to inform) our public policy through close study of specific issues of contemporary national, political and global significance such as international migration, poverty, and corporate globalization. Throughout the course we will explore the question of how and when morally evaluative judgments are possible concerning the practices of individuals, communities, corporations, and nations. Requirements of the course will include two papers, a mid-term and final examination, and class participation.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: NOTE: THIS COURSE WILL NOT FULFILL THE LOGIC REQURIEMENT FOR PHILOSOPHY MAJORS Despite (supposedly) being the only rational animals, humans by and large are no good at reasoning. We harbor biases, are prone to irrational ways of thinking, and simply make mistakes. We often have trouble making good arguments in support of things we do believe, and perhaps more often fail to recognize good arguments in support of things we donât believe. But we can do better. And that is the goal of this class â to help you become better at reasoning through the acquisition of the basic tools of logic and sustained practice in applying them. By regularly attending class and completing assigned readings and exercises, you can expect to improve your skills in: (1) The identification, analysis, and evaluation of informal arguments. (2) The use of common forms of inductive reasoning, including analogy, causal inference, and probabilistic inference. (3) The use of deductive reasoning in formal systems. These skills, apart from being good in themselves and necessary for being a good thinker, will help you succeed on certain standardized tests like the LSAT and GMAT. Weâll spend part of the semester looking at arguments that aim to establish their conclusions with certainty. In looking at these arguments, weâll work with systems like syllogistic logic (All men are mortal, Socrates is a man, thereforeâ¦) and truth-functional logic. (IF Socrates is a man, THEN he is mortal. Socrates is a man. Thereforeâ¦) Then weâll look at arguments in which the conclusions only probably follow from the premises. Weâre given reasons like this all the time; sometimes these kinds of arguments come from statistics, sometimes from scientific experimentation. The kind of reasoning at work here is fundamentally different, and weâll spend time trying to make sense of the rules for this sort of reasoning as well. Grade is based on 4 exams.

title: Intro to Logic
description: Introduction to Logic: PHIL-151 Professor Davis Logic is the study of arguments, more specifically, of what distinguishes sound arguments from fallacious ones. We will study inductive as well as deductive logic, but the bulk of the course concerns formal deductive logic. We begin by studying the main patterns of inductive reasoning: the argument from analogy, Millâs method, and Hypothetical Induction. Then we will study the traditional Aristotelian logic of syllogisms, the Boolean approach characterized by the use of Venn Diagrams, and finally modern symbolic logic which utilizes truth tables and the method of deduction. A high degree of technical or mathematical ability is not required; this is the most elementary course in the logic sequence and does not presuppose any previous knowledge of logic or philosophy. There will be one short paper, three quizzes, and a final exam. PHIL-151 will satisfy the logic requirement for philosophy majors Required Readings: Davis. An Introduction to Logic, 2nd. Edition (Kunos Press).

title: Philosophy of Emotion
description: Itâs difficult to overstate the importance of emotions for our lives. Itâs perhaps even more difficult to come to a clear understanding of them. Emotions are notoriously elusive phenomena. In this course we will investigate a number of important philosophical questions about the nature of the mind by way of an exploration of emotions. These questions include: (1) What are mental states? Are emotions essentially feelings, cognitive evaluations, bodily responses to the environment, social constructs, or something else entirely? (2) What is the relation between mental states and brain states? Does functionalism, behaviorism, dualism, or identity theory best explain this relation? (3) What can we know about our own mental states? Are we the ultimate authorities on what we are thinking and feeling? Can we be mistaken about what we are experiencing? How do we know what we are feeling? And are subconscious emotions possible? (4) What is rationality? Are emotions in conflict with reason, or are they fundamentally bound up with it? (5) What are our moral judgments about? Are they expressions of our emotions and sentiments, or are they judgments about properties in the world? (6) How do social structures influence the mind? Does emotion affect our understanding of gender, race, and culture? (7) Why do we have conscious mental states at all? Are they products of evolution, or do they require some kind of metaphysical explanation? In this class we will survey classic and contemporary texts in order to address each of the above questions. We will read some of the classic works in philosophy, including texts by Plato, Aristotle, and Epictetus from the ancient period, and texts by Rene Descartes, David Hume, and Adam Smith from the modern period. We will also read a selection of contemporary pieces, including work by Gilbert Ryle, Laura Sizer, Paul Griffiths, Robert Solomon, Naomi Scheman, and Martha Nussbaum. Finally, we will read a number of relevant texts from sociological and scientific perspectives on the mind as well. The class will be largely discussion-based, and students will be expected to engage in structured conversation about difficult but important texts.

title: Pluralism & Conflict
description: Nowadays, pluralism is usually acknowledged as one of the fundamental and non-negotiable principles of democracy. However, pluralism has often been viewed, and is still viewed by some political theorists, as an obstacle rather than an aid to the fulfillment of the true scope of any democratic society: the âcommon goodâ or âcommon interest.â Pluralism has often been linked to individualism, and accused of exacerbating critical differences (social, political, religious, ethical, and so forth) between fellow citizens, thereby stirring conflict and/or inequality, and making it harder to pursue common objectives in a spirit of peaceful cooperation. On the other hand, the organicistic and solidaristic ideologies that seek either to neutralize multiplicity through uniformity, or to reconduct it to a higher, unifying order (not necessarily imposed from above), have been considered by some as more genuinely democratic. We will read some classics of political philosophy from Antiquity to modern times, paying attention to the historical and social context in which authors lived. We will discuss each text in the light of the following questions: What particular conception of pluralism emerges from the text, and how is it connected to other major political concepts such as liberty, equality, harmony, privacy, autonomy, non-conformity, etc.? Why is pluralism so relevant to democracy? Does pluralism necessarily lead to conflict? Is conflict inherently bad? Would it be realistic to conceive of a world where conflict does not exist at all? Bibliography You will be asked to read individually and then discuss in class selected parts of the following texts. Further readings will be suggested during the course. Plato, Republic (choice of passages from books 1, 5, 6, 8) Aristotle, Politics (choice of passages from books 1, 2, 5, 7) Machiavelli, Discourses on the first ten books of Livy Rousseau, Social contract Tocqueville, Democracy in America Mill, On liberty Marx, On the Jewish question; Manifesto of the Communist party Arendt, Civil disobedience (in Crises of the Republic) Foucault, Security, territory, population (lecture of 03/01/1978) For further reference: Princeton Readings in Political Thought, edited by Mitchell Cohen and Nicole Fermon, Princeton University Press, 1996 A Companion to Contemporary Political Philosophy, edited by Robert E. Goodin, Philip Pettit, Thomas W. Pogge, Wiley-Blackwell, 1996 The Cambridge Companion to Ancient Political thought, edited by Stephen Salkever, Cambridge University Press, 2009

title: Walking with Socrates
description: Plato had a profound influence on philosophy and on how we answer the âbig questionsâ about reality, knowledge, ethics, and so on. But Platoâs influence can also overshadow the ways in which his approach to philosophy differs from our own. In particular, he wrote in fictional stories, thought of philosophy as an investigation rather than its result, and saw the authority of reason as a controversial conclusion that must be proven. The aim of this class is to think about philosophy in the way that Plato thought about philosophy. We will transport ourselves back in time to the turn of the 4th century BCE, and explore Plato's motivations, assumptions, problems and concerns. We will address topics including: What is justice? How is knowledge possible? Are humans essentially self-interested? How should we live our lives? What form should our government take? What is love? And many others. We will read many of Platoâs well-known dialogues (Euthyphro, Apology, Crito, Republic, Gorgias, Theaetetus, etc.), and also several of his lesser-known dialogues (Clitophon, Laches, Lysis, etc.). With each dialogue, we will attempt both (a) to provide a plausible interpretation of that dialogue as a literary text and (b) to reconstruct the philosophical arguments contained therein. In many ways, this class blurs the lines between philosophy and literature. Students will be assessed on the basis of small quizzes, presentations, interpretive essays, and argumentative essays.

title: Happiness & Discontents
description: What should we strive for in life? A common answer is that we should strive to be happy. But what is happiness, and what is it that makes people happy? Perhaps more importantly, should happiness even be our most important goal? What about living a moral life? What about living a flourishing life, or a meaningful life? What constitutes a flourishing or meaningful life? Do we need God for our lives to be meaningful? Do people in different nations have a different conception of the good life, or is there an underlying cross-cultural consensus? Using the tools of philosophy, economics, psychology, and more, we will attempt to answer these questions.

title: Authority
description: Authority is like power. It is everywhere and it determines what sorts of conduct we are permitted, encouraged and required to pursue. As such, it has a tremendous impact on the whole of our lives. Or so we tend to suppose. But is authority really the sort of thing that belongs to anyone? If so, to whom does it belong? And if not, then what exactly is it? What does it take to justify a claim to authority? Do we even need rulers? What if there is no legitimate authority anywhere? Would this be a good thing? Is it even possible? In this course we will explore questions like these with an eye to enriching our understanding of the nature and basis of authority in our society and of the roles it can and should play in our lives. We will seek help from a wide variety of thoughtful voices, including those of Aristotle, Thomas Hobbes, John Stuart Mill and Emma Goldman. Our readings will be drawn from the Western tradition of moral, social and political thought and include perspectives ranging from defenders of authoritarianism to feminist critics of patriarchy. The courseâs graded content will include one short and two medium-length written assignments as well as class participation, of which contributions to in-class and online discussions of the assigned material will form a part.

title: Contemporary Political Thought
description: This course largely carries on from where Political and Social Thought left off, taking a more in-depth look at modern political thought. It provides an overview of the most influential contemporary schools of political thought. Each week students read texts. I will lecture on the topic, and then we will have a general class discussion on the same topicâsometimes on the same day, sometimes on succeeding days. The course will cover the following topics: overview of contemporary political thought, liberalism, conservatism, Marxism, communitarianism, feminism, environmentalism, post-structuralism and post-modernism, multi-culturalism, political thought beyond the Western tradition, and democratic theory.

title: Democracy & Inequality
description: Over the past year, the Occupy movement has brought fresh attention to two of political philosophyâs perennial questions: 1. How should societies go about making political decisions? 2. How should they distribute resources? Members of the Occupy movement and almost all of their fellow citizens agree on the basic answer to the first question: we ought to make political decisions democratically. However, individuals disagree about the details of how democratic decisions should be made. For instance, members of the Occupy movement have argued that our current political system needs to be significantly changed to prevent wealthy individuals and corporations from unfairly influencing the democratic process. Others argue that the way in which wealthy individuals and corporations influence the political process is justified in virtue of their right to free speech. In the first part of this course, we will explore the question of how political decisions should be made in a democracy by reading and discussing the work of political philosophers such as Locke, Hume, and Rawls. We will address general theoretical topics such as whether a social contract exists and, if so, what its nature is, as well as particular concrete topics such as campaign finance reform. In the second part of the course, we will investigate the question of what, if anything, justifies the existence of inequality in societies. We will once again take both theoretical and concrete approaches to answering this question. We will explore general theories of distributive justice, such as those of Marx, Rawls, and Nozick, as well as particular concrete topics such as income tax rates and inequality in K-12 education.

title: Enquiry: Science and Philosophy
description: While the enterprises of philosophy and science are importantly different, they are also fundamentally intertwined in a way that deeply influences our conception of the world. In this course, we will examine how science and philosophy are related and how this affects us. Why think that abstract philosophical reflection and scientific investigation of the real world intersect? The first section of the course considers how philosophical enquiry and scientific enquiry have affected each other throughout their histories. Weâll explore the connections between contemporary philosophy of mind, computation theory and psychological and physiological research; the development of optics and Early Modern philosophy; and the question of causation. In the second part of the course, we ask how the intersection of science and philosophy influences, or should influence, our daily lives. One way weâll address this topic is by discussing the notions of species, sex and gender in philosophy, biology and our everyday intuitions. Our overall goal is to clarify how we can and do navigate the world in light of the interrelation of these two ways of thinking. Weâll do this by exploring the differences, similarities and mutual influence of our scientific and philosophical thinking.

title: Civic Engagement & Education (CBL)
description: This course combines reflection and action. In addition to the twice-weekly meetings on campus, students will be expected to invest significant additional time working with high school students at a low-performing public school located in Southeast D.C. The school serves a student population that is 100% African American and predominately from low-income families. GU students will travel to the high school two times a week to lead a group of students as they all work together to identify and address a community-based problem. Georgetown and the high school students will plan and carry out a public event at which they will share the work they have done during the semester. Students should expect to commit roughly 6-8 hours per week traveling to and from the school and working with the high school students. The GU and high school students will also plan and carry out a public event at which they will share their work with the community. In addition to the work at the high school and the conference, the course will include several other out-of-the classroom responsibilities such as hosting a visit by the high school students to the GU campus and planning with GU classmates for the meetings with the high school students. These community-based experiences will be supplemented by readings, class discussions, and blog writing assignments. We will study the role of schooling in preparing and enabling (or failing to prepare and enable) youth to have a voice in their schools and communities. We will focus specifically on the relationships between democracy and schooling; the kinds of knowledge, skills, and character traits needed to be effective in working toward social change through community action; the conditions that must be provided if students are to become active participants in democratic life; and the role of the hidden curriculum in denying students from particular demographic backgrounds access to the kind of learning experiences that enable effective civic and academic engagement. The focus will be primarily on schools and students in Washington, D.C. public schools. All GU students enrolled in the course are expected to take a very active role in the conduct of the course itself and in the work within the high school. However, this course does not require any previous experience working with youth or in schools. While the course is very demanding in several senses, students who enroll report that it is also an incredibly valuable learning experience unlike anything they have experienced through their other, campus-based coursework.

title: Political Philosophy
description: The purposes of this course are to enable you to find your way around the field of political philosophy, identify its main intellectual problems and solutions and apply its characteristic patterns of thought when reading new texts or understanding current political issues. Weâll do this by examining some classical, modern and contemporary works of political philosophy. Weâll start with the classical philosophers, Plato and Aristotle, and see how their views are transformed by the medieval thinker, Aquinas. Then we will read the modern social contract theorists, Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau, and their critic, Hume. Weâll cover Kant and Mill, noting the fundamental contrast in the way they ground the political ideals of freedom and autonomy. Weâll end with the contemporary philosophers Rawls, Nozick, Taylor and Habermas, focusing on the contrast between the liberal and communitarian conceptions of public life. One organizing theme is social justice, that is, how the benefits and burdens of collective life should be distributed. Another theme concerns the different notions of freedom as the absence of constraints, participation in political life and obedience to a law one gives oneself. Every conception of social justice and human freedom presupposes a theory of human nature and a social ideal â a vision of the aims or purposes of social cooperation and collective life generally. Another important goal of the course is to make explicit and evaluate these competing visions of human nature and the social life. At the end, you should have a sense of how these philosophers understand the fundamental political notions of justice, democracy, liberty and equality. Throughout we will have an eye on contemporary applications of these notions.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Linda Zagzebski says that âWhat should I believe about the ultimate matters of the universe?â is âthe question at the intersection of philosophy and religion.â The core of this class will be an exploration of how three of the most important figures in the history of philosophy â Thomas Aquinas, Immanuel Kant, and David Hume â have thought about the ultimate matters of the universe, and how they have (or have not) made a place in their thoughts for God, the soul, miracles, and other subjects of religious belief. In addition, we will also have units on the nature of religious belief, the relationship between faith and reason, and the implications of religious diversity for religious faith. In a philosophy class, one goal is to acquire an understanding of various interesting ideas that have been proposed. But another goal, which is at least as important as the first, is to learn to understand and assess the lines of reasoning that have been offered in defense of those ideas. So, in the course, you will write several short papers in which you will explain and assess the defense of some idea weâve encountered. When I say you will assess it, I mean that you will come to a verdict on it and then back up your verdict with your own line of reasoning. Regular contributions to the class blog will also be required.

title: Philosophy of Law
description: TBA

title: Philosophy of Law
description: There are three commonplaces that guide investigation in the philosophy of law: first, that law is (in some important sense) a matter of social fact; that law is (in some important sense) authoritative; and third, that law is (in some important sense) for the common good. The aim of this course is to get clearer on these commonplaces and on the questions in philosophy of law that they bear on, and to understand and criticize various proposed answers to them. The topics we will consider include: the nature of law; the fundamental roles in legal systems (citizen, legislator, judge); the appropriate aims of law; the nature and justification of criminal law; and the nature and justification of tort law. We will also consider various views that aim not to flesh out the guiding assumptions but to show them up as unrealized or unrealizable. Requirements include active and prepared class participation, two papers, and a final exam.

title: Philosophy of Music
description: This bridge course will be designed especially for students who are engaged in music production, but is open to all. We will ask a range of fundamental questions about music: what is music? What is an aesthetic response to a musical or other artwork? Is there a fact of the matter about what music is good? How does music evoke emotion, and how central is that to its nature? How does music function in forming social groups, especially political movements? Is it legitimate to judge music by it's political value? The text for the course is Aesthetics: A comprehensive anthology (Blackwell), Steve Cahn, and Aaron Meskin (ed) Students will actively participate in discussion, write 3 short papers, and develop a final project in consultation with the professor.

title: Philosophy and Star Trek
description: Star Trek is very philosophical. What better way, then, to do philosophy, but to watch Star Trek, read philosophy and hash it all out in class (and on Blackboard)? Thatâs the plan. This course will center on topics in metaphysics that come up again and again in Star Trek. In conjunction with watching Star Trek, we will read excerpts from the writings of great philosophers, extract key concepts and arguments and then analyze those arguments. Questions we will wrestle with include: I. Is time travel possible? Could you go back and kill your grandmother? What is time? II. What is the relation between your mind and your brain--are they separate items or identical? Can persons survive death? Could a machine someday think? Is Data a person? III. What is a person? Must you have the same body to be you? Same memories? When do we have one person, and when do we have two (think of the episodes where people "split" or "fuse"). IV. Do you have free will, or are you determined by the laws of nature to do exactly what you wind up doing (while believing you have free will)? Or both? What is freewill? Text: Metaphysics: Classic and Contemporary Readings, 2nd ed., Ed. by Hoy & Oaklander (Wadsworth, 2005). Four short papers.

title: Pragmatism
description: This course will introduce students to a fruitful, if an unfortunately minority, research program in philosophy: pragmatism. Like Jazz's relationship to music, pragmatism is America's unique contribution to philosophy. This semester, we will first examine the "traditional" doctrines that were commonly advocated here in the U.S. as well as in Europe (both on the Continent and in the British Isles): Cartesianism, representationalism, and the correspondence theory of truth. Subsequently, we will examine the development of the pragmatist rejection of all three of these doctrines in the works of C.S. Peirce, William James, and John Dewey. In the final section of the class, we'll examine how more contemporary philosophers have developed the pragmatist insight--both in core philosophical fields as well as in questions of social and moral thought. Grading will be via a combination of examinations and philosophical essay assignments.

title: Democracy and Religion
description: SUMMER 2011 TPhilosophy 181âDemocracy and Religion This course introduces students to major themes in national and international justice by investigating the ideal of democracy and the challenge of religion. The course is divided into two parts. The first lays conceptual tools about democracy and religion, tracing the emergence of democracy as a political ideal in Plato, Rousseau, and Locke and draws parallels to modern conceptions of democracy in Rawls, Kymlicka, Taylor, and Sen. We ask, and try to answer, these questions: Is democracy the only just political arrangement? How might religion, and multiculturalism more broadly, pose a challenge to the republican, liberal, and participatory versions of the democratic ideal? The second half of the course moves from these issues of national justice to the problem of international justice, particularly with respect to the Middle East. A reading of Senâs Identity and Violence will help bridge the gap and get us started thinking about religionâs role in the world today. In the rest of the section, we will consider questions like: How should a democratic state act toward other sorts of regimes? Do different principles of justice and toleration apply across nation-states than apply within nations? How should religious identity be taken into consideration when contemplating and carrying out military action? The course endeavors to present a variety of perspectives about religion's role in today's world: from assessments of the justice of secular and religious states, to the ethical issues religion presents in very practical situations.

title: Phenomenology
description: Phenomenology is one of the more vibrant and influential philosophical movements to come out of the twentieth century. Today, phenomenology is a part of numerous philosophical discussions, from cognitive science to environmental ethics. This course will begin by surveying its most influential founding figures (including Brentano, Husserl, Heidegger, and Merleau-Ponty) before moving into more recent applications and debates. Consequently, in addition to becoming familiar with phenomenology as a movement, this course will also introduce students to a number of important contemporary debates in areas such as mind, language, perception, and ethics.

title: Pursuing the Truth
description: This course is about a project we are all engaged in to one extent or another -- sorting through the beliefs, opinions, values, and commitments that we either hold or find available in order to distinguish the true from the false, holding fast to the former and jettisoning the later. How does this pursuit of truth, and the holding fast to it when we take ourselves to have found it, play out in our lives? How should it? In many of Plato's dialogues, Socrates argued that this pursuit was not a solitary endeavor. He attempted to make the case that the way to pursue the truth was in conversation, or 'dialogue,' with others. He argued that a commitment to the truth of our beliefs should not lead us to attempt to persuade others to see it our way by, say, giving a rousing speech. Rather than such speech-giving -- which he labeled 'rhetoric' -- he thought such a commitment to the truth of our beliefs should lead to conversation with others and the cooperative examination of the reasons for or against maintaining such beliefs. If we believe what we do because we take it to be true, should we not hear others out -- and even seek them out -- if they claim to have good reasons not to believe as we do? And what if they seek us out? Would this be merely a nuisance? We will engage questions such as these primarily by examining some of Plato's key dialogues -- the *Gorgias*, the *Protagoras*, and others. These dialogues will also raise important issues in metaphysics, epistemology, political philosophy, and ethics. They will also illuminate Plato's views about important philosophical questions such as the nature of knowledge, the proper objects of knowledge, the relationship between emotion and reason, the nature of the soul, the good life, and more. To make our discussions more concrete, we will also look to contemporary media, culture, and politics as we discuss these issues, asking questions such as the following: To what extent does the political discourse that surrounds us, especially in an election year, count as pursuing the truth through conversation or dialogue? Whatever the answer, why might it matter? Is it bad to 'flip flop,' or change one's position on important political matters? Why or why not? What might be the proper way for an American Idol contestant to take Simon Cowell's sharp criticisms? And what about, on the other end of the spectrum, Paula Abdul's generally quite positive feedback? What -- not simply 'who' -- is the subject of Stephen Colbert's satire? Is such satire importantly different than what it is intended to criticize?

title: Pursuit of Happiness
description: âWe hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal, that they are endowed â¦ with certain unalienable Rights, that among these are Life, Liberty and the Pursuit of Happinessâ These are, I am sure, very familiar words to us all. Today, however, many of us likely have a very different interpretation about what the term âPursuit of Happinessâ entails than Thomas Jefferson might have intended. So, this class will center around questions regarding happiness. What is happiness? And how might we improve our pursuit of it? Readings will include some precursors/influences to Jefferson on these questions including: Aristotle, Seneca, Lucretius, and Rousseau. We will also read some of what Jefferson himself wrote on these questions. However, since we are not Greeks, Romans, or even living in the Enlightenment, we will also consider some modern discussions on Happiness,including recent historical and psychological insights into the phenomenon.

title: Hallucinating
description: We all make mistakes. We all sometimes remember things that didn't really happen and sometimes forget things that really did. We all get confused by optical illusions, and phantom pains and itches. We all sometimes believe false things other people tell us. We all sometimes confuse our dreams for reality. We all sometimes believe things because we really want them to be, even though we have good reasons for thinking they aren't. And sometimes we're just plain hallucinating. How can we be sure that we're not mistaken about everything? What kinds of thing can we know for sure? What is knowledge anyway? We'll look at some old, not so old, and even less old answers to and ways of thinking about these questions. Some of these will come from philosophers. But some will come as well from science, science fiction, literature, film, art, etc. The views we come across will be very different, and we'll spend a lot of time trying to sort out our own reactions to these questions. We'll all probably end up with different answers ourselves---but at least they'll be our own answers, and we'll have good reasons for them. (That's the philosophy part.) Here are just a few of the things we'll be looking at: * The films The Matrix and Existenz * Kafka's ``The Metamorphosis"; Thompson's Fear and Loathing in Los Vegas; Burroughs' Naked Lunch; Ellison's The Invisible Man; etc. * Hume's A Treatise of Human Nature (parts) * Recent research in cognitive science and psychology (of individuals and groups) * Some selections from Stanislaw Lem (the good stuff) * The Star Trek pilot episode, ``The Cage" * Some more philosophy stuff * Parts of Don Quixote * Religion and other mass delusions.

title: Sex, Science, Society
description: Man and woman, male and female. These categories are, intuitively, quite easy to identify and apply. We use themâboth deliberately and without noticingâto navigate the world every day. But when we examine these categories, they are hard to pin down: what are the necessary and sufficient conditions to qualify as a woman (or man) and how are they determined? What about being female (or male)? Are the answers to those questions just the same, or might they be different? In the first section of this course we will examine the nature of categories of sex and gender with help from scientific views about the character of natural kinds and related notions like species. In light of our discoveries, we will reexamine the social and political roles of these categories. This course will serve as a foundation for understanding issues related to science, technology, gender studies and social justice. We will read articles and selections from books from contemporary philosophical and scientific sources, including authors such as W.V.O. Quine, John DuprÃ©, Cordelia Fine, Anne Fausto-Sterling, Sally Haslanger and Iris Marion Young. Assignments will principally consist in two short papers and one long paper.

title: Ethical Theory
description: This course will be a systematic, intensive exploration of philosophical ethics. It aims to provide philosophy majors and minors with a broad overview of ethical theory, but in a way that enables a deep level of engagement with its central concerns and considerations. Students will gain or improve their grasp of the most influential versions of normative ethical theory, including Aristotelian and Humean virtue ethics, Stoic ethics, Kantianism, utilitarianism, and social contract theory. We will consider the accounts of moral psychology presupposed by those theories and how well they cohere with contemporary research. We will also consider important questions in metaethics such as the existence of moral properties, the status of moral claims, and the nature of reasons. Finally, we will examine the resources of ethical theory for resolving practical ethical problems. The class will be conducted in a seminar fashion. Students will be responsible for presenting the material and leading discussions about it. Students will also be required to write two short (2-3 page) papers and one longer (10-12 page) paper with a mandatory first draft and revision. There will be a take-home, essay-based final exam. The primary textbook for the course will be: Cahn and Markie, Ethics: History, Theory, and Contemporary Issues 4th ed. (Oxford, 2009) Students will also be asked to purchase a copy of Kantâs Doctrine of Virtue, if they do not already own it.

title: Honors Thesis Res Tutorial
description: Individual research tutorial for students in the Honors Program in Philosophy. Tutorial culminates in submission and defense of an Honors Thesis. Admission by permission of the Department's Undergraduate Director only.

title: Honors Thesis Tutorial
description: Permission Required.

title: Text Seminar
description: Course Goals & Description In this text seminar you will learn to read philosophical texts carefully and to write philosophical essays. The text that will be the object of our study will be Arthur Danto's NARRATION AND KNOWLEDGE, which is a study of the nature of historical knowledge and the epistemology of justifying historical theses. We will also use Anthony Weston's A RULEBOOK FOR ARGUMENTS as a methodology guide to writing philosophy. Texts & Readings Arthur Danto, NARRATION AND KNOWLEDGE, 3rd ed. (New York: Columbia Univ. Press, 2007). Anthony Weston. A RULEBOOK FOR ARGUMENTS, 4th ed. (Indianapolis: Hackett Publishing, 2008). Assignments & Expectations of Students Students will write around six (6) short papers, ranging in length from one (1) to six (6) pages. Students should expect to write a short paper about every other week. What Students Should Know This course is only for philosophy majors. Approval by the Dept.'s Director of Undergraduate Studies is required.

title: Text Seminar
description: Descartesâs Meditations on First Philosophy Huaping Lu-Adler Course Description In this course we closely read and analyze Descartesâs Meditations on First Philosophy. This work is truly revolutionary in the history of philosophy. The metaphysical and epistemological issues it raised have exercised many of the greatest philosophical minds ever since its publication. Following the most recent Descartes scholarship, we will sort out and discuss Descartesâs own views on these issues in light of his interest in the scientific revolution. To facilitate such discussions, we will read John Carrieroâs Between Two Worlds: A Reading of Descartesâs Meditations (2009) along with the Meditations itself.

title: Foundation of Education (CBL)
description: TBA

title: Philosophy of Evolutionary Biology
description: When one considers the vast diversity of independent factors occurring in concert needed to make the original spark of life possible and the unimaginably complex set of ongoing contingencies involved in its subsequent evolution, it becomes difficult not to think that this awesome drama had to have been guided by some kind of coordinating influence. Moreover, this difficulty becomes even more pressing if one also considers the exquisitely precise order and efficiency displayed by even the smallest operational part of a living tissue available to scientific inspection. In the light of these considerations, improbability seems to disqualify coincidence as a credible explanation. On the other hand, there are serious concerns that motivate a strong resistance to viewing these developments as the result of some sort of design. Much in the very best of contemporary philosophical writing on this subject is based on the conviction that to invoke design as an explanation would necessarily involve a form of speculation that goes beyond the reach of cogent argument and empirical confirmation. My purpose in this course is to introduce the class to some of the best of the more recent writing on these topics. The course will maintain a strict neutrality on the issues and will provide the students with as wide a range of positions and arguments as time and availability of material permit. The goal is to let the students come to their own conclusions. We will read selectively in Robert Brandonâs Concepts and Methods in Evolutionary Biology (Cambridge, 1996) and Colin Allenâs (et al.) Natural Purposes (MIT, 1998). The heart of the course will be the discussions of assigned articles. Students will take turns at priming the discussion by presenting a short critical commentary on the designated writing. Finally, everyone will present review of a recent book (not used in the course) that represents a broader view of the issues than can be found in articles. The book may be one selected from my library on this subject or one (with my approval) from another source. This presentation together with a brief written outline will be given on a date agreed to by the student and will constitute the main source of the grade for the course. Required texts: Concepts and Methods in Evolutionary Biology. Robert Branden (0-521-49888-0) Natureâs Purposes. Colin Allen et al. (0-262-51097-9)

title: Kant, Hegel, Marx: The philosophy of History
description: Because of the deficiencies of the Registrarâs Software, the full name of this course is not displayed on its page. The full name is: Kant, Hegel, Marx: the Philosophy of History. We will be reading Kantâs short essays on the philosophy of history, all of Hegelâs lecture series on the philosophy of history, and selections from Marx on the topic. Among the themes of the course will be the idea of âhistoricismâ itself as an approach to philosophical, intellectual and political questions, and whether it makes any sense to speak of political and moral progress. The course itself will be both historical and philosophical. We will be looking at the contexts in which the authors lived and wrote, and we will be evaluating them in light of what kinds of arguments they are making.

title: Bioethics & Mental Illness
description: In the first part of the course we will address the first rule of ethics: do good and avoid evil. We will address that question in light of some recent publications or research reports. First, Whitaker reports that the number of disabled mentally ill in the United States tripled over the past two decades. This raises the question as to whether mentally ill people receive the best care or whether there are preventable (?) causes which raise needlessly the number of mentally ill people in the US. Second, Irving Kirsch reports that the placebo effect is clinically not so different from the results of medication in the treatment of depression, except for severe depression. Kirsch also reports on the effect of placebo on knee pain, ulcer etcâ¦. He also reports that the placebo effect is better if the doctor first listens to the patient. Walter Brown brings out the fact that two positive trials are sufficient to approve a drug. This raises further questions about social policies about the treatment of mental illness. We will also include in the discussion the advise of Bertram Karon, a researcher and practicing clinician: if medication works use it and consult your doctor for any change in medication. In the second part we will address the debate of the causes of mental illness. We will include in the debate the question of the causes of schizophrenia and psychosis as well. We will make use of the large study by Tienari et al. to demonstrate both the hereditary factor in mental illness as well as what is called the environmental factors (psycho-social factors). We will refer to the work of Courtenay Harding to demonstrate that ability to work has a healing effect. Thus we will have introduced the bio-psycho-social model of mental illness. The psycho-social dimension of mental illness will provide arguments that the mind is more than brain activity. It is also more than an individual performance. The mind and mental well-being or mental health is also an intersubjective and social phenomenon. We will try to use the concept of recognition as one important element to conceptualize the intersubjective and social dimension of mental illness and its opposite mental well-being. We will finish this part by looking at the questions asked about the very conception of illness in the psychiatric classifications in the DSM manuals. We will take a close look at some of the psychological and epistemological consequences for refusing to look at causes in the classification of mental illnesses in the recent DSM manuals. In a third part we will address two psychological models for the explanation of the psychological dimension of mental illness and well-being: the attachment theory and the triangular theory of Lacan. Attachment theory stresses the maternal role. Lacan stresses the paternal function. Both give an important role to language as an explanation for mental well-being or mental illness. The revolutionary change in treatment of mental illness brought about by Freud was also connected with the introduction of talk therapy. We will read Freudâs article demonstrating that hysterical paralysis does not follow the rules of the anatomy of the body but can be explained by the characteristics of language. We will then see how many approaches to mental illness introduce talking as one element of the treatment of the mentally stressed or the mentally ill. In a fourth part we will compare the ethical guidelines of the different mental health professionals. We will study in detail the exceptions to confidentiality. We will compare the legal requirements to âviolateâ the confidentiality of information obtained in talk therapies with the strict confidentiality of confession respected by the law. We will study the question as to why the mental health professionals accepted the legal infringement of confidentiality. We will consider both the claim that such legal intrusion is sometimes detrimental and is sometimes beneficial for the treatment In the fifth part we ask the question as to whether bio-ethical rules can be applied to the treatment of the mentally ill or whether different norms should be introduced. In particular we will ask the question of the role of autonomy as âaâ or âtheâ central guide. In the sixth part we will study the idea that a mental health professional should have an ethical position or attitude: i.e., the task is not so much giving advice as to promote or elicit agency in the patient. This idea is most explicitly defended for psycho-dynamic or psychoanalytic treatments. It is also present in a Rogerian approach. We will be making use of Lacanâs publication: âThe Ethics of Psychoanalysis.â Bibliography: Bloch, Chodoff and Green (Eds). Psychiatric Ethics. Third edition(2000) Oxford University Press. ISBN 0-19-262899-2 Codes of Ethics for the Helping Professions. (2007). Thomson. Brooks/Cole.ISBN 0-495-18717-8 Quick Reference to the Diagnostic Criteria from DSM-IV-TR. ISBN 0-89042-027-0 Whitaker, Robert. (2010)Anatomy of an Epidemic.New York. Crown Publishers. Articles and chapters will be provided on blackboard or through the library.

title: Symbolic Logic
description: Logic examines the principles of correct reasoning. In Symbolic Logic we use and investigate one theory that seems to have successfully captured a variety of such principlesâclassical first order logic. The notions of formal language, proof and derivation, and truth under an interpretation will be considered in detail. For the most part we will be working within classical first order logic, but some time will be devoted to proving things about that theory and to possible alternatives to it. One of the main tools for this kind of âmetatheoreticâ investigation is mathematical induction. For this reason, and since this mathematical induction is itself a useful method of reasoning, we will spend some time on this. There are no pre-requisites for this course, but those who wish a slower-paced course that omits the metatheory and mathematical induction and aims more to improve reasoning ability should consider taking Introduction to Logic instead. This course is more suitable for those with theoretical interests in philosophy, mathematics, linguistics, computer science, or cognitive science. There will be no papers and little reading, but there will be weekly homework assignments, a midterm and a final exam.

title: Heidegger
description: In this advanced seminar, we will read a significant portion of Martin Heideggerâs magnum opus, Being and Time (1927). Time permitting, we will also read a handful of Heideggerâs most important essays, including âThe Origin of the Work of Artâ and âThe Question Concerning Technologyâ. We will focus on mastering the framework of Heideggerâs project, its terminology, and its differences from traditional philosophical views and methods. Heideggerâs goal was to reawaken the question of the meaning of being, which he takes to have been neglected throughout much of the history of Western philosophy. In posing and pursuing the question of what it means to be rather than not, Heidegger develops a novel and powerful account of the human being as the understander of being â as both responsible for and responsive to the sense that things make. Drawing on Aristotle and Kant as well as Kierkegaard and Augustine, Heidegger analyses our embeddedness in the world and society, and our openness to meaning through action, tradition and language. His later essays extend his account to cover (for example) how art works to produce and shape the sense that things make, and how the modern technological attitude impoverishes the sense that things make. Assessment will consist primarily in short answer exegetical exercises, which will force students to confront Heideggerâs difficult prose and ideas directly. Students must be prepared to commit to grappling with a notoriously obscure thinker and his famously impenetrable texts.

title: History of Ancient/Medieval Philosophy
description: The course will be an in-depth survey of ancient and medieval philosophy, beginning with the pre-Socratic philosophers and ending with figures of the fourteenth century. The first part of the course deals with ancient Greek philosophy (taught by Asst. Prof. Kate Withy). We will trace the ways in which the metaphysical questions of the pre-Socratic philosophers become entangled with ethical questions in post-Socratic philosophy (Plato, Aristotle). Topics include: reality, God, causation, the soul, knowledge, the good life, and philosophical practice and method. The second half of the course will be taught by Professor Mark Henninger. In this medieval part of the course we will trace how metaphysical questions influenced the medievalsâ thinking on ethical questions. After beginning by examining the relation of philosophy and theology, we will look at topics including: how language âhooks up withâ the world (the problem of universals), the metaphysical basis of ethical theory in a world of stable Aristotelian natures, the relation of ontological and moral goodness, and finally we will look at the medieval formulation of issues dealing with free will. Texts: Aristotle, The Basic Works of Aristotle, ed. Richard McKeon. The Modern Library, 2001. ISBN: 0375757996. List price: $21.95 Plato, Five Dialogues, Second Edition, tr. G.M.A. Grube, rev. John M. Cooper. Hackett, 2002. ISBN: 0872206335. List price: $7.95 Medieval Philosophy: Essential Readings with Commentary, ed. by Gyula Klima with F. Allhof and A. Jayprakash Vaidya. Blackwell Publishing, 2007. ISBN-13: 978-1-4051-3565-8, paperback. List price: $43.95

title: History of Modern Philosophy
description: Course Goals & Description Classic philosophical texts of the 16th through the 18th centuries (from the scientific revolution through Kant) will be examined in the context of historical developments in the philosophy of the period. The emphasis in lectures will be on textual analysis of major texts and the historical development of modern philosophy, often with discussion of the adequacy of the various competing philosophical theories. The major texts of the figures of this period who are generally recognized as major philosophers will be carefully examined, but the writings of several less renowned, but historically significant, figures will also be examined (e.g. Newton, Malebranche, Pascal, and Reid). The assigned material falls into the following general areas of philosophy: Metaphysics and Epistemology (M&E) Moral and Political Philosophy (MPP) The history of modern philosophy covered in this course dictates that most of the assignments be in M&E, but the student will be given ample opportunity to study assignments in MPP, especially when writing papers. A part of the design of the course is to allow students to work in areas that interest them the most.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Philosophical questions about God and religion are as old as philosophy itself. In this class, we will discuss several such questions: â¢ Is there a God? What are the arguments for Godâs existence? Are these arguments sound? What, if anything, does science tell us about Godâs existence? â¢ How can we reconcile the existence of an all-knowing, all-powerful, and completely good God with the existence of evil in the world? â¢ Is faith a substitute for reason? Is it permissible to believe in God without evidence? â¢ What is the nature of God? What is Godâs omnipotence? What is Godâs relation to time? â¢ Should we believe in the reality of miracles? â¢ Can more than one religion be true, or is there only one true religion? â¢ Does morality depend on God? Can there be morality that is independent of Godâs commands, or of Godâs nature?

title: Philosophy of Science
description: What, if anything, is special about the method of the sciences? In what way, if any, does this method provide a sure route to knowledge production? Is there a single method of inquiry that characterizes modern science, or is it a simple grab-bag of ad hoc techniques whose only justification is that it produces results? We will explore these and other questions in this course. Some main themes: scientific explanation (do the sciences explain things? if so, how?); scientific theories (is there any such thing? what kind of thing is a scientific theory?); epistemology of science (what confidence should we have in the results of scientific inquiry? does science lead us to the truth about nature? is there a truth about nature?). Spring.

title: Philosophy of Biology
description: Perhaps the most important thing we have learned in the last 200 years is that we are natural creatures just like all the others on the planet and that there are no ``essences" that constitute the various species---no such thing as ``dogness" or ``catness" or even ``humanness". While this profound result was established by Darwin and Wallace in the 1850s, its full significance is still not entirely clear. Beyond the fact that, as Theodosius Dobzhansky puts it, ``nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution," lies the fact that it is still quite difficult to make sense of evolution. This course will be concerned principally with contemporary theories of evolution (with a little history), and we will focus on: the structure and confirmation of evolutionary theories; the implications of such theories for our self-conception as knowing subjects; controversies both within and without the theories. Of particular interest will be: â¢ Levels of selection debates. What the heck is natural selection anyway? How and where does it act? on species? groups? individuals? genes? â¢ So-called intelligent design theories. â¢ The connection between ``evo-devo" (the science of the organismal development) and natural selection. Are there other mechanisms beside natural selection responsible for evolution? â¢ Laws of biology. Are there any? Living systems seem so specialized and contingent that it's hard to see what a true law of nature could be that applies to biology. Laws are supposed to be universal and exceptionless. Is that really possible in biology? We will look at these issues principally from the perspective of philosophers. That is, we will be attempting on the one hand to clarify and evaluate the concepts involved in the theory and on the other hand to try our hands at adjudicating and understanding the continuing debates in and about the theory. Very little in the way of biological background will be required, but students should have completed at least their first two philosophy courses.

title: Twentieth Century Continental Philosophy
description: âWe are unknown to ourselves, we knowersâ¦â â Nietzsche, On the Genealogy of Morality While it is important not to overstate the extent to which one can elide the methodological and stylistic differences between the thinkers who make up âcontinental philosophy,â Nietzscheâs comment provides an opening onto this diverse group. Continental thinkers are united, in part, by a belief that the views on subjectivity, knowledge, and society handed down by the Enlightenment are at the very least inadequate, and possibly also pernicious. In articulating this thought, they not only developed new approaches to traditional issues in epistemology, political and social philosophy, aesthetics, and ethics, but also inaugurated new ways of doing philosophy. This course will provide an overview of these developments, focusing on three of the main movements in twentieth-century continental thought: phenomenology, critical theory, and post-structuralism. Authors to be read include Husserl, Heidegger, Sartre, de Beauvoir, Adorno, Benjamin, Foucault, and Derrida.
