description: Spring 2006: Cognitive neuroscience is borne out of the marriage of psychology and neuroscience. Advances in functional brain imaging technologies have made possible the characterization of healthy mind/brain relationships in vivo. These relationships have been further elucidated by fractionation of cognition due to brain damage and psychiatric disease. This complementary approach is revealing how the brain produces the mind at an unprecedented pace. The objective of this graduate seminar is to journey through the field via seminal and contemporary articles that embody its interdisciplinary nature. The course is organized around functional domains and we will piece together their biology by examination of processes that mediate the functional experience and clinical conditions, psychiatric and degenerative or acute lesions, that perturb it. Readings will comprise review articles, seminal research papers, and opinion/perspective articles. My hope is that you will leave the course with at least a few insights into the organization of mental function in the brain and some outstanding questions, to ponder in your armchair or laboratory.

title: INTERMEDIATE MICROECONOMICS FOR DEVELOPMENT
description: This course is designed to provide students with an in-depth analysis of microeconomics theory at the intermediate level. We begin with the basic concepts (supply and demand), and then turn to consumer and producer theory, and finally examine markets and prices. The goal of the course is to equip you with the tools and techniques to analyze a wide variety of issues in developing economies. While we will be using mathematical tools when useful, the goal is to develop intuition and rigor in the way you think about the real world.

title: INTERMEDIATE MICROECONOMICS
description: In this course we examine the theory of the consumer and the theory of the firm under a variety of market structures. Throughout the course we study the concepts of opportunity cost, marginal analysis, economic incentives, efficiency, and market power; and apply them to public policy issues.

title: MACROECONOMICS
description: What determines aggregate economic variables such as Gross Domestic Product (GDP), inflation, interest rates, and the trade balance? What role does government policy play in the determination of these variables? This course develops analytic models of the macro-economy to answer these and other questions. We will develop short-run models to examine the business cycle and government monetary and fiscal policy. We will build models to look at the longer-run implications of low national saving and persistent budget deficits. Finally, we will develop economic growth models in order to understand better both cross-country differences in income per capita and the determinants of long-run economic growth. THIS IS ONE OF THE SELECTIVE COURSES FOR INTERNATIONAL POLICY & DEVELOPMENT STUDENTS.

title: PUBLIC FINANCE
description: This course serves as a continuation of Intermediate Microeconomics. It explores the impact of a variety of factors that may result in market failures, including public goods, externalities, information asymmetries and uncertainty. A rationale for government policies as a corrective device for market failures is then developed. This framework is then used to discuss a variety of government transfer programs and their financing, with particular emphasis on the incentive effects of such programs and policies. An introduction to cost/benefit analysis is incorporated as a mechanism for evaluating various programs.

title: PUBLIC FINANCE FOR DEVELOPMENT
description: This course builds on the material covered in Intermediate Microeconomics for Development and develops a set of tools for analyzing the role of government in a developing economy. In particular, we will examine justifications for government intervention, and study the impact of government programs and taxation systems on the welfare and behavior of its citizens. We pay special attention to the role of information asymmetries, uncertainty and externalities in the design of policy; as well as the role of non-tax revenues as a means of financing expenditures.

title: REGRESSION METHODS FOR DEVELOPMENT
description: This course builds on the material covered in Statistical Methods for Development Policy by further developing the studentâs understanding of the motivation for, and application of, statistical techniques to policy analysis in developing countries. The course introduces ordinary least squares regression models (OLS), the assumptions underlying OLS, and the consequences of violating those assumptions. The course also presents the treatment of limited dependent variables that don't lend themselves to OLS techniques. Students gain hands-on experience exploring policy questions using data and examples from developing countries, and a deeper understanding for how econometrics can be used to draw key policy insights. The course provides students with a framework for selecting an econometric model to evaluate policy, generating and interpreting results, and identifying the limitations of the model. PREREQUISITE: PPOL 506: STATISTICAL METHODS FOR DEVELOPMENT.

title: STATISTICAL METHODS FOR POLICY ANALYSIS
description: This course introduces students to research methods and to descriptive and inferential statistics that are often used in public policy research. The course aims to provide students with a solid foundation for analyzing data, conveying analyses in convincing and appropriate ways, and developing further statistical skills. Thus, the emphasis is on understanding research questions, describing data, and developing a core understanding of the scientific method in social science research. Topics covered include: causality, a brief introduction to experimental and nonexperimental research designs, measures of central tendency and dispersion, probability and probability distributions, hypothesis testing and confidence intervals, ANOVA, Chi-Square, correlation, simple regression, and a brief introduction to multiple regression. Students use Stata (a statistical software application) to develop their data analysis skills.

title: REGRESSION METHODS FOR POLICY ANALYSIS (QUANT II)
description: This class is the second of a three-part sequence in Quantitative Methods. In this course, students further study multivariate regression analysis with a main focus on ordinary least squares (OLS) regression and a modest focus on probit and logit regression. Topics include hypothesis testing, heteroskedasticity, omitted variable bias, measurement error, and topics related to model specification. The course concludes with section on research methods. Topics include causality, internal validity and external validity, sampling and weighting, measure validity and reliability, and missing data. The statistical package Stata is used.

title: ADVANCED REGRESSION & PROGRAM EVALUATION METHODS
description: This course is an introduction to advanced, but commonly used, methods of statistical analysis. The emphasis (as in Quantitative Methods I and II) is on applied learning; formal proofs and mathematical rigor are presented but not the principal focus of the course. Instruction will concentrate on how to determine the appropriate econometric approach in addressing various types of policy questions. Topics to be covered will include: Maximum Likelihood Estimation; Limited Dependent Variables (Linear Probability, Logit & Probit Models); Truncated/Censored Dependent Variables (Tobit Models); Panel data; Instrumental Variable methods; and Time-Series analysis.

title: PUBLIC POLICY PROCESS
description: This course analyzes the politics, institutions, norms, and actors involved in the agenda-setting, legitimation, and decision-making of public policy in the US. Students learn how to use analytical frameworks that explain how the policymaking process works, relates to the substance of policy, and applies to real world issues. Students also investigate how the policy making process varies across different issue areas. As one of several assignments, students write a policy memo suitable for submission to a member of Congress or other key political actor.

title: SOCIAL AND ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT POLICY
description: This course provides a framework for understanding the process of development, with an emphasis on the scope for policy to affect this process. The course begins by considering theories and evidence of growth processes as applicable to developing countries, including the role of innovation, structural transformation, geography, political institutions, international trade and capital flows, and foreign aid. Turning then to microeconomic underpinnings of development, the course highlights areas of market failure that provide a rationale for policy intervention in development: in credit, in insurance, in land, and in labor. Specific attention will then be drawn to the accumulation and impacts of human capital â education and health in particular. Finally, the course discusses the political economy of development policy, with a focus on the role of corruption and conflict. Throughout, the course will draw upon historical and contemporary examples from a range of developing countries, and will contrast policy challenges arising in both low income and rapidly growing emerging market economies.

title: COMPARATIVE PUBLIC POLICY PROCESS
description: This course provides a cross-national perspective on the institutions and processes of public policy-making. The first section of the course examines a series of theoretical perspectives for analyzing constraints on and opportunities for policymaking, including political culture, globalization, and feedbacks from past policy choices. The central section of the course focuses on political institutions as venues and vehicles for policymaking. The final section of the course looks at case studies of specific policy sectors, such as pensions, health care, and the environment. Throughout the course, there will be a balance between general and theoretical materials and a more intensive examination of a small number of countries.

title: INTERNATIONAL SOCIAL DEVELOPMENT POLICY
description: This course examines economic growth, human development and social policies, with an emphasis on rising countries. There is no prerequisite to enroll in the class. The course is intended for students with an interest in development who expect to work in public organizations or NGOs such as Oxfam, CARE, USAID, Inter-American Development Bank, the World Bank, etc. The course is aimed at giving students an overview of current development debates by presenting different analytical approaches to analyze the empirical evidence on welfare in developing countries. The course will be part lecture and part class discussion. Students are expected to actively participate and will be asked questions in class on the readings.

title: POVERTY AND THE SOCIAL SAFETY NET
description: This seminar course examines the nature and extent of poverty in the U.S., and looks at public policies aimed at creating a social safety net as protection against the effects of poverty. Anti-poverty policies - social insurance, income maintenance and other programs- are discussed with an emphasis on the behavioral impacts of the policies and their ultimate effectiveness in reducing poverty. Education and labor market policies for the disadvantaged are considered as well.

title: CHILD DEVELOPMENT AND PUBLIC POLICY
description: The optimal development of children is central to the goals of a humane society and to the future of our nation. The course will provide an overview of child development drawing primarily from the perspectives of developmental psychology and sociology. Assigned readings and lectures will explore approaches to and challenges for measuring healthy child development and highlight their applications in current scholarly research and government policy. Along with laying a foundation of the contexts that contribute to favorable child outcomes, the course will examine circumstances that place the nationâs children at risk - including high poverty rates, growing inequality, fragile families, teenage childbearing, divorce, and imbalances in work and home responsibilities. Within each topic area, students will critically evaluate relevant current policy responses as well as identify new avenues that would be suggested by a child development perspective.

title: THE POLICY AND POLITICS OF ENTITLEMENTS
description: This course examines the policy and political issues surrounding âentitlementâ programs. The concept behind the course is that different entitlement programs -- like Medicare, Medicaid, welfare and social security â raise a common set of policy and political issues. These issues include income redistribution, intergenerational equity, means-testing vs. universality, compulsory participation vs. individual choice, and individual vs. social responsibility.

title: RISK MANAGEMENT
description: Resources are finite, and policy-making commands trade-offs. For example, an agency or department might focus on the prevention of nuclear terrorism at the expense of other priorities, such as violent crime policing or pandemic planning and response. But such a decision might not represent the best use of scarce resources, depending on the risk associated with each priority. This course examines risk management in the public policy context. It introduces students to the concepts and methods of risk, risk assessment (the bulk of the course), and risk management; and it presents examples of applications of these concepts and methods in policy settings including homeland security, financial regulation, public health, and the environment.

title: CAMPAIGN MANAGEMENT & STRATEGY
description: Beyond the headlines, debates, television ads and stump speeches, the public rarely gets to see what actually goes into putting together a winning political campaign. This course focuses on the nuts and bolts of building a campaign strategy and organization from the ground up â from targeting, budgeting, research, polling, message development and delivery to Getting Out the Vote. Students will get the inside scoop from top political campaign operatives and will spend the semester developing their own campaign plans.

title: PUBLIC POLICY, ADVOCACY AND SOCIAL CHANGE
description: This course examines the historic role nonprofit organizations have played in influencing public policy, building movements, organizing issue campaigns, and fostering civic engagement, as well as the changing relationships nonprofits have had with government, business and the media. It looks at many of the current policy and advocacy initiatives in fields such as, the environment, poverty, civil rights, consumerism, health, tax/budget issues, and government reform. The course also provides an overview of the diverse strategies being used by nonprofit organizations to carry out their mission, such as organizing, legal services and litigation, research, voter education, lobbying, policy formulation, public education, and coalition building. Class involves a mix of guest speakers, student engagement, and faculty lecture. A small, informal reception will follow each class to give students an opportunity to interact with outstanding guest speakers.

title: STATE & LOCAL GOVERNANCE
description: The goal of this course is to provide students with a basic knowledge of how state and local governments work in the United States. It is designed for students interested in state and local management as a career and for those who feel they can benefit from knowledge of governance at state and local level. State governments have major independent roles in making public policy. They are also important partners of the federal government in the implementation of many policies. Accordingly, the first part of this course focuses on state governments by examining how formal governance structures and institutional processes influence and constrain policy choices and administrative decisions at the state level. The theme of the second part of the course is the study of the local governments and their functions. This part of the course covers the origins and development of local government, its constitutional and statutory foundations, and political and social contexts of counties and municipalities. Finally, the last part is devoted to the case studies and to qualitative and quantitative analyses of state and local governments in order to give students a framework to understand, interpret and predict policy outcomes at the lower levels of the government. NOTE: This is a full semester course. If you took the PPOL 536 module in Fall 2010, you CANNOT repeat this course.

title: STATE & LOCAL GOVERNANCE
description: The goal of this course is to provide students with a basic knowledge of how state and local governments work in the United States. It is designed for students interested in state and local management as a career and for those who feel they can benefit from knowledge of governance at state and local level. State governments have major independent roles in making public policy. They are also important partners of the federal government in the implementation of many policies. Accordingly, the first part of this course focuses on state governments by examining how formal governance structures and institutional processes influence and constrain policy choices and administrative decisions at the state level. The theme of the second part of the course is the study of the local governments and their functions. This part of the course covers the origins and development of local government, its constitutional and statutory foundations, and political and social contexts of counties and municipalities. Finally, the last part is devoted to the case studies and to qualitative and quantitative analyses of state and local governments in order to give students a framework to understand, interpret and predict policy outcomes at the lower levels of the government. NOTE: This is a full semester course. If you took the PPOL 536 module in Fall 2010, you CANNOT repeat this course.

title: CITIES & CRIME
description: This course examines the relationship between place and crime from a theoretical, empirical, and policy perspective. While attention will be paid to the historical relationship between cities and crime, this course will focus principally on contemporary American theories, research, and practice. The goal will be to provide students with a solid understanding of the urban environment from a criminological perspective and whether and how crime policy is responsive to empirical evidence on cities and crime. For instance, have changes in urban crime control strategies over the past few decades occurred because of, or in spite of, theoretical developments and empirical research on crime and place. Specifically, students will learn how to: 1) sharpen their analysis of current urban crime control policies; 2) assess the value and application of theoretical orientations of urban crime from a policy perspective; 3) identify assumptions and values supporting public policy and their implications for the criminal justice system; and 4) learn to make effective policy arguments based in theory and empirical research on place and crime.

title: PROGRAMS & POLICIES FOR THE US LABOR MARKET
description: This course will review policies designed to affect employment outcomes in the U.S. Topics will include minimum wage and overtime laws, job training, unemployment insurance, workers' compensation, immigration policies, equal opportunity laws and Affirmative Action, and occupational safety and health regulation.

title: FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT FOR NOT FOR PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS
description: Provides students with the concepts, principles and theories essential for making intelligent financial decisions in not for profit organizations. In this process the students will develop analytical skills and techniques used in solving financial management problems. Emphasis will be placed on reading and interpreting not for profit financial statements and understanding finance and accounting terminology. Students will gain knowledge of basic financial concepts including budgeting, liquidity and cash management, financial controls and accountability, capital budgeting, financial ratios and other aspects of not for profit organizations. This introductory course is designed for students with little or no prior experience or training in finance or accounting and who might eventually have leadership roles in not for profit organizations.

title: TOPICS IN NATIONAL ECONOMIC ISSUES
description: (Rivlin) National Economic Issues is designed to give students an understanding of major issues facing economic policy makers at the national level and how federal economic policy is made. The course will focus on monetary policy decisions of the Federal Reserve, budgetary decisions of the administration and the congress, social security reform, and the federal government's fiscal interaction with state and local governments. It will also highlight some challenges to free-market capitalism, such as ensuring corporate integrity and providing adequate incomes. (Montgomery) The course is designed to help students identify key economic issues and understand and participate in the development of solutions for the new Congress and President following the 2012 election cycle. It will deal with the economics and politics of the formulation of big policy ideas and the interaction between the two. Students will examine the facts around the Fiscal Cliff and consider different approaches to dealing with our short and long term debt, taxes and spending. The course will also focus on strategies for promoting job creation and contrast these approaches to those taken in other countries such as Germany. The course will examine the role and scope of monetary policy in light of the housing crisis and developments in the EU. The course will finish by analyzing trade policy, human capital/education policy, energy policy, and regulatory policy. PREREQUISITE: MICROECONOMICS OR PERMISSION FROM INSTRUCTOR.

title: JUVENILE JUSTICE  POLICY & PRACTICE
description: This course is designed for students interested in deepening their knowledge related to juvenile justice policy and practice. Students will be provided an opportunity to think critically about the efficacy of current policy and practice and the key areas for reform. Topics to be covered will include: the history of the juvenile justice system, including a review of the current status of the system and juvenile crime trend data; research examining the pathways that children and youth follow into delinquent behavior; and trends in current policy and practice. The course will also investigate special issues in juvenile justice, such as the disproportionate representation of children of color in the system; provision of educational, substance abuse, and mental health services; and gender based disparities in treatment. The course will conclude with an exploration of effective practices in the prevention of delinquency and intervention programs that work to reduce recidivism and achieve better long-term outcomes for youth; multi-systems approaches in juvenile justice reform; youth and family engagement strategies; and leadership of systems reform efforts. The course format will combine lecture, group discussions, jurisdictional case studies, and limited guest presentations.

title: School Choice, Accountability & Quality
description: PPOL 550: SCHOOL CHOICE, ACCOUNTABILITY & QUALITY. The first part of the course will broadly address the issue of how effective schools are in educating students and what policies can make them better. Particular topics include teacher pay, teacher quality, class sizes, and pre-K education. The second part of the course will focus on school choice and school accountability systems. We will begin by understanding the No Child Left Behind Act. We will then focus on the effects of school accountability systems on academic achievement as well as the unintended consequences of accountability systems. We will also study the related issue of school choice, including Tiebout choice, charter schools, and vouchers for private schools. The topics in the course will primarily pertain to primary and secondary schools in the United States, although issues from higher education or developing countries will occasionally come up.

title: POLICYMAKING IN A POLARIZED ENVIRONMENT
description: This course examines the phenomenon of political polarization as exhibited in the U.S. We will consider how polarization complicates decision-making in the American democratic process, why so many big policy issues are stuck in gridlock, and what can be done to help politicians find common ground. The class will examine poll evidence to discover how much is known about trends in polarization of opinion and party identity; and discuss conflicts in values that affect policy decisions and trends in those conflicts. It will ask how the public gets information and how they know it is accurate; and explore dialogue and other techniques of conflict resolution. We will examine several examples of polarized decisions that did get resolved, such as prohibition and welfare reform, and explore several current issues still stuck in gridlock, such a climate change and immigration. The class will have a seminar format with emphasis on student presentations, debate, and discussion.

title: IDENTITY POLITICS & INTEREST GROUPS
description: One might argue that American politics of the last fifty years has been dominated by conflicts over identity. Ranging from women's suffrage through the civil rights movement up to the current debate over same-sex marriage, different groups of Americans have organized and mobilized on the basis of certain shared identities--from race to ethnicity to sexual orientation to gender and more--and many of these minority groups have achieved dramatic successes in reducing legal barriers to equality. Despite these successes, the problems of prejudice, stereotype, intolerance and inequality persist, with some people blaming identity politics itself for perpetuating these divisions. In this course, we will examine the politics of identity. We will explore the social and political experiences of various identity-based minority groups in the U.S., and consider the role that government policy has played in their experience, whether through subordination or empowerment. We will study the strategies used by different minority groups as they have sought to mobilize members and supporters, and we will evaluate their successes and failures in achieving their political goals. Finally, we will analyze the debate over whether identity politics and policy serves to divide and polarize Americans, or if it increases tolerance and support for diversity in an increasingly multicultural nation.

title: HOUSING FINANCE & POLICY
description: The worst economic crisis since the Great Depression starkly illustrated the significance of the housing sector to the nationâs economic base. As we look to the future, policymakers will be making important decisions about the future of housing finance and policy that will shape the availability and affordability of housing for the next generation. This course will review the history of housing policy and finance, how the housing public policy process works, the role key stakeholders (e.g., public, private and nonprofit organizations) play in driving housing policy decisions and the major challenges currently facing todayâs housing sector. Policymakers are currently facing key decisions about the reform of the housing finance system, including the future of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, the implementation of major new financial regulatory reform, and how to address other areas of housing policy, such as rental housing, low-income housing and the homeless. The objective of this course will be to understand the key policies, programs and institutions that shape housing in the United States by analyzing and evaluating a variety of program and policy initiatives.

title: ETHICS, VALUES AND PUBLIC POLICY
description: (Holst) The analytic tools of economics are not all we need to be able to craft good public policy. Moreover, policy debates are inevitably shaped by policymakersâ ethical convictionsâthat is, by our visions of a good life. Taking these conclusions as our point of departure, in this course we will ask: 1) Is importing our ethical values and reasoning into our analysis of political problems consistently appropriate? 2) Can considering a problem in need of policy remedy from different ethical perspectivesâe.g., Aristotelian, utilitarian, or Kantianâlead us to craft better policy? And, 3) How can we appropriately accommodate particular ethical values (which are not the same as preferences or interests) in the policy process? Throughout the semester, students will be asked to engage in practical (as opposed to scientific) analysisâthat is, analysis that starts from the recognition of a problem and culminates in a recommended course of action. (Rom) This course provides an introduction to the following course themes: 1) Values (and not just interests) are fundamental to public policy, and so sophisticated policy analysts should understand the roles that values can play in policy analysis and the policy process; 2) Values are contested in that political actors interpret core values (e.g., equity, liberty, justice, security, efficiency) in conflicting ways and also place different weights on different values; 3) Differing institutional arrangements (markets, democracy, authority) enhance certain values and potentially suppress others; 4) Differing forms of reasoning (e.g., utilitarianism, deontology, or casuistry) offer differing ways to reach conclusions regarding value choices, but that no method can be demonstrated to provide the answer to these choices; 5) The systematic analysis of values can provide policy recommendations that are superior to those made without systematic reflection; 6) Policy analysts should be able to provide a reasoned explanation regarding the values embedded in the policy recommendations they make. (Pierce) This course will explore the ethical imperatives, complexities, and challenges of public service. The course will link theory and practice, using the case-study method to illustrate the issues and drawing on appropriate theories to illuminate them. One basic premise of the course is that the very nature of public service imposes distinctive moral and ethical obligations on public officials, some of which are tied directly to democratic theory and principles. The case studies will cover classic real-world challenges commonly faced by public officials in the performance of their official duties, including deception and lying; ends-and-means; privacy vs. the public good; disagreement, dissent and civil disobedience; and responsibility and accountability. On the theoretical side, it will draw upon the ideas of, among others, Sissela Bok, J. Patrick Dobel, Reinhold Niebuhr, and Michael Walzer. The course will be conducted in seminar style with intensive examination of the case studies and deliberate reflection on which theories best apply to each case study, and how to use those theories to probe the issues and discern better ways, and poorer ways, of managing the situation. This course should equip students with an enhanced understanding of democratic principles and the ethical dimension of public service, as well as a robust âtool-kitâ of frameworks and models for ethical decision-making.

title: ETHICS IN A GLOBALIZED WORLD
description: This course is designed to raise studentsâ awareness and understanding of the moral context of international affairs (North and South), and their own role as effective moral agents. The course will challenge students to cultivate their abilities in the discipline of reflective moral thinking, and introduce them to the spectrum of prominent moral theories and their application to critical challenges in the pursuit of human well-being, lives of meaning, and ecological integrity. There are many strong indications that there is a growing influence of explicitly moral thinking in international development, conflict, environment, and political analyses, theory, policy, and practice â a premise that warrants close scrutiny from the outset of the course and repeated consideration in the weeks that follow.

title: POLICY, POLITICS AND THE MEDIA
description: (Fall: Dionne; Spring: Begala) Few issues are more inherently controversial than the relationship of the media to politics and policy-making. There is even dispute over what constitutes "the media." This course is organized around particular problems raised in the debate and specific media institutions. Some of the problems given particular attention are the press' coverage of presidential campaigns, the ability of media institutions to police themselves and the role of journalists in political campaigns. Attention will also be paid to how the media cover issues of war and peace - and how policymakers attempt to influence media coverage of national security. Guests with particular expertise and experience have been invited to help lead sessions devoted to their areas of interest. The reading list includes popular and journalistic works, as well as the work of scholars. Students will be encouraged to bring their own perspectives to bear, and are strongly urged to add to the reading list and to exchange readings with each other (and their instructor). Students will be required to produce one research paper (15 to 25 pages in length) touching on a problem of their choosing in the area of media and politics. Class participation is mandatory and will be an important part of the grade. There will be a mid-term exam.

title: WOMEN AND LEADERSHIP
description: This course examines the unique challenges and opportunities faced by women in leadership positions in government, business, non-profit organizations and the military. The course does not teach women how to become leaders, but rather examines womenâs leadership roles in the context of the general literature on leadership. We will examine theories of leadership, women leaders in historical context and will look at case studies of past and present women leaders. While the course will primarily focus on the United States, womenâs leadership roles in other countries will be discussed as well.

title: TRANSPORTATION POLICY
description: Have you ever wondered why cities look the way they do, why transit systems seem to work well in some cities but not others, or why congestion just keeps getting worse? This course covers the history of highway and transit in the United States, the transportation policies and travel patterns that shape the cities in which we live. We will explore externalities that are generated by our need to travel and the transportation choices we make. In addition, we will look at the connections between mobility and land-use, accessibility goals, finance, privatization, and other supply and demand-side approaches for relieving congestion, improving air quality and increasing safety.

title: GLOBAL FINANCIAL CRISIS
description: THE GLOBAL FINANCIAL CRISIS: CAUSES, POLICY RESPONSES AND CHALLENGES. The global economy is experiencing the deepest downturn in the postâWorld War II period, as financial contagion rapidly spreads. A large number of advanced economies have fallen into recession and economies in the rest of the world have slowed abruptly. Global trade and financial flows are shrinking, while output and employment losses mount. Credit markets remain frozen as borrowers are engaged in a drawn-out deleveraging process and banks struggle to improve their financial health. The recent crisis has been unusual in its global nature, affecting countries with a speed and virulence not seen since the Great Depression. Major advanced countries and countries recently integrating with the European Union (EU) are most affected. In the past, crises have been largely limited to specific regions or economiesâthe Nordic countries in the early 1990s, Latin America in the mid-1990s, Asia in the late 1990s, and the emerging market economies of the early 2000s. The course will examine macroeconomic causes and contributing factors to the current global financial crisis. Additionally, the course will focus on evaluating policy responses aimed at the prevention and management of financial crises in the light of recent experience. Topics covered will include: the role of central bank policies in crisis management and prevention; fiscal stimulus packages and bank support schemes; financial regulatory reform and the G20 process; role of the multilateral institutions; the fiscal legacy of the financial turbulence and the economic recession; and the European sovereign debt crisis. The course, using case studies, will also discuss earlier regional crisis (like the Asian financial crisis) and the Greece debt crisis to draw policy solutions for the present global financial crisis.

title: ENERGY, SOCIETY & POLITICS IN DEVELOPING COUNTRIES
description: Energy is a perennial domestic and foreign policy issue for countries around the world. Motivated by the challenges of climate change, energy security, and poverty alleviation, politicians and the public alike are seeking a lower carbon energy future fueled by renewable energy technologies and more energy efficient practices. Drawing on the instructorâs USAID and State Department programmatic and negotiating experience, the course will explore how the technological systems born of energy choices affect political structures and social institutions in developing countries. This course will assess the energy policy debate through the framing question: âHow do the energy technologies we adopt embody and shape the social and political values we espouse?â Through a range of theoretical propositions and empirical findings the course will challenge students to integrate philosophical principles into policy analysis. Students will be asked to think critically and write analytically about the various socio-political ramifications of energy choices in developing countries. Case studies drawn from international diplomatic negotiations and development projects will augment selected readings and guest speaker presentations.

title: GROWTH DIAGNOSTICS & DEVELOPMENT STRATEGIES
description: Growth diagnostics (GDs) have become an essential tool for developing country governments to help focus their own development strategies, and for professionals in the field of economic development. This course examines the theory and application of GDs against the backdrop of the latest thinking by practitioners in development policy. We will discuss specific challenges in using the tool, and will spend time applying it to different countries, including fragile states. By the end of this course, you will have developed analytical tools that will enable you to contribute to new and ongoing GDs in the field that have been requested by developing countries, and that are increasingly being financed by donor agencies.

title: PUBLIC MANAGEMENT
description: This course introduces students to public management: the art and science of planning and implementing public programs. Participants will examine the constraints public managers face in a democratic society; how the challenges of public management vary across different organizational and policy settings; and how public management and policy analysis frequently intersect. A key objective is to offer students a useful mix of theoretical knowledge and practical skills. Throughout the course, emphasis is placed on management as a function performed in all sectors (public, nonprofit, and private) but, when appropriate, participants focus on what factors make public management distinctive and more challenging. Some of the specific topics covered include: executive leadership; the role of front-line operators and middle managers; organizational culture; problems of bureaucratic coordination and dysfunction; how Congress, the President, and the courts attempt to control the public bureaucracy; managing people; managing budgets; privatization and contracting out; ethics in public management; performance management and strategic planning; and management reform strategies. Case studies are used extensively to explore specific challenges and scenarios.

title: COMPARATIVE PUBLIC MANAGEMENT
description: A key component in the study of public policy is the ability to work through how public policy gets put into action. Successful policy implementation requires more than technical policy analysis; it requires a critical understanding of the underpinnings of good management and governance. This course uses theoretical and case study materials to introduce students to the difficulties of public management in a comparative perspective. We will focus specifically on the challenges facing less developed countries as well as the options and resources available to effectively implement policies in those countries. Through class discussion, simulations and assignments, students will apply theoretical concepts in comparative public management to actual situations

title: SURVEY METHODS: DESIGN, SAMPLING, WEIGHTING & ESTIMATION
description: This course explores the process of designing, implementing, preparing and disseminating the results of social science surveys. The goal of the course is to provide students with a thorough grounding in the processes that are used to produce the data used in policy analysis. Quite often the efficacy of policy prescriptions can rest critically on the nature and assumptions underlying the data collection and estimation systems that produce the supporting data. There is a focused emphasis in the course on each of the survey stages involved in the production of estimates, including the use of universe frames, drawing samples, developing reweighting plans, estimating item and survey non-response bias, techniques for handling missing data, edit reconciliation, and estimating survey error. The methods underlying the survey process draw from a variety of disciplines, including cognitive research, statistics, economics, sociology, computer science and management. The course begins with an overview of the entire survey process as applied to select Federal Government Survey Programs. This is followed by a more in-depth examination of each of the components of the survey process mentioned above. The final section of the course is a holistic examination of the entire survey process exploring measures of survey quality, and understanding the role and nature of types of survey data (cross-sectional, time series, longitudinal).

title: PUBLIC OPINION & POLITICAL BEHAVIOR
description: In this course, we will survey the major literature on public opinion and political behavior. We will seek to understand how citizens come to develop attitudes towards politics and policies and what they do about their political attitudes, be it to debate, organize, vote, advocate for policy, run for office, or nothing at all. Theories of political and social psychology, economics, cognition and collective action will provide different perspectives on the topic. Given the significant role that survey research plays in this field of study, we will also study how public opinion is measured and analyzed. Though the focus of this course will be on American politics, much of the literature has comparative implications and parallels. Research in this field is largely quantitative so we will see many examples of applied statistics.

title: US BUDGET PROCESS: POLICIES AND PRACTICE
description: This course is designed to provide a hands-on approach to studying the Federal Budget Process. Initially, students will learn budget definitions, concepts, procedures and schedules integral to the budget process. Students will track the Budget Process beginning with the President's transmission of the budget to the Congress in early February. Students will then monitor the three-step Budget Process: 1. Processing. Through both House and Senate Budget Committees; 2. Debating. On the House and Senate floors; and 3. Conference. Ultimately passed by April 15th as mandated in the Budget Act. Will Congress and the President take the necessary action needed on the Fiscal Cliff to avoid a certain crash? Or do they kick the can down road again on taxes and spending cuts and possibly trigger a recession? Students will be required to read The Federal Budget: Politics, Policy, Process, by Allen Schick and Felix LoStracco and The Challenge of Legislation: Bipartisanship In A Partisan World, by John L. Hilley.

title: SURVEY OF POPULATION PROBLEMS
description: This course is designed to provide a broad overview of the field of population studies. It introduces students to basic methods of demographic analysis and explores social science perspectives on population problems. Topics covered include: a survey of past and current trends in the growth of the population of the world and of selected regions; analysis of the components of population change and their determinants; and the social and economic consequences of population change. You will find the readings on the bottom shelf of the bookcase outside room 313. Course requirements include a mid-term examination (40 percent of the final grade) and a final exam (40 percent). Class discussion and weekly assignments comprise the remaining 20 percent of the grade. All students are expected to participate in class discussions, and occasionally lead one of the discussions during the second half of the course.

title: PUBLIC LEADERSHIP: PRINCIPLES, PRACTICES & REALITIES
description: This course is designed for students seeking to become effective public leaders--as government officials and staff, issue advocates, or social entrepreneurs. Students will be challenged to think critically about the moral responsibilities and ethical dilemmas of public leadership; to understand the competing demands on leaders trying to accommodate politics, institutional constraints, and the multiple agendas of interested parties; to examine your own capacity for leadership; and to discover new ways to think about and exercise leadership for the public good. We will explore leadership principles and practices in a variety of ways: analysis of the current political scene, key actors, and forces influencing leadership decisions; case studies of leaders, ranging from grassroots organizers to presidents; class discussions with experienced public leaders and the journalists who cover them; and participation in class activities designed to allow you to practice your own leadership skills. Students will have an opportunity to interview two leaders about the issues they face, the strategies they use, and the advice they offer to emerging public leaders

title: PUBLIC LEADERSHIP: PRINCIPLES, PRACTICES & REALITIES
description: This course is designed for students seeking to become more effective leaders in the public policy arena, as government officials and staff, legislative directors, issue advocates, or nonprofit managers. We will focus on effective leadership strategies for analyzing and diagnosing public problems and needs; for implementing creative and responsible solutions through policies or program delivery; and for mobilizing resources and support across organizations and sectors. Students will be challenged to think critically about the moral responsibilities and ethical dilemmas of public leadership; to understand the competing demands on leaders trying to accommodate politics, institutional constraints, and the multiple agendas of interested parties; to examine their own capacity for leadership; and to discover new ways to think about and exercise leadership for the public good. We will explore leadership principles and practices in a variety of ways: analysis of the current political scene, key actors, and forces influencing leadership decisions; case studies of leaders in action, from issue advocates to presidents; class discussions with experienced public leaders and the journalists who cover them; and participation in class activities designed to allow students to practice their leadership skills. Students also will have an opportunity to interview two leaders of their choice to learn about the issues they face, the strategies they use, and the advice they offer to emerging public leaders.

title: COST BENEFIT ANALYSIS
description: This course covers the theory and practice of financial and economic project appraisal. The course is designed to introduce the theoretical basis for measuring the costs and benefits of investments, while applying the theory to carry out full cost-benefit analysis. Applications to infrastructure provision such as roads and water will be discussed, as well as investments in social sectors such as education and health, with the goal of using CBA analysis to inform decisions on the viability of investments. Topics include the role of project appraisal in the project cycle, data constraints, and revenue and expenditure projections. The first part of the course will focus on financial appraisal and will develop a model for financial cost-benefit analysis. The second part will introduce concepts of economic appraisal including measuring the social cost of capital, impacts on labor, and distributive effects. PRE-REQUISITE: PPOL 503 (Public Finance) or permission of the instructor.

title: RURAL DEVELOPMENT
description: Economic development in rural areas of developing countries has a number of characteristics that differentiate it from urban areas. It depends substantially on growth in the agricultural sector leading to first food self-sufficiency for rural residents and then a marketable surplus and increased exports. Beyond growth, it also includes the broader concepts of improved nutrition and health, education, infrastructure development and non-farm job creation, all leading to better livelihoods. This course will evaluate the key trends over the past 10 to 15 years and dissect the bottlenecks in the rural development process that obstruct better outcomes. The course examines issues such as food production and food insecurity, the prevalence and stubbornness of hunger and poverty and alternative approaches to promote development in rural areas. It also identifies the role of key actors in the developmental process â government, international donors, local and international NGOs and, importantly, the villagers themselves. Using case studies, the course takes an in-depth look at a series of rural interventions and policy initiatives such as microfinance, changing agricultural technologies, water management, etc. â both successes and failures â to analyze the core determinants of development outcomes in rural areas. Students will be asked to write a series of short papers or âmemosâ on development problems and initiatives to address them, taking positions on key current debates within the development community or evaluating actual programs tried developing economies." This includes presenting and defending these positions in class discussions. A key objective will be to expose students to development practices and become aware of what works and why as well as what has failed in the past.

title: SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
description: The core challenge of development has traditionally been virtually synonymous with the dynamic and closely related processes of economic growth and poverty reduction. Concerns about the viability of unbounded economic growth, given finite natural resources, has led to increasing attention being devoted to the pursuit of âsustainable development,â which the Brundtland Commission in 1987 defined as âdevelopment that meets the needs of the present generation without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their needs.â The concept has evolved somewhat over the years, and now is increasingly recognized as encompassing âthe three Esâ â economics, environment and equity. But as concerns about global climate change have deepened, the differences in emphases both across and within countries about the priorities of development have become more visible. In most developed countries, policy makers are increasingly seeking ways to alter the patterns of consumption, both domestically and internationally, that contribute to global climate change. In many developing countries, however, sustaining economic growth remains the critical political and policy objective, even in places where the externalities of environmental degradation are becoming more apparent. Consistent with the MIDP program as a whole, this course seeks to tackle the issues of sustainable development: 1) with an emphasis on development in the context of developing country priorities; and 2) with an emphasis on the emerging scientific evidence of the welfare trade-offs associated with policy and program responses. The course will also cover the views of other international actors and how their approaches to investment, trade and foreign assistance incorporate and reflect their views of sustainable development, including the most recent discussions surrounding the idea of having universal Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) to replace or supplement the MDGs as they expire in 2015 and what this would mean for developing countries. Throughout the course, we will draw on case studies from around the world to look at how the demands of sustainability are challenging and altering the way that public policy decisions are made and how they shape the range and pattern of choices available to developing countries.

title: ENERGY POLICY & POLITICS
description: this seminar examines the policy and politics of energy in the United States. The course analyzes a wide-range of energy sources, including oil, natural gas, coal, nuclear power, and renewables, as well as topics such as energy markets, transportation, innovation, and pollution abatement. The main purpose of the course is to consider the fundamental political and economic factors that affect energy use in the United States, the market failures that arise in the energy sector, and how government policy can be designed to mitigate these failures.

title: ENVIRONMENTAL INEQUALITY: U.S. AND INTERNATIONAL DIMENSIONS
description: This seminar examines the U.S. and international dimensions of environmental inequality. The course will critically evaluate the evidence of disproportionate burdens in pollution and unwanted land uses through a careful review of the interdisciplinary literature on environmental justice. The course will also explore the policy responses of the U.S. government, other national governments, and international institutions. In considering these issues, we will also examine the environmental justice movement that has emerged to respond to environmental inequality.

title: ECONOMICS OF REGULATION & DEREGULATION
description: This course explores in depth the theoretical and practical rationales for economic regulation, regulatory reform, deregulation, and restructuring and applies them to a number of key industries. These industries include telecommunications, energy, transportation, and water. The course begins with an assessment of the market failures that have historically motivated regulatory intervention, goes on to examine the differing theories of regulation, explores how different regulatory regimes and mechanisms are implemented, and evaluates the economic and legal aspects of regulatory reform and sectoral restructuring. Economy-wide competition ("antitrust") laws are also analyzed, including the complementary roles of sectoral regulation and competition law enforcement.

title: FEDERALISM AND INTERGOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS
description: Federalism is one of the key features of our system of government, yet it is poorly understood. In this course, we discuss how federalism works and how it might work better. Although this course is taught from a political science perspective, we will pay some attention to economics (fiscal federalism) and law (court decisions). Our principal goal is to understand how federalism and intergovernmental relations vary across policy domains. But we will also devote some thought to how the division of labor between governments might be redesigned in specific sectors, to promote greater effectiveness or efficiency or justice. In this course, we will devote substantial attention to education policy, health policy, environmental policy, and welfare policy. We will also devote some attention to homeland security policy and the politics of disasters. Enter this course with an open mind!

title: ENVIRONMENTAL POLITICS AND POLICY
description: This course is an introduction to U.S. environmental politics and policy, focusing on the institutions and political actors involved in environmental protection. The course provides a survey of the primary laws, regulations, and policies that comprise pollution control and natural resource management policy, with an emphasis on the former. The course will also consider relevant theoretical frameworks and empirical approaches that social scientists use to study the political, economic, and social context of U.S. environmental policy-making. Among the topics examined will be air and water pollution, toxic wastes, public lands, endangered species, and climate change.

title: STRATEGY IN PUBLIC POLICY
description: PPOL 639: STRATEGY IN PUBLIC POLICY. New national and departmental strategies of sorts are released every few weeks on topics ranging from defense to cyberspace to nanotechnology. But are these foundational documents expected to drive change throughout entities within their scope, or are they simply marketing pieces? More fundamentally, what, exactly, is strategy (or what should it be)? Why is strategy important? How is it developed? This course seeks to answer such questions by examining topics ranging from grand strategy to strategy/strategic planning in the policy arena; differences between strategy, strategic planning, operational planning, and budgeting; net assessment and futures analysis; the use of strategy/strategic planning in business, as a counterpoint to policy; and criticisms of strategy and examples of good and bad strategies across government.

title: HEALTH CARE QUALITY: RECENT POLICY ISSUES
description: This course provides an introduction to how public policy can be used to improve healthcare quality and the public reporting of quality. Topics include: (1) The Quality Gap; Disparities; The National Quality Strategy; (2) Debates over defining quality and international views: comparative effectiveness research; disease-centered models versus patient-centered models; shared-decision making; end of life care; POLST reforms; (3) Designing incentives for rewarding quality: pay-for-performance models versus behavioral economic models (Nudge theory, default theory, peer group effects, shame); shared-savings models (ACOs); (4) Protecting the patient: Patient safety and medical errors; the Checklist Manifesto; alternative tort reforms, the Apology Initiative; no payment policies for hospital-acquired conditions; (5) Coordinating care: Electronic health records; the medical home model; bundled payment design; (6) Measuring preventive care quality: Debates over screening guidelines; the science of measuring the cost offsets and outcomes of preventive care and pharmaceuticals; (7) Reporting quality: Report cards; consumer use of public reports; physician use of internal reports; the science of quality measures, reports, and rankings. By the end of the course, students should be able to think critically about the key health policy issues related to improving healthcare quality.

title: HEALTH POLICY & POLITICS
description: This seminar explores the policy and politics of health care by developing and assessing reform proposals as if students were participating in the Executive or Congressional decision process. This examination of reform will draw on experience from the national debate on comprehensive reform (1993-1994) and more recently on incremental changes to health care access and coverage (1996-/PRESENT/). The objective of the course is to engage students in weighing both the policy and political tradeoffs raised by health reform.

title: HEALTH CARE ACCESS DEMAND ISSUES
description: This course provides an introduction to the fundamental issues in health economics and health services research related to health care demand. In the first part of the course, models are developed to analyze the demand for health and medical care, health production and health behaviors. Particular emphasis is placed on the role of insurance in affecting the market for medical care by analyzing employment-based insurance, Medicare, Medicaid and the problems of the uninsured and underinsured. The second part of the course uses these theoretical foundations to evaluate the key health policy issues in the United States. Students will read a variety of articles ranging from classic analyses in health economics and health services research to up-to-date current analyses. By the end of the course, students should be able to think critically about the key health policy issues related to health care costs and health care access.

title: HEALTH SUPPLY: PHYSICIANS & HOSPITAL ISSUES
description: This course will focus on improving our understanding of the health care system by studying the behavior of health care suppliers. The course will focus specifically on the behavior of physicians and hospitals and how their behavior is affected by financial incentives. In studying physicians, the course will examine how physicians respond to fee changes; factors that influence physiciansâ decisions to treat patients with different types of insurance; malpractice insurance and tort reforms; physician ownership of ancillary services such as diagnostic imaging and physical therapy services. In studying hospitals, we will examine the financial incentives inherent in the inpatient prospective payment system; the growth of multi-hospital systems; differences between for-profit and not-for-profit hospitals; the effects of physician ownership of specialty hospitals and freestanding ambulatory surgery centers on competing full-services community hospitals; how to address the growing problem of uncompensated hospital care and trauma care. The overall goal is to improve our understanding of the way supplier behavior affects the workings of health care markets.

title: FAMILY DEMOGRAPHY & POLICY ISSUES
description: This course is designed to bring a demographic perspective to bear on family change and family policy in the U.S. The objective is to identify the demographic processes that lie behind the dramatic social changes that have occurred in the past quarter century and to analyze the role that government has played and can play in affecting the course of family life. Thus, three questions will be used to focus each of the content areas covered in the course: 1) what are the trends and what demographic processes underlie them? 2) what role has public policy played in shaping these trends? 3) what role can public policy play in shaping future trends?

title: POLITICAL RISK ANALYSIS
description: This course starts from the premise that â whether state or nonstate actor â increased interdependencies between polities and economies are exposing us to rising levels of political risk. Understanding how political events, processes or policy decisions can threaten stability, continuity in international relations or disrupt investment, trade and commerce is essential to enhance the quality of our decision-making. Through a combination of lectures, debates and case analysis students will learn to identify drivers of political risk, study different methods and analytical frameworks to measure it, and apply tools to specific real world cases both from the public and private sector to manage it.

title: GLOBALIZATION: ISSUES, ACTORS & CHOICES FOR THE 21ST CENTURY
description: Globalization â the rising interconnectedness of people the world over - poses multiple opportunities to increase public goods and wellbeing, while highlighting global inequities and challenging existing policy mechanisms in the same breath. The class begins with an initial grounding in the âmust readâ literature on globalization, from the perspective of proponents as well as critics. Case studies, issue papers and projects will follow to walk students through the real-life policy questions and trade-offs that globalization poses across a range of arenas: financial markets, trade flows, judicial systems, climate change, and protection of vulnerable lands and peoples.

title: FINANCE IN DEVELOPMENT
description: The aim of the course is to introduce students to the trade-off between the growth promoting and the volatility creating role of financial markets in developing and emerging market economies. On the one hand, a broad strand of the policy debate has dealt with the so-called finance-growth-nexus. In particular, financial liberalization and the implementation of growth enhancing financial policies and accompanying instruments such as microfinance (e.g. microcredits, microinsurance, Islamic finance) have achieved great prominence in the debate on financial development. On the other hand, even before the outbreak of the current global financial crisis, the so-called finance-volatility-nexus has been discussed intensively as a potential down-side risk arising from liberal financial market governance practices. Insofar, the course will introduce students to underlying mechanisms behind phenomena such as lending booms, international over-borrowing, and financial crisis with a special focus on the role of political economy aspects. In doing so, the course will consist of a combination of case studies, as well as empirical and theoretical approaches to address these multifaceted aspects of financial development.

title: INTERNATIONAL DEVELOPMENT: WHAT WORKS, WHAT DOESN'T & WHY?
description: The challenge of international development lies in finding the means to bring about lasting improvements in the wellbeing of the worldâs poor, against a tide of socio-political and economic forces sustaining a world divided into rich and poor, powerful and weak, protected and vulnerable. This course will begin with a brief review of the contributions of development theory to todayâs thinking on development and the causes of poverty. Against this backdrop, the course will then delve into a series of case studies examining some of the more striking successes â and dismal failures â in the development field, and the causes that underlie success or failure. Cases will be selected to illustrate the varied levels at which development policy may be enacted, from macroeconomic policy to small-scale, local projects, and the range of actors on which development efforts depend. The case study approach will be augmented with a range of other source material, including impact evaluation results and presentations by select guest speakers bringing the day-to-day practice of development to the classroom. This course is designed for students preparing themselves for a career in international development; it may be more broadly of interest for anyone desiring a solid grounding in the realities and challenges of this field.

title: BRICS & THE GLOBAL ECONOMY
description: Three out of every seven people in the world today live in BRICS countries of Brazil, Russia, India, China, and recently added South Africa. BRICS today account for over a third of the global foreign exchange reserves and its output is projected to surpass that of the USA by 2018. This course will provide, using workable in- class case studies, an in depth analysis of comparative economic development across BRICS economies from a policy-oriented perspective. The course will examine how these countries became the âdriving economiesâ, marshaling substantial resources and large populations to industrialize, growing at rapid rates, and maintaining central positions in their respective regions and the international political economy. Current growth strategies of BRICS economies also offer us the opportunity to look into the larger questions of global order and national development. How can states and markets work together to generate equitable and participatory growth? How should the BRICS economies be factored into the 21st centuryâs policy challenges, especially in reworking international financial institutions after the recent economic crisis? What do the experiences of the BRICS mean for the many economies that still lack such rapid growth? The course will evaluate if BRICS are the next drivers of economic growth for the global economy?

title: INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL  ECONOMY
description: The key objective of the course is to introduce students to key concepts of modern international political economy. Starting from a closed economy perspective, the course will address topics related to the question how political economy forces shape economic policies in industrial, transition and developing economies. In this respect, the emphasis will be on modern theories of political economy and recent empirical findings thereof. In particular, the course will focus on the role of special interest groups, popular electoral pressure and institutional design in driving economic policies and outcomes. Although a minor share of course material will be based on formal models, the aim is to assist students in developing strong analytical skills and a sound intuition concerning the identification of political motives in driving primarily macroeconomic outcomes, rather than to engaging in excessive economic formal modeling exercises. Starting with some basic tools for analyzing driving forces behind political decision-making processes, the course will cover a variety of policy areas such as institution building, fiscal and monetary policy, as well as government responses to financial crises episodes in industrial, transition and developing economies. In order to get a deeper understanding of certain international political economy concepts, selected case studies, for instance on the Arab Spring, the Eurozone crises and the reform of the International Monetary Fund will complement the course.

title: INTERNATIONAL ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT POLICY
description: This course introduces tools to assess the key determinants of economic development. It starts out by trying to understand what drives economic growth and why some countries grow faster than others. It looks at the respective roles of leadership, institutions, geographical factors and global markets in promoting development. The course also highlights similarities and differences in the nature of challenges faced by low and middle income countries. Finally, it reviews the empirical evidence on more and less successful economic development policies and draws lessons from policy experiences

title: HISTORY OF DEVELOPMENT
description: The History and Theory of Development is a survey of the economic histories of a number of societies and regions such as South America, Africa, Japan and Russia. Designing development policy today requires a careful understanding of socio-political and economic contexts. The aim of this course is to discuss a few contexts in some detail that will inform a term project describing the economic histories of a country of the student's choice.

title: INTERNATIONAL ECONOMICS
description: This course is divided into two parts. The first deals with the essential issues of globalization: Why nations trade with each other, the importance of trade, the factors that determine trade and production, the gains that accrue from trade, the effect of trade on the distribution of national income, the formation of regional trade blocs, and the nature and costs of government policies that impede trade. The second part deals with issues of international finance: the balance of payments, the determination of exchange rates, alternative exchange rate regimes and their implications for domestic monetary and fiscal policies, the macroeconomic effects of international capital movements, and dollarization.

title: HOMELAND SECURITY
description: This course is intended to provide a broad and deep understanding of homeland security policy. The September 11th terrorist attacks put homeland security at the very top of the national public policy agenda. Securing the homeland is an extremely complex mission that requires a coordinated and integrated effort from all levels of society: the federal government, state and local governments, the private and nonprofit sectors, and the American people. The concept of homeland security focuses on protecting the nation from terrorist attacks but it also encompasses planning and preparing for natural disasters. The goal is to provide students a thorough understanding of the policy, strategy, legal and organizational issues and challenges associated with the defense of the U.S. homeland, the efforts underway to meet those challenges, and the range of means and methods that are or could be brought to bear on this subject.

title: POST-CONFLICT RECONSTRUCTION
description: The purpose of this course is to provide students with an introduction to the components and challenges of post-conflict reconstruction. Students will gain familiarity with planning, the Essential Tasks Matrix, the interagency and multinational process, civil-military relations in PCR, and project monitoring and evaluation. Topics include the benefits of sequenced vs simultaneous approaches; phases of reconstruction; and operating in a complex, unstable, unsustainable post-conflict environment. Case studies will serve to highlight âlessons learnedâ from previous and on-going reconstruction efforts.

title: CYBER CONFLICT & NATIONAL SECURITY POLICY
description: This class examines the concepts and reality of cyber statecraft, international conflict, competition and cooperation in cyberspace and discusses the resulting challenges faced by the national security community. We will focus not on the technology of cyberspace but the national security threats, challenges, and policy responses including lessons from history and other kinds of conflict. The class is primarily concerned with national security policy, but may look at domestic issues such as balancing security and privacy, regulation and innovation.

title: CLIENTS IN THE POLICY ANALYSIS PROCESS
description: This course is designed for MPP students in their last semester and students who receive the consent of the instructor. It focuses on the role of clients in the policy analysis process. One core aspect of policy analysis involves the relationship between the analyst and the individual he or she is advising â the client. This seminar includes examination of the literature on clients in policy analysis as well as exposure to the range of clients and their relationship to analysts. Students will be asked to examine their own analytic work and to think about the clients who would be involved in that topic. This course provides an opportunity for students to develop an understanding of: - The differences between policy research and policy analysis - Identifying and dealing with clients - Defining the expectations of clients - Defining a policy problem - Changing relationships between analysts and clients - Mapping the policy environment - Writing a policy analysis memo to a specific client

title: THESIS WORKSHOP
description: The thesis is a two-semester project required of all public policy students for graduation. For the thesis, students conduct original research on a government or public program or issue of their choosing, and summarize their findings in a thesis to be completed during the second semester. GPPI faculty have developed The Thesis Guide on the GPPI website as a reference and aid for students who are completing their thesis projects (//gppi.georgetown.edu/audience/current/thesis/40182.html).

title: THESIS WORKSHOP II
description: The thesis is a two-semester project required of all public policy students for graduation. For the thesis, students conduct original research on a government or public program or issue of their choosing, and summarize their findings in a thesis to be completed during the second semester. GPPI faculty have developed The Thesis Guide on the Web as a reference and aid for students who are completing their thesis projects.

title: AFRICAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: PPOL 702: AFRICAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. The main objectives of this module are to familiarize students with the most important economic development issues confronting and challenging African countries today and to demonstrate how economic analysis may be used to better understand and address these development issues. The module begins with an analysis of Africaâs post-independence development experience, often characterized as a growth tragedy, and the factors, both exogenous and policy-induced, that help to explain that performance. The module then turns to current African development issues, including: the transition to more market-friendly policies and the effectiveness of structural adjustment programs; Africaâs unfinished reform agenda, particularly downsizing and changing the role and functions of the state in the economy, including the establishment of new regulatory systems and the divestiture and privatization of state-owned enterprises; macroeconomic stabilization, including the role of Africaâs central banks and exchange rate regimes; the persistence of poverty and income inequality in African countries; the capacity and credibility of public sector institutions and the difficulties of governance and economic policy making in ethnically divided and war-torn African states; Africaâs marginalization in world trade and limited access to private capital markets; Africaâs investment in human capital and the fight against HIV/Aids; the constraints imposed by Africaâs limited physical infrastructure; how to deepen Africaâs financial sector; the agricultural sectorâs role in supporting broad-based economic growth; the effectiveness of official development aid (ODA); the burden of Africaâs external debt and the highly indebted poor country (HIPC) initiative; and finally the scope for regional economic cooperation among African countries. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT IN AFRICA
description: This module will examine the main causes of poverty in sub-Saharan Africa and efforts to accelerate economic growth on the continent. It will first focus on key internal and external factors that help explain the lack of economic growth, including the evolution of development thinking, as well as the relationship between growth and other outcomes, such as welfare and poverty reduction. It will then explore some of the domestic and international actors trying to alleviate poverty, looking at the most prominent trends, actors, and institutions. Lastly, it will examine some of the current debates and issues in the international development field as they relate to Africa.

title: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF FOREIGN AID
