description: We will cover official development assistance (ODA) by major bilateral and multilateral donors (including USAID, the World Bank, etc) as well as assistance from new donors like China. We will also discuss new forms of aid delivery, in particular by global funds (e.g., Global Fund for AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria) and large NGOs and foundations (e.g., Save the Children; Gates Foundation). We will review trends in aid flowsâcontrasting aid with other ways to finance development (FDI, trade, debt relief, remittances). We will examine the new aid paradigm (country ownership and country-driven programs, selectivity, donor harmonization) â and contrast it with old style foreign assistance and conditionality common during the Cold War period (from the 1950s to the early 1990s). Our emphasis will be on whether aid is effective and achieves measurable results. We will consider the "macro-micro paradox" in the effectiveness of foreign aid, namely that aid achieves micro successes, but they are not matched by visible macro growth effects. Macro growth effects are hard to achieve because the magnitude of aid may not be sufficient to affect recipient countries macro variables, and harder to observe because causality is difficult to establish in cross-country regressions. Micro effects are easier to observe and can be detected through rigorous impact evaluation methods. However, micro successes must meet the challenges of scalability and sustainability. We will consider the economic and social impact of aid, using empirical evidence, asking whether and how aid contributes to growth and poverty reduction in developing countries. We will discuss project and program evaluation methods, and applications to concrete examples.

title: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF FOREIGN AID
description: This module will examine how the foreign aid business works. We will look at mechanisms typically used by USAID and other bilateral aid agencies to deliver aid. We will also look at new forms of assistance such as the Gates Foundation. The economic impact of aid will be considered in depth, including food aid and relief aid. The potential impact of the proposed Millennium Challenge Account will also be analyzed. Finally, we will look at whether the aid business might be reformed to work more effectively. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: CONTRACTING
description: Contracting for public services has become a primary tool to implement public policies and programs â so much so that todayâs public managers should have a basic understanding of contract management issues and challenges. Governments will continue to contract and contracting will grow. Being for and against contracting is like being for or against the weather. It really doesnât matter. âContractingâ is the business arrangement between a government agency and a private entity in which the private entity promises, in exchange for money, to deliver certain products or services to the government agency or to others on the governmentâs behalf. Purchase of service contracting is a distinctive form of government procurement which involves purchase of services not only for the governmentâs own use, but often to deliver services to eligible groups of beneficiaries or âclients.â The relationship between government (the âbuyerâ) and the private non-profit and for-profit agencies (the âsellerâ) is one of mutual interdependence in financial and technical terms. The contracted programs and activities they support have developed constituencies of their own, with direct pressure to preserve them. How do we ensure that the work done by contractors serve the public interest? To do so, this course will explore the distinctive features of government contracting and examine the outsourcing/insourcing decision. Students will seek to understand the management challenges of contracting through case studies and an exercise to develop a contract statement-of-work. Students will conclude by preparing a memo to Administrator of the U.S. Office of Federal Procurement describing what policy actions they would recommend to improve government contracting.

title: CONTRACTING
description: Contracting for public services has become a primary tool to implement public policies and programs â so much so that todayâs public managers should have a basic understanding of contract management issues and challenges. Governments will continue to contract and contracting will grow. Being for and against contracting is like being for or against the weather. It really doesnât matter. âContractingâ is the business arrangement between a government agency and a private entity in which the private entity promises, in exchange for money, to deliver certain products or services to the government agency or to others on the governmentâs behalf. Purchase of service contracting is a distinctive form of government procurement which involves purchase of services not only for the governmentâs own use, but often to deliver services to eligible groups of beneficiaries or âclients.â The relationship between government (the âbuyerâ) and the private non-profit and for-profit agencies (the âsellerâ) is one of mutual interdependence in financial and technical terms. The contracted programs and activities they support have developed constituencies of their own, with direct pressure to preserve them. How do we ensure that the work done by contractors serve the public interest? To do so, this course will explore the distinctive features of government contracting and examine the outsourcing/insourcing decision. Students will seek to understand the management challenges of contracting through case studies and an exercise to develop a contract statement-of-work. Students will conclude by preparing a memo to Administrator of the U.S. Office of Federal Procurement describing what policy actions they would recommend to improve government contracting. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: GLOBAL WORKER RIGHTS
description: PPOL 718: GLOBAL WORKER RIGHTS. This module is designed to provide students with an understanding of international labor standards, specifically child labor, forced labor, employment discrimination and freedom of association and collective bargaining. For example, wanting ILS in trade agreements is a political issue, while wanting developing countries to implement them is a development issue. With increased international economic interdependence over the past half century, conditions in the workplace and treatment of workers have become more global. Globalization has become more contentious and raised a number of economic issues to the forefront for policymakers to consider. For example, anti-globalization and other demonstrations at high-level international meetings are becoming more commonplace. Expanded open trade has increased the size of the world economic pie; however, how this pie is being sliced up remains an issue. We will examine the role of the international labor standards in this controversy. (1.5 credits; 1st module)

title: US IMMIGRATION POLICY
description: While the architecture of US immigration policy has much to be admired, there are long-standing problems and new challenges. This module covers the basics of immigrant history, demographics and integration; policies on admission and enforcement; and today's debates over immigration reform. The goal is to understand how policy has changed and how it is structured today. At the same time, attention is given to the demographic composition and socioeconomic integration of the foreign born which, in large part, results from past policies. Students will be expected to participate in class discussion, write critical memos, and write a short final, subject paper. (1.5 credits; 2nd module)

title: ENERGY POLICY
description: The module examines energy policy and issues from an applied microeconomics perspective. Particular attention is paid to the institutional features of the energy industry and the reasons for and effects of major pieces of energy legislation. We will also examine environmental issues related to the energy industry and energy policy.

title: ENERGY POLICY
description: The module examines energy policy and issues from an applied microeconomics perspective. Particular attention is paid to the institutional features of the energy industry and the reasons for and effects of major pieces of energy legislation. We will also examine environmental issues related to the energy industry and energy policy. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: ECONOMIC ANALYSIS FOR PUBLIC POLICY
description: PPOL 730: ECONOMIC ANALYSIS FOR PUBLIC POLICY. Economic analysis is widely used in various public policy fields such as environmental policy, social welfare, and labor market analysis. Economic principles of consumer and producer theory, social welfare economics, public goods and externalities, market failure, market structure, production theory, and fiscal policy offer many insights into use of economics to derive solutions for public policy problems. This course will provide students with an understanding of how economic theory can be used to assess the desirability of government interventions, their justification, and implications for efficiency and fairness, using specific policy areas as examples. FOR MPM STUDENTS ONLY.

title: INTELLIGENCE & PUBLIC POLICY
description: This module examines major intelligence activities and issues, exploring what intelligence is and why policy-makers need it. The module covers the organization of the U.S. Intelligence Community â both before and after 9/11 â and examines the roles and responsibilities of the various intelligence entities that comprise the U.S. Intelligence Community. Counterintelligence, covert action, and the ethical dilemmas inherent in all intelligence operations will be addressed as well as the role of intelligence in a democratic society such as the United States and the relationship between the media, the public, and the Intelligence Community. Students will examine intelligence issues in the context of past, current, and future world events, and gain an understanding of the complexity of intelligence and the kinds of issues that intelligence practitioners much consider.

title: INTELLIGENCE ANALYSIS & PUBLIC POLICY
description: This module examines major intelligence activities and issues, exploring what intelligence is and why policy-makers need it. The module covers the organization of the U.S. Intelligence Community â both before and after 9/11 â and examines the roles and responsibilities of the various intelligence entities that comprise the U.S. Intelligence Community. Counterintelligence, covert action, and the ethical dilemmas inherent in all intelligence operations will be addressed as well as the role of intelligence in a democratic society such as the United States and the relationship between the media, the public, and the Intelligence Community. Students will examine intelligence issues in the context of past, current, and future world events, and gain an understanding of the complexity of intelligence and the kinds of issues that intelligence practitioners much consider.

title: WATER POLICY IN THE U.S.
description: This course will delve into the main policy drivers affecting water quality and water quantity issues in the U.S. We will begin by analyzing the strengths and limits of the key statutes guiding water policy in the U.S. In addition to the laws, we will discuss the successes and shortcomings of government voluntary programs that provide educational, financial and technical assistance. We will also analyze the pros and cons of voluntary, collaborative policy-making bodies that have emerged in the Chesapeake Bay and the Mississippi River Basin-Gulf of Mexico. We will analyze the track record of market-based mechanisms for achieving efficient water resource management like the longtime water allocation rights systems in the west and the emerging nutrient trading programs in the east. And we will look into recent corporate social responsibility efforts to contribute to improved water management. Given the dominance of the agricultural sector as both the largest consumer of water and the largest source of nutrient pollution, this course will also focus on the pros and cons of existing policy and programmatic approaches addressing the farm sector.

title: SOCIAL INNOVATION
description: Social innovation is increasingly of interest in the policy arena. This course explores the topic, beginning with an introduction to innovation in the business sector and moving to an understanding of innovation in the social sector outside of government. We will then analyze ways that government policy inhibits, advances, and incorporates social innovation, including both historical and contemporary examples. Specific topics will include social entrepreneurship, disruptive innovation, social program design, the roles that volunteer and national service play in social innovation, and cross-sector collaboration. The class will employ multiple learning formats including case discussion, selected reading, and guest speakers involved in social innovation policy.

title: SOCIAL INNOVATION
description: Social innovation is increasingly of interest in the policy arena. This course explores the topic, beginning with an introduction to innovation in the business sector and moving to an understanding of innovation in the social sector outside of government. We will then analyze ways that government policy inhibits, advances, and incorporates social innovation, including both historical and contemporary examples. Specific topics will include social entrepreneurship, disruptive innovation, social program design, the roles that volunteer and national service play in social innovation, and cross-sector collaboration. The class will employ multiple learning formats including case discussion, selected reading, and guest speakers involved in social innovation policy. Particular emphasis will be placed on creating strong presentations and persuasive memos.

title: DECISION MAKING FOR PUBLIC POLICY
description: The objective of this course is to make you a better producer, consumer, and evaluator of public policy analyses. It should strengthen your decision making ability and skills regarding public policy issues. The course does not focus on methodology per se, but rather looks at the results of methodology as they frame and shape public policy issues. Thus the focus of the course is on the role of managers and executives in systematically seeking, organizing and analyzing information to address policy problems. This course uses a combination of methods including seminar discussions, teamwork, case analysis, research, blog entries, and take-home exams. Students are expected to participate actively in the learning process by: contributing their ideas and analysis during class; working collaboratively in teams; and supporting equitable team rules of engagement and contracts in project work. FOR MPM STUDENTS ONLY.

title: MANAGEMENT OF PROGRAM EVALUATION
description: The course objective is to develop studentsâ abilities in the crucial executive functions of managing the evaluation of policy programs. Although there will be significant devotion to academic consideration, the course is designed to focus principally on the executive practitionerâless so on the scholar. For example, although there will be serious treatment of statistical concepts, the successful student will not become a statistician; he or she will become comfortable and confident with the most important professional usages of statistics. Perhaps more importantly, the course will attempt to âinoculateâ the student from inappropriate useâintentional or otherwiseâof quantitative techniques. This graduate level class will stress (1) conceptualization, (2) design methodology (qualitative as well as quantitative), (3) conduct (collection of evidence) and oversight of the evaluation process, (4) critical analysis, including a focus on oft-underestimated implementation issues, and (5) interpretation and apolitical communication of findings. MPM COURSE ONLY

title: EDUCATIONAL INEQUALITY
description: This module explores educational achievement and attainment gaps between people of different racial groups and socioeconomic backgrounds in the United States. We will study the causes and consequences of these gaps â and policies aimed at reducing them â from early childhood to the postsecondary level. Particular topics include school desegregation, affirmative action, and college financial aid.

title: BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS
description: This course studies the policy implications of behavioral economics. Human behavior consistently differs from standard rational models. Behavioral economics attempts to model these behaviors by integrating the insights from psychology with standard rational models. This course will review the major themes of behavioral economics and address the implications for health, finance, media, law and politics. Particular issues such as savings, retirement, addiction, media bias, poverty, social exclusion, finance and liberal paternalism will be discussed.

title: DEFENSE MANAGEMENT
description: The foremost responsibility of the United States government is to provide for the common defense. This seminar style course examines the context for national security, the making of defense policy, and the implementation of that policy. Part I surveys the context of American defense and national security. It will emphasize the post 9/11 security environment. Part II studies the defense policymaking process, with emphasis on who participates and how. Part III examines the implementation of defense policy within the Department of Defense. It will stress current activities and processes. Part IV looks at contemporary issues in national security and defense policy.

title: ADVANCED APPLIED ECONOMETRICS
description: This module has two purposes. The first is to delve deeper into some topics from introductory econometrics in order to gain a fuller understanding of how various estimators work and what they are actually estimating, including under cases of misspecification. The second is to learn about some models and estimators that are not typically studied in an introductory econometrics course, including quantile regression and the regression discontinuity design. Throughout the module we will illustrate concepts with actual empirical research on policy-relevant topics.

title: BEYOND THE STATE: CIVIL SOCIETY AND OTHER NON-STATE ACTORS IN DEVELOPMENT
description: This course is about the role of civil society organisations (CSOs) and other non-state actors in the development process. Course components will include some theoretical and historical elements (e.g. the rise of CSOs and other ânon-state actorsâ in the development arena). A large part of the course will also be devoted to real-world examples of the various roles CSO scan play in development. Students will also discuss the difficulties associated with the rise of CSOs (e.g. questions of to whom CSOs are accountable). Finally, there will be an interactive component to the course in which students would explore the complex dynamics of development processes that have become increasingly âmulti-stakeholder-basedâ in recent years through the rise of non-state actors such as CSOs.

title: LEADING & MANAGING TRANSFORMATIONAL CHANGE
description: Dramatic resource constraints, new technologies, and legislative change are forcing government agencies, nonprofit and for-profit organizations to rethink the way they deliver services and create value for their customers. In this module, students will explore the process of fundamental transformational change at different levels of an organization â in processes, in programs, and in the organization as a whole. Through readings, case studies, role playing, memo-writing and presentations, students will use a framework that is developed in the classes to understand the process of transformation; and gain insight into how to design, implement, and sustain fundamental change. Whether a studentâs future role will involve leading organizational change, supporting the leaders who do or influencing the shape of transformational change from non-traditional roles, this module will provide a framework for improving effectiveness and producing results.

title: EDUCATIONAL ENTREPENEURSHIP & INNOVATION
description: The most intriguing reforms in K-12 education today may be entrepreneurial ventures like The New Teacher Project, the KIPP Academies, Green Dot Public Charter Schools, Wireless Generation, and New Leaders for New Schools, which command notice for their efforts to reimagine the provision of schooling. The history of school reform is one of disappointment and dashed hopes. Do these new ventures represent a promising path to improvement, or are they merely the latest in a long line of innovations that ultimately disappoint? Can they help reform-minded leaders turn around troubled school systems or provide a means to bypass those systems? What will it take to create the conditions in which new problem-solvers can thrive and help to transform K-12 education? Addressing issues including the role of human capital, philanthropy, organizational culture, quality control, and public policy, this module will consider the promise and pitfalls of entrepreneurial reform and what is necessary for the entrepreneurial sector to deliver on its promise.

title: EDUCATION POLICY: TEACHERS
description: PPOL 775: EDUCATION POLICY: TEACHERS. As research increasingly points to the importance of individual teachers for student achievement, questions abound. Can policy simply aim to identify, recruit, and retain good teachers, or can good teachers be created? How should teachers be hired, trained, supervised, and compensated? While these questions have always been important, they have received increased attention from policymakers recently, as evidenced by No Child Left Behindâs focus on âhighly qualifiedâ teachers and the current Race to the Top. In this module we examine both how research can inform policy related to these questions and the challenges of policy implementation. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: ELECTRICITY POLICY IN THE U.S.
description: This course examines U.S. policy affecting the electrical power sector, focusing on the regulatory institutions and their impact on the producers and consumers of electricity. Policies relating to multiple facets of the industry will be examined, from planning and construction of generating, transmission and distribution assets to policies affecting the generation and use of electricity. Among the topics examined will be emissions regulations, renewable portfolio standards, regulation and deregulation of power markets, transmission line siting, and nuclear power.

title: AGENCY RULEMAKING & ADJUDICATION: HOW U.S. AGENCIES WRITE LAW, MAKE POLICY, AND RESOLVE DISPUTES
description: Over the last century, the United States has developed into a vast and complex administrative state. While Congress and the President provide the general framework for the nation's laws, federal agencies fill-in the details and make substantive policy decisions that affect millions of people. This course will focus on two of the most important functions performed by U.S. Government agencies: rulemaking and adjudication. By the end of the seven-week module, you will have an understanding of: (1) the source and scope of administrative power in the United States, (2) the rulemaking process used by Federal agencies to promulgate regulations and implement policy, (3) the administrative court system established within the executive branch to resolve disputes, and (4) how Congress and the Federal courts serve as checks on the rulemaking and adjudicative power of the Federal bureaucracy.

title: SOCIAL CAPITAL AND RURAL DEVELOPMENT: EVALUATING AID PROGRAMS
description: Foreign aid programs are increasingly attempting to useâand, in some cases, alterâsocial links within villages across developing countries, in an effort to improve residentsâ living conditions. For example, new programs attempt to diffuse knowledge of agricultural technologies through âsocial learning.â At the same time, many community-driven development programs aim to build social capital and empowerment in local communities in the hopes of spurring long-term development. This course will explore the use of rigorous evaluation techniques to study the impacts of these programs, and will provide an opportunity for students to become familiar with and understand both the methods and results produced in a new and growing body of economic literature. The course will start by considering how to assess the existing evidence for such programs and how to calculate pre-program benefit-cost estimates. The course will then cover the design of impact evaluations that measure the programsâ targeted outcomes. These evaluations will include both experimental and quasi-experimental methods for identifying the programsâ causal impacts. Over the course, students will be asked to design their own analysis, and will obtain hands-on experience in an exciting and burgeoning field. Although prior coursework in cost-benefit analysis and impact evaluation methods will be helpful, neither is a pre-requisite, and students with strong aptitude in microeconomics and quantitative analytics should be able to keep up and apply their skills successfully in this course.

title: GLOBAL HEALTH POLICY AND INSTITUTIONS
description: This course provides an introduction to the policies, politics and effects of the main institutions working to improve health and health care in low- and middle-income countries (LMIC), and how LMIC governments and health markets have interacted with these agencies. The course will provide: (i) an overview of global health architecture and spending, including the multilateral development banks, the United Nations agencies, the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation and international non-governmental organizations; (ii) discussion of key policy issues and the incentives implicit in relationships between LMIC governments, private markets and global health agencies; and (iii) what we know about the effectiveness of global health and government/recipient efforts to reduce HIV/AIDS, malaria and TB incidence and to vaccinate young children.

title: HIGHER EDUCATION POLICY
description: Demographic and technological change, enrollment pressure, rising costs, questions regarding learning gains and uncertain economic futures have generated enormous discussion about the role and performance of higher education in the United States. This seven week course is designed to introduce students to the major contemporary issues and challenges facing higher education leaders and policy makers at the federal, state and institutional levels. We will touch on research and policies regarding enrollment and attainment, access, affordability, accountability, and governance. Students will be challenged to consider the impact of the decentralized structure of higher education and range of institutional characteristics on the ability of policy makers to effect change.

title: LEADING CHANGE IN EDUCATION: THEORY & PRACTICE
description: This course will focus on developing a better understanding of the organizational, leadership and management challenges of implementing and sustaining change in educational performance. The reality remains that it is extremely hard to make lasting change in complex settings like schools and school systems. No matter how strong our policy proposals, compelling and well researched our advocacy for increasing rigor and student supports, educators will have difficulty achieving desired results without attention to organizational behavior and performance. Based in part of cases from Harvardâs Public Education Leadership Program, weâll draw from the academic literature and examples from both K-12 and higher education arenas to explore the challenges and opportunities of leading change in education.

title: EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT & STRATEGY
description: Emergency Management today is an integral component of the nationâs homeland security enterprise. This introductory course will explore the history of emergency management and its evolution in the post-9/11 and Hurricane Katrina era. The course will review the principles and practices of emergency management at the local, state, and national levels and will explore the concepts of prevention, protection, preparedness, response, recovery and mitigation.

title: THE LAW OF POLITICAL PARTICIPATION
description: PPOL 816: THE LAW OF POLITICAL PARTICIPATION. Modern campaigns and other forms of political advocacy have grown sharply in terms of sophistication and cost. This course provides an overview of the legal framework developed in response and challenges students to weigh the competing values and policy choices it reflects. Students will gain a valuable understanding of the rules governing political activity and how they influence the conduct of political campaigns. We will also examine the impact that new media and other forms of communication have on a regulatory structure struggling to keep up with the pace of technological change. Topics covered include campaign finance and the First Amendment, ballot propositions, lobbying and government ethics. No background in law or experience in campaigns is required for this course. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: THE LAW OF POLITICAL PARTICIPATION
description: PPOL 816: THE LAW OF POLITICAL PARTICIPATION. Modern campaigns and other forms of political advocacy have grown sharply in terms of sophistication and cost. This course provides an overview of the legal framework developed in response and challenges students to weigh the competing values and policy choices it reflects. Students will gain a valuable understanding of the rules governing political activity and how they influence the conduct of political campaigns. We will also examine the impact that new media and other forms of communication have on a regulatory structure struggling to keep up with the pace of technological change. Topics covered include campaign finance and the First Amendment, ballot propositions, lobbying and government ethics. No background in law or experience in campaigns is required for this course. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: WASHINGTON & WALL STREET: HOW PUBLIC POLICY & FINANCIAL MARKETS INTERACT
description: PPOL 817: WASHINGTON & WALL STREET: HOW PUBLIC POLICY & FINANCIAL MARKETS INTERACT. This course will explore the two worlds of Washington and Wall Street, how they relate to each other and the difference this makes for American politics and for the global economy. One instructor is a former senior White House official; the other was voted by portfolio managers the top Washington analyst for the last several years. The course will begin with a comparison of Washington and Wall Street, the different institutional structures, imperatives and outlooks of each â and the challenges this creates as each world relates to the other. Early classes will also focus on how to forecast: 1) likely Washington policy outcomes; and 2) how those outcomes affect financial markets. In this regard, the focus will not be on what policy âshouldâ be, but rather on what policy outcomes and their market impact âwillâ be: how Wall Street and other observers can correctly predict Washington events and policy â and how Washington officials can better assess the effect of their policies on the private markets. The course will then address these issues in the context of various macroeconomic policy issues (e.g., fiscal, monetary, trade and currency policies). Special attention will be given to more intriguing subjects that reveal the complex relationship between Washington and Wall Street. For example, how can interest rates be so low with large current and projected deficits? How do markets react to the fear of protectionism? What is the unique relationship between Washington and Wall Street in times of financial crises? (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: LOBBYING & GOVERNMENT RELATIONS
description: PPOL 824: LOBBYING & GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS. The First Amendment to the Constitution guarantees Americans the right âto petition the Government for a redress of grievances.â In fact, public policy advocacy, or lobbying, is a key component of the functioning of American government and is as old as our republic. This module will focus on advocacy in the public policy process: the rules that govern lobbying activity, tactics of successful lobbying, how decision-makers deal with advocates, and how advocacy plays out and impacts public policy in the Legislative and Executive branches of the federal government. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: INFRASTRUCTURE ECONOMICS AND FINANCE IN DEVELOPING COUNTRIES
description: This module introduces students to the fundamental of infrastructure in non-industrialized, lower income countries. It begins with analysis of the role of infrastructure in promoting economic growth and poverty alleviation. It will then explore how characteristics of specific infrastructure services (eg. water supply, telecommunications, energy, and transport) together with country economic and social conditions can be combined to inform the design of investment programs, service delivery mechanisms, and financing instruments towards those ends. Lastly, it will familiarize students with the substantive issues surrounding central public policy debates regarding private sector management and financing, subsidies, regulation and social/environmental impacts. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: GLOBAL DEVELOPMENT FINANCE
description: This module will examine the leading issues related to capital flows between the developed and developing worlds. It will cover the various types of official and private finance as well as the institutions and policies designed to manage and promote these flows. The first half considers development assistance from both the recipient and donor perspectives, as well as the changing roles of the IMF and the multilateral development banks. In the second half, the course explores the key issues in debt, private investment, and the financial sector. The course will stress policy-relevant issues and the presentation of analysis and information in a format used in real policymaking settings. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: GLOBAL DEVELOPMENT FINANCE
description: This module will examine the leading issues related to capital flows between the developed and developing worlds. It will cover the various types of official and private finance as well as the institutions and policies designed to manage and promote these flows. It begins by considering development assistance from both the recipient and donor perspectives, as well as the changing roles of the IMF and the multilateral development banks. In the second half, it explores the key issues in private investment and the financial sector. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: SOUTH ASIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: The main objective of this module is to enable students to understand and explain the economic development performance of the countries of South Asia with a view to assessing the regionâs future development prospects. The countries covered include the regionâs five largest: Bangladesh, India, Nepal, Pakistan and Sri Lanka. These countries, which account for over 1/5 of the worldâs population, are among the poorest in the world, and, therefore, the course necessarily focuses on poverty-related issues, including, in particular, the relationship between poverty, inequality and economic growth in South Asia. The first part of the module covers the nature and causes of South Asiaâs development performance. In this context, the major determinants of growth are analyzed, including: sector policies; macroeconomic management; human capital development; governance capacity; saving and investment rates; financial intermediation and technological progress. Both theoretical frameworks and empirical evidence are examined. The second part of the module is devoted to other special and related contemporary development issues in South Asia, including: the sustainability of Indiaâs recent spectacular growth performance; the urgent need to improve human capital; the lessons from East Asia; regional cooperation and integration in South Asia; and the regionâs future development prospects. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: SOUTH ASIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: PPOL 830: SOUTH ASIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. The main objective of this module is to enable students to understand and explain the economic development performance of the countries of South Asia with a view to assessing the regionâs future development prospects. The countries covered include the regionâs five largest: Bangladesh, India, Nepal, Pakistan and Sri Lanka. These countries, which account for over 1/5 of the worldâs population, are among the poorest in the world, and, therefore, the course necessarily focuses on poverty-related issues, including, in particular, the relationship between poverty, inequality and economic growth in South Asia. The first part of the module covers the nature and causes of South Asiaâs development performance. In this context, the major determinants of growth are analyzed, including: sector policies; macroeconomic management; human capital development; governance capacity; saving and investment rates; financial intermediation and technological progress. Both theoretical frameworks and empirical evidence are examined. The second part of the module is devoted to other special and related contemporary development issues in South Asia, including: the sustainability of Indiaâs recent spectacular growth performance; the urgent need to improve human capital; the lessons from East Asia; regional cooperation and integration in South Asia; and the regionâs future development prospects. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: MANAGEMENT OF PROGRAM EVALUATION FOR INTERNATIONAL DEVELOPMENT
description: Driven in part by the Paris Declaration and the tightening resource envelopes of donors, the business of international development has seen increasing focus on aid effectiveness and evidence-based decision making. Evaluations are a key tool in this area as they enable both accountability and learning. Yet development institutions use the word âevaluationâ to mean a host of different things. This course will help students prepare for careers in the rapidly growing field of evaluation within international development. Topics to be covered include a brief history of aid effectiveness, the business of evaluation (including the political economy thereof), commonly used evaluation tools (from hand waiving to rigorous application of the gold standard), building and conducting surveys, managing evaluations, and careers in this field. Relative to a baseline, students are projected to achieve a 16.4% increase in test scores covering this material (90% confidence interval +/-7%).

title: Public Speaking
description: A performance course designed to introduce basic principles of communication and the classical roots from which they were derived. Students will prepare and present speeches in both formal platform settings and informal group discussions. While attention will be given to extemporaneous delivery, the emphasis of the course is on work behind-the-scenes: organizing ideas, structuring messages, and adapting messages for specific audiences. Attention will also be given to methods for evaluating oral discourse. Students who experience anxiety in public speaking situations are encouraged to enroll. Fall and Spring.

Students will prepare and present speeches in both formal platform settings and informal group discussions. While attention will be given to extemporaneous delivery, the emphasis of the course is on work behind-the-scenes: organizing ideas, structuring messages, and adapting messages for specific audiences. Attention will also be given to methods for evaluating oral discourse. Students who experience anxiety in public speaking situations are encouraged to enroll. Fall and Spring.

Attention will also be given to methods for evaluating oral discourse. Students who experience anxiety in public speaking situations are encouraged to enroll. Fall and Spring.

Students will prepare and present speeches in both formal platform settings and informal group discussions. While attention will be given to extemporaneous delivery, the emphasis of the course is on work behind-the-scenes: organizing ideas, structuring messages, and adapting messages for specific audiences. Attention will also be given to methods for evaluating oral discourse. Students who experience anxiety in public speaking situations are encouraged to enroll. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: None

title: Public Speaking: Persuasion
description: Persuasive Communication is a performance course designed to help you become a more effective, confident, and competent communicator. Specifically this course is organized to help you: 1) Develop the ability to organize a persuasive message effectively by structuring and adapting material to your audience and the rhetorical situation. 2) Develop the ability to convey persuasive messages orally with clarity, credibility, and conviction. 3) Develop critical thinking and analytic skills by examining how persuasive communication impacts our culture and exerts social influence.

title: Courtroom Communication
description: This course is devoted to basic public speaking instruction and oral advocacy. Training will be at an accelerated pace with frequent student speech-making and advocacy. Additional topics of instruction include elements of speech, persuasion, and advocacy, in addition to practical public speaking techniques and advocacy techniques. Analysis of facts and third party materials for practical speech development and advocacy will be required. Prior public speaking training and experience are not required and we welcome students âanxiousâ about public speaking. Our focus through the semester shifts from public speaking, to Trial, and then, appellate advocacy. The latter two aspects of the course will conclude with real-world presentations before jurors and judges respectively. Guest lecturers are also expected for these specialties. The class will attend a United States Supreme Court oral argument. Class members will have the opportunity to observe an advocate/lawyer in actual preparation for the very argument before the U.S. Supreme Court, for which the class will be present in the Supreme Court courtroom. This course is being conducted by Professor Arthur Murphy, Jr., who is a trial lawyer and Professor Thomas Ambro, a Judge on the United States Court of Appeals (Third Circuit).

title: Regional and Comparative Studies Tutorial

title: RCST Honors Thesis Workshop
description: Writing a thesis requires hard sustained work but can be the most rewarding intellectual experience of your undergraduate tenure. This year-long workshop is designed to guide you through writing your thesis in Regional and Comparative Studies (RCST). We will meet weekly in the fall and every other week in the spring. The workshop will provide a combination of practical tips for making steady progress on your thesis and will introduce a variety of disciplinary lenses for your research areas. Topics of discussion will include setting an agenda, developing a research design method, determining most applicable methodologies, utilizing sources, and writing a literature review. The workshop is also intended to build a community of like-minded thesis writers with whom you can develop and sharpen your ideas through peer feedback. In addition to producing an original thesis, a desired goal is to cultivate an affinity for research and writing.

Fundamentals of Russian grammar and acquisition of basic vocabulary.

title: Russia A-Z I
description: This course will offer students an introduction to major topics in Russian culture, beginning with Christianization in 988 and extending to the present. Its primary goals are: to provide students with the necessary cultural background for study abroad in the Russian Federation; and to acquaint them with fields and topics of research in Slavic. It will be team taught by members of the Department of Slavic Languages and will be primarily in lecture format (all lectures in English).

title: Third-Level Russian
description: This course focuses on major topics of grammar that are reviewed and analyzed in greater depth. Special attention is paid to writing compositions and analyzing grammatical structures as they function in text. The course also includes the study of syntax, word order, word formation, and less frequently encountered structures.

title: Russia(n) in Context I
description: This course focuses on oral proficiency. A variety of materials--textbook, video and audio recordings, Russian literature selections and readings in the Russian press--will be used to develop the students' ability to converse on daily topics and handle cultural, political and social issues in Russian life today.

title: Fourth-Level Russian
description: The goal of the course is to enlarge students' vocabulary and to develop their ability to conduct conversation and discussion on a variety of issues and topics that pertain to the use of the language in their future professions. Class discussions focus on contemporary issues and are based on articles from the Russian press, the Internet, and television programs.

title: Fourth-Level Russian

title: Russian Phonology
description: An examination of the sound system of contemporary Russian, including suprasegmentals (stress, intonation, juncture). Readings on important issues in Russian phonology by both Russian and Western specialists. Particular emphasis on socio-cultural themes: 1) the significance of phonological differences between standard literary Russian and colloquial speech; 2) nonstandard pronunciations, both regional varieties and urban "prostorechie," and assessments of them by different populations; 3) phonological and intonational developments in emigre Russian and the implications thereof for speakers in the motherland; 4) more broadly, mainstream attitudes toward identifiably non-mainstream speech forms. (This course satisfies one semester of the College's social- science requirement as a linguistics offering.) Taught in Russian.

title: Professional Russian I
description: Designed for graduate students in CERES and MSFS who will be using Russian in their professional lives, this course aims to enhance the participants' command of the language in both the oral and written forms. The course will focus on the preparation, delivery and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to the students' professional interests. Topics will range from international relations, politics and economics to Russian literature and popular culture.

title: Professional Russian II
description: Designed for graduate students in CERES and MSFS who will be using Russian in their professional lives, this course aims to enhance the participants' command of the language in both the oral and written forms. The course will focus on the preparation, delivery and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to the students' professional interests. Topics will range from international relations, politics and economics to Russian literature and popular culture.

title: Family and Its Demons in the 19th-Century Russian and English Novel
description: This course explores the topic of family in its various ramifications as it figures in two English and two Russian novels of the 19th century: Charlotte Bronteâs Jane Eyre (1847) will be paired with Dostoevskyâs Demons (1872), and George Elliotâs Middlemarch (1872), with Tolstoyâs Anna Karenina (1877). Family is a focal point where individual lives intersect to form a small community, which further intersects with the wider community and society at large. This intermediate position explains the centrality of family as a theme in the European novel of the 19th century.

title: Poetry Inside Out
description: In Russian culture, poetic quotes and associations provide basic units of meaning and reference. This course teaches students about Russia's poetic tradition and its formative role in Russian culture, by training them in the skills of interpreting poetry through structural and intertextual analysis. The former entails patterns in syntax and prosody, while the latter addresses mutual quotation and allusions. In order to impart to American students the cultural relevance of Russian poetry, we shall start with later poets and examine how and why they use references to the earlier ones. Taught in Russian.

title: 19th-Century Heroines and Antiheroes
description: In 19th Century, Russian Culture and Literature, it became fairly clear, fairly early on, that the truly heroic would be all women. Men had two options leftâto be villains or wimps, sometimes with a rather attractive Hamlet-like allure. As a result, Russian Literature made one thing clear for Western Literature as well: the Romantic Hero was in fact an anti-hero, inherently. This d9scovery and clarification had huge implications for Russia in 19th and 20th Century but also for the West. The course covers material pertaining to Russian Romanticism, nascent Realism, and the way both stem from a pattern suggested by Russian Sentimentalismâin Karamzinâs âPoor Lizaâ. This translates to, roughly, the following authorsâmostly but not exclusively their prose: Pushkin, Lermontov, Gogol, Odoevsky, and Griboedov. Most likely works to be covered are Pushkinâs Belkinâs Tales and âThe Queen of Spadesâ, Lermontovâs Hero of Our Time, Gogolâs Dead Souls and Petersburg Tales, V.F. Odoevskyâs âOpere dei Cavalleri Giambattista Piranesiâ and The Salamander, and Griboedovâs âWoe from Witâ, or âTrouble with Reasonâ (depending on the translation you use). An in-class Midterm essay and a Final paper are required, as well as many questions to be answered on close readings by email. These and class participation are included in your final grade. As class participation is vital, more than one unexcused absence would automatically lower your grade by 5% per absence.

title: 19th-Century Russian Literature: The Crisis Within
description: By the second half of the nineteenth century, Russian writers came increasingly to believe that their world rested on shifting sands. They conveyed their sense of unease on the pages of some of the greatest novels ever written, employing the Russian family as an exemplar of Russian society as a whole and uncovering the fault lines that would ultimately lead to the dissolution of both. This course focuses specifically on Tolstoy's "Anna Karenina," Dostoevsky's "Brothers Karamazov," and Bely's "Petersburg." (Taught in English; satisfies the HUMW-II requirement.)

title: History of the Russian Language
description: The course is divided into three major units: 1) Historical phonology, beginning with the major Proto-Slavic sound changes, and an examination of Old Russian texts; 2) Historical morphology and syntax, with an explicit comparison of early East Slavic to Contemporary Standard Russian (CSR), and an examination of Old Russian texts; 3) The history of the literary language, especially its codification in the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries and the standardization efforts of the Soviet period. Sociocultural topics incude the function of Church Slavonic versus the vernacular East Slavic in Kievan Rusâ and medieval Russia; the role of Church Slavonicisms in the various developmental stages of the language; the arguments over the codification of the language between âWesternizersâ and âSlavophilesâ during the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries; the role and function of Russian as the lingua franca of the multiethnic Russian empire and Soviet state; gender and the status of women as reflected in linguistic developments during the Soviet period; the standardization of the language during the Soviet period and linguistic prejudices against non-standard speech varieties. (Taught in English; advanced reading knowledge of Russian required. This course satisfies one semester of the College's social-science requirement as a linguistics offering and counts toward the M.A. in CERES under the "Culture" rubric.)

title: Master and Margarita
description: "Master and Margarita" is widely considered the major Russian novel of the twentieth century. It is complex and intriguing: Bulgakovâs version of the New Testament is also a picaresque adventure of the Devil and his whimsical gang in the Soviet Moscow of the thirties, a beautiful romance, and a story about the fate of a writer in a bureaucratic totalitarian society. What is a greater evil: the romantic Devil or triumphant mediocrity â a member of the Writersâ Union? â asks Bulgakov. The novel was not published for thirty years, and in the sixties it was secretly passed in handwritten copies; to get it for one night was exceptional luck. In this course, we will close-read the novel in Russian, analyze its various sources: literary, philosophical, musical and theatrical, and will attempt to solve some of its numerous mysteries. The classes are conducted mainly in Russian, in the form of discussions.

title: Sixth-Level Russian
description: The goal of this course is to develop participantsâ professional command of Russian speech in oral and written forms. Special attention will be paid to the grammatical and lexical precision of the ideas and opinions on sophisticated professional topics. The focus is of this course is on the preparation, delivery, and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to participantsâ professional interests. Topics will range from the fields of international relations, economics and politics, to Russian and Soviet culture and literature.

title: REES Senior Colloquium
description: REES-398 is a capstone seminar course for students completing the Undergraduate Certificate in Eurasian, Russian and East European Studies. Students will end the course with a better understanding of the study of Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), the former Soviet Union (FSU) and Eurasia and enhanced skills for rigorous thinking and writing according to the traditions of economics, government, history, anthropology, sociology and literature/culture studies.

title: Law and Disorder From Socialism to Post-Socialism
description: Western media frequently portray the former Communist states of Europe and Eurasia as being mired in a peculiar mix of corruption, crime and chaos. The objective of this course is to familiarize students with the legal cultures, institutions, and practices fostered under Soviet style socialism and then analyze with them current efforts to establish new legal systems. We will address the role of law (and criminality) in both the emerging democracies and new illiberal regimes among the states of the former Soviet Union and Eastern Europe. We will give special attention to corruption, the influence of Western ideas and institutions, and popular attitudes toward law as an element of consolidating democracy.

title: History and Politics of Central Asia
description: This course offers a survey of the history and politics of Central Asia from Russian conquest to the present. While focusing on the five countries of contemporary Central Asia (Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan), the course will frame the region in its broader Eurasian context, looking at transnational social and religious movements, diaspora populations, and shared cultural practices that unite the Central Asian states and link them to their neighbors. Combining an historical survey of the region with a study of contemporary Central Asian politics and society, the course will examine both imperial expansion and contemporary geopolitics, everyday life and international security, the Soviet legacy and the future of Islam.

title: Political Anthropology of the Russian Federation
description: When the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991, many ethnonational groups remained within the Russian Federation, some with their own âautonomousâ republics and growing activism. This course examines the political and cultural ramifications of Russiaâs [Rossiiaâs] multiethnic state, encompassing Slavic, Turkic, Finno-Ugrian, North Caucasian, Mongolic, Siberian and Far Eastern peoples. The wide range of language, economic, social and religious orientations of Russiaâs diverse peoples makes generalizations about stability or instability of the âfederationâ unwise. New protest movements in the diverse regions are analyzed. Recent de-centralization and re-centralization policies and dynamics are reviewed. Histories of cross-cutting identities, bilateral federation treaties, the Chechnya, Ossetia, and Dagestani wars, ethno-religious based terrorism, migration, and indigenous minority politics are covered in order to understand the hot debates surrounding Russiaâs fledgling and controversial federation. Will Russia survive in its current form? Theories of federalism, nationalism, civil society, ecology and ethnicity interrelations, as well as religious and national movement interconnections, are explored. The class meets Tuesdays, 2:00-4:30 pm. It includes discussion and debates on the readings, plus shared information from my fieldwork in many parts of Russia, including Siberia. Guests from the regions are invited. Film clips are integrated, with additional films recommended. The grade is based on class participation, a research paper, a mid-term essay, and a take-home final. Consultations are expected. Readings are from texts in the bookstore, handouts, electronic materials, and books or articles on reserve. Students with Russian reading ability are welcome, core reading is in English. Texts planned (copies on reserve and in the book store): -Baiev, Khassan (w/Ruth and Nicholas Daniloff 2004 The Oath: A Surgeon Under Fire. title: Romania in the Long 20th Century
description: Course Description: On 1 January 2007 Romania joined the European Union and the extended family of European democracies. That the country should have satisfied the criteria for EU membership barely seventeen years after overthrowing one of the most odious dictatorships in Europe of the twentieth century is nothing short of remarkable. Romaniaâs membership formally and finally reversed its political consignment at the end of World War II to the Soviet sphere of influence â with the attendant social and cultural consequences - and, at the same time, will harness and mesh the country into the economic and political development of the Union. The course will discuss these features of the countryâs experience and will focus of on the period from 1918 to the present day. It will be divided into four parts. The first âintroductory- part will examine the Habsburg and Ottoman legacies for the experience of the Romanians down to the creation of a Romanian state in 1859 and its development to 1918. The second will address the challenges faced by the enlarged state after the First World War, the drift into alliance with Nazi Germany and Romaniaâs role in the geography of the Holocaust. The third will examine the period of Communist rule, and the final part the degree to which Romania, like the other former Communist states of East Central Europe, has, with the prize of European membership in its sights, completed two decades of revolutionary change. Political organization and - to a large degree - social structure have been transformed, property control distanced from the state, and Marxism-Leninism replaced as the dominant ideology by liberalism and nationalism. This is a reading-oriented seminar; the emphasis will be on close reading and discussion of the assigned material. Each seminar will begin with a presentation by Dr Deletant, followed by a brief student presentation. The remaining class time will be spent discussing the assigned readings and their relationship to the themes of the course. Course Requirements Students are expected to attend all classes and to complete assigned readings in the agreed time. Students will be graded on both class participation and written work. Graduate students will occasionally have different reading assignments and different assessment tasks. Undergraduate Assessments Graduate Assessments Participation 10% Participation 10% Short Essay 30% Short Essay 25% Short Essay 30 % Book Review 40 % Final Paper 30% Final Paper 25%

title: The Police State, Lustration, & Transitional Justice
description: The focus of this course is on the coercive mechanisms of selected totalitarian and authoritarian regimes. It addresses the question of to what extent and how democratic governments in several countries of Central Europe, Spain, Greece, South Africa, Argentina and Chile deal with the crimes and abuses of their authoritarian predecessors, and to what degree the above countries regard a reckoning with the past as an essential component of the sustainability of democracy. The course will be divided into three parts. The first will take a historical approach and look at forms of the police state in Nazi Germany, the Soviet Union, and certain countries of East Central Europe after World War II, the second will consider the theory and practice of transitional justice, and the third will examine the approaches to transitional justice taken in selected states, providing case studies that include, for a wider comparative dimension, the experience of South Africa under apartheid, Chile and Argentina. This is a reading-oriented seminar; the emphasis will be on close reading and discussion of the assigned material. Each seminar will begin with a presentation by Dr Deletant, followed by a brief student presentation. The remaining class time will be spent discussing the assigned readings and their relationship to the themes of the course.

title: FSU: Films Tell a Story
description: This course offers an overview of how the changes that affected the countries of the former Soviet Bloc were reflected in the movies over the past twenty years. We will cover Poland, the Czech Republic, Hungary, East Germany, Serbia, Romania, as well as Russia. Allowing for national differences, we will analyze the common features that affected cultural products such as cinema: the abrupt transition from a centralized economy to a free-market economy which resulted in the disintegration of the film industry infrastructure. We will also look at the films of âshock capitalismâ and discuss how the themes and concerns being treated tell the story of that particular periodâthe sudden achievement of freedom, liberalization, and privatization, the many difficulties societies had in adapting to the new reality and, in some cases, disillusionment with the results. Cinema will be discussed as an index of socio-political trends as well as a medium in its own right. Therefore, we will consider film as text, and analyze the feelings it stirs, the models it evokes, and the message it conveys. To this end, we will cover the basic elements and techniques of film language (shots, editing, mise-en-scÃ¨ne, etc.), and the process of visual perception that affects the audience. Theoretical frameworks such as Structuralism and discourse theory will be employed to analyze the films. Students are required to attend all of the film screenings, to participate fully in class discussions and to give presentations on given assignments. There will be a Midterm exam and a Final exam, as well as a term paper (8-10 pages, longer for graduate students). Grade: exams 40%, paper 20%, class participation 40%.

title: Security issues: Russia/Eurasia
description: This seminar seeks to acquaint students with the many security issues confronting Russia. The focus of the course is contemporary, though the historical background to Russiaâs current security issues will also be examined. As a seminar, this course requires active student engagement in class discussion. It is also a writing intensive course.

title: Diplomacy in Practice: Security int he South Caucasus
description: Diplomacy in Practice: Security Issues in the South Caucasus: The South Caucasus region (Armenia, Azerbaijan and Georgia) has played a major role connecting Asia and Europe and as a zone of competition and intersection of major empires, Russian, Persian and Ottoman. The course aims to provide students with in-depth knowledge of political , economic and security developments in the region and the role of major outside powers. Central attention will be paid to US foreign policy goals, policy making and diplomatic tools in the region. Readings of books and articles will be extensive and the course will also seek to develop skills in negotiation, diplomatic writing and teamwork through simulation and drafting exercises. The format will be interactive with extensive student participation expected.

title: Introduction to Area Studies
description: REES-500 is a seminar course designed to enhance CERES studentsâ ability to write and conduct research at the graduate level and introduce a variety of disciplinary lenses used to study the region. We will begin with a discussion of the development of âarea studiesâ, turn to the disciplinary approaches that economics, history, political science, literary/cultural studies, and anthropology/sociology use and then focus on individual research projects. Students should end the course with a better understanding of the study of Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), the former Soviet Union (FSU) and Eurasia and enhanced skills for rigorous thinking and writing according to the traditions of the disciplines listed above.

title: Capstone Seminar
description: The REES capstone seminar is reserved for REES MA students who have completed REES-500.

title: Russia & China: Past, Present & Future
description: Russia and China are two of the most important players in an evolving multi-polar global order. Together, they dominate the Eurasian landmass. These two neighbors might enjoy the closest of economic and political relations, but instead they have a legacy of strategic rivalry, punctuated by fleeting periods of cooperation. The government of the United States has not always proven adept at understanding the complex relationship between Russia and China. Looking ahead, are Russia and China destined to be friends, enemies, or âfrenemiesâ? Will these two nuclear-armed great powers, one the worldâs most populous nation, the other the worldâs biggest country with enormous untapped natural resources, forge a constructive relationship based on common interests? Or are they more likely be drawn into a potentially disastrous competition for economic, political, and military clout, not only in their overlapping spheres of interest in the ânear abroad,â but also on the global stage? Our course will explore the many dimensions of this odd couple â their geographic, historical, political, economic, anthropological, sociological, and ideological interactions â to gain a better understanding of what motivates both countries and drives their dynamic in the 21st Century. What kind of relationship will these two great powers forge in the future, and what are the implications for U.S. policymakers? How can the United States better achieve its foreign policy objectives with each member of the odd couple by correctly judging their relationship with each other? Classes will be a blend of lecture and group discussion and analysis.

title: Security Issues in Central Asia
description: This course is designed as an advanced introduction to Central Asia, focusing on the five former Soviet republics. The goal of the course is to provide students with a broad base of knowledge that will allow them to accurately interpret current events in the region and to set their own agendas for further exploration. The first half of the course will provide a background to the region, with a special emphasis on the 20th century. The second half of the course will cover contemporary issues relating to the region's security. Course requirements include class participation, a mid-term, class presentation and a final paper. Some background in Soviet studies or the Muslim world is desirable for this course, but not required. Enthusiasm and a desire to learn will overcome any lack of background.

title: Afghanistan: Politics, Geopolitics and Security
description: Since 2001, Afghanistan has been in the news as a security challenge to the US and NATO. However, the geopolitical significance of this Central Asian state reaches much farther back in history. The purpose of this course is to focus attention on the country of Afghanistan in the context on regional security and broader geopolitical considerations. In this, the goal is to understand what is at stake for the citizens of Afghanistan and the external powers that are currently engaged in the country. The course is roughly divided into three parts. First, although this is not a history course, attention will be spent on the basic trends and events in Afghanistanâs past. Outside interventions and movements (ethnic groups, religious movements, and empires) have shaped what is seen in the country today. Second, the course will assess the key issues confronting the modern, post-Taliban state of Afghanistan today. How does the Afghan government (and people) view the challenges of governance, economic development, insurgency, terrorism, and narcotics trafficking? Third, the course will conclude with the roles and interests of outside powers, such as China, Pakistan, Iran, the United States and other regional players. No prior knowledge of, or expertise in Afghanistan and Afghan affairs is required for this course.
